0.title: Welcome to the World's End
0.body: This is the setting guide for Stonetop , a hearth fantasy roleplaying game set in an iron age that never was. It describes the PCs' home—the village of Stonetop—and the World's End, the region in which Stonetop sits. It's a gazetteer of the places, peoples, and powers that make up or shape the region. This is Book II ; for the rules of the game and guidance on how to play or run it, see Book I: Stonetop.

1.title: How to use this book
1.breadcrumb: Welcome to the World's End › How to use this book
1.body: If you're the GM, or thinking about running a game of Stonetop, then this book is for you. Before your first session, browse this book to familiarize yourself with the setting and to get excited for play. Start with the maps to get a sense of place. Skim the village of Stonetop entry (page 12). When it mentions a topic that catches your interest, jump to that topic and read it, too. Topics are cross-referenced. If you're reading this as a physical book, use a bookmark or two to keep track of where you came from. If you're reading this as a PDF, all the page references are hyperlinked and the "back" button (or ALT + left arrow in most PDF readers) is your friend. Between sessions, review topics that you think will be relevant soon. Use the details and resources therein to help you prep. During play, refer to this book to help you describe the environment, to ask questions, and to make GM moves. Use it to introduce dangers and discoveries, or to improvise sites for the PCs to explore.

2.title: Exploit the setting guide
2.breadcrumb: Welcome to the World's End › Exploit the setting guide
2.body: This book is a resource, not a bible. It asserts many truths about the setting, but it also poses many questions for you and your group to answer during play. When you establish some truth that contradicts what's written in the book, your group's truths take precedence. Use whatever appeals to you and makes sense for your game. Ignore or change the rest. If you think the Golden Oak would be better as a sacred place out of time, where past and present and future all meet, then by all means make it so in your game.

3.title: Should the players read this?
3.breadcrumb: Welcome to the World's End › Should the players read this?
3.body: Players aren't expected to read this book. There's a Setting Overview in the game's digital files (also downloadable from lampblackandbrimstone.com). Players should definitely read that. It's okay for players to read this book if they want to. Some players like to better understand the setting, to help shape their play or inform their answers to GM questions. Some enjoy that feeling of dread when they recognize what monster the GM is hinting at, or that feeling of awe when the GM introduces an arcanum that they've read about. Just remember that every group's version of Stonetop is different. If you're playing in a game and you've read this book, don't be a snob about it. The lore that you and your group establish in play is what's important. And to be clear: if you're the kind of player who prefers to discover a game's world through play, to experience it like your character would, then don't read this book. You're certainly not expected to!

4.title: The Vicinity
4.breadcrumb: Welcome to the World's End › The Vicinity

5.title: The World's End
5.breadcrumb: Welcome to the World's End › The World's End

6.title: The Village of Stonetop
6.body: Size village ( 300 souls) Population +0 - Prosperity +0 - Trade with... - ... Gordin's Delve (metal, tools) - ... Marshedge (textiles, herbs, glass)

7.title: Resources
7.body: - Farming (beans, potatoes, oats, barley) - Hunting/trapping (fur, meat, hides) - Distilling (whisky) - Stone (taken from the Old Wall) - Cistern (filled with rain, snow) - Tradesfolk (midwife, potter, publican, smith, tanner) - Two draft horses, two iron plows, two carts, a wagon

8.title: Defenses +0
8.body: - Village militia - The Ringwall (low, stone) - Three watchtowers - Spears & shields in every home - Some bows A farming village on the eastern edge of the Flats , on a bluff overlooking the Great Wood . A great, rune-etched megalith stands amidst a cluster of squat stone homes, surrounded by an arc of farmland. The PCs call this village home. It's a tightknit community, poor but a good place to live. Everyone is expected to pull their weight, but few think badly of those who suffer misfortune. The town pulls together; its community is its strength.

9.title: Places
9.body: - The Stone is well over 50 feet tall and etched in faded runes. It seems older than even the Maker -ruins The Makers upon which the village was founded. When storms roll in, lightning strikes the Stone repeatedly. - The Cistern is an underground vault, clearly an artifact of the Makers. Locals fill it with rain, snow, and water from the Stream . - The public house is a large hall, where folks meet after sundown to drink and socialize. It offers floor space to travelers and a small stable under the same roof. The town's horses are kept here. - The granary is a warehouse built on a Maker-ruin foundation. Foodstuffs are stored here. Everyone contributes, everyone shares. - The pavilion of the gods is an openair structure with shrines to Aratis , Danu , Helior , Tor , and maybe others Gods and religion. As close as Stonetop gets to a temple. - The Ringwall is a waist-high fence of fieldstones, 2-3 feet thick. It won't keep out serious raiders, but provides a little cover if needed. Homes beyond it are newer, built by immigrants or younger families. - Watchtowers stand along the Ringwall, a dozen feet tall or so. One to the north, one to the southeast, one to the southwest. Villagers rotate through watch duty at night. - A switchback path leads down the bluff to the Stream and the Great Wood . Climbing it takes 10-15 minutes, longer with a heavy load. - The fields stretch out around the town, about 1,000 acres total. A third of the land sits fallow each year, with the rest growing barley, oats, beans, and potatoes. Farmers work the fields communally, rather than as familial plots. - The Old Wall wraps around the fields, about a mile from the Stone itself and at the end of the West Road . Clearly once a mighty rampart, it has tumbled and been buried by the ages. When the villagers need stone for construction, they dig it out of the old wall and haul it back to town.

10.title: Questions
10.body: - What's your favorite tale about how the Stone came to be? - In what little way do locals interact with the Stone day-to-day? - What do the runes on the Stone make you think of? - What do scholars of the South claim of the Stone's origin? Why do you think that's nonsense? - What's the opening to the Cistern like? A low stone wall? A building over an open shaft? A crack in the earth? Or what? - Has anyone been down in the Cistern, that you know of? What did they supposedly find? - How is the public house laid out? What's the most notable furnishing? - Where do children most like to play? - Where do teens sneak off to for romance?

12.title: Always
12.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Impressions › Always
12.body: - The air: tingling, slight smell of ozone - People and beasts: unfazed by loud noises - Voices: always a little bit raised - Chickens, goats, sheep, pigs, cats, dogs: all about, underfoot, bleating, barking, clucking - Children: playing, crying, yelling, running errands, doing chores - Folks around the Cistern: gossiping and chatting - The public house: music, singing, dancing, chatting

13.title: Activities
13.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Impressions › Always › Activities
13.body: - Cooking, grinding grain, baking - Spinning, weaving, sewing, hand-crafts - Rendering fat, making oil & rushlights - Cleaning pens, coops, homes, clothes - Collecting & hauling water, to and from the Cistern - Smithing, tanning, pottery, midwifery - Maintenance (buildings, clothes, tools, etc.) - Manning the watchtowers at night - Drilling with spear and shield

14.title: Spring
14.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Impressions › Spring
14.body: - Drip drip drip of snow melting from roofs, collecting in jugs and puddles - Petrichor smell on a southerly breeze - Hopeful green poking through dead-brown grass and soil - Bare skin reveling in the still-chilly sun; cheerful voices; smiles and songs

15.title: Activities
15.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Impressions › Spring › Activities
15.body: - Harvesting winter potatoes - Spreading seed, planting beans/potatoes - Harrowing soil (to cover seeds, plantings) - Chasing birds from the fields (child's work) - Spreading manure & plowing fallow fields - Kidding goats, sheep - Picking spring vegetables - Clearing and planting gardens - Harvesting/cutting deadfall for firewood - Fur trapping, light hunting (for meat)

16.title: Questions
16.body: - What do you most look forward to each spring? - What rites precede the planting of the fields?

17.title: Summer
17.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Summer
17.body: - Blinding sun outside, cave-like dark inside - Crops swaying and rippling in the breeze - Fireflies like galaxies over the fields at dusk - Dark storm clouds looming in the south, rumbling, flashing - CRACKOOM of thunder, blinding flash as lightning touches the Stone

18.title: Activities
18.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Summer › Activities
18.body: - Haymaking (from Flats-grass, fallow fields) - Weeding crops/gardens - Spreading manure and replowing fallow fields - Weaning goat kids and lambs - Milking goats, shearing sheep - Picking summer vegetables - Berry-picking from gardens and the Wood - Light hunting/trapping (for meat, not fur)

19.title: Questions
19.body: - What project have you been putting off that you hope to get done this summer? - How does the village observe midsummer?

20.title: Autumn
20.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Autumn
20.body: - Crops bobbing heavy with seed, the Great Wood aflame with color - Work-song, shouts and laughter, grunts of effort - Rosy cheeks, breath visible in the morning air - Cold, steady rain

21.title: Activities
21.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Autumn › Activities
21.body: - Harvesting beans, barley, oats, potatoes - Gleaning fallen seed from fields (child's work) - Threshing, winnowing, sieving, storing crops - Plowing fallows and planting winter potatoes - Picking and preserving autumn vegetables - Breeding goats, sheep - Foraging for nuts, fruits in the Wood - Heavy hunting/trapping (fur & meat) - (If able): harvesting timber from the Foothills

22.title: Questions
22.body: - What is your favorite autumn food or drink? - How does the village celebrate the harvest?

23.title: Winter
23.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Winter
23.body: - Roofs covered in thick white blankets - Trodden-down paths in the snow, brown and gray - Cloaks drawn tight against a bitter wind - Families gathered 'round the hearth, hands busy with inside chores - Yeasty smell of fermenting barley

24.title: Activities
24.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Questions › Winter › Activities
24.body: - Collecting snow for the Cistern - Distilling & aging whisky - Tending to livestock, stockpiling manure - Heavy trapping (for fur) - Hunting as able (for meat) - Slaughtering/butchering livestock as needed

25.title: Questions
25.body: - What's the worst/best part of these long cold nights? - How do locals mark midwinter/the new year?

26.title: Names
26.body: Welsh and Welsh-inspired: Aderyn, Aeronwen, Afanen, Afon, Alun, Andras, Aneirin, Awstin, Bedwyr, Berwyn, Betrys, Braith, Briallen, Bronwen, Bryn, Cadi, Cadoc, Cadwygan, Caron, Cefin, Ceinwen, Ceridwyn, Cerys, Colwyn, Deiniol, Dilwen, Dylis, Eifion, Eirlys, Eluned, Emrys, Enfys, Eurwen, Gaenor, Garet, Gethin, Glyndir, Heledd, Hywel, Ifan, Iorwerth, Iwan, Leuca, Lewela, Linos, Mado, Maldwyn, Malon, Mared, Marged, Martyn, Meirion, Menwen, Mererid, Neirin, Nia, Ofydd, Olwyn, Owain, Padrig, Parry, Pryce, Pryder, Rheinal, Rhisiart, Rhosyn, Rydderch, Sawyl, Siana, Sioned, Talfryn, Tegid, Tiwlip, Tomos, Tudyr, Winifred, Yorath.

27.title: People
27.body: Around 300 souls live in Stonetop, or about 50 families. Everyone knows everyone else, at least by name and reputation. Most families have been here for many generations, tied together by kinship and marriage. The village supports only a few full time tradesfolk : a smith, a tanner, a potter, a midwife, and a publican. (5 souls, plus 5-10 assistants/apprentices) Hunters and trappers ply the wood for meat and fur, and also forage for fruits, vegetables, mushrooms, and useable wood. Trappers produce much of the village's wealth, but hunters range farther and have more prestige. (10-20 souls) Farmers work the fields, producing the village's staple foods. Hard, physical work. Most practice "minor" trades on the side, like carpentry, stonecutting, thatching, husbandry, distilling, etc. ( 75 souls) Homemakers keep the village running. They cook, clean, tend gardens, spin, weave, sow, mind children, practice a dozen minor trades, and otherwise manage a household. Families might have 2 or 3 homemakers each, from across generations. ( 100 souls) Elders have retired from the fields or the rigors of homemaking, but still help as they can. Many still serve the village as keepers of lore and wisdom. ( 25 souls) Children play and learn and grow. They also help with chores, tend livestock, chase birds from the fields, forage near the Stream, haul water, mind younger children, and so forth, gaining more and more responsibilities as they grow. ( 100 souls)

28.title: Home & hearth
28.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Home & hearth
28.body: Homes are low, squat, made of stone from the Old Wall. Roofs are thatched with barley straw. Each is heated by a central hearth. Most have small cellars, a standing loom, a small vegetable garden, and pens for livestock (inside and out). Families pass homes down between generations. Larger families might occupy two or even three adjacent buildings. New homes are occasionally built just outside the Ringwall. Homemakers grind grain with quernstones (as the village has no mill), to make flatbread, porridge, stew, ale, and whisky mash. They catch rainwater in clay vessels or draw water from the Cistern. Washing happens down at the Stream , but folks rarely go alone. Clothes are mostly leather, fur, and handspun wool. Belts, pouches, torcs, and brooches are common. Shoes are cut from single pieces of leather, with tabs that wrap up and around the feet, laced together into a simple sandal or pump. To make "boots," folks add thick wool socks, maybe lined with fur. Timber is relatively scarce. Because of the Compact , villagers won't fell a living tree from the Great Wood. Folks scavenge deadfall for firewood and smaller projects, and grow saplings around the fields and Old Wall for spears, bows, and arrows. Large-scale construction requires trading for timber or a logging trip to the Foothills . - Where does \ \ \ live? - What task or chore are you working on? - What's the best/worst thing about this chore? - What's cooking on the hearth? - What here makes the place feel like home? - What about your home would you change if you could?

29.title: Trade & commerce
29.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Trade & commerce
29.body: Stonetop's economy is based on cooperation and sharing, trade and barter, gifts and favors, debts and honor. "Shops" aren't a thing and prices aren't fixed. Coins are rarely used between neighbors; that stuff's for outsiders and passing merchants. Crops from the fields go to the granary, to be rationed out to households. Each family tends its own gardens (vegetables, gourds, fruits, and herbs) and livestock (chickens, goats, sheep, some pigs). Folks practice trades part-time (spinning, weaving, sewing, leatherwork, thatching, carpentry, distilling whisky, stonecutting, husbandry, etc.). Neighbors swap goods and favors freely, with little worry about trades being "fair." If you need a thing that someone isn't using, they'll probably let you borrow or even take it. Maybe you help them fix their roof next month, or your husband sends over a jug of his next batch of whisky. Hunters, trappers, foragers, and full-time tradesfolk tend to freely share their skills and goods, just as the farmers share their crops. Still, they are often awash in gifts of gratitude from other households, and these positions all carry respect and status in town. Merchants come through at least once each season (except winter). Those coming from Marshedge bring textiles, rope, salt, herbs, glass, and finer goods from Lygos and the South . Merchants coming from Gordin's Delve bring metals, tools, and sometimes timber. Both are keen to acquire whisky and furs. Merchants heading to Gordin's Delve are always interested in grain and livestock. - What sorts of livestock does your household keep? - What from your (family's) garden are you (they) most proud of? - Who taught you how to \ \ \ ? - Who in the village is the best weaver/carpenter/\ \ \ ? - Who in town has a reputation as a layabout? - Who tends to handle trade with outsiders?

30.title: Whisky
30.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Trade & commerce › Whisky
30.body: Distilling whisky is a local tradition. Most households have a basic still and make a batch or two each year. Ferment some barley mash, distill it into moonshine, age it for a few weeks in a ceramic jug with toasted wood chips. Repeat. A handful of locals (like the publican, perhaps?) have larger operations with charred wooden firkins or barrels. Stonetop whisky is a much-valued trade good, one of the village's chief exports. The secrets of making it are poorly understood outside the village, and even emigrants have struggled to reproduce it in other locales. - Who in town makes the best whisky? - According to local lore, where did the art of distilling whisky come from? - Why do you think outsiders have failed to reproduce this process? - What secret ingredient/step sets your (family's) whisky apart?

31.title: Governance
31.body: Stonetop has no government, just leaders. Everyone knows who they are: the wise, the cunning, the brave. It's not a formal thing. - When big decisions need to be made, how does the village make them? (A big meeting? A council of elders? Or what?) - Who rations out crops from the granary? - Whose lead do the farmers follow? - To whom do the homemakers listen? - Whom do the hunters/trappers respect? - To whom do folks look to settle disputes/break up fights/deal with trouble? - What consequences are traditional for lying/theft/assault/murder/etc.?

32.title: Defense
32.body: Able-bodied adults are expected to know how to fight and protect the village when needed. Everyone keeps a spear, a round wooden shield, and maybe a bow. Folks rotate through guard duty, manning the watchtowers each night. The chief concerns are monsters from the Great Wood , especially crinwin , and bandits or Hillfolk raiding from the Flats . Most nights, though, the worst things you have to deal with are boredom, nerves, and bad weather. - Who runs drills for the militia? Where do they typically drill? - Have the watchtowers always been there, or are they a recent thing? - How are watchtower shifts assigned? - What's the worst part of watch duty? - What's the best part? - How do you keep yourself awake? - How do those on watch signal trouble?

33.title: Culture and lore
33.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Culture and lore
33.body: Stonetop shares a tongue with Marshedge , though the accents are quite distinct. Much lore is passed on verbally, through stories, songs, and poems. Local decorations are reminiscent of the runes etched in the Stone itself, and of the designs found on the Maker-ruins from which the village was built. Tor (Rain-maker, Thunderhead, Slayer-of-Beasts, Tor, the Rain-maker) is the most popular god, and associated with the Stone itself. The pavilion of the gods also holds shrines to Aratis , Danu , Helior , and perhaps to other local gods or spirits . - What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to the people of Stonetop? - What game/pastime/tradition is unique to Stonetop? - What's your favorite tale of Stonetop's history? - What's the scariest story that the elders tell? - What's the most obvious way in which the locals honor Tor? - How do folks mark or celebrate a birth? - How do folks mark one's coming of age? - What do folks do with their dead? - Do folks revere/recognize any local spirits (of house or hearth, of field or stream, of their ancestors, etc.)? How so?

34.title: Hooks
34.body: - Two youths wish to wed, despite objections from (one of?) their parents - Tools left out have gone missing; now things start to vanish from inside homes - One villager kills another when a heated argument gets out of hand - A flock of starlings and a grwgnach make spring planting impossible - Drought strikes the land, threatening ruin and disaster - A merchant that the PCs were counting on fails to arrive - Bandits threaten to burn the crops unless paid tribute

35.title: Mysteries
35.body: The following are for you and your table to decide.

36.title: The Stone
36.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Mysteries › The Stone
36.body: Where did the Stone come from? Why does it draw lightning from the sky? Who carved the ancient runes upon it, and what do they mean? What other secrets does it hold? Perhaps the Stone… - … is a shard of primordial power , once revered by the Makers; - … marks Tor 's Tor, the Rain-maker own grave; - … was carved from Tor's Fist and placed here by the god himself; - … is the petrified remains of Stormcatcher ; - … binds an emanation of the Things Below The Things Below; - … channels lightning to power an artifact deep beneath the village; - … was brought here by the Tempest Lords from far across land and sea; - … is some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

37.title: The Cistern
37.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Mysteries › The Cistern
37.body: What's down there, really? What did the Makers build it for? Perhaps… - … it connects to a complex of chambers and tunnels; - … some evil thing slumbers in the water; - … it hides an arcanum, like a variant of the Preserving Runes or the Ward of Still Waters ; - … it's just a big vault for storing water (the Makers needed to drink, too); - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

38.title: Ruins of the past
38.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Mysteries › Ruins of the past
38.body: What was this place, in the time of the Makers? Perhaps it was… - … a border-fort of the Stone Lords ; - … a trading post of the Green Lords , from before the Fae ) rebelled, where they would truck with other Makers; - … an outpost of the Tempest Lords , watched over by Stormcatcher and her tower before it fell into ruin; - … a free city and market, where the various Makers came to trade and learn; - … a place of pilgrimage for the Makers, a temple to whatever gods or powers they revered; - … some combination of the above, perhaps changing over time; or - … something else entirely. The answer should reflect and be reflected in other details. If this was a Stone Lord border-fort, then chunks of the Old Wall could be found at the base of the bluff, down in the Great Wood. If this was a Tempest Lord outpost, then stones from the Old Wall would bear carvings like those in the Ruined Tower .

39.title: Origins
39.breadcrumb: The Village of Stonetop › Mysteries › Origins
39.body: The village has been here for a dozen generations at least, but the true origins of its people are shrouded in mystery and conflicting folktales. Whence came the people of Stonetop? What led them to settle here? Perhaps they were… - … subjects of Stormcatcher , who fled here when her tower fell; - … freed servants of the Stone Lords and refugees from the Barrow Builders ; - … migrants from east of the Dread River , distant kin to the Manmarchers and South Manmarch) - … migrants from across the Huffel Peaks , who fled the boiling seas and found succor here; - … a people out of time and space, emerging from the portal beneath the Ruined Tower ; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

40.title: Aratis, the Lawkeeper
40.body: Aratis is one of the four main gods of Stonetop, the patron god of civilization and records, of laws and wisdom, of order over chaos. Her disciples say that she has been with us since people first stacked stone upon stone and called it home. If one of your initial PCs has the Judge playbook, they will answer many of the questions in this section via their presence, their background, and the decisions they make during character creation and introductions. Defer to that PC as the expert on Aratis in your game, but don't forget to ask other PCs for their opinions and experiences, too.

41.title: Themes
41.body: Choose or roll 1 or 2, and combine.

42.title: 1d12 theme
42.body: - 1-2 Civilization/society/settlements - 3-4 Laws/rules/traditions - 5-6 Chronicles/record-keeping/writing - 7 Judgement/arbitration/wisdom - 8 Cooperation/organization/peace - 9 Authority/responsibility/duty - 10 Truth/promises/oaths/honor - 11 Hospitality/sanctity/generosity - 12 Defense/resolve/punishment

43.title: Questions
43.body: According to the tales: - How did Aratis come to be (a god)? What's her origin story? - Among which people did the worship of Aratis originate? - Which god is her rival/ally/lover/kin? - What god or demon did she personally defeat/cast out/seal away? - What constellation is tied to Aratis? How so? - Who was the first Judge? What great deed is attributed to them? - What is said to be contained in the Tower Eternal? Use the answers to help decide what Aratis's true nature is Gods and Religion.

44.title: Hooks
44.body: - The Judge makes an unpopular ruling; an influential NPC wants them gone. - A Judge from another settlement arrives and finds things in Stonetop lacking. - A true disciple has a vision of a powerful agent of chaos breaking its bonds. - While digging stones or excavating a new foundation, folks in Stonetop discover a long-lost Chronicle of Aratis, sealed and mostly intact. - Legend tells of the Tower Eternal, made of black iron , with doors so heavy only a team of horses can open them. Three hermits, all true disciples of Aratis, guard it and whatever it contains.

45.title: Shrines & temples
45.body: Aratis has a shrine in Stonetop's pavilion of the gods. Outside of Stonetop: consider whether this community hosts a shrine or temple to Aratis. Small shrines to Aratis might also be found at waysides along the Makers' Roads The Makers' Roads, or perhaps at spots where different groups of Hillfolk gather and meet. For any given shrine or temple: - Where is it located? - Who tends this place? - How is Aratis herself represented? - What symbols/trappings adorn it? - Which of her themes (previous page) are emphasized? - What high holidays are celebrated here, and how? - What is this shrine or temple like? Perhaps it is… (pick 1) - … a hub of the community, a place of frequent rites, petitions, and celebrations; - … used only on high holidays, for each home keeps its own shrine above the hearth; - … neglected by most, tended only by a handful of true disciples; - … a grim place of judgement and punishment, shunned by all but her chosen; - … newly established, cramped and spare; - … some variation of the above; or - … something else entirely.

46.title: Judges
46.body: Some communities are home to a Judge of Aratis, who settles disputes, keeps records, and stands guard against agents of chaos. For any given community, consider/ask: - Does a Judge of Aratis reside here? - Who are they? What are they like? What sets them apart? - Has there always been a Judge here? Or is this a recent thing? - How much influence do they hold? - Do they have any apprentices? - How much contact do they have with Judges from other communities? - What is their greatest strength as a Judge? What is their greatest flaw? Maybe pick or roll a theme (previous page) for each. Judges are charged with maintaining a Chronicle, a history of the community they serve, its people, their knowledge, and their traditions. If a community has (or had) a Judge, consider/ask: - How are the records kept? - Where are they? In the Judge's home, a temple, a vault, or what? - How far back do they go? - How well organized are they? Should an NPC Judge (or a community-spanning order of Judges) oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (an institution ). Remember: NPCs don't have playbooks. An NPC Judge might have tags or moves reminiscent of a PC Judge, or not. See Aratis, the Lawkeeper for some potential NPC Judges.

47.title: True disciples
47.body: True disciples are those who pledge themselves to Aratis, her principles, and her observances. For any given community, consider/ask: - How many true disciples of Aratis reside here? - Who is most notable among them? - How devout are they, really? - How much influence do they have, as a group? - What contact do they have with disciples from other settlements? - How are they typically chosen? (By birth, by calling, by invitation, etc.) - What rites do they lead/perform, both publicly and in secret? What does Aratis require of her true disciples? The answer might vary from community to community. Perhaps she demands… (pick up to 3) - … truth, honesty, and forthrightness; - … hospitality, freely given to all who ask for it; - … the punishment of thieves & oathbreakers; - … adherence to strict rules of diet and dress; and/or - … respect for authority, property, and rank. Should the true disciples of a community oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (an institution ).

49.title: Various treasures
49.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Artifacts › Various treasures
49.body: - A blanket woven in the knot-language of a long-dead sect of Aratis's ( fragile , Value 0, or 2 to the right buyer) - A maul of black iron , utterly immune to magic ( close , forceful , awkward , +1 damage, Value 2) - A ◇◇ shield of makerglass , etched with Aratis's symbol ( indestructible , +1 armor, +1 Readiness of a Defend 7+, Value 2) - A ◇ helm set with a "jewel" of dark ice , grants advantage to resist mind-affecting magic ( magical , Value 2) - An old ◇ book ( fragile , Value 2), the Chronicle of a failed settlement, ending mid-sentence - A very large sapphire, perfectly cut and polished, a symbol of Aratis floating in its depths (Value 4)

50.title: Minor arcana
50.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Artifacts › Various treasures › Minor arcana

51.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
51.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
51.body: - 1 A grim peat mound - 2 A sealed cave - 3 A timeless vault - 4 A crumbling arch - 5 A silvery signet ring - 6 Some other dangerous minor arcanum, with potential to spread chaos, locked away for safekeeping.

52.title: Major arcana
52.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
52.body: The Rune-laden Scales might be associated with Aratis, or at least revered by her followers.

54.title: Associated entities
54.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Dangers › Associated entities
54.body: - Ancestral spirits or other little gods of home, hearth, and civilization - The tomb-bog spirit (page 85\), sent to execute a criminal - An archon , called up to smite some agent of chaos - Perhaps even the Pale Hunter or his hounds

55.title: Judge of Aratis
55.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Judge of Aratis
55.body: Group , leader , organized HP 8; Armor 2 (shield, leather cuirass) Damage mallet d8 ( hand ) or maul d8+2 ( close , forceful , awkward ) Special qualities bound by the requirements of a true disciple (previous page), at least publicly Stalwart instinct to see things as black and white (+ tough , +4 HP) - Decry someone/thing, publicly - Enforce a law/tradition/contract, even despite good sense - Inspire others with their resolve Ambitious instinct to advance their own agenda (+ cunning ) - Make a reasonable-sounding ruling, to their own advantage - Suss out someone's motives - Keep calm under pressure or scrutiny Circumspect instinct to try to make everyone happy (+ friendly , + cautious , deals damage with disadvantage) - Stall, delay, make excuses - See both sides of an issue - Find a different way

56.title: Oathbreaker's curse
56.breadcrumb: Aratis, the Lawkeeper › Judge of Aratis › Oathbreaker's curse
56.body: When you swear a proper oath before a shrine of Aratis , in full view of witnesses and with an appropriate offering, you are oathbound. Make note of what you promised, and the nature of the curse you invite should you forswear yourself. When you forswear your oath , hold 3 Ruin. While you hold Ruin, you have disadvantage whenever you or a companion rolls the Die of Fate. When you roll a 10+ on a move , you can spend 1 Ruin to turn the 10+ into a 6- instead. The GM can refuse; if they do, you retain your Ruin. If they accept, the results should reflect the nature of the curse you invited upon yourself.

57.title: Chaos
57.body: A PC Judge can use their Censure move to denounce another as "an agent of chaos." When they do, the move works regardless of what you think. You can ask questions about their motivations or their thinking, but they're the Judge, not you. With the Hound of Aratis move, the Judge can easily identify things "tainted by chaos," and The Hammer and the Book lets them smite things "of supernatural chaos." The Things Below count, as does anything corrupted by them or infused with their power. Beyond that, consider what the Judge has Censured and the doctrine of Aratis that's been established in play. Ask the Judge what they think counts. Consider what you know about the entity in question, and make a ruling. If in doubt, defer to the Die of Fate or the results of a relevant move.

58.title: Barrier Pass
58.body: Size village ( 200 souls) Population +0 Prosperity +0 - Trade with... - ... mammoth herders (ivory, wool, meat) - ... other fortress-monasteries

59.title: Resources
59.body: - Taxes from nearby farm valleys - Farming (terraced gardens, barley) - Livestock (goats, sheep, milk, fine wool, meat) - Timber (from Foothills) - Parchment, vellum, ink - Trades: smithing, weaving, tanning, scribing

60.title: Defenses +2
60.body: - A score of guardians - A handful of adepts - A number of tulpas - Blunt weapons, nets, bolas, bronze shields and mail - Exceptional fortifications Five days north of Stonetop via the Highway , after winding through the Foothills and climbing into the Whitefang Mountains , you'll come to a pass between two rocky slopes. The pass is blocked by a mighty wall, with dwellings and grander structures on the cliffs above. Long ago, this was the southernmost outpost of the Rime Lords . Today, it is home to descendants of their disciples. They are a stoic, unfriendly people who want little to do with those from the south. In Stonetop, they tell tales of folk coming down from Barrier Pass to trade ancient wonders for goats or grain, but none alive remember such a visit.

61.title: Questions
61.body: - According to the tales, what did the last visitors from Barrier Pass bring to trade, all those years ago? - When was the last time anyone from Stonetop tried to trade with Barrier Pass? Were they ignored? Chased off? Or what? - What went wrong when villagers last crossed paths with a party from Barrier Pass up in the Foothills? - Who do you know (in Gordin's Delve, perhaps) who hails from Barrier Pass or beyond? Why did they leave? - What do you think lies past that great big wall? - Who or what do you think they put in those cages that line the Highway as one approaches? - Why do you think their warriors carry only blunt weapons? If a PC has visited Barrier Pass, or beyond: - What brought you there in the first place? - Why did they let you in? - What surprised you the most about how they live? - What taboo or point of etiquette did you run afoul of? How so? - Do they worship the same gods up there? If so, how does that worship differ from down here in Stonetop?

62.title: Places
62.body: - The approach : after winding up switchbacks, the Highway straightens and enters a wide pass with steep rocky slopes on either side. - Large cages line the slopes , swinging in the wind, filled with human (?) bones or starved, raving prisoners. - The Wall , grand and implacable, 50 feet tall and 20 feet wide, spanning the width of the pass. - A gate in the Wall , closed of course, the Highway ending 50 feet before it. - The East Slope , dotted with dwellings, stables, and terraced gardens, all connected with narrow stairs and switchback paths. - A waterfall pours down the east slope, north of the wall. Diverted to gardens and cisterns. - The West Slope , featuring old giant-scale structures, now used as dormitories, kitchens, and so forth for monks and guardians. - The Great Hall , its façade elaborately decorated, leading into a vast chamber carved from a mountain cavern. Where residents gather, meditate, train.

63.title: People
63.body: The people of Barrier Pass descend from the Rime Lords' mortal disciples. Their names are Nepalese- and Tibetan-inspired (see The Whitefang Mountains). The Three Honorable Sages are powerful adepts who preside over Barrier Pass. Each leads one of the three sects. The Empty Sky sect values preservation and stillness; its monks spend much time in meditative stasis. Led by Gar Torjeng ( Instinct to avoid disruption and distraction). The Unmoved Mountain sect teaches extreme ascetic disciplines, pushing adherents to continually test themselves. Led by Preshi Kav ( Instinct to drive her students towards perfection). The White Crystal sect is devoted to order. The majority of guardians and layfolk follow this sect. Its few monks often lead patrols, mete out justice in nearby farms, and hunt monsters. Led by Oparma Div ( Instinct to tame chaos). Monks from each sect dwell in the fortress, ranging wildly in experience and aptitude. ( 40 total, including novices) The Guardians are sworn to defend Barrier Pass. Many are failed monks. They bear ancient bronze shields and armor, but wield no edged weapons. ( 20 total) The rest of the inhabitants are lay folk: families and individuals who garden, herd, cook, clean, and otherwise keep the fortress-monastery running. ( 140 souls total)

64.title: Stoic and unfriendly
64.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › People › Stoic and unfriendly
64.body: Why are the people of Barrier Pass so unwelcoming or even hostile to southerners? That's for you and your table to decide, but perhaps… - … Aratis herself Aratis, the Lawkeeper charged them to preserve the Rime Lords' teachings, and eschew the debased peoples of the south; - … their ancestors fought off the Barrow Builders and they still hold a grudge; - … one of the thirteen surviving Rime Lords is hidden in their mountain chambers; - … a prophecy foretells doom coming from the south; - … the fortress is tainted by Y'aaw'kara , and any who dwell here must guard against the Howling Curse ; - … some combination of the above; or - … something completely different.

66.title: TRADE WITH BARRIER PASS
66.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › TRADE WITH BARRIER PASS
66.body: Surely it would benefit both communities were we to trade with each other! Requires getting them to talk to you in the first place, which requires 1 of these: - Bringing them a valuable artifact of the Rime Lords, as a gift or to trade - A resident in good standing, vouching for you And then, all of the following: - Making a compelling offer - Convincing at least two of the Honored Sages to accept - Two successful trade missions, each in a different season When you mark all the requirements , increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Trade with Barrier Pass (timber, ivory, parchment, fine wool, goats, sheep)". Henceforth, when spring bursts forth and Stonetop has at least 1 Surplus , Stonetop gains +1 Surplus from trade. Also, when you Trade & Barter for timber, ivory, fine wool, goats, sheep, parchment, vellum, or ink , you have advantage.

67.title: Trade & Barter
67.body: The PCs must convince the folks of Barrier Pass to deal with them before any of the following matters.

68.title: Commonly available
68.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › Trade & Barter › Commonly available
68.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in Barrier Pass—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Crops and produce are usually in demand. arms and armor value --------------------------------------------------------------------- --------- ◇ Cudgel, bronze ( close ) 0 Bola ( thrown , grabby , awkward , -1 die size) 0 ◇ Mace or flail, bronze ( close , forceful ) 1 ◇◇ Mancatcher ( reach , grabby , -1 die size) 1 ◇◇ Shield, bronze ( clumsy , +1 Armor, +1 Readiness on a 7+ to Defend) 1 goods value ------------------------- --------- Fine wool garment 0 ◇◇ bolt 1 cartload ( immobile ) 2 Parchment, a few sheets 1 Ink, vial, and quills 1 ◇ Empty book, parchment 1 +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs livestock value ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------- Whitefang goat ( sure-footed , curious , wooly ); HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ) Instinct to explore; can butcher for ◇ provisions (6 uses); produces fine wool; sickly in warmer climes 2 Sheep ( timid , hardy , wooly ); HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ); Instinct to follow the herd; can butcher for ◇ provisions (6 uses) 1 Sheep hound, follower ( keen-nosed , herder , fierce ); HP 6; Damage d6 ( hand , grabby ); Instinct to bully, threaten; Cost: care & grooming 1

69.title: Special items
69.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › Trade & Barter › Special items
69.body: The following might be acquired in Barrier Pass, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. When PCs Trade & Barter in Barrier Pass , they use the local Prosperity (+0 by default). If they wish to acquire or sell a bladed weapon, they have disadvantage. On a 6-, they don't mark XP, and it's just not available/no one is interested—they might have better luck with a nearby farm or band of mammoth herders. goods value --- --- Robe/shawl, fine wool, intricately patterned ( warm , beautiful ) 1 Ivory a bit of finery 1 ◇ a substantial piece 2 a mammoth tusk ( immobile ) 3 cartload of mammoth tusks 4 Dark ice ( magical , see Rime Lords) a small piece ( magical ) 1 ◇ a large chunk ( magical ) 2 with special characteristics +1 a functional tool or weapon +2 An object of black iron Primordial Powers +2 +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs

70.title: Services and favors
70.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › Trade & Barter › Services and favors
70.body: PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by locals, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price. service/favor value ------------------------------------------ ------- A few nights of austere food and shelter 0 A layfolk's labor for a day 0 A crew of layfolk, working for 1 week 1 Consultation with a monk 1 Audience with an Honorable Sage 2 Weeks of guided study with a monk 2 A guide into the mountains, to… … a nearby farm valley 0 … another known, nearby spot 1 … a dangerous/unknown spot 2 … a secret/deadly place 3

71.title: . trade opportunities .
71.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › Trade & Barter › . trade opportunities .
71.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Barrier Pass might offer the following: - A fair exchange of goods, plus someone to vouch for them should they wish for future trade (see Trade with Barrier Pass , page People). - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus. - Two or three ivory mammoth tusks (Value 3), in exchange for help with a problem. - A Value 3 mundane item, in exchange for some less-valuable artifact of the Rime Lords - Dark ice in substantial quantities - An arcanum from the Rime Lords , the spirits of the wild , or death and the undying .

72.title: Secrets
72.body: - Secret paths/doors lead in and out of the compound. Outsiders are blindfolded when taken through them, or condemned to death if they learn of their existence. - There is a hidden vault deep in the mountainside, protected by all manner of magics and tulpas . Only a few inhabitants know it exists; fewer know what it contains. - One of the monks has started down the path of becoming a false adept . She is popular and (apparently) quite talented. Only Preshi Kav (Honored Sage of the Unmoved Mountain) suspects anything.

73.title: Hooks
73.body: - The PCs acquire an artifact of the Rime Lords; maybe they can leverage it into opening trade negotiations? - A party from Barrier Pass comes to Stonetop, offering ivory/gold/arcana for food/livestock; they are tight-lipped, haunted, on edge. - A condemned criminal escapes from Barrier Pass and seeks refuge; a shantyara pursues them.

75.title: Surroundings
75.body: - Steep, treacherous, rocky slopes - Harsh mountain weather - Hypothermia & frostbite , if you stay out in the cold - Rockslides or avalanches in winter

76.title: Fortifications
76.body: - Minor tulpas watching the approach, warning the fortress of visitors - A wall of smooth stone, 50 feet tall and 20 feet wide - Walkways and crenelations atop the wall - A final approach, 50 feet or so with no protection from the Makers' Roads - A massive gate of wood and bronze - Beyond the gate, a barbican lined with murder holes - Cleverly concealed doors that open only from the inside - A pair of khyagseng guarding the entrance to the Great Hall

77.title: Folk from elsewhere
77.breadcrumb: Barrier Pass › Dangers › Folk from elsewhere
77.body: - Mammoth herders or valley farmers , here to trade, pay tribute, or seek redress - An adept , visiting from another fortress-monastery or some cave in the wilds - A mkhalang , claiming to bring tidings but really just stirring up trouble - A bandit or criminal from one of the nearby farm valleys, condemned to death, pleading for mercy - A spirit of the wild , come to visit or debate with the Honored Sages - A thrall of the Things Below The Things Below, here under false pretense, urged to spread chaos and woe

78.title: Guardian
78.body: Group , organized , skilled HP 6; Armor 3 (bronze shields, mail) Damage bronze mace d8 ( close , forceful ) or mancatcher d6 ( reach , grabby ) or bola d6 ( thrown , grabby , awkward ) Instinct to subdue or drive off threats - Deftly execute a group maneuver - Ensnare a foe - Club someone senseless - Resort to deadly force in the heat of the moment

79.title: Monk
79.body: Group , cautious , disciplined , hardy HP 10; Armor 0 Damage staff d8 with disadvantage ( close ) Special qualities spirit sight; inured to hunger, cold, deprivation Empty Sky instinct to ignore the suffering or needs of others - Remain unperturbed - Send their mind forth into the spirit world, their bodies insensate - Calm the elements around them Unmoved Mountain instinct to push and test themselves - Perform a feat of extreme athleticism or endurance - Endure or absorb raw elemental power - Refuse to adapt or change White Crystal instinct to impose order - Take charge - Do their duty - Suppress the emotions of others

80.title: Novice
80.body: Group , cautious , disciplined , meek HP 6, Armor 0 Damage staff d8 with disadvantage ( close ) Special qualities unreliable spirit sight Instinct to veer between their masters' teachings and their base needs - Do what their superiors ask - Hesitate, balk, flinch - Show great promise, or great weakness

81.title: Barrow Builders
81.body: When the servants of the Stone Lords rebelled, slaying their masters and unleashing the Things Below , those who died in the uprising were hailed as martyrs. The survivors raised mounds of earth and stone to honor them, simple barrows built with human hands and human arts. Society collapsed. Horror swept the land. Petty tyrants and sorcerer-kings rose to power. They had barrows built to honor loyal vassals or to seal away the ghosts of those they had betrayed. Fleeting kingdoms rose and fell. Rulers raised grand barrows and had themselves interred with riches, slaves, and followers in tow. Fueled by dark magic and the power of the Things Below, some of these tyrants reigned on even after death. In time, the power of the Things Below diminished. Subjects rose up and overthrew their masters. Tyrants began to fall. Those who could not be killed were sealed away in barrows. The current age arrived. Many barrows still remain. A few are simple graves. Many are haunted or cursed. Some imprison ancient evil. They dot the World's End, especially in the Steplands . The Hillfolk shun them, and warn all travelers away. For they descended from the Barrow Builders, and those who cast them off. They know what horrors those earthen mounds contain.

82.title: Names
82.body: Breton- or Cornish-inspired, similar to Hillfolk names but with more vowels: Alana, Arzhel, Brandan, Corent, Deniel, Enora, Gwenael, Herve, Judikael, Karenza, Katell, Maben, Margh, Nolwenn, Piran, Ronan, Winoc, Yanick, Yezekael.

83.title: Hooks
83.body: - An artifact that the PCs want lies in a haunted barrow mound - Some fool unseals a barrow and lets phantom riders loose - Someone acquires the tether of a deathless one - Hillfolk are furious that one of the barrows was looted

85.title: 1d12 theme
85.body: - 1 Revolution: hunting down the Stone Lords, upending the status quo - 2 The works of the Makers: torn down, vandalized, repurposed, lost - 3 Societal collapse: famine, shortage, hoarding, violence - 4 Sorcerers and necromancers: recklessly wielding power they can't control - 5 Human sacrifice, blood magic, the courting and open worship of the Things Below - 6 The horrors of war: pillaging, looting, slaughter, dislocation - 7 Refugees, desperate and afraid, seeking safety, settling for survival - 8 Sorcerer-kings and petty warlords with their grand, bloody ambitions - 9 Conquest/oppression/enslavement - 10 Intrigue/betrayal/civil war - 11 Rulers clinging to power, even from the grave - 12 Heroism and hope and perseverance, the casting off of tyrants, the slaying of monsters; the forging of a new age

86.title: Questions
86.body: - Where is the nearest barrow-mound that you know of? - What strange sign marks it? - What tall tale have you heard of it? - What story do you recall, from the terrible days after the Makers fell?

88.title: Everyone knows:
88.body: - That old barrows dot the region - That they're smaller and cruder than Maker-ruins - That Hillfolk shun them - That they supposedly contain great treasure and great danger

89.title: One might know:
89.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Lore › One might know:
89.body: - Where a few barrows can be found - That they were built by the Hillfolk's (reviled) ancestors - Of a particularly (in)famous figure among them - The horrors from those times - A tale about a specific barrow

90.title: Very few know:
90.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Lore › Very few know:
90.body: - What a given barrow contains - Where a specific figure is buried - The true story behind a barrow

91.title: Sites
91.body: Barrow Builder sites are most common in the Steplands , especially around Blackwater Lake and Three Coven Lake . Plenty can also be found in the Flats , the South Manmarch , and along the Dread River . A few can be found in the Huffel Peaks and as far north as the Foothills . To create a Barrow Builder site, pick one or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

92.title: 1d6 site
92.body: - 1 A cave , in which they or their victims lived/hid/died/were interred (combine with 1 or 2 themes, Barrow Builders) - 2 An old battlefield or mass grave, likely haunted - 3-4 A reclaimed Maker-ruin (see below) - 5-6 A barrow

93.title: Reclaimed Maker-ruin
93.body: Choose or roll for its origin. Create the ruin from the appropriate tables, rolling with advantage for its condition at the time the Barrow Builders claimed it.

94.title: 1d12 origin
94.body: - 1 A Green Lord ruin - 2 A Rime Lord ruin - 3-4 A Tempest Lord ruin - 5-6 A Forge Lord ruin - 7-10 A Stone Lord ruin - 11-12 A ruin of multiple groups (roll 1d10 twice) Then pick or roll for a theme Barrow Builders to reflect how the Barrow Builders used it, 1-3 signs of their presence, and its current condition. Populate the site with artifacts and dangers of the Barrow Builders, as well as those of its original owners.

95.title: 1d12 signs
95.body: - 1-2 Ghosts /psychic imprints - 3-4 Remains/bones/graves/memorials - 5-6 Damage/plunder/vandalism/graffiti - 7-8 Distinctive material culture: refuse/pottery/tools/art/statuary/textiles/etc. - 9-10 Crude, human-sized additions/adaptations/repairs - 11 Altars/idols/offerings/arcane symbols - 12 Maker artifacts, repurposed

96.title: 1d12 current condition
96.body: - 1-4 Shattered and laid waste by time, disaster, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable - 5-8 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away - 9-10 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact - 11-12 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved For more examples, see Blackwater Lake and Three Coven Lake . The PCs are searching for the fiery heart of the Mindgem Mindgem. I think it's in a Stone Lord ruin that the Barrow Builders reclaimed. Looking at the tables on 1d6 I end up with a warren of multi-storied, brutalist apartments—housing for laborers—built around a single, giant-sized manor where the overseers dwelt. Maybe this was a plantation, with a small army of lithic servants Stone Lords maintaining the complex? For the Barrow Builders' theme, I roll an 11 ("rulers clinging to power, even from the grave"). For signs, I roll a 5 ("damage/plunder/vandalism/graffiti") and 10 ("crude, human-sized" modifications). For the current condition, I roll a 9 ("buried, overgrown… mostly intact"). I ponder a bit, and come up with this: A sorcerer led the humans here to rise up against their overseers. The sorcerer then claimed the manor as his own, along with the device that charged and controlled the lithic servants—a device powered by the Mindgem's makerglass heart! He turned the manor into a crude palace and sent "his" people back to work the fields. When they eventually rebelled, he was mortally wounded but transferred his soul into the device that ran the lithic servants. With his mind now spread among the many constructs, he squashed the uprising. His rule grew increasingly erratic, brutal, and ineffective. The tenements became more squalid and desperate. Folks fled. The sorcerer didn't care. Now, the complex lies empty: overgrown, half-buried, stripped of anything of value, and home only to a dwindling army of lithic servants and the fractured mind of a paranoid sorcerer. The PCs are following the River Sruth out of Marshedge and through the South Manmarch when they encounter a barrow. I roll a 10 for terrain ("tree(s), savannah, thickets"), a 9 for a theme ("conquest/oppression/enslavement"), a 12 for size ("manmade hill"), a 1 for purpose ("to honor the interred"), a 3 for architectural elements ("obvious entrance"), an 11 for condition ("strangely intact, fully visible"), and a 12 for features ("roll twice"). I then get a 4 ("corrupted by the Things Below") and a 10 ("treasure," with a 2 for the amount remaining). For the corruption, I turn to The Things Below and come up with a poisonous place, with themes of death and broken bones, first caused by a blood-offering and then by a seal being broken. I ponder a little, and come up with this: A lone hill rising from sparse woods on the river's south bank. The daughter of a warlord commissioned it. Hundreds of slaves built it. When it was done, she had the slave's skulls caved in, to be interred with her father and his riches. After the daughter's brief reign ended, some fools pried open the stone doors to loot the warlord's tomb. They found chambers full of death, madness, and cursed gold. Those few who made it out were never the same. The barrow still lies open and treasure still remains, but no one with a lick of sense goes near it.

97.title: Barrow
97.body: They are the most obvious remnants of these bloody times: dome shaped earthen mounds. Most folks assume they were all tombs, but that's hardly the case. Pick or roll for themes Barrow Builders, its size, its purpose, 1 or 2 architectural elements, its condition, and a feature. Place the barrow in an appropriate terrain for its region. Fill it with artifacts and dangers of the Barrow Builders or the appropriate region.

98.title: 1d12 size
98.body: - 1-4 Small: just a few paces across, no chambers to speak of - 5-8 Medium: big as a house, a few cramped halls/chambers - 9-12 Large: a manmade hill, riddled with chambers/passages

99.title: 1d12 purpose
99.body: - 1-3 To honor the interred - 4-6 To bind/torment the interred - 7 To protect the interred - 8-9 To hide something away - 10 To leverage/maintain fell magic - 11-12 Roll 1d10, twice

100.title: 1d12 architectural elements
100.body: - 1-2 Megalith(s)/cairn(s)/dolmen(s) - 3-4 Obvious entrance (likely sealed) - 5-6 Irregular/elaborate/oval shape - 7-8 Works of the Makers, recycled - 9-10 Walls of dry-stacked stones - 11-12 Built around a spring: water bubbling up/pooling/flowing out Even in large barrows, chambers and passages are cramped and claustrophobic, often with packed-dirt floors and lined with large slabs of stone.

101.title: 1d12 condition
101.body: - 1-4 Collapsed, torn open, only hints remaining - 5-6 Partially collapsed or torn open, but largely intact - 7-10 Mostly intact, but overgrown or partly buried; easy to miss - 11-12 Strangely intact, fully visible

102.title: 1d12 feature
102.body: - 1-2 Haunted , marked by dool trees - 3-4 Corrupted by the Things Below - 5 Protective spirit(s)/construct(s)/demon(s)/spell(s)/trap(s) - 6 Connects to caves - 7 Inhabited by local beasts/bandits - 8 Useful or valuable flora growing on/in/near it - 9-10 Treasure (roll 1d6 to inform how much remains) - 11-12 Roll again, twice For more examples, see Blackwater Lake .

103.title: Artifacts
103.body: The Barrow Builders gladly looted and repurposed whatever treasures they could, and often dabbled with dangerous powers. Their sites often contain artifacts related to: - Death and the undying - The Things Below - The Stone Lords - Other Makers , especially the Forge Lords or the Tempest Lords

104.title: Various treasures
104.body: - A rusty Y-shaped pendant (Value 0), symbol of the cult who fed the Voice of the Eternal Maw - A bronze blade from a metal construct 's Forge Lords limb, remade into a brutal axe ( close , messy , awkward , +1 damage, Value 1) - A map ( fragile ) to the barrow of a famously rich warlord - Ancient grains in wax-sealed jugs, still good and worth 1 Surplus ( immobile ) - A Maker-era gold coin (Value 2), found in a mass grave, the tether for a ghost - A fine torc of aetherium around a skeleton's neck; shocks its wearer if they try to remove it or engage in any sort of violence - A ◇ spear ( close , thrown , beautiful , indestructible , 2 piercing, Value 3) made of makerglass , shaft and all, found in a hero's tomb - A ◇◇ Stone Lord's cleft skull, coated in gold and studded with gems ( cumbersome , Value 4)

105.title: An old bronze dagger
105.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Artifacts › An old bronze dagger
105.body: hand , magical Tinged green, but sharp and sturdy. In the chaotic days after the Makers fell, it was the weapon of a desperate father. He killed his own brother with it, to protect his children's food. He killed others, too, to make sure his family had enough. So many, many others. The dagger holds a shard of that father's desperate will. When you Seek Insight about someone while carrying the dagger , add the following to the list of questions you can ask. If your hand is on the hilt , you can ask one of these questions for free, even on a 6-. - What of mine do they most want? - How am I most vulnerable to them? - What do they carry that is useful to me? If a PC keeps the dagger, treat it as a threat (a MacGuffin ; Instinct to cause paranoia).

106.title: The Three-star Crown
106.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Artifacts › The Three-star Crown
106.body: ◇ magical , beautiful , Value 4 A circlet of whitest gold, set with three black sapphires that shine like stars. It was made by a renowned smith, an apprentice of the Forge Lords , at the command of a petty warlord. The warlord was so pleased, he maimed the smith's hands so that she could never make its rival. The smith killed herself in despair, cursing the crown to steal the hopes of all who wore it. When you wear the crown and interact with someone who fears or respects you , you roll +CHA with advantage. Alas, each time you don the crown , roll +WIS: on a 10+ , tell the GM one of your hopes and why you doubt it can be achieved; on a 7-9 , tell them a hope you have abandoned; on a 6- , you fall into a deep despair that lasts until you rouse yourself to face a mortal danger. If the crown is destroyed —the gems shattered, the metal reduced to slag—then the despair is lifted and all lost hopes are restored.

107.title: A lead bracelet
107.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Artifacts › A lead bracelet
107.body: magical , Value 1 This heavy, black metal torc is set with a red gem. It feels good upon your wrist. Makes you feel important. When you wear the torc and threaten someone that you consider beneath you , lose HP equal to the torc's current Cost (1d4 to start). They are filled with fear; the craven will flee, cower, or grovel, and even the brave will flinch and hesitate. Each time you use the torc, the Cost increases by 1 die size (max 1d10). When you wear the torc for a week without anyone questioning your authority , decrease the Cost by one step (min 1d4).

108.title: Minor arcana
108.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Artifacts › A lead bracelet › Minor arcana

109.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
109.body: - 1 An old scroll case - 2 A tattered mantle - 3 A disturbing mask - 4 A vellum scroll - 5 A silvery signet ring - 6 A mummified hand

110.title: Major arcana
110.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
110.body: The following are particularly likely to be associated with the Barrow Builders: - The Blood-quenched Sword - The Hec'tumel Codex - The Blackwood Fetishes

112.title: Hazards
112.body: - Unstable construction - Perilous stone - Cursed objects - Darkness, deep in a barrow - Low ceilings, cramped quarters, poor lines of sight

113.title: Banefire
113.body: Curtains or pillars of heatless flame, blue or green or purple, with no obvious source of fuel. Banefire features in many tales of the barrows: to repel, to imprison, or just as an uncanny feature of the mounds. - Spring forth with little/no warning - Blind them with sudden light - Block a path or a line of sight - Burn spirits, the undead, and living souls (d8 damage, ignores armor) Something interesting: Ghosts and other spirits fear banefire as much as mortals, but the inhabitants of some barrows can control it. Something useful: Banefire burns only where it's meant to; it never spreads. Each banefire is anchored to a blackened bone, all that remains of the mortal whose body and soul were sacrificed to fuel the flames.

114.title: Traps
114.body: They put all that effort into building barrows. They weren't going to leave them unguarded. To create a trap, consider the barrow's purpose and what it contains, and where the trap is found. Then pick or roll for the trap's intent, its trigger, its tell, and as many elements as makes sense. Imagine how it works. If you like, prep GM moves and/or an impending doom for the trap, and maybe a custom player move.

115.title: 1d12 intent
115.body: - 1-2 To kill/maim/injure - 3-4 To repel/separate/deny/isolate - 5-6 To weaken/hinder/delay - 7-8 To capture/punish/torment - 9-10 To alert/awaken/prepare - 11-12 Roll d10 twice

116.title: 1d12 trigger
116.body: - 1-2 Stepping/entering/being/staying somewhere - 3-4 Touching something - 5-6 Moving something/picking it up - 7-8 Breaking/disturbing something - 9-10 Opening/closing something - 11-12 Perceiving/examining/tinkering with something 1d12 tell --- --- 1 A brief warning before it goes off 2-4 A tiny detail, easy to miss 5-7 Unusual construction/decor 8-10 Bait 11-12 Clear signs—it's obviously a trap 1d12 elements --- --- 1 Camouflage/illusion/deception 2 Gravity/weight/mass/hardness 3 Blades/spikes/needles/pointy bits 4 Wire/rope/pulleys/contraptions 5 Tension/pressure/springs/balance 6 Chemics/poison/acid/gas/fire 7 Special materials (see below) 8 Runes/glyphs/sigils/marks/spells 9 Light/dark/heat/cold/noise/quiet 10 Thoughts/emotions/mind games 11 Spirits/constructs/undead/vermin 12 Previously triggered/disarmed Special materials might include hazardous growths , orichalcum , black iron , dark ice , makerglass , aetherium , red crystal , and/or other things of your devising.

117.title: Examples:
117.body: - A pit, 15-feet deep, the bottom lined with rusty spikes; covered by taut fabric, pebbles, and dust - A gap runs down the ceiling of a low tunnel; stepping on a flagstone releases a swinging axe - A puzzle-box on a decoy coffin; futzing with it releases stabby little constructs from the coffin - A corpse's sword; moving it pulls a wire, which upends an urn full of salt and releases a trapped wraith - A rune-etched doorway; any who try to enter stop, turn around, and forget the last few minutes - Opening the door to the final vault requires removing a keystone; the whole barrow will collapse - Sconces of glowing makerglass; break the rune-seal on the sarcophagus, get fried by lightning - A dead-end, the word VIGILANCE carved on the far wall, the room full of colorless, odorless gas; causes headaches, irritability, fainting, death

118.title: People
118.body: - Hillfolk , warning away explorers or chasing them off - A hdour , antiquarian , or sorcerer , seeking knowledge/artifacts - Adventurers , hunting treasure - A necromancer , consulting with those long-dead

119.title: Monsters
119.body: - Ghosts and revenants , haunting barrows, reclaimed ruins, old battlegrounds mass graves - Constructs , stolen from the Stone Lords or made using their arts - Corrupted beings , ancient pets of ancient sorcerers, or local fauna affected by lingering evil - Spirits of the wild , bound by old spells

120.title: Barrow wight
120.body: Group , undead , corrupted , terrifying , hardy , hoarder HP 14; Armor 4 (resilience), 2 vs. bronze or fire (mail) Damage rusty sword d8+2 ( close , forceful ) or crushing grip d8+2 ( hand , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities bound to their barrow Instinct to protect their hoard - Utter an echoing warning - Block an exit - Stand fast, refusing to be moved - Keep fighting with or reattach a severed limb As you might expect, grave robbing was always a problem for the Barrow Builders. But a clever sorcerer had a solution! So many hateful spirits roamed the world in those days. Why not bind some to the corpses of the deceased, and let the dead guard their own treasure?

121.title: Phantom rider
121.body: Group , large , spirit , undead , organized HP 14; Armor 2 (lacks organs, shield) Damage spear 1d8 ( close , thrown ) or trample d8+2 ( hand , forceful ) or bow d8 ( near ) Special qualities bound to their bones; can travel far and wide; can't manifest in daylight; takes +1d4 damage from silver Instinct to seek tribute, to lord over others - Speed across the landscape like the wind itself - Form a body of soil, dust, and mist - Flank and harry their foes For a time, it was common for warriors to pledge both their lives and their deaths to their lieges, and to be interred with their horses and their fellows in their master's tomb. Thus, they could live on in death, still basking in a grim and joyless glory.

122.title: Deathless ones
122.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Phantom rider › Deathless ones
122.body: In the time of the Barrow Builders, many warlords and sorcerers were obsessed with immortality. A number of them found it—or something like it—through undeath. To create a deathless one, imagine a warlord (someone similar to a Manmarcher chief ), a sorcerer , a necromancer , or a hdour . Consider making them corrupted by the Things Below. Add the undead tag, and give them +4 HP because they are animated by more than biology. From there, pick or roll for the tether that sustains them, how it sustains them, and its limitation. Then pick or roll for the deathless one's current state. Consider where and how they (or their influence) might be encountered. Let this all inform their story, their personality, and their stat block.

123.title: 1d12 tether
123.body: - 1-2 Small (needle, torc, ring, etc.) - 3-4 ◇ (sword, scepter, crown, scroll, mummified hand, etc.) - 5-6 ◇◇ (shield, standard, urn, etc.) - 7-8 ◇◇, cumbersome (bust, cauldron, coffer, tapestry, etc.) - 9-10 immobile (hoard, tree, cave, statue, sarcophagus, etc.) - 11-12 fragile and roll d10 again (egg, porcelain, mirror, device, etc.)

124.title: 1d12 how it sustains them
124.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Phantom rider › 1d12 how it sustains them
124.body: - 1-2 It hides their death/heart/soul - 3-4 It anchors their soul to the world - 5-6 It feeds/renews/recharges them - 7-8 It gives them leverage over the Lady of Crows - 9-10 It shields them from the Lady and the Pale Hunter - 11-12 It suffers age/death/decrepitude in their place Unless their tether fails or is destroyed, they cannot truly die. Their body will eventually heal or regenerate, or their spirit will possess some hapless victim.

125.title: 1d12 limitation
125.body: - 1-2 They can't leave its presence - 3-4 It must be fed/charged/maintained - 5-6 It must be relocated often - 7-8 Its destruction results in their instant, terrible, final death - 9-10 If "killed," they require outside aid to be restored - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

126.title: 1d12 current state
126.breadcrumb: Barrow Builders › Phantom rider › 1d12 current state
126.body: - 1-3 Greatly diminished, perhaps destroyed and waiting to return - 4-6 Imprisoned and largely forgotten - 7-9 Hidden, apathetic, self-involved - 10 Trapped, but with some influence - 11 Secretly active, pursuing schemes - 12 Roll 1d10 twice I've established that the Mindgem's fiery heart is in the clutches of a deathless sorcerer, whose soul inhabits an arcane device that controls a number of lithic servants (see Stone Lords) . I start with a sorcerer , corrupted by El'rash-Orra . For gifts, I give him a terrifying gaze and the ability to inflict false sensations. For marks, I give him slow-healing wounds and strange fascinations (he's a voyeur). I already decided that he had "anchored his soul" to an arcane device in a Stone Lord manor ( immobile , fragile ). For a limitation, I think the device "must be fed/charged/maintained," and that "its destruction results in his final death." For a current state, I think he's "trapped, but with some influence" via the lithic servants he can possess. I picture him in his oversized manor, seated on a makeshift throne, permanently wounded, unable to move on his own. A dozen faceless stone servants attend him, moving him like a puppet when needed. Behind the throne is a contraption of rune-carved stone and crystal, the makerglass heart burning at its core. His mind is spread among his servants, some of whom scour Three Coven Lake for artifacts, gossip, and materials to maintain his device. Should the device fail, his consciousness will splinter and his body will finally die. The servants, each containing a splinter of his soul, will fight each other for primacy. None will succeed. I write up his stat block like this:

127.title: MYGHAL, DEATHLESS SORCERER
127.body: Solitary, undead, corrupted, paranoid, magical, hoarder, terrifying HP 16; ARMOR 4 (wards) DAMAGE word of false pain d8 (near, area, ignores armor) SPECIAL QUALITIES tethered to the device; broken body; aware of all that his servants perceive INSTINCT to jealously hoard secrets - Inflict false sensations on others - Fix someone with his terrible gaze - Reveal a plan or a horrid truth - Control his lithic servants like they were extensions of himself - Recover from any wound, slowly and never fully

128.title: Blackwater Lake
128.body: In the west of the Steplands , in the shadow of the Huffel Peaks , there is a large and placid lake. A low ridge surrounds it, and a wide, rocky shore. The Aberzh River flows in from the west. No rivers or streams flow out. The water is dark, and very deep, and very, very still. It is always cold but never freezes. There are no waves. The wind shuns the surface. No birds dare alight. This was once great Dulenn, city of Dark Waters, the slow-beating heart of the Stone Lords ' vast domain Stone Lords. Whereas Teirstered was a city of towers and splendor, Dulenn was a solemn place. A quiet warren, carved and sung from bedrock with the beauty of a limestone cave. There was no lake, then. Not above the ground. But it was called "Dark Waters" for a reason. For seven deep tunnels led to seven mighty seals, each guarded by Stone Lord sentinels who sang a ceaseless lulling song. Behind those doors was a vast and lightless lake. A hole in the earth. A Mouth of Daagon . When the Green Lords roused the Things Below to help them quell the Fae , the seals below Dulenn cracked. Daagon began to whisper. Began to sing. Resentment grew among the Stone Lords' human servants. Leaders rose among them: cunning, confident, and eloquent, knowing things they oughtn't, awash in uncanny power. Cults of liberation formed. A conspiracy grew. Seven sorcerers infiltrated Dulenn. They found the tunnels. They slew the sentinels. They broke the seals. And Daagon, Who Waits in Deep Water, waited no more. It thrashed. It opened its yawning dark wet jaws. Dulenn fell into the earth. And the Dark Water, the Blackwater, surged in to flood the hole.

129.title: Impressions
129.body: - Striations in nearby outcrops, tilted towards the lake - The damp, mineral smell of wet stone - Clouds, stars, shoreline—all reflected in the lake - Stillness, quiet, echoes of the slightest sound - At times: thick banks of fog, wet, cloying, isolating Tip: lower your voice; speak slowly, softly, make them lean in to hear you.

130.title: Hooks
130.body: - Servants of Daagon are roaming further from the lake than usual. - A thrall of Daagon drags a victim here to sacrifice. - A deathless one 's Barrow Builders tether is hidden near the lake. - Hillfolk spirit-talkers sing a certain song here every 7 years to keep the lake subdued. - A dangerous artifact needs to be cast into the Mouth of Daagon. - Certain NPCs hear a silent song, calling them to Blackwater Lake.

131.title: Getting there
131.body: To reach Blackwater Lake from Stonetop: - First you must travel to the Steplands, which will take at least 4 days. - From there, it will take at least 2 days and corresponding amount of supplies - You need a knowledgeable guide. - Even then, you risk getting lost near the lake. - The terrain itself is treacherous, you risk injury on the way. - The way is grueling, you risk exhausting yourselves/your resources. - You need to watch out for horrors from the lake. - The way is perilous, plagued with danger.

132.title: Areas
132.body: - Nearby hills: A soft place, where paths might change in the fog, full of ruins, barrow mounds , and caves . Bedrock and ruins all tilt towards the lake. - Outer ridge: An escarpment that rings the lake, dropping 50-100' to the shores. Lightly wooded up top. Holds a number of ruins, buried at odd angles, some poking out. - Shore: Extends 100-200' from the outer ridge. Lots of rubble, much of it ancient masonry. Thick lichen, grass that leans away from the lake. - The Aberzh River: Cuts through the western ridge, then widens and flows lazily to the lake. Muddy, shallow, rocky. Lots of dark algae. - The Pillar: On the north shore, 40' wide and over 100' tall. Made of salvaged masonry stones, crudely stacked. No obvious entrance. A precarious climb. Feels cursed. - The lake: About 2 x 3 miles. Impossibly still, dark, reflective. Always cold, never freezes. Hundreds of feet deep near the center.

133.title: Terrain
133.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

134.title: 1d12 terrain
134.body: 1d12 Terrain --- --- 1 Trees/shrubs/succulents/tall grass 2-3 Ridge wall/prominence/overhang 4-5 Scree/rubble/rocks/boulders 6-7 Ruin or barrow (next page) 8 Very large boulder(s) 9 Pool/pond/spring/rivulet 10 Peninsula/cove/inlet 11 Mud/mire/shingle/shallows 12 Drop-off/deep water

135.title: 1d12 ruin or barrow
135.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Terrain › 1d12 ruin or barrow
135.body: - 1 Partly collapsed mound of earth and stones, each carved with fear-runes. Holds bones, clay vessels full of rotted grain. - 2 Warehouse-sized bunker of massive blocks, buried at a severe angle. Bay doors visible, heavily marred, but still intact. - 3 Warren of human-sized rooms, dug into outer ridge. Confusing layout, odd angles, unstable, lit by glowing crystals. - 4 Door into the ridge, leading to a crude, vaulted room. Holds a 12' statue of a squat, fishy beast. Its mouth opens to a deep shaft. - 5 Dozens of makerglass tubes, each smaller than the last, lined up in ranks and fused together. Chime hauntingly when struck. - 6 Walls of a cubical stone room with many niches. Tilted 30°. Crystalline fixture in one wall. Words spoken here blare from somewhere across the lake. - 7 Series of dissimilar rooms and tunnels, leading to a massive underground amphitheater, like a cyst in the earth. Largely flooded. - 8 Stone head, bigger than a house, barely recognizable. Eye sockets open to a chamber. Interior walls covered in runes, useless levers. - 9 Barrow on the ridge, hidden by trees, brush. An open doorway. Inside: a withered corpse bound by bronze, a pile of offerings. - 10 Small hill of stacked stones; one slab hides a door. Inside, an icy crystal lures in wraiths, binds them; scores are trapped already. - 11 Cavernous, buried room, tilted but mostly whole. Full of chisels and warded pavestones : mostly smashed, a few in progress, some buried but intact. - 12 Onion-shaped dome of stone, 30' across. Hollow, an opening facing down, water pooling impossibly on the ceiling inside. The above are just examples. To create more Stone Lord ruins, see 1d6 but roll for condition with disadvantage. Those that are at all intact will be tilted and askew, and are likely to have been reclaimed by the Barrow Builders at some point. To create more barrows, see Barrow

136.title: Discoveries
136.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

137.title: 1d6 discovery
137.body: - 1 A change of terrain Blackwater Lake and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 An unnerving tableau (see below) - 4 An encounter (see below) - 5 An artifact of... (see below) - 6 An opportunity (see below)

138.title: 1d6 unnerving tableau
