138.body: - 1 An altar, trappings of ceremony, blood stains, slimy trails - 2 Wards/bindings on an open entrance, marred and broken - 3 A slowly rotting pile of skin, like it was sloughed off - 4 Skeleton(s), dry and brittle, tiny little bite marks (?) all over the bones - 5 Partial skeleton(s); some of the bones are missing, others seem… melted - 6 A large mammal, like a horse or aurochs; staggers out into the lake, gets yanked below water

139.title: 1d6 encounter
139.body: - 1 A thrall of Daagon, heavily marked, full of shame - 2 A lone servant of Daagon , dragging something of value to the lake - 3 A hdour , come to bargain with Daagon or its servants - 4 A spirit of the wild , likely corrupted but still curious or indifferent - 5 A confused wanderer, following a voice in their head - 6 Hillfolk : exiled, on the hunt, checking wards, etc.

140.title: 1d6 artifact of...
140.body: - 1-2 … the Things Below - 3-4 … the Stone Lords - 5 … Some other group of Makers - 6 … the Barrow Builders

141.title: 1d6 opportunity
141.body: - 1 A boat/raft (roll 1d6 for condition) - 2 Large game (aurochs, deer, boar, etc.); confused, strangely docile - 3 A treasure, left in shallow waters or set on a rock not far out from shore - 4 Hillfolk in trouble and wary but grateful for help - 5 A good place to rest/restock/lay an ambush - 6 A sacred site , somehow uncorrupted

143.title: Hazards
143.body: - A stretch of water and reason to brave it - Rocks to climb and a reason to try it - Poor footing (mud, unstable rubble, scree, slick wet stone, etc.) - Rockslides on the outer ridge - Unstable construction or perilous stone - Disquiet and unease , whispers and visions , unnatural phenomena , and corruption

144.title: Fog
144.body: Fog forms often, especially on breezy, warm days or during still, chilly nights. Sometimes it comes with no warning. And sometimes, reality becomes… malleable… in the fog. - Have mist start to gather, thicken - Obscure their surroundings, make them struggle to navigate - Separate them, perhaps inexplicably - Hint at/reveal the presence of servants of Daagon - Have them end up far from where they should be - Reveal that the landscape has changed, rearranged itself

145.title: The Blackwater
145.body: The lake is always frigid but never freezes over. It is weirdly dark and still, smooth like glass. Any disturbance ripples out and out and out. It feels like it is sleeping. Like it's lying in wait. Hazards include: - Instinctive dread, from a generational fear of water - The fact that they probably can't swim - Shivering cold, the threat of debilities or even hypothermia - Poor footing (slick rocks, mud, algae) - Steep, unexpected drop-offs - Getting dragged down by their gear

146.title: Drowning
146.body: When you plunge beneath the water , hold Breath equal to 1 + your CON. If you first took a deep breath, hold 2 extra Breath. When you make a move while under the Blackwater , and that move involves a roll, choose 1 of the following after the move is resolved: - Spend 1 Breath - Mark a debility - Lose yourself in the Blackwater When you lose yourself in the Blackwater , time seems to stop and you find yourself adrift in a watery, hungry void. Roll +WIS: on a 7+ , you snap back to reality, somehow holding 2 Breath. But also, on a 10+ , choose 1; on a 7-9, choose 2: - Gain a mark The Things Below of Daagon; you are now corrupted - Name something you possess that you love or treasure; it's gone - Choose something about yourself that you value (a move, your instinct, something about your appearance); lose it On a 6- , either embrace Daagon as your master (gain the Thrall insert), or else make one last move before you are swallowed by the Mouth of Daagon (next page).

147.title: Call of Daagon
147.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Dangers › Call of Daagon
147.body: Daagon still sings its silent song. It calls out to the weak-willed, and to those who nurse jealousy or greed in their souls. It whispers to them from afar, and compels them towards Blackwater Lake. It urges the weak-willed to enter the water and be devoured. To the jealous and the greedy, it offers power, wealth, or immortality. For a price, of course.

148.title: Mouth of Daagon
148.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Dangers › Mouth of Daagon
148.body: At the bottom of the lake, hundreds of feet down, is a Mouth of Daagon. Anything dropped into the deep water will make its way there in time. Valuables left on the shore or cast into the shallows will likely be found by the servants and delivered to the Mouth on your behalf. Dangerous artifacts left for the servants might be delivered and consumed, or not. When the Mouth swallows something , Daagon consumes it utterly. When the Mouth swallows someone , they are gone, body and soul. When you feed the Mouth with something or someone beloved (by anyone, not just you), Daagon rewards you.

149.title: 1d6 reward
149.body: - 1 A physical change (scales, rubbery flesh, gills, a tentacle, etc.) and perhaps a gift The Things Below to go with it - 2 An item of luxury or status (Value 2) - 3 A precious item (Value 3) - 4 A "priceless" item (Value 4) - 5 A way to best your rivals or defeat your enemies - 6 A way to acquire great power or long life Whatever the reward, it arrives soon: while you sleep, in your dreams, or via vision or coincidence or happy accident.

150.title: Monsters
150.body: - Pypeds or unbound wraiths , desperate and ever-hungry, drawn here by Daagon's call - A deathless one , dwelling in or bound to a nearby barrow - Cultists, sorcerers, or thralls , bearing many gifts and many marks The Things Below - Hillfolk , ready to assume the worst about the PCs - Stone Lord constructs in long-buried ruins, probably damaged, behaving erratically, and/or corrupted - Hdours , adventurers or antiquarians , answering Daagon's call - Any number of corrupted beings or emanations , such as the Hand of Daagon

151.title: Servants of Daagon
151.body: Terrifying , violent , wretched , either corrupted or emanation , see below HP variable; Armor variable Damage see below Special qualities amphibious (or close enough) Instinct to devour, then to steal - Emerge from deep water or thick fog - Snatch up something valued/treasured - See below Horrid things, wet and wriggling. Maybe they're emanations of Daagon, or mortal beings so thoroughly corrupted as to seem utterly alien to this world. They crawl forth. They feast on flesh and bone. They drag home wealth and beauty and treasured things to feed their master's hunger. Choose or roll for their number appearing and their size. Then pick or roll for 1d4 traits and 1d4 additional moves. Think about things that live in the water. Imagine the creatures implied by the results.

152.title: 1d12 number appearing
152.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Servants of Daagon › 1d12 number appearing
152.body: - 1-3 Many, at least half a dozen ( horde ; HP 3; Damage d6) - 4-9 A few, like 2-5 ( group ; HP 6; Damage d8) - 10-12 Just one ( solitary ; HP 12; damage d10)

153.title: 1d12 size
153.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Servants of Daagon › 1d12 size
153.body: - 1-3 Child-sized ( small ; reduce damage die one size, hand ) - 4-9 Person-sized, wolf-sized, boar-sized, etc. ( close ) - 10-12 Horse-sized ( large ; +4 HP, +2 damage, close , reach )

154.title: 1d12 trait
154.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Servants of Daagon › 1d12 trait
154.body: - 1-2 Cowardly, hesitant ( craven ) - 3-4 Overwhelmed by hunger ( ravenous ; instinct is just "to devour") - 5 Surprisingly intelligent ( cunning ) - 6 Aggressive ( fierce ; +2 HP, rolls damage with advantage) - 7 Blubbery/scaly hide (2 armor) - 8 Powerful ( forceful ; +2 damage) - 9 Big claws/fangs ( messy ; 1 piercing) - 10 Stealthy and cautious - 11 Tentacles/pincers/etc. ( reach , grabby ) - 12 Projectiles ( near )

155.title: 1d12 additional move
155.breadcrumb: Blackwater Lake › Servants of Daagon › 1d12 additional move
155.body: - 1-2 <sup ä</sup Wriggle free of danger/restraint - 3-4 <sup ä</sup Smother/constrict/engulf prey - 5-6 <sup ä</sup Mesmerize the weak-willed - 7-8 <sup ä</sup Heal at a prodigious rate - 9-10 <sup ä</sup Dissolve organic material - 11-12 <sup ä</sup Paralyze with venom/poison

156.title: Crinwin
156.body: They've always been there, in the Wood, lurking in the trees. For generations, the Forest Folk hunted them and kept their numbers in check. No longer. - Out-of-place noises: partial bird calls, off-season insect buzz, a bear's growl from the treetops, snippets of nonsense speech - Figures scurrying, leaping, swinging through the branches - Sinewy strong fingers around your throat - Clammy, blackish blood; too cold and putrid to be natural Tip: pick a sound or word that the PCs have made, and repeat it over and over at different volumes and tones.

157.title: Questions
157.body: - What odd noise, heard in the night, is attributed to the crinwin? What is it said to mean? - What common thing do these vermin seem to covet above all else? - When did crinwin last openly raid the village? What did they take? - What's the surest sign of crinwin having been nearby? - Who among you hates those things, more than anyone else does? Why? - What makes you think they might be more than simple beasts, despite what everyone else says?

158.title: Lore
158.body: Something interesting: There's something that everyone agrees will keep crinwin at bay (a smell, a charm, a sound, a little ritual, etc.). What is it, and why are you sure that it's next to worthless? Something useful: There really is something that keeps crinwin at bay, but it involves something uncommon, impractical, or both. What is it, and why isn't it common knowledge (or at least commonly accepted)?

159.title: Hooks
159.body: - The crinwin have dug something up, something evil and powerful, and it's… changing them. - A hunter stumbles on a colony of nests, 5 or 6 in one place. - Crinwin numbers near Stonetop are steadily growing. Is something leading them? Compelling them? Or scaring them closer to the village? - Crinwin start boldly raiding homes at night. They soon steal something precious (an heirloom, an arcana, an infant). - A trapper's child has secretly befriended a crinwin, meeting it in secret, playing and trading gifts.

160.title: Origins
160.body: What are they, and where did they come from? That's for you and your group to decide, but possibilities include: - They were made by the Fomoraij , as warriors to fight the Fae . - They were a failed experiment of the Green Lords , escaped into the wild when the Fae rebelled. - They were once Forest Folk , who were cursed or corrupted by some witch or sorcerer. - They came through the cracked portal of Vor Svetelik , from some distant place (or even another world). - They crawled out of some lake, vile spawn of the Things Below .

161.title: Nests
161.body: Big papery structures, like giant wasp nests, high up in the boughs of trees. The bigger the trees, the better. The mightiest trees might have two or three nests in them. A small, light person who dares the climb could squeeze inside. It's pitch black inside, with platforms and walls of hexagonal paper cells. Cramped chambers are connected by vertical shafts. The outer walls are surprisingly damp and resistant to fire, but the insides will go right up if exposed to open flame. Around a dozen full-grown crinwin live in each nest. They store food (rotten, decaying) in the hexagonal cells, and maintain tableaux showcasing flawed trappings of civilization (next page). The largest chamber in each nest is a nursery: warm, moist, and carved out of the living wood. Hexagonal tubes line the chamber, each stuffed with goo and larval crinwin. Pupae squirm about the chamber, feeding on goo and even each other. The nurseries are tended by an old, fat crinwin or—in the oldest, largest nests—a broodfather .

163.title: Flawed trappings of civilization
163.breadcrumb: Crinwin › Discoveries › Flawed trappings of civilization
163.body: They imitate whatever people(s) they observe, but always get it wrong. - A line of stones, stacked two or three high, surrounding the tree(s) that hold the nest(s). - Crudely tilled "fields," planted with nonsense: bits of bone or teeth, pot shards or broken tools, dead wasps, shiny stones, etc. - A deer carcass, tethered to a nearby tree with a vine around its neck - A hole, dug like a well, but far too shallow - A chamber in the nest, filled with old cups, potsherds, scraps of cloth - "Children" made of sticks and papery mass, clothed in filthy stolen fabric, tucked safely away in a nest or carried about - Graves or cairns, empty yet revered or else housing still-living, sickly crinwin - A crude shrine to… something? - A mundane item or random artifact , misused or cast aside.

164.title: Something long thought lost
164.breadcrumb: Crinwin › Discoveries › Something long thought lost
164.body: - Something shiny, a family heirloom perhaps, gone missing in the crinwin's last raid - A keepsake, given by a loved one, but lost in the Wood years ago - Something owned by a hunter or trapper who never came home

165.title: Strange growths
165.body: Enormous pustules, found on some of the crinwin that are a little larger, less agile, and less aggressive. Pop them and they ooze goo, maybe with a small, pale grub, squirming and hissing pitifully as it dies in the open air.

166.title: Outcast
166.body: An albino crinwin, more curious than hostile towards outsiders, living apart from the others in a landbound nest of stone and paper. Watching the PCs intently, or trying to perform a craft in some painfully wrong way, or getting harassed or beaten by its peers.

167.title: Prisoner
167.body: A crinwin (beaten, cut, bruised), either being dragged into the area right now by others, or already lashed on a high-up tree branch for who-knows-how-long. - How can you tell, just by looking at it, that this one comes from different stock than the ones who live here?

168.title: Midden heaps
168.body: A short ways off from the nest. - Signs of crinwin biology (remains of what they eat/won't eat, or excrement, or the utter lack thereof) - Cast-off possessions, giving hints to what they value and what they don't - Evidence of contact with the Forest Folk , over a decade old and buried deep

170.title: Crinwin
170.body: Horde , small , hoarder , cautious , stealthy HP 3; Armor 1 (reflexes) Damage claws, rocks, choking d6 ( hand ) Special quality climbs and jumps like a squirrel Instinct to covet - Mimic noises, words, cries for help - Hide or vanish into the trees - Snatch something and dart away

171.title: Broodfather
171.body: Solitary , stealthy , terrifying HP 12; Armor 0 Damage spindly choking fingers d8 ( close ), maw full of teeth d10+2 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities blind, tremorsense Instinct to feed the younglings - Unfurl from its hiding place in shocking glory - Lash out with a choking tongue (d6+2, reach , forceful , grabby ) - Drag a grabbed foe closer Unstable nest When you roll +any stat while in a crinwin nest and the dice come up doubles , pick 1 (in addition to the move's normal outcome): - You crash through the floor or wall, your limb(s) dangling or stuck - The nest lurches and twists, teetering - Wasps living in the nest begin to swarm

172.title: Wasps
172.body: Wasps often colonize crinwin nests, living symbiotically among them. Such nests thrum and buzz ominously. When you are stung by a single wasp , it hurts like the dickens and takes some effort not to cry out. When the wasps swarm around you , take 1d4 damage (ignores armor) and you're wracked with pain. Doing anything other than curling up and writhing means that you're Defying Danger (likely with CON). Each subsequent time you take damage from the swarm, increase the die one step (max d10). When you flee a wasp swarm , roll +CON: on a 7+ , you escape the swarm but pick 1 (on a 10+) or 2 (on a 7-9): - Suffer damage from the swarm - Mark a debility from the venom - Run straight into another danger

173.title: The Crombil
173.body: A great scaly wurm, hundreds of feet long. It sleeps somewhere beneath the Great Wood , waking every few decades to chew and burn a swath of destruction.

174.title: What is it, exactly?
174.breadcrumb: The Crombil › What is it, exactly?
174.body: That's for you to decide. Maybe… - … it's a primordial titan , a remnant of the first age; - … the Green Lords made it, as a weapon, or just because they could; - … it's the first and oldest draventao , a mighty Forge Lord who succumbed to the fire within; - … it's a manifestation of the Things Below , all gluttony and fire and nightmare; or - … it's just a big, dumb beast.

175.title: Hooks
175.body: - The PCs wish to destroy some fell, indestructible artifact, and learn that feeding it to the Crombil might work. - The Crombil will soon wake, and an important site—like a ruin of the Green Lords or a Red Grove —lies in its path. The PCs must act soon to explore or preserve the site before it's destroyed. - It's been almost fifty years since the Crombil woke, and its last rampage ended maybe a day or two from Stonetop. Elders remember the smoke and the sounds of destruction, but few ever knew the cause. But now the Crombil stirs, and this time it might head towards Stonetop. - The Crombil's periodic rampages have been drawing it towards Vor Svetelik . This next time, it will plow into the ruined city and release any number of terrors from stasis and imprisonment, perhaps even the Fomoraij themselves. Perhaps this was their plan all along. - Cultists of the Things Below seek to turn the Crombil into a vessel for their master, breaking the cycle of sleep and rampage and ushering in an age of lasting destruction.

176.title: Signs & portents
176.body: - Tremors and earthquakes, of increasing frequency and intensity - Dreams of shifting earth, hunger, fire, and doom - A small hill, covered in trees and brush but strangely warm and venting smoke - Beasts and spirits giving wider and wider berth to the Crombil's lair - Strangers arrive in town and talk of "the Awakening" - Dreamers wake from troubling dreams, "the Crombil" on their lips - Some suggest offering sacrifice to appease the Crombil, others feel compelled to go and worship as it wakes - Vast flocks of birds fleeing to the west - A terrible grinding and cracking of earth and trees; a pillar of smoke; a deep and bellowing cry

177.title: The Crombil, Awakened
177.body: Solitary , huge , terrifying , fearless , fireproof HP 26; Armor 3 (scales, boney ridges) Damage jaws like a cave d12+7 ( reach , forceful , messy , 3 piercing), thrashing coils d12+5 ( close , area , forceful ) Special qualities fiery heat, stomach like a furnace, too big to hurt with most weapons Instinct to devour - Chomp right through anything - Swallow up someone whole - Upend the terrain around it - Belch cinders, start fires When someone's been swallowed by the Crombil, start using these moves against them: - Batter them with debris, cinders (d6 damage) - Make them fight for any sort of purchase or bearings - Shove them further towards its furnace-like stomach - Blister skin, burn lungs, singe hair (d8 damage)

178.title: Danu, the Earth Mother
178.body: Danu—the Great Mother, the Goddess, She-Who-Provides—is one of the four main gods Gods and religion of Stonetop. She is the deity of the earth and the wild places, of beasts and birds, of green growing things. She has long been revered by all peoples, though not always worshipped, nor served by priests. If one of your initial PCs has the Blessed playbook, then they will answer many of the questions in this section via their presence, their background, and the decisions they make during character creation and introductions. Defer to that PC as the expert on Danu in your game, but don't forget to ask other PCs for their opinions and experiences, too.

180.title: 1d12 theme
180.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Themes › 1d12 theme
180.body: - 1-2 The earth itself/soil/stone - 3-4 Beasts of the land/birds of the air - 5-6 Growing things (plants, fungus, agriculture, etc.) - 7 Spirits of the wild Spirits of the wild - 8 Wild, untamed places - 9 Fertility/reproduction/(re)birth - 10 Secrets/mysteries/bindings - 11 The natural order/seasons/cycles - 12 Healing/purging/protection

181.title: Questions
181.body: According to the tales: - How did Danu come to be (a god)? What's her origin story? - Which god is her child/lover/rival/ally? - Why are white elder trees traditionally associated with Danu? - What feature of the landscape is said to have been shaped by her hand? - How exactly are the brennauwed The Brennauwed said to be related to Danu? - What humble beast or bird does she hold to be most sacred? - How did the Forest Folk's Forest Folk view of her differ most strikingly from Stonetop's? Use the answers to help decide what Danu's true nature is Gods and Religion.

182.title: Hooks
182.body: - An offering to Danu goes missing, just before an important rite. - Stunted crops/sickly livestock/earthquakes/etc. point to Danu's displeasure. - One of Danu's blessed is accused of sorcery and blamed for bad luck/illness. - The priest(s)/initiate(s) of Danu oppose some project the PCs wish to undertake. - A natural site has been corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below; Danu sends a warning via dreams.

183.title: Holy places
183.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Holy places
183.body: Danu has a shrine in Stonetop's pavilion of the gods. Outside of Stonetop, consider whether any given community maintains a holy place to Danu. For any given holy place: - What is it? A shrine, a temple, a sacred site , or what? - Where is it? In the center of town, on the outskirts, in the nearby wilds, etc.? - How is Danu represented, if at all? - What symbols/trappings adorn it? - Which of her themes (previous page) are emphasized? - Who, if anyone, maintains it? - What is this holy place like? It might be… (pick 1) - … loved, well-used, dripping with offerings and petitions; - … little more than a token of respect, her true holy places being elsewhere; - … given wide berth, approached only with care and propitiation; - … neglected and all but forgotten, except by a few; - … some variation on the above; or - … something else entirely. Likewise, ask or consider what offerings are typically left there. These might include… (pick up to 3) - blood and/or burnt flesh - figurines and/or effigies - fruits of the harvest - incense and sage bark - pure rain water - metal nails and/or tools - salt and crystals - whisky or other alcohol Think about what the answers say about how this community sees Danu, and why? Do they see her as a nurturing mother? As an inscrutable natural force? As a hungry mother-of-monsters, a deity to appease and avoid? Or what?

184.title: Her blessed
184.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Her blessed
184.body: In any given community, there are those who learn some of Danu's secrets, who bear some of her gifts, who make offerings to her and perform her most important rites. For any given community, these folk might be… - … initiates of a formal order/cult/priesthood; - … the practitioner(s) of a particular trade (the midwife, herbalists, hunters, etc.); - … a respected elder, and their apprentice(s); - … an outsider, set apart from the community; - … some combination of the above; or - … someone else entirely. Whoever they are, ask or consider: - Who are they? What are they like? - Where do they live? What sets them apart? - How much influence do they have? - What about them puts others ill at ease? - What contact (if any) do they have with their peers in other communities? - What rites do they lead/perform, both publicly and in secret? - What special gifts/skills do they possess? If one of your PCs has the Blessed playbook, they almost certainly fill this role for Stonetop (though there may be others who do so, too). Should a blessed NPC (or group of NPCs) oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (likely an institution or maybe a wildcard ). Remember: NPCs don't have playbooks. A blessed NPC might have tags or moves reminiscent of a PC Blessed, or not. See the Hillfolk spirit-talker and the Initiates of Danu insert for examples.

186.title: Various treasures
186.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Artifacts › Various treasures
186.body: - A few menhirs atop a high place, with carvings that map the movements of certain auspicious stars ( immobile ) - Dried, hallucinogenic mushrooms (2d4 uses, Value 1) - A ◇ pouch of bendis root ( 10 uses, burnt fumes repel perversions of nature; each use lasts 1 hour, reach , area , Value 1) - A bezoar (swallow to cure any poison, Value 1) - A ◇ pouch of pink salt, good for cooking/preserving and bane to corrupted creatures (Value 1) - A ◇ sacred pouch of rune-stitched drakeskin; when sealed, nothing within can be detected or found by magic, nor can anything within escape or affect the outside world ( magical , Value 2) - A silver-alloy dagger, useful against undead spirits ( hand , Value 2) - A 6-foot tall statue of Danu, carved from a geode, the torso open and filled with amethyst crystals ( immobile , Value 3) - A scroll ( fragile , Value 4 to the right buyer) with cryptic instructions on how to create a Redwood Effigy

187.title: Minor arcana
187.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Artifacts › Various treasures › Minor arcana

188.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
188.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
188.body: - 1 A folktale - 2 A giant oak leaf - 3 A crumbling arch - 4 A path in the woods - 5 A patch of rainbow moss - 6 A beaded satchel

189.title: Major arcana
189.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
189.body: The following might be associated with Danu, or at least revered by her followers: - The Twisted Spear - The Shield of the Wisent Witch - The Redwood Effigy

190.title: Perversions of nature
190.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Perversions of nature
190.body: The Danu's Grasp move from the Blessed playbook affects "perversions of nature," as does the Ranger's move, Warden of the Wild. This includes the Things Below and anything corrupted by them or infused with their power. Undead probably count, too, as well as some of the Green Lords' more extreme creations. Ask the Blessed or Ranger what they'd consider a perversion of nature. Consider what you know about the entity in question, and make a ruling. When in doubt, defer to the Die of Fate or the result of an appropriate move.

192.title: Associated entities
192.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Dangers › Associated entities
192.body: - Any sort of natural beast or spirit of the wild , who acknowledge Danu's authority - The brennauwed in particular, who claim kinship with her - Many of the Fae , who honor her as a respected elder - Fen-walkers , who know better than to offend her - Hdour , who know many of her secrets - Spirit-talkers and gouzadn , who hear her voice and bear her gifts - Perhaps the Pale Hunter , the Willow Witches , the other gods , or the great spirits , any of whom might share a bond of kinship, familiarity, or common cause

193.title: Signs of her displeasure
193.breadcrumb: Danu, the Earth Mother › Dangers › Signs of her displeasure
193.body: - The Forest's Wrath - Disease - Volcanic activity and earthquakes , and what they let loose - Mudslides or rockslides , out of the blue - Perhaps the stirring of the Crombil , decades before its time - The ire of various natural beasts or spirits of the wild - Blighted crops/gardens, sickly livestock, scarce game (disadvantage to generate Surplus in summer and autumn) - Some manner of curse, upon an individual or a community (threat type affliction ; Instinct to punish)

194.title: Death and the Undying
194.body: Everyone who lives, dies. When you succumb to injury or illness, or just reach the end of your allotted days, then the Last Door will swing open for you. You will see it. The Lady of Crows will beckon you through. And there on the other side, your soul will join those who have gone before: the honored ancestors, the countless multitudes of the quiet dead. At least, that's how it's meant to go—the natural order of things. But not every soul passes through the Last Door when their time has come. Some linger, others are bound. Some are called back to this world. Everyone who lives, dies. But not everyone dies the same.

195.title: Themes
195.body: Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

196.title: 1d12 theme
196.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Themes › 1d12 theme
196.body: - 1 Death personified: the Lady of Crows, the Pale Hunter , other gods/spirits of death - 2 The Last Door/brushes with death/the afterlife/things from beyond - 3 Graves/funeral rites/grief/mourning - 4 Death foretold: prophecies, omens, visions, harbingers - 5 Good death: peaceful, dignified, bravely met, an end of suffering - 6 Ignoble death: traumatic, desperate, torturous, terrifying - 7 Untimely death: sudden, surprising, things left unsaid or undone - 8 Violent death: murder, assassination, execution, battle, suicide - 9 Mass death: famine, plague, war, cataclysm, collapse - 10 The fear of death: denial, bargaining, panic, cheating - 11 The curse of undeath: respite denied, eternal hunger/pain/loneliness/regret - 12 Necromancy and death-magic, likely with aid from the Things Below

197.title: Hooks
197.body: - An abandoned house, out near the Old Wall , is said to be haunted. - A shade gets called up at the Crossroads and refuses to go back. - A haunted grove near the West Road is said to hide buried treasure. - A necromancer comes to town, offering a chance to speak with the dearly departed.

199.title: Everyone knows:
199.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Lore › Everyone knows:
199.body: - About the Lady of Crows, the Last Door, and what is said to lie beyond - A ghost story or two - That dool trees are creepy and often haunted - That most undead come out only at night

200.title: One might know:
200.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Lore › One might know:
200.body: - That undead can't cross an unbroken circle of salt (a ◇ pouch full, Value 1) - That a handful of salt will burn most ghosts (1d8 damage, hand , close ) - That ghosts are bound to a tether , and what that entails - That silver can harm most undead - The tale of a prominent haunted site - What a given entity is capable of - What necromancers are capable of

201.title: Very few know:
201.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Lore › One might know: › Very few know:
201.body: - The details of an obscure haunting - The true nature of the Lady of Crows, the Last Door, the afterlife - How necromancy works, exactly

202.title: Questions
202.body: - Have you seen the Last Door and the Lady of Crows? What do you recall? - What do folks in Stonetop say awaits us, beyond the Last Door? What do they say in/among the \ \ ? - What do you think awaits us? Why? - Who among the dead do you miss most? - What ghost story scared you the most as a child? - When did you first see someone die? - Who was the first person you murdered?

203.title: Death's Door
203.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Death's Door
203.body: When the PCs are dying and they make the Death's Door move ( Book I ), they see the Last Door and the Lady of Crows. The move prompts them to describe it, but feel free to ask questions. - What does the Door look like? Big and grand, small and humble, or what? - Is it flung open, or just ajar? - What appears to lie beyond? - Where does the Lady stand? What does she wear? What adorns her brow? - What is she like? Imperious? Friendly? Sad? Smug? Inscrutable? Or what? - Does she speak? What is her voice like? What does she say? - What detail catches your attention, that you'd never have expected? - How much of this will you remember? As for the afterlife—the land beyond the Last Door—its true nature is a mystery. But it is home to dool spirits , gwyllgi , and the shades of the dead. Some of those shades, when called, are eager to stay in the land of the living. So for some, it would seem, the afterlife is not a pleasant place at all.

204.title: The Lady of Crows
204.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › The Lady of Crows
204.body: She has many titles. Carrion Queen and Black-Feather Woman. The Kindly One. The Keeper of Secrets. She Who Dances on the Field of Battle. None know her true name, if she even has one. Most call her simply: the Lady of Crows. Her nature and demeanor are for you and your table to decide, but ask/consider: - What is her role? Psychopomp? Judge? Ruler of the afterlife? Or what? - What does she want? What pleases her? - Is she worshipped as a god? If so, what are her observances (page 184)? - What is her origin said to be? - What is her relationship to the Pale Hunter ? - How do tales of her differ among \ \ ? What is notably the same?

205.title: Haunted sites
205.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Haunted sites
205.body: These are places where the undead linger, or where the Last Door stands ajar. If the site itself has yet to be established, combine the terrain in which it's located with a feature.

206.title: 1d12 feature
206.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Haunted sites › 1d12 feature
206.body: - 1-3 A creepy (un)natural landmark (a dool tree, a skull-shaped rock, etc.) - 4-5 A liminal place (a pass, a bridge, a tunnel, a gate, a crossroad, etc.) - 6-8 A tomb/barrow/cemetery, left by the locals or their ancestors - 9-10 An abandoned home/fort/steading - 11-12 A ruin of the Makers or the Barrow Builders , or a lingering sign of their presence Once the site's physical nature is established, pick or roll a theme Death and the Undying, how long it's been haunted, and the origins of the haunting.

207.title: 1d12 how long
207.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Haunted sites › 1d12 how long
207.body: - 1 Since the days of the Makers , at least - 2-3 Since the Time of Cataclysm , when the Makers fell - 4-5 Since the Barrow Builders , after the Makers' fall - 6-7 For generations, longer than the living remember - 8-9 For decades, within living memory - 10-11 For the past decade at most - 12 Not long at all, a recent change The PCs are exploring Three Coven Lake. They've found an ancient Stone Lord workshop carved into a bluff above the lake. I think it's haunted! The nature of the site is established, so I don't roll for a feature. I roll a 6 for theme ("ignoble death"), a 6 for how long ("for generations"), and a 4 for origins ("the death of a few, intimate and intense"). Because deaths were involved, I roll another 5 for the cause of death ("exposure/cold/thirst/starvation"). I ponder a bit, and come up with this: A century ago, some adventurers explored these chambers. A great slab of stone fell into place, trapping them in a side chamber. A grate in the ceiling (far out of reach) provided enough fresh air and rainwater to sustain them for several grueling weeks, but one of them murdered and ate his fellows before slowly wasting away. His soul lingers as a wraith, tethered to his bones.

208.title: 1d12 origins
208.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Haunted sites › 1d12 origins
208.body: - 1-2 The death of many, all at once - 3 The death of many, over time - 4 The death of a few, intimate and intense - 5-6 The death of one, a singular event - 7 The desecration/violation of a grave - 8 The arrival of an artifact or tether - 9 Something about the place itself, attracting/ensnaring dark entities - 10 A ritual, magic, or artifice: failed or run amok - 11 A ritual, magic, or artifice: working as intended - 12 The loss or failure of an artifact/ward/binding that kept the dead in check If the origins involved a death or deaths, pick or roll for the cause of death.

209.title: 1d12 cause of death
209.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Haunted sites › 1d12 cause of death
209.body: - 1 Old age/infirmity/malady/disease - 2-3 Suicide/despair/apathy/upset - 4-5 Exposure/cold/thirst/starvation - 6-7 Injury/trauma/wounds/poison - 8-9 Burning/drowning/suffocation - 10 Falling/accident/weather/disaster - 11 Exertion/exhaustion/shock/fright - 12 Magic/an entity/a curse From there, choose the ghosts , revenants , or other dangers that haunt the place, and their tether (s) Tethers if appropriate. Populate it with artifacts tied to death and the undying, and dangers or discoveries appropriate to the surrounding area and/or whoever dwelt here in the past.

210.title: Dool trees
210.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Dool trees
210.body: Dool trees often grow in or near haunted sites. A grove stands near the Crossroads , and lone dool trees are often found near Barrow Builder tombs Barrow Builders. They are thick and knobby and multi-stemmed. The bark is papery, the color of bleached bone. The Pale Hunter shuns them, and they are often home to dool spirits if nothing else. The leaves of a dool tree are deep purple, almost black, shot through with pale white veins. Dried leaves can be steeped to make a bitter tea, which brings on a death-like sleep from which some folk never wake.

212.title: Various treasures
212.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › Various treasures
212.body: - A clue as to what keeps a ghost from moving on (a letter, a toy, etc.) - A ◇ wooden idol of the Lady of Crows (Value 0, crude ), found on a crude altar - A vial of lethal poison (1 use, ingested , dangerous , Value 1) - A felled dool tree , its wood valuable to the right person (Value 1 for a ◇◇ bundle, Value 2 for a cartload) - A silver-alloy dagger (Value 2), useful against the undead - ◇◇ Fine funerary goods, like linens, salts, myrrh, etc. (Value 2) - A ◇ gold neck torc (Value 3), fitted around the neck of a murdered corpse - A silver sarcophagus, inlaid with runes ( immobile , Value 4)

213.title: A blackened splinter
213.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › A blackened splinter
213.body: crude , magical , fireproof Undead who see it are drawn to it, or else deeply agitated by its presence. When you touch the splinter to an undead entity, they see the Last Door yawning open before them. Roll 4d6. If the total is greater than the entity's current HP, they are drawn through the Last Door and leave this world for good. Otherwise, they are transfixed for a few moments before the Door slams shut. Regardless, the splinter vanishes.

214.title: A spindle of fine thread magical
214.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › A blackened splinter › A spindle of fine thread magical
214.body: A length of fiber, maybe 50 feet long, spun by a ghost from all the hair that ever fell from her daughters' heads. The thread is light, and fine, and almost unbreakably strong, but it can be handled only by one who has seen the Last Door. Only the edge of a silver blade can cut it. It can be used to bind a ghost fast, if you're clever and can handle the thread yourself.

215.title: A pale wooden sword
215.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › A pale wooden sword
215.body: ◇, close , crude , magical Carved from a dool tree with a silver knife, and polished with oils infused with moonlight. The grip is worn smooth but the "blade" is chipped and notched, unlikely to stand up to iron or bronze. When you wield the sword against a manifest undead spirit , you can hurt it (deal damage normally). If you reduce it to 0 HP, the manifest form dissipates but the spirit can reform at its tether normally.

216.title: A tattered standard
216.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › A tattered standard
216.body: ◇◇, magical Threadbare, ancient, but remarkably well-preserved. Embroidered with a stylized aurochs whose horns encompass the sun. It fills you with hope and pride to look upon it, as well it should. For this was the standard of the Heol-Tarv, a heroic band who gave their lives in hopeless battle against the Anan Gllo , securing their people's escape. When you unfurl the tattered standard and hold it aloft for all to see , you and your allies are immune to supernatural fear and unshaken by undead or otherwise unnatural foes.

217.title: Minor arcana
217.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › A tattered standard › Minor arcana

218.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
218.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
218.body: - 1 Carvings in a cave - 2 A grim peat mound - 3 A tattered mantle - 4 A scroll & bone flute - 5 A chain of rusty locks - 6 A vein of milky crystal

219.title: Major arcana
219.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
219.body: The following are associated with death and the undying: - The Demonhide Cloak - The Hec'tumel Codex - The Blackwood Fetishes - The Twisted Spear

220.title: Ghosts
220.body: Ghosts are the souls of the dead, those who linger in this world. They vary wildly in personality, motives, and capabilities, but all have the undead and spirit tags, and a move that reflects them manifesting a presence in the world. They are animated by more than biology (+4 HP) and lack vital organs (+3 HP, +1 armor). Consider the context in which they are encountered. If not already established, pick or roll for their cause of death Death and the Undying. Then pick or roll for what they need to move on, their manifestation, and 1-6 abilities and/or weaknesses. Establish their tether . Let all these elements inform their story and their stat block.

221.title: 1d12 what they need
221.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 what they need
221.body: - 1-2 Kindness/patience/persuasion, a guide to the Last Door - 3-4 Justice (or vengeance) served - 5-6 A proper burial - 7 A reunion with a loved one - 8 The truth, unearthed/acknowledged - 9 A task completed; an oath fulfilled - 10-11 Their tether, destroyed or unmade - 12 To be put down, like a rabid dog

222.title: 1d12 manifestation
222.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 manifestation
222.body: - 1 Just a whisper/wind/mote of light\ - 2-3 A form of shadow/cold/mist/etc\ - 4-6 A spectral form, ethereal and eerie, but much as they looked in life\ - 7-9 A spectral form, horrible, clearly dead\ - 10 A body made of soil/straw/rags/etc. - 11 A solid body, horrific and terrifying - 12 A solid body, seemingly alive but for a few telltale signs - \ Harmed only by silver or salt, or some other thematically appropriate substances

223.title: 1d12 ability
223.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 ability
223.body: - 1-2 Causes eerie, unnatural phenomena - 3 Feels (feeds on?) emotions/vitality/sensations of the living - 4 Can possess certain hosts/vessels - 5 Unearthly moan/wail/shriek - 6 Inflicts its pain/feelings on others - 7-8 Telekinesis (roll 1d6 for scope) - 9 Nasty/vicious/ghostly attack - 10 Mind games/phantasms/compulsions - 11-12 Can manifest in multiple forms

224.title: 1d12 weakness
224.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 weakness
224.body: - 1 Afraid of some mundane thing - 2 Inarticulate, or unable to speak - 3 Obsessive/predictable behavior - 4 Easily frustrated/angered/enraged - 5 Can't form new memories - 6 Driven off by singing/prayer/etc. - 7-12 Shuns/powerless in daylight

225.title: Tethers
225.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › Tethers
225.body: Ghosts are bound to an object or place. Pick or roll for the tether itself, and how tightly bound the ghost is.

226.title: 1d12 tether
226.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 tether
226.body: - 1-4 Their corpse/remains/bones/hair - 5-6 The place where they died - 7 (Part of ) their grave/pyre/altar/etc. - 8 The weapon that killed them - 9 An item they once cherished - 10 A prominent place from their life - 11-12 The focus/source of whatever magic binds them

227.title: 1d12 how tightly bound
227.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ghosts › 1d12 how tightly bound
227.body: - 1-2 Can't leave its immediate presence - 3-6 Must stay on the premises/site - 7-10 Can roam a few miles; returns daily - 11-12 Can wander far and wide, but must return periodically If a ghost's manifest form is reduced to 0 HP, they dissipate and return to their tether. They usually won't manifest before the next sunset; if they do, their max HP are halved. If a tether is destroyed, then the ghost might… (pick 1) - … fade away, utterly destroyed; - … pass through the Last Door (now, or after some final act); - … devolve into a wraith ; - … possess a corpse, as a pyped ; or - … cling to their current host/form, but fade utterly if it is destroyed. Decide based on the ghost's history, power, and relationship to their tether.

228.title: The Gwraig Wen
228.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › The Gwraig Wen
228.body: Solitary , spirit , undead , forlorn HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage bare hands d6 w/disadvantage ( hand ) Special qualities bound to within a few miles of her bones; can't form new memories; leaves no footprints Instinct to drag others into her search - Manifest in moonlight as a pale, bedraggled woman - Ask for help, insist on it - Grow ever more agitated, flickering and vibrating as she does - Vent her rage with a deafening wail, d10 damage ( near , area , loud , reload , ignores armor) - Vanish when no one's looking Those who ply the Great Wood tell an old tale of a bride, newly married to a handsome trapper. He failed to come home one night, and she unwisely entered the forest to find him, falling into a deep pond and drowning. Ever since, her ghost has roamed the woods on moonlit nights: wet and shivering, desperate and pleading, certain that her husband is still out there.

229.title: The Ghostly Legion
229.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › The Ghostly Legion
229.body: Horde , spirit , undead , organized, terrifying , hoarder , violent HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage ghostly short sword or spear d6 w/advantage ( hand , close ) Special qualities roams far and wide, but tethered to the cave; shuns daylight; can only touch the world through violence; upsetting to/afraid of dogs Instinct to lift their curse - Manifest a spectral form (harmed only by silver, salt, or a loyal dog) - Ogle a mortal as they eat or drink - Threaten violence, carry through - Force a mortal to carry their treasure They were a band of southern mercenaries, come north in search of treasure. They heard tell of a hdour and his hoard, trapped him in his cave, and slew him. Then they divvied up the loot and left. Only later did they learn why the Hillfolk fear to kill a hdour. For he had cursed them all. Cursed their souls to linger after death, doomed to endless hunger and endless thirst. Doomed to fear the sun, to be hounded like the dogs they were. Cursed, indeed, until every piece of the far-flung loot they stole is returned to the hdour's cave.

230.title: Specter
230.body: Solitary , spirit , undead , stealthy , terrifying , cruel HP 23; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage telekinesis d10+2 ( hand , close , near , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities bound to its tether; one or more weaknesses Death and the Undying Instinct to torment the living - Create eerie environmental effects (bleeding walls, green flames, etc.) - Manifest a form of flitting shadow (harmed only by silver or salt) - Manifest a nightmarish physical form - Speak through an unconscious or catatonic victim A specter is a soul consumed by hatred and envy, cruelty and rage. Maybe they were a sadistic murderer in life, or a victim whose death was so traumatic that they lash out at any living thing. Maybe they were a pining ghost whose loved ones rejected them, or a fettered soul long- and cruelly-bound, now set free. Regardless: they are now a thing of spite and vengeance, wanting only to punish those with the temerity to live.

231.title: Wraith
231.body: Solitary , spirit , undead , stealthy , terrifying HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage life drain d10 ( hand , ignores armor) Special qualities bound to its tether (usually), powerless in daylight Instinct to feast upon living souls - Manifest a form of shadow and cold (harmed only by silver and salt) - Hurl itself at the living - Suck vitality from its prey Wraiths are cursed with endless hunger. They are damaged souls, wounded by dark magic, another wraith, or the dehumanizing effects of starvation or addiction. They are little more than beasts, driven by the emptiness within, with only the vaguest sense of who they once were. Other ghosts might become wraiths when their tethers are destroyed. If they linger in the world, they find themselves filled with gnawing need, and must sustain themselves on the living. These unbound wraiths are weaker, but often form packs with others of their kind ( group , vicious instead of stealthy , 13 HP each, d8 damage, afraid of firelight). When a wraith reduces you to 0 HP and you survive , your soul is wounded. Reduce your max HP by 4. Until your soul is healed, your instinct becomes "Hunger: to fill the emptiness inside you with excess," and should you roll a 6- on Death's Door, you die and become a wraith (a monster in the GM's control).

232.title: Other ghosts
232.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Wraith › Other ghosts
232.body: - A cinderwraith - The Quiet Twins - Phantom riders - Ferocedes Ogran

233.title: Revenants
233.body: Revenants are the living dead, corpses animated by a soul, a demon, or foul magic. They vary wildly in personality, motives, and capabilities. They are undead , animated by more than biology (+4 HP). Consider the context in which a revenant is encountered. If not yet established, pick or roll for their cause of death Death and the Undying. Then pick or roll for what they were brought back by, their visage, and 1-6 abilities and/or weaknesses. Let all these elements inform their story and their stat block.

234.title: 1d12 brought back by
234.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Revenants › 1d12 brought back by
234.body: - 1-3 A thirst for vengeance - 4 A longing that transcends death - 5 Stubbornness/habit/will - 6 Guilt, like heavy chains - 7-8 An oath/vow, stronger than iron - 9 A curse, well-deserved or not - 10 Their own designs, some dark ritual/deal/preparation - 11-12 The will or working of another (and maybe roll 1d10 for why/how)

235.title: 1d12 visage
235.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Revenants › 1d12 visage
235.body: - 1-2 Naught but bone, a grinning skull - 3-5 Tattered, rotting, horrible - 6-8 Dried out, gaunt, clearly dead; but can pass for living at a distance - 9-10 Seemingly alive, but clearly dead and horrible in sun/moonlight - 11-12 Seemingly alive, except for a few telltale signs

236.title: 1d12 ability
236.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Revenants › 1d12 ability
236.body: - 1 Uncanny speed/grace/prowess - 2 Unholy strength ( forceful , +2 damage) - 3-4 Unnatural resilience (+4 HP, +4 armor except vs. silver or fire) - 5-6 Can regenerate/reform/rise again - 7 Limbs that can keep acting and reattach even if severed - 8 Minions (bugs, its own severed hand or floating eye, a crow, etc.) - 9 Mesmerizing/terrifying gaze - 10 Aura of cold/heat/smoke/rot/etc. - 11 Magic, related to how they died - 12 Shapeshifting (mist/beasts/etc.)

237.title: 1d12 weakness
237.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Revenants › 1d12 weakness
237.body: - 1 Daylight (blinded/burned/fears it) - 2 Fire (fears it/takes +1d4 damage/etc.) - 3 Bound to a tether - 4 Hungers for blood/flesh/life/fear/etc. - 5 Unmistakable odor/aura - 6 Compulsively counts/cleans/sorts/etc. - 7 Easily frustrated/confused/provoked - 8 Burned by salt/rain/moonlight/\ \ - 9 Repelled by a certain plant/animal/symbol/etc. - 10 A taboo (can't show its face, can't cross running water, etc.) - 11 Can't abide singing/music/laughter - 12 Burns HP over time/to use its powers

238.title: Anan Gllo
238.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Anan Gllo
238.body: Horde , undead , corrupted , tireless , fearless , terrifying HP 11; Armor 5 (resilience, armor), 1 vs. bronze or fire Damage weapon d6 (tags by weapon) or crushing grip d4 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities weaken over time (see below) Instinct to follow orders - Advance implacably - Unnerve foes with their silence, their empty eyes - Fight on despite crippling injury The Hillfolk speak of dark times, when the Things Below gave a gift to a petty chief. Depending on who tells the tale, it was a patch of loose soil or a deep pool of water, the hollow of a dool tree or a great cauldron of rusted iron. Regardless: a corpse placed within this vessel would rise as one of the Anan Gllo, the Empty Dead. The chief raised an undead army, and set out to make themselves king. Something interesting: The chief 's power was broken when a hero entered the vessel while still alive, giving their life to destroy it. A number of Anan Gllo linger, though, inert and waiting. Something useful: An Anan Gllo regains all its HP when it either drinks fresh blood or re-enters the vessel; it cannot otherwise regain HP. And every dawn, an Anan Gllo loses 1 HP. At 0 HP, it becomes inert until its HP are restored.

239.title: Dialeddwr
239.body: Solitary , undead , terrifying , implacable HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. silver or fire Damage choking grip or powerful blows d8+2 w/advantage ( hand , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities cannot open doors, eat, or drink Instinct to avenge - Pass for alive, except in sunlight - Unerringly track a target - Lock eyes with someone, show them the meaning of fear - Bask in moonlight to regenerate even terrible wounds Sometimes, when a great wrong is done, when a good person is cruelly slain, when the killers go unpunished… well, sometimes they come back. Called back by a prayer or perhaps a spell. Or sent back by their fellow victims. Or guided back by one of the Lady's own crows. They dig themselves free of their graves, reborn and whole. And vengeance shall be theirs.

240.title: Pyped
240.body: Solitary , undead , terrifying , craven HP 16; Armor 1 (speed) Damage bone-snapping grip d8+2 ( hand , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities burned by silver (+1d4 damage); harmed by salt (1d8 damage, hand , close ); foul stench; sustained by bone marrow Instinct to avoid the Last Door - Move with a surprising, jerky speed - Jumble up its memories with those of its host & those whose bones it's eaten - Possess a new corpse, if it can/must When an untethered ghost or a shade possesses an empty corpse, it becomes a pyped. They are wretched things, all rotting flesh and lurching movements, hungering for sweet marrow, scavenging on carrion and graves. But hunger can make them bold. Bold enough to hunt the weak and sick at the edge of a village, or a lone traveler caught unawares. Pypeds rise only from corpses that are relatively intact, and which have not undergone proper funeral rites. If a pyped is destroyed, the spirit within will seek a new, viable corpse. A shade must find one before the sun banishes it. An untethered ghost must find one before its memories fade and it becomes a wraith .

241.title: Ulliam Unlucky
241.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ulliam Unlucky
241.body: Solitary , undead , stealthy , terrifying , loyal HP 16; Armor 0 Damage whatever's at hand d8 w/disadvantage (tags vary) Special qualities burned/blinded by daylight; chill aura Instinct to pine for his family - Keep out of sight, or try to - Get frustrated, agitated, angry - Lose his temper and drain heat from someone, d10 damage w/advantage ( hand , close , reach , ignores armor) Ulliam Unlucky dragged his family from Marshedge up to Gordin's Delve , in search of a better life. But things never did work out. Last time he went out prospecting, he got lost. Then the blizzard rolled in. And that's how Ulliam died. But say what you will about Ulliam, he loved his wife and kids. Still does. Dragged his frozen self home to see 'em again. He knows they can't see him like this, but he can't keep himself from lurking. And when some lech comes sniffing around his wife, or some fool notices him and makes a fuss… well, there's only so much a man can take. And that little spark of warmth he draws out of them? Well, he's starting to acquire a taste.

242.title: Other revenants
242.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Ulliam Unlucky › Other revenants
242.body: - Mummified Green Lords and their servants - Thralls of the Things Below - Nemurvojak - Barrow wights - Deathless ones

244.title: Hazards
244.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Dangers › Hazards
244.body: - Creepy effects: flickering torches, cold spots, things going bump in the night - Superstition and fear, affecting the behavior of NPCs - Rising dread and terror , affecting even PCs - Traps placed in old tombs - Curses on tombs or tomb-goods - Disease , transmitted from fresh or rotting corpses

245.title: Other undead
245.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Dangers › Other undead
245.body: - Iron hounds - Hollow Fae - A false adept

246.title: Dool spirit
246.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Dool spirit
246.body: Group , spirit , terrifying , stealthy , devious HP 13; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage feast on fears d6 w/disadvantage ( hand , close , ignores armor) Special qualities powerless in bright light; bound to its tree or a victim's bones Instinct to taste sweet, delicious fears - Sense a victim's doubt and worries - Shape sound and shadow to unnerve and frighten - Manifest as its victim's fears (harmed only by one who masters their fear) - Bind itself to a victim's bones Dool spirits hail from beyond the Last Door. They are not undead, for they never lived, but are native to the afterlife. They sometimes slip into this world, haunting the dool trees from which they get their name. When a dool spirit takes a liking to a victim's fears, it leaves its tree and binds itself to that victim's very bones. It proceeds to haunt them, and only them, driving them mad with fear. The spirit remains bound to that victim's bones even in death, until a new dool tree sprouts where those bones are buried, and the spirit can haunt the living once more.

247.title: Gwyllgi
247.body: Solitary , spirit , cautious , stealthy , terrifying HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience, 0 vs. silver) Damage claws d8 ( close ) or bite d8+2 ( hand , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities hates daylight, must be manifest to feed Instinct to feed on fear and despair - Manifest as a black wolf with blazing red eyes - Appear to someone out alone at night and lay a death curse on them (see below) - Breathe forth a baleful cloud, d6 damage ( reach , area , ignores armor) A gwyllgi is a harbinger of death, a being from beyond. A necromancer might call one forth to send against their foes. Or, one might slip through the Last Door when dark magic or the death of many cause it to swing wide open.

248.title: Death curse
248.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Gwyllgi › Death curse
248.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to crank up tension and dread - Cause an accident or coincidence - Drive them to anxiety, desperation - Push them towards a bad decision - Get them, in the end When a gwyllgi curses a victim , they know they will die before the next full moon. Something interesting: A gwyllgi can curse only one victim at a time, and it manifests each night to feed on its victim's emotions. Something useful: You can lift the curse by destroying the gwyllgi's form, banishing it back through the Last Door.

249.title: Necromancer
249.body: Solitary , cautious , cunning , magical , death-wise HP 12; Armor 0 (or by worn armor) Damage silver-alloy knife d8 ( hand ) Special qualities can sense active spirits Instinct to hoard secrets/knowledge - Call up, speak with, bind, or lose control of an undead entity - Reveal or act on knowledge they should not possess - Send the undead against a foe - Cast a death-related spell ( reload ) 'Tis an odd sort, who would have truck with spirits of the dead. But some are born with the Sight, and a morbid curiosity. If they can avoid getting themselves killed by hungry ghosts or outraged locals… well, the dead have all manner of secrets to teach. Necromancers often find themselves consorting with entities even darker than the dead, and are prone to corruption by the Things Below. They are likely to possess one or more arcana , which they do not hesitate to use. And they are often attended by one or more bound entities.

250.title: Shade
250.body: Solitary , spirit , undead , cautious HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage none Special qualities banished by daylight Instinct (pick 1 or roll 1d6) 1: to refuse to go back, 2: to fulfill its longings, 3-4: to bemoan its fate, 5-6: to return to its rest - Manifest as a whisper and a dim image, unable to affect the physical world - Share what it knew in life - Reveal a secret learned in death - "Ride" a willing/prepared host - Possess an intact and uninterred corpse, creating a pyped Shades are the souls of the quiet dead, called back through the Last Door. Or perhaps they are merely echoes of a dead person's personality, given shape by magic. Either way, they might be called up at the Crossroads , via an arcanum like a strange skull and antlers , or by some other form of necromancy. A shade can ride a living, willing host, or one who has been properly prepared (with arcane markings, by bearing an appropriate object, via a potion, etc.). It can withstand daylight while doing so, but only with great effort. It experiences what its host experiences, and can communicate with its host via only impulse and desire. When a shade rides you and compels you to act against your will, mark XP if you do as it wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.

251.title: Tomb-bog spirit
251.breadcrumb: Death and the Undying › Tomb-bog spirit
251.body: Solitary , spirit , terrifying , implacable HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage smothering grip d10+2 ( hand , forceful , grabby ) Special qualities harms only those whose life is forfeit Instinct to consume the dying breaths of the condemned - Track down one whose life is forfeit - Wait until they are alone - Shape a hulking form from peat, soil, and mud (takes half damage from stabbing, slashing, or fire) - Whisper its victim's crimes, freezing them with fear In Ferrier's Fen , not far from Marshedge , there lies a peat mound where the town buries criminals alive. Over the years, the place has given birth to a spirit of death and punishment. The spirit is young and eager, and can be set to work upon criminals; see the grim peat mound minor arcanum for details.

252.title: The Dread River
252.body: The Dread River is the eastern border of the World's End. It flows down from the Whitefang Mountains , through the North and South Manmarch and 350\), fed by numerous creeks and streams along the way. In its lower reach, the river itself is wide and deep, never less than a quarter-mile across and dozens—if not hundreds—of feet deep. From afar, it appears placid and still. But it flows with a terrible and relentless current. The western shore alternates between wetlands, wide-open flood plains, and rugged bluffs, ruins scattered all along its length. It would be an idyllic place to live, except that the length of the river is cursed.

254.title: Always
254.body: - From afar: a sense of stillness - As you get closer: the rush of the current - The far shore: ruin-dotted, lonely, mysterious - Near the water: an uneasy feeling, like anything could be in there watching, waiting, hungry

255.title: Spring
255.body: - The river swollen and flooded from snowmelt, spilling out over its banks - Swarms of flies, like clouds of smoke - The smell of mud and rotting fish

256.title: Summer
256.body: - Brilliant green along the shores, sunlight sparkling on water - Endless chorus of waterfowl, starlings, insects, current - The occasional splash—just a fish, or a spooked turtle, maybe?

257.title: Autumn
257.body: - Riot of autumn colors along the shores, reflected in the water - Blankets of mist, hiding the river but amplifying its rush and gurgle - Honk and flap of waterfowl flying south, or wheeling overhead before alighting

258.title: Winter
258.body: - White-capped trees and bluffs and slopes - Ice growing out from the shores - Slate grey water, more menacing than ever with the promise of deadly cold

259.title: Questions
259.body: - What famous treasure is said to lie in the Ruins at Road's End? - What once drove you to spend a night encamped along the Dread River? What evil did you encounter? - Who do you know who once saw the Suileach, the Wurm with Two-Hundred Eyes? How did it change them? - Aside from empty ruins, what is noticeably strange about the lands across the river? - Does one cross the Dread River en route to Lygos? If so, how? And what separates the southern lands from those east of the river? Mountains? A desert? A sea?

260.title: Hooks
260.body: - Bandits or cultists take up residence in ruins on the Dread River's shores. - The PCs acquire a cursed treasure , originally looted from the Ruins at Road's End. - Some fool is mounting an expedition to cross the Dread River. - People from east of the river land on the western shore.

261.title: The Ruins at Road's End
261.breadcrumb: The Dread River › The Ruins at Road's End
261.body: The Highway runs from Barrier Pass through Marshedge and ends at the Dread River. Where the road ends, there was once a sizeable city, a bustling hub of trade and learning. The Tempest Lords called this city "Northernmost," for it was founded as their most far-flung outpost. The Stone Lords called it Aavonkaer ("River City"). To the Green Lords it was Pazara ("The Marketplace"). The Rime Lords called it Jalampravahati ("Through Where the Waters Flow"). Now, though, the city is called the Ruins at Road's End. For when the Stone Lords' servants rebelled and woke the Things Below , the Dread River belched forth countless horrors. The city's inhabitants were slaughtered in a single night. Throughout the time of the Barrow-Builders , various warlords and would-be sorcerer-kings claimed the ruined city. They sought to recover the lost artifacts of the Makers, or to harness the city's restless souls, or to bend the horrors in the river to their will. Sometimes, they even succeeded. But never for very long. The ruins sprawl for miles along the shore, and similar vestiges of the past are visible across the river. The legs of a once-mighty bridge still poke out from the water. Everyone agrees that great wealth and troves of lore remain among the crumbling walls. But everyone also agrees that those old cellar holes and skeletal structures—along with anything dug out of them—are cursed .

262.title: East of the River
262.breadcrumb: The Dread River › East of the River
262.body: Beyond the Dread River lie more ruins, bluffs and woods, and gently sloping plains. The Manmarchers migrated from east of the Dread River sometime before the Time of Cataclysm . But if people dwell there still, they keep well away from the shore. And if any have ventured across the river and returned, they're keeping quiet about it. Who or what lies east of the Dread River? That's for you and your group to decide, but perhaps one might find… - … the husk of a sprawling empire, home now to only jackals and wolves; - … nomadic raiders, bloody-handed even by the Manmarchers' standards; - … descendants of the Makers , greatly diminished, in isolated fiefs - … a people in thrall to an undying sorcerer-king; - … a land of horrors, thoroughly tainted by the Things Below ; - … a barren land, dead and still, bereft of spirits and magic; - … the Forest Folk ; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

263.title: Terrain
263.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

264.title: 1d12 terrain
264.body: - 1 Open water, who knows how deep? - 2 Rocks/eddies/rapids - 3 Waterfall/smaller stream feeding the river - 4 Swamp/drowned trees/logjam - 5-6 Shallows/marsh/mud/reeds - 7-8 Sandbar/beach/shingle/floodplain - 9 Slope/hill - 10 Bluff/promontory/rocky outcrop - 11 Caves - 12 Island If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

265.title: Discoveries
265.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

266.title: 1d6 discovery
266.body: - 1 Change of terrain (previous page) and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 Useful or valuable flora - 4 encounter (see below) - 5-6 point of interest (next column)

267.title: 1d6 encounter
267.body: - 1-2 Big and dangerous beast, easily provoked: aurochs , boar , a cave bear , a thunder drake , a horned drake , etc. - 3-4 Game, mostly harmless and prone to bolt: deer, water fowl, rabbits, etc. - 5 People , exploring, in trouble, or hiding out - 6 Spirit of the wild , curious or indifferent

268.title: 1d6 point of interest
268.breadcrumb: The Dread River › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
268.body: - 1 A tomb, site, or artifact of the Barrow Builders - 2 A site sacred to the spirits of the wild - 3-4 A ruin or fortification of the Stone Lords - 5 A ruin, site, or artifact of the Tempest Lords - 6 A ruin or artifact of some other group of Makers For any point of interest, decide or roll to see what taints it (if anything).

269.title: 1d6 what taints it
269.breadcrumb: The Dread River › Discoveries › 1d6 what taints it
269.body: - 1-2 The Things Below - 3-4 Death and the undying - 5 Both of the above - 6 Nothing, strangely

271.title: Hazards
271.body: - A stretch of water, and reason to brave it - A bluff to climb and a reason to climb it - Rockslides or mudslides - The river's current, surprisingly strong - Poor footing (mud, silt, branches, dropoffs) hidden by water/reeds - Winter ice, thinner than it looks - Swarms of midges or mosquitos—annoying, distracting, painful, diseased - Starlings—obscuring, drowning out sound, buffeting, possibly host to a grwgnach

272.title: People
272.body: - Bandits , hiding among the ruins, possibly cursed/corrupted - Cultists , led by a sorcerer - Manmarchers : outcasts or foolhardy explorers - Adventurers or antiquarians - A hdour or necromancer , trawling the ruins for power

273.title: Monsters
273.body: - Beasts: aurochs , a thunder drake , boars , a cave bear , a horned drake , wild horses , etc. - Predators: a cougar , wolves , a grochslon , a kleztigr , spitting drakes , etc. - A llamudwr , lurking in wetlands or flooded forests - Spirits of the wild - The ghosts or revenants of those who died here - Emanations of or creatures corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below, lurking in the river or the ruins

274.title: Cursed object
274.breadcrumb: The Dread River › Dangers › Cursed object
274.body: When you remove something valuable from the ruins that dot the Dread River , roll (+nothing, just roll). If you have warded yourself from the evil of this place, roll with advantage. On a 10+ , your theft goes unnoticed, or at least unpunished; on a 7-9 , the GM will choose (or ask you to roll for) a lesser curse; on a 6- , the GM will choose (or ask you to roll for) a greater curse.

275.title: 1d6 lesser curse
275.breadcrumb: The Dread River › Dangers › 1d6 lesser curse
275.body: - 1 Food and drink become bland, then off-putting, then vile (yet you must still eat and drink) - 2 Nightmares plague you and those with whom you share a roof; restful sleep is fleeting - 3 Wild beasts attack you with unnatural frequency and focus; domestic beasts are agitated in your presence - 4 You age quickly, a year for every month - 5 You cannot abide the sound of singing/the touch of loved ones/food cooked by another/any gift freely given/some other thing that ties you to other people - 6 You hear whispers and visions , urging you to do things

276.title: 1d6 greater curse
276.breadcrumb: The Dread River › Dangers › 1d6 greater curse
276.body: - 1 Horrors pursue you, intent on dragging you and the item back to the ruins, killing any who cross their path. Destroy or bind them, and more will come. - 2 You contract a disease ; it merely makes you miserable, but infects those nearby with a deadly sickness. - 3 Those around you find themselves on edge, their emotions running raw, and are subject to whispers and visions . Unstable folk quickly succumb. - 4 You grow thin, haggard, and unwell. Mark a debility each week, which you cannot clear. Should you die, you become a revenant , tethered to the item, obsessed with either protecting it or returning it to where it was found (your choice). - 5 Wherever you go, plants wither, food spoils, crops fail, beasts grow barren and sickly. - 6 Those who die in your presence are unable to pass through the Last Door. They linger as revenants or ghosts . Tailor a curse's specifics to the item, to where and with what it was found. Incorporate the themes and aspects of any relevant entities. And feel free to create entirely new curses, riffing on the options above. How does one free oneself of a curse? Possibilities include… - … returning the item to where it was originally found; - … destroying the item utterly: burning it, melting it, tossing it into lava, etc.; - … packing it in salt and burying it at a crossroads; - … driving a bronze spike through it, into an ancient elder tree; - … ritually cleansing the item, binding the curse to an appropriate vessel, and burying/hiding the vessel somewhere; or perhaps - … passing ownership of the item (and thus the curse) on to another willing party; they need not know it is cursed, but must claim the item as theirs.

277.title: Luglfsk
277.body: Horde , small , amphibious , corrupted HP 3; Armor 1 (hide) Damage electric shock d6 ( hand , reload ), or chew d4 ( hand ) Special qualities lightning-proof Instinct to feed - Sense a creature's electrical field - Stun or paralyze with a touch, bzzzt - Swarm a stunned creature Each one is a few feet long, like a cuttlefish crossed with a giant maggot, squirming wet and gray, a face full of feelers whose touch stings like lightning. They spend most of their lives in the water, congregating near power-crystals and aetherium artifacts Tempest Lords that have been claimed by the river. They seem entranced by the passive energy, almost lulled to sleep. But when they sense something more dynamic, something meatier, they wriggle out of the water fast enough. Some Hillfolk claim that luglfsk are just tadpoles, and tell stories of larger, many-legged terrors that fling lightning from face-feelers that stretch as long as a man grows tall. But those are probably just spook-stories to scare travelers. Probably.

278.title: Jellied horror
278.body: Solitary , large , stealthy , amorphous , mindless HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage pseudopod d10+1 ( reach , hand , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities immune to stabbing; takes half damage from weapons unless they're forceful ; acid-proof; harmed by chalk/quicklime/similar chemics Instinct to seep, to consume, to grow - Lurk in water, moving slowly, seeking or waiting for food - Dissolve organic material—weapons, clothes, flesh, etc. - Engulf, d10+3 damage with advantage ( hand , messy , ignores armor) Sometimes, the waters of the Dread River overflow their banks and flood the cursed ruins and tainted places. And sometimes, when the floodwaters recede, they leave behind… something. Something that just isn't right.

279.title: The Suileach
279.body: Solitary , huge , magical , terrifying , aquatic , corrupted HP 26; Armor 2 (scales) Damage jaws like a cave d10+7 ( reach , forceful , messy , 3 piercing) or thrashing coils d10+5 ( close , area , forceful ) Special qualities many-eyed; impossible to surprise Instinct to dominate the Dread River - Sense an intruder upon its domain - Transfix someone with one of its myriad eyes (see below) - Drag intruders to their doom It's a thing of legend. A great scaly wurm, they say, hundreds of feet long, festooned with eyes along its bulk. Hundreds of eyes, so very many. They say it sees all that happens along the river. They say it brooks no tresspass, that it drowns or devours all who encroach on its domain. But that's not entirely true. Some folks have met the Suileach and lived to tell the tale, haunted by what they saw reflected in its eyes. When you stare transfixed into one of the Suileach's eyes , mark dazed and pick 1: - You see yourself, unburdened. Name an NPC whom you love; your heart grows cold to them. Then, gain a new move from your playbook. - You see yourself as you might have been. Change your instinct and swap one of your playbook moves for another. - You see your death, as it will occur. The next time you would roll for Death's Door, don't—you get a 10+. The second time you would roll for Death's Door, don't—you get a 6-. - You see the truth reflected, darkly. The GM will tell you something interesting and useful, yet thoroughly disturbing.

280.title: Totnatter
280.body: Solitary , large , stealthy , terrifying HP 16; Armor 2 Damage venomous bite d10+1 ( reach , close , hand , 1 piercing) Special qualities thermal vision; extraordinary sense of smell Instinct to toy with its food - Scent prey, track it down - Rattle its tail, paralyzing prey with fear - Pump them full of venom, then retreat - Follow, waiting for its prey to collapse These grey-white rattlesnakes grow to absurd size, big enough to eat a boar, a deer, or even a person. Manmarchers say that they haunt the bluffs and ruins along the river, waiting months, years, or even decades between meals. While most rattlesnakes rattle to warn off threats or predators, totnatter use their rattles to hunt. They delight in the terror their rattle causes, and use it even when they could just bite their prey unawares. When a totnatter pumps its prey full of venom , start the following countdown: - ☐ Their hands shake, their bodies shiver (mark weakened ) - ☐ They grow confused, thinking things that aren't true (mark dazed ) - ☐ They struggle to breathe, to keep going (mark miserable ) - ☐ They are dying (roll for Death's Door)

281.title: Fae
281.body: Strange folk live in the Great Wood and Ferrier's Fen . Wondrous and dangerous, beings out of legend. The Forest Folk called them "Good Neighbors" and "Hidden Ones," and took pains to avoid insulting them. They cautioned Stonetop to do the same. And while the Forest Folk are gone, the Fae remain. The Green Lords bred the Fae long ago, to herd spirits and to weave wondrous realms. They relied on the Fae to manage their estates, to build the waystones , and, later, to create the Green Lord's idyllic afterlives. Some Fae resented their servitude. Some found loopholes in the promises and oaths that bound them. Ringleaders arose, creating webs of debt and obligation that undermined their masters' hold. An uprising began. Rebels gutted tombs, burned plantations, put their masters to the spear. Gangs of free Fae roved the Great Wood, burning, liberating, recruiting. Gangs became armies. The Green Lords grew desperate. They called on the Things Below , corrupted themselves with unholy power. They bred living weapons, hunted down the rebels, and took terrible vengeance. The uprising nearly failed, but the Green Lords had drawn the ire of their fellow Makers. The Stone Lords , in particular, were horrified by the Green Lords' corruption. They worked a terrible spell to shatter the heart of the Green Lord's domain, turning it into the ruined bog that today we call Ferrier's Fen. Then they allied with the Fae, and waged war against the Green Lords who survived. After the siege of Vor Svetelik , the Fae found themselves adrift and bereft of purpose. Many were no longer bound by oath or obligation. Ringleaders bickered and squabbled. Factions formed. Armies disbanded. And the Fae became the fractured people they are today.

282.title: The good neighbors
