420.body: Horde , tiny , organized HP 1; Armor 3 (carapace, size) Damage acid-dripping mandibles d6-2 ( hand , messy , 3 piercing), acid glob d6-2 ( reach , messy , reload , 1 piercing) Instinct to protect the colony - Burrow into/out of/through loose soil - Swarm over a threat - Burst with acid when killed, d4 damage ( close , area ) These red-shelled insects look like a cross between an ant and a beetle, but the size of a small cat. They form colonies a few-hundred large, typically on higher ground. A colony will harvest all the dead grass in a few miles, hibernate through winter, and then relocate in spring to a part of the Flats that hasn't burned or been harvested in a few years. They mostly ignore people and other large animals, but they'll swarm if threatened.

421.title: Klezln
421.body: Solitary , large , stealthy HP 16; Armor 3 (carapace) Damage pincers d10+3 ( hand , close , grabby ) Special Qualities senses vibrations, strong burrower Instinct to wait patiently for food - Undermine the soil, creating a sinkhole waiting to happen - Wait for prey to trigger its trap and get pinned in loose dirt, d6 damage ( grabby ) - Burst forth from the loose soil to snag a victim - Drag it beneath the soil, to be crushed and supped on at leisure Great big beetles that live out beneath the Flats, preying on large game like horses and aurochs. The Hillfolk know what signs to watch for, but they rarely tell outsiders. What better way to keep the Flats free of fools?

422.title: Pack drake
422.body: Horde , small , stealthy , organized , cautious HP 3; Armor 1 (quick reflexes) Damage sawtooth teeth d6 ( hand , grabby ) Instinct to bring down easy prey - Dart through tall grass, unseen - Surround their prey - Bite down and pull in one direction while a packmate pulls in the other (+ messy ) Sneaky little dog-sized critters that prowl the Flats. Hunt in packs ranging from six to a couple dozen. Just the worst.

423.title: Questions:
423.body: - What's the surest sign of their presence? - What do all wise travelers do to keep them at bay? - Which of you has seen them tear apart their prey? What haunts you about it to this day?

424.title: Shellback drake
424.body: Solitary , cautious , hardy , patient HP 16; Armor 3 (carapace, scales) Damage snapping, bone-crushing bite d10 ( hand , close , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) Special Qualities nearly blind, senses vibrations Instinct to lash out at "food" in range - Burrow into soil - Wait for prey half-buried - Withdraw into its shell (Armor 5) Like a snapping turtle, but 6 feet long and 400 pounds, with big, badger-like claws. Way faster than you think. It'll dig itself under a layer of soil, and wait long enough for the gwead to start growing over it. Then, when something comes near? SNAP! An intact shell is worth quite a bit ( immobile , Value 2). But good luck killing one without cracking the shell.

425.title: Thunder drake
425.body: Solitary , large HP 20; Armor 3 (scales, crest) Damage gore, trample, swat d10+3 ( hand , close , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Special Qualities lightning-proof, hard of hearing Instinct to protect its turf - Bellow ( near , area , loud , forceful , reload ) - Burrow and gnaw through rock - Sometimes: belch forth a bolt of lightning, d10+3 damage ( near , forceful , loud , ignores armor) Sometimes, out on the Flats, you'll see what looks to be small hills in the distance. I advise you to steer clear. They probably aren't hills, really, but huge piles of dirt that a thunder drake has moved as it digs. And hoo boy, you do not want to stumble into one of them on their home turf. Something interesting: Thunder drakes eat mostly grass and shrubs, but they have a taste for certain ores. They spend their time grazing, sleeping, and digging up rocks. Something useful: They particularly savor the taste of copper, and relish aetherium . If they find a source of it, they'll chew it right up and spend the next few days belching lightning bolts. When you are near a thunder drake when it bellows , roll +CON: on a 10+ , pick 1; on a 7-9, pick 2; on a 6- , all 3. - Drop what you're holding and cover your ears - Stagger back a few steps - You're deafened and disoriented for a few moments

426.title: Wild horse
426.body: Group , large , cautious , swift HP 10; Armor 0 Damage trample d6+3 ( hand , close , forceful ) Instinct to panic - Sniff trouble on the wind - Run free! - Vault over an obstacle They roam the flats, in herds ranging in size from a half-dozen to a couple dozen. The larger herds will give rise to a ceffylwraig .

427.title: Fomoraij
427.body: The Green Lords damned themselves. Oh, they'll tell you they had no other choice. That their treacherous servants drove them to it. That they knew not the cost. That they were lied to by the Things Below . But, no, they knew what they were doing. They just didn't care. And why would they have cared? Had they not lost everything? Had they not been driven from their homes and hunted like beasts, by the very servants they had created? By those who owed them everything ? So, yes damn you, the Green Lords made their deals. They called up the darkness and the poison. Let it flow through their minds. Their flesh. Their lands. And when not even that was enough to turn the tides, when they were betrayed again—this time by their fellow Makers —and they were corralled into one last trap… well, what did they care? What could they care? Let the traitors have the surface. For now. And so, twisted and mad with hatred, they went below. Into the bosom of their masters. There, they wait. They spawn new horrors. They hate. And one day, perhaps, they will return.

428.title: Names
428.body: Slavic-sounding, but with more vowels and softer consonants: Aawnaswasiya, Blaagu, Doogromil, Eleema, Loobimoor, Miwooslawa, Oolliyanaw, Rabomoor, Weelimir, Yoanaw

429.title: Hooks
429.body: - Infected servants are watching villagers near the Stream - An earthquake opens a crack in a cave bear's den, exposing them to a Fomoraij's lair and influence - Hunters/trappers go missing, then turn up weeks later horribly mutated - A Fomoraij's minions steal children from their beds and drag them to their master - Forest Folk glyphs show people filing into a cave, laden with gifts (for the Fomoraij that secretly entranced them)

430.title: Dwellers beneath
430.body: The Fomoraij have dwelt beneath the Great Wood for centuries. Their influence can be felt, certainly, and they sometimes send forth minions, but they have not seen the surface since the fall of Vor Svetelik . To create a Fomoraij, start with 2 themes from the Things Below . Combine with a constraint that keeps it from emerging, the nature of its lair, and 2-3 forms. Give it a name and perhaps an ominous title. Write it up as a threat (a villain , a beast , or a magical entity ). If the PCs are likely to fight it, also write it up as a monster (see example on page INFECTED HAGR).

431.title: 1d12 constraint
431.body: - 1-2 Confinement: by accident, by artifice, by its own size or nature ( Instinct to capture others, to escape) - 3-4 Disdain: of the world above, of the world that betrayed them ( Instinct to punish anything that reminds it of its past) - 5-6 Decay: of its mind, its power, its sense of self ( Instinct to lash out) - 7-8 Fear: of the Fae, of the Makers, of the much-hated light ( Instinct to see threats everywhere) - 9-10 Obsession: with hoarding, experimenting, digging, etc., to the exclusion of all else ( Instinct to pursue its obsession) - 11-12 Patience: it waits, it gathers power, it makes preparations ( Instinct to further its plans)

432.title: 1d12 lair
432.body: - 1 A yawning cavern, flooded; dotted with islands and unlikely structures - 2 Endless rough-hewn tunnels, like an ant hive - 3 An inverted forest of roots, tubers, fungus hanging from the ceiling - 4 A series of underground streams, rivers, lakes, all teeming with horrors - 5 A deep, vertical shaft ending at a vault of white stone - 6 Tunnels and chambers, every inch lined with bones - 7 The petrified belly of a gargantuan, primordial titan - 8 Vaulted tunnels lined with roots of iron-infused wood, rusted and bleeding - 9 Limestone caverns, the walls pulsing with red crystal - 10 Limestone caves and columns, wet and dripping, festooned with slimy life - 11 Strangely smooth tunnels coated in warm, pulsing organic material - 12 A vast chamber of white stone, a mighty dead tree growing down from the ceiling Fomoraij lairs are always found underground and beneath the Great Wood , accessed via caves and Green Lord ruins . The sites above are often corrupted by the Things Below .

433.title: 1d12 form
433.body: - 1 A swarm of smaller things/a hive-mind consciousness - 2 Boneless/squeezing/amorphous - 3 Fungal/lichenous/mossy - 4 Insectoid/shelled - 5 Too many limbs/eyes/mouths - 6 Massive/expansive/sprawling - 7 Pallid/fleshy/knobby - 8 Powerful/burrowing/rending - 9 Plantlike (roots, rhizomes, bulbs) - 10 Slimy/oozing/mucous - 11 Sporting tentacles/tendrils/feelers/antennae - 12 Worm-like/slug-like/grub-like Combine 2-3 of the above with the vaguely humanoid form it once had as a Green Lord. The PCs have encountered some horrors in the Great Wood—some crinwin oozing silver slime and wasps with hallucinatory venom. I already decided that these things were corrupted by the Things Below, based on the themes of "insanity/chaos/ruin/quicksilver" and "corruption/disease/poison/vermin." Now I'm thinking that a Fomoraij is behind them. I keep the two themes, and roll a constraint of "confinement." For its lair , I roll "a series of underground streams, rivers, lakes, all teeming with horrors." For its form , I get "too many limbs/eyes/mouths," and "a swarm of smaller things" and "slimy/oozing/mucous." I ponder, and come up with: Yaarowslow the Many is trapped away on an island in an underground lake, fed by tiny rivulets of groundwater. It has transformed its flesh into an oozing, silvery nest, host to an endless swarm of skittering and buzzing horrors that serve as its eyes and hands and mouths. For centuries, the vermin have burrowed through stone to reach the surface. It has recently succeeded, and its silvery spawn now taint a spring deep in the Great Wood.

435.title: Various treasures
435.body: - Preserved chimera corpse, mounted for display ( immobile , Value 1) - A ◇ pouch of shimmering powder (○○○○○ doses, Value 2 to the right buyer); ingest a dose and start compulsively telling secrets to those around you - A polished slice of agate the size of a dinner table, bringing nightmares and dark thoughts to those who linger in its presence ( immobile , Value 2) - A pair of what seem to be rubies (Value 3) set as the eyes of a horrible statue; they're actually red crystals and impart a cruel bloodlust on whoever holds them.

436.title: Bloodshot amber
436.body: ◇ each, magical 1d4 fist-sized gems, riddled with red-black intrusions. When you drip blood onto an amber , name a manifest spirit in your presence and roll +CHA. If the spirit has an intact tether , roll with disadvantage. On a 10+ , the spirit is imprisoned in the amber; on a 7-9 , the amber starts to suck the spirit in, but it may yet fight back; on a 6- , the spirit wrests free and the amber shatters. When you imprison a spirit in a bloodshot amber , it is wracked with pain and cannot manifest. Only one spirit can be imprisoned at a time. When you touch the amber with bare skin , you can communicate with the spirit within and release it if you want (even if you weren't the one who imprisoned it). When a bloodshot amber is found, it likely contains a spirit that has been trapped in agony for centuries.

437.title: Dream-lily pool
437.body: A raised pool of clear water. A strange lily grows within, its tendrils pouring over the lip and coating the floor, hiding bleached bones. The tendrils rustle when living things approach, and a gentle wind promises prophecy and wisdom. When you let a lily-tendril slither under your skin , take 1d4 damage (ignores armor) and fall deeply asleep. Roll +WIS: on a 10+ , you dream of wisdom—ask the GM any question and get a helpful, honest answer, though one cloaked in symbolism; on a 7-9 , you dream of wisdom (as on a 10+) but also of hunger, desperation, and want—mark a debility when you wake; on a 6- , you are trapped in endless nightmare—mark two debilities and you shall not wake unless you find an exit or someone rescues you from without. Regardless, your dreams are clearly visible in the waters of the pool. You get the sense that something else may be watching, too.

438.title: Minor arcana
438.breadcrumb: Fomoraij › Artifacts › Dream-lily pool › Minor arcana

439.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
439.body: - 1-2 A large wooden jar - 3-4 A rusty cauldron - 5-6 A strange pendant

440.title: Major arcana
440.breadcrumb: Fomoraij › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
440.body: These might very well be associated with the Fomoraij. - The Staff of the Lidless Orb - The Demonhide Cloak

442.title: Various creations
442.body: - Chimerae , bred for war or sick amusement - Hazardous growths , protecting the Fomoraij's lair or extending its influence - Revenants of chimerae, possessed by dark spirits - Crinwin , llamudwr , rhagedn , swyn , or zrajedak —but somehow different, somehow worse - Almost any of the horrors found in Vor Svetelik

443.title: Attendant spirits
443.body: - Emanations of the Things Below The Things Below - Spirits of the wild , bound into wretched servitude - Bound ghosts , kept on a tight leash - Unbound wraiths , like jackals sniffing around a butcher shop

444.title: Infected servant
444.body: Fomoraij sometimes ensnare beings of the world above, capturing them, luring them in with promises of power, or compelling the weak-willed to attend them. They then infect the victim, turning them into extensions of their will. Start with a base creature, and then pick or roll for the nature of the infection, the means of control, and up to 3 gifts. Interpret them based on what you know about its master. Update the base creature's tags, stats, qualities, instinct, and moves accordingly.

445.title: 1d12 infection
445.body: - 1-2 A bug/worm/vine, burrowed into their brain/spine - 3-4 A foul substance, injected/inhaled/imbibed - 5-6 A fungus/plant, growing under their skin - 7-8 A spirit possessing their body, bound by intricate scars/tattoos - 9-10 A mass of vermin/a single horror, eating them from the inside out - 11-12 One or more shards of red crystal embedded in their skull

446.title: 1d12 means of control
446.body: - 1-2 By crushing their will - 3-4 By eating away at what they once were - 5-6 By filling them with euphoria/pleasure - 7-8 By promising them power/immortality/revenge/a cure/etc. - 9-10 By wracking them with pain - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, or make up something new If you like, roll 1d6 for the extent of control.

447.title: 1d12 gift
447.body: - 1 A reach , near , or far attack (tentacles, spitting globs, flung spines, etc.) - 2 A terrifying presence; an ability to spread panic/fear/horror - 3 An area attack (cloud of poison, blast of nettles, psychic scream, etc.) - 4 An amorphous/malleable/shapechanging form - 5 An ongoing link to its master, an ability to share senses/thoughts/words/power - 6 A way to spread its infection and/or its master's influence (intentionally, incidentally, or both) - 7 Camouflage/stealth/ability to avoid notice - 8 Exceptional strength/speed/ferocity/deadliness - 9 Influence/control over the minds of others - 10 Minions/servants/ability to send out extensions of itself - 11 Mobility (jumping, flying, climbing, burrowing, squeezing, etc.) - 12 Unnatural resilience/vigor/durability Should a PC or an important NPC become infected by a Fomoraij (or one of their servants), write it up as a threat (an affliction ). If a PC willingly becomes an infected servant, consider creating a playbook insert, using the Thrall for inspiration (see Book I ). I want a big scary servant of Yaarowslow the Many Fomoraij, a hagr The Great Wood that stumbled on the tainted spring. I roll "a foul substance, injected/inhaled/imbibed" and think of Yaarowslow's silvery slime. I get "by eating away at what they once were" as a means of control, and imagine the silver slime corrupting it from the inside, replacing its blood and organs and overwhelming its mind with voices and delusions. For gifts, I pick "unnatural resilience/vigor/durability" and "a way to spread its infection and/or its master's influence." I then roll for a third gift and get "an area attack." I update the hagr's stat block to reflect these changes, and end up with this:

448.title: INFECTED HAGR
448.body: - Solitary, large, terrifying, hardy, magical - HP 20; ARMOR 4 (resilience), 1 vs. bronze (blubbery hide) - DAMAGE meaty hands d10+3 (hand, close, grabby, forceful) or tree trunk d10+5 (reach, area, crude, forceful) or hurled heavy thing d10+3 (thrown, forceful) - SPECIAL QUALITIES infectious blood; suffers from delusions, behaves erratically - INSTINCT to spread its master's influence - Fix someone with its evil eye, inflicting overwhelming delusions - Pluck things from the ground as if weightless - Obsessively carve its master's sigils into things - Fly into a senseless rage - Spew forth an infectious, silvery cloud (reach, area, reload)

449.title: "Typical" Fomoraij
449.body: Solitary , large , magical , cunning , cautious , terrifying HP 26; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage mighty blows d12+3 ( hand , close , forceful ) Special qualities can sense anything that happens in its lair (but might not think to) Instinct [Use constraint as a guide] - Overawe anyone in its presence - Twist, mutate, or transform living things with a gesture - Wield nearby plants and beasts as extensions of itself - Regenerate wounds from anything but orichalcum No Fomoraij is truly typical; use the stats above as a starting point. A Fomoraij's constraint Fomoraij dictates its instinct. Add or change its tags, stats, special qualities, and moves based on the themes The Things Below, constraint, lair, and forms Fomoraij that you've identified. Yaarowslow the Many is a mass of flesh that oozes infectious silvery slime, and the host of skittering, slithering, and buzzing horrors that serve as an extension of its will.

450.title: YAAROWSLOW, THE MANY
450.body: Solitary, large, magical, terrifying, amorphous HP 24; Armor 5 (resilience, lacks organs), 2 vs. bronze (thick hide) DAMAGE spurts of corrosive slime d10+1 (hand, close, area, messy, 1 piercing) SPECIAL QUALITIES largely immobile, hive-mind connection with its vermin swarms; half damage from stabbing or smashing INSTINCT to escape - Overwhelm foes with horror and thousands of squealing voices - Infect minds and bodies with silvery slime - Wield its swarms and its silvery slime as an extension of itself - Regenerate wounds from anything but orichalcum

451.title: The Foothills
451.body: Two days northwest of Stonetop, the rolling Flats give way to hills, climbing up into the Whitefang Mountains . The Highway cuts through these hills, leading to Barrier Pass . To the east, the hills wrap around the Great Wood . The Foothills are rugged and forest-strewn, crisscrossed with streams, dotted with gorges and ponds and small lakes. In ages past, it was a borderland between the Rime Lords and the Green Lords , and later it was the extent of the Barrow Builders' reach Barrow Builders. Nowadays, it is mostly uninhabited, but both Stonetop and Barrier Pass come here to harvest timber.

453.title: Always
453.body: - Hills rising up from the Flats, mountains looming behind them - Young thin trees on the southernmost slopes, getting bigger and older as you press into the hills - Echoes of birdsong, beast-bellows, tumbling rocks, raised voices

454.title: Spring
454.body: - Thin branches drooping with fresh green leaves - Mud slipping and squishing underfoot - Surprising chill of shadowed vales, where snow still lingers

455.title: Summer
455.body: - Muggy, humid heat - Sweat stinging your eyes as you hump up another slope - Cold clear water from a gurgling stream, tasting better than anything

456.title: Autumn
456.body: - Slopes turned red and orange and yellow, then black and brown and gray - Plump squirrels and other critters, busily foraging for winter - Cold, clammy mist lasting well into the day

457.title: Winter
457.body: - Waist-deep snow drifts in the valleys - Frozen-over streams, water gurgling ominously from below - Squeak of trees, swaying in the cold wind - Short, magical twilight as the sun drops below the mountains

458.title: Questions
458.body: - What striking difference do you notice between the trees here and those in the Great Wood? - Who typically leads Stonetop's logging trips up to the Foothills? - What dread beast or monster is said to lurk in the Foothills? - What is said to draw its ire? - Who claims to have encountered it? When, and how so? - Why do you/don't you believe them? - What mundane dangers up there worry you more than some legendary beast?

459.title: Hooks
459.body: - A logging expedition goes missing - Loggers stumble upon a ruin or corrupted site - Someone went missing on a logging trip years ago, and now they reappear - Heavy rains wash out a stretch of the Highway, blocking access to good timber - An adventurer claims to know the location of an old barrow in the Foothills, said to be laden with gold and other treasure

460.title: Logging trips
460.body: A typical logging trip generates a wagonload of timber (Value 2), and requires: - A wagon and two horses; - 20 able-bodied workers; and - Pulling Together over 9-12 days (for travel and work) Trips traditionally take place in autumn, after the harvest is complete and only if there are no more-pressing needs in town. The Palisade steading improvement requires 1,000 logs of good timber (Value 3). A wagonload of timber (Value 2) contains 40-50 logs, meaning you need 20-25 wagonloads. To gather that much timber: - You need 20 able-bodied workers, a wagon, and two horses working for the course of a season - You must talk to a few town elders, and get their support - You risk accidents and attacks - You must spend 1 Surplus (to feed and equip the loggers) and reduce Fortunes by 1 (because of so many people and resources being away for so long) - The steading must Pull Together over the course of the season

461.title: . steading improvement .
461.breadcrumb: The Foothills › Logging trips › . steading improvement .

462.title: PERMANENT LOGGING CAMP
462.breadcrumb: The Foothills › Logging trips › . steading improvement . › PERMANENT LOGGING CAMP
462.body: Suppose we built a few cabins and kept loggers up there year-round? Requires all of the following: - Two or three permanent dwellings, just off the Highway - Supplies, furnishings, and tools for those dwellings (Value 2) - 5-6 capable adults, willing and able to relocate and log year-round - An extra wagon (Value 3) and extra horse or mule (Value 3) to haul timber to and from Stonetop - Four seasons of operation, each generating a wagonload of timber (Value 2) When you mark all the requirements , add "Logging: timber, wood" to the resources list and increase Fortunes by 1. Henceforth, as long as the camp is in operation, the steading has a ready supply of timber for improvements and other projects, and it consumes 1 less Surplus than usual in winter. However, the logging camp consumes 1 Surplus every summer or else it ceases operation.

463.title: Terrain
463.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

464.title: 1d12 terrain
464.body: - 1 Large pond/small lake - 2 Creek, stream, gulley - 3 Steep slope: barren, treacherous, unstable - 4 Dense thicket/brush - 5-6 Swath/copse of one type of tree - 7-8 Gentle slope or valley - 9 Steep slope: forested, overgrown - 10 Sheer rock face, bluff, rocky outcrop - 11 Clearing, meadow, burn site - 12 Cave, cavern, grotto If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

465.title: Discoveries
465.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

466.title: 1d6 discovery
466.body: - 1 A change of terrain The Foothills, and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 folk from barrier pass (next column) - 4 Some other encounter (next column) - 5 A point of interest (see below) - 6 Useful or valuable flora

467.title: 1d6 folk from barrier pass
467.breadcrumb: The Foothills › Discoveries › 1d6 folk from barrier pass
467.body: - 1 A war party of 4-8 guardians and/or monks, hunting something dangerous - 2 A patrol of 2-4 guardians, led by a White Crystal monk - 3 A monk and/or novice, on their own to study/practice/meditate/make pilgrimage - 4 A logging crew (4-12 layfolk, plus a few guardians) - 5-6 1-4 layfolk hunting or foraging Consider having someone roll the Die of Fate for their disposition. See Barrier Pass for guardian, monk, and novice stats.

468.title: 1d6 encounter
468.body: - 1 A chimera , wary or indifferent but not outright hostile - 2 A Fae , curious or indifferent - 3 A tulpa , likely tethered to a nearby Rime Lord site - 4 A spirit of the wild , curious or indifferent - 5 A dangerous beast—like a boar , cave bear , cougar , ice drake , mountain apes , or wolves —wary or indifferent, but easily provoked - 6 Game: good eating but prone to bolt (deer, rabbits, pheasant, etc.)

469.title: 1d6 point of interest
469.breadcrumb: The Foothills › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
469.body: - 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below - 2 A place haunted by death or the undying Death and the Undying - 3 A site or artifact of the Barrow Builders - 4 A ruin or artifact of the Green Lords or a Fae domain - 5 A ruin or artifact of the Rime Lords or their human disciples - 6 A place sacred to the spirits of the wild

471.title: Hazards
471.body: - Getting lost among the hills and trees - Treacherous terrain (loose footing, scree, slippery slopes, potholes, deadfall, etc.) - Flash flooding, especially in ravines and valleys - Harsh weather, especially rain and snow - Something tall to climb, and reason to climb it - Hazardous growth , from the time of the Green Lords

472.title: Monsters
472.body: - Ornery beasts: boars , cave bears , ice drakes , mountain apes , wisents - Predators: butcherbirds , cougars , feathered drakes , or wolves - Spirits of the wild - Fae - A hagr or two, come up from the Great Wood - Tulpas , tied to Rime Lord sites or perhaps to Barrier Pass - Chimerae and other remnants of the Green Lords - Ghosts or revenants , lurking near old barrows, tombs, or battlefields - A copse of dool trees and its resident spirits - A bhoka , come down from the mountains - Whatever dangerous beast or monster your players tell you dwells in these hills

473.title: Forest Folk
473.body: All the tales agree: the Folk were in the Great Wood long before Stonetop was founded. They welcomed the newcomers, or at least suffered them, but demanded that villagers never fell a living tree from the Wood. The Forest Folk mostly kept to themselves. But over generations, contacts were made. Favors exchanged, friendships won, stories shared. And more shared, too, judging by the odd eyes that some villagers bear. The villagers did not notice when the Forest Folk left, not right away. Encounters were never common. Then they became rare. Then there were none at all. Their absence echoes through the Great Wood. Their crude glyphs can still be found, carved into trees and boulders. Their sacred places stand empty. And dangerous creatures—things once hunted by the Folk—now breed freely.

474.title: Names
474.body: Slavic-sounding, often diminutives: Analiya, Asya, Avo, Boyko, Danja, Dimvo, Dobri, Galya, Genko, Ivka, Inko, Kosta, Lyubo, Milada, Milko, Mincha, Mitko, Nadya, Neli, Penko, Polya, Radko, Raya, Sajka, Sasho, Tsvetanka, Valya, Vasko

475.title: Questions
475.body: - Who (among you/among your kin/that you know) bears the strange eyes of the Forest Folk? What do they look like? - How are folk with "the forest eyes" treated in Stonetop? What about their parents? - Did you ever meet one of the Forest Folk? Aside from their eyes, which of their inhuman features did you find most disturbing? - It's a mystery for sure, but what do you think happened to the Forest Folk? Why?

476.title: Lore
476.body: The Forest Folk's ancestors were mortal servants of the Green Lords . They lived nomadic lives among the trees, traveling in small bands and living off the land. Something interesting: The Folk would often commune with spirits of the wild , and sometimes had dealings with the Fae . Maybe they know where the Folk went? Something useful: You know of a place where the Forest Folk used to congregate, not too far from Stonetop, or a Fae they once dealt with frequently, or a sacred site they revered—tell us about it! Also, tell us why you think it's a good place to start investigating what happened to the Folk.

477.title: Where did they go?
477.breadcrumb: Forest Folk › Lore › Where did they go?
477.body: That's for you and your group to decide, but possibilities include... - … they fled east, to escape the waking of the Crombil ; - … they went to fight the Fomoraij , maybe at the behest of the Fae; - … a servant of the Things Below hunted them down, chased them off; - … a curse transformed them into living trees, though not all at once; - … a powerful Fae whisked them off into its domain; - … they were ravaged by sickness; the survivors left in search of a miracle; or - … their numbers were always few, and they simply died out.

478.title: Hooks
478.body: - A hunter finds a mass grave of the Forest Folk, from, oh, about 10 years back - A cave is covered with glyphs and pictograms that reveal valuable lore, but some of the glyphs are visible only to those with the forest eyes - A trapper swears that they've seen one of the Forest Folk, or signs of their recent presence - 60 years ago, Stonetop gave the Forest Folk a precious gift, which they took to one of the sacred sites; perhaps it's still there?

479.title: Forest eyes
479.body: Those who inherit the "forest eyes" see well at night. They see by mere starlight as well as most humans can see under a full moon; they see by moonlight as well as most humans can see at dusk. Beyond that, certain plants, lichens, and rocks shine with indescribable colors that can only be seen with the forest eyes. The Folk would sometimes be seen staring rapt at a mundane grove or meadow, marveling at some unseen beauty. Humans with the forest eyes sometimes do likewise. If a PC was born with (or develops) the forest eyes, feel free to use these GM moves on them: - Reveal glyphs, markings, or patterns that only they can see - Distract them with colors and patterns of impossible beauty

480.title: Glyphs and pictograms
480.body: The Forest Folk left crude marks and drawings throughout the Great Wood. When the PCs encounter a point of interest in the Great Wood, and it was around before the Folk disappeared, pick or have someone roll the Die of Fate:

481.title: 1d6 markings
481.breadcrumb: Forest Folk › Glyphs and pictograms › 1d6 markings
481.body: - 1-2 None to be found - 3 Hidden, incomplete/marred - 4 Hidden, intact - 5 Obvious but incomplete/marred - 6 Obvious and intact "Hidden" glyphs might be hidden intentionally, obscured by happenstance (vines, growth, etc.), and/or made with pigments that only the forest eyes can see. To an outsider, these glyphs and pictograms seem like nonsense—arbitrary shapes and crude, stylized drawings. But the Forest Folk had an elaborate visual vocabulary, and most of these markings are rich with meaning. A PC can Know Things to attempt to discern the meanings of any such glyphs, with disadvantage if they are incomplete or marred. On a 7-9 , they discern a vague concept, like "dangerous plants," "bound spirit" or "sorrow/loss". On a 10+ , they glean the full meaning, like "these plants are dangerous to eat" or "this tree binds a spirit of darkness" or "many Forest Folk died here at the hands of a sorcerer."

482.title: Sites
482.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

483.title: 1d6 a place where they...
483.breadcrumb: Forest Folk › Sites › 1d6 a place where they...
483.body: - 1 … stood guard over something - 2 … fought/died/interred their dead - 3 … performed rituals/communed with spirits/worked magic - 4 … dwelt/worked/made things/stored things - 5 … gathered/congregated/held festivals - 6 … left signs of what happened to them Pick or roll for an appropriate terrain . Populate it with signs of their presence (below), artifacts , and dangers of the Forest Folk or the Great Wood . Consider combining with another point of interest from the Great Wood. Signs of the Forest Folk's presence include: - Elaborate glyphs and pictograms - Strips of leather, often dyed, hanging from trees or wrapped around trunks - Tools/weapons of stone, wood, bone: adzes, scrapers, arrowheads, spears, etc. - Containers of carved wood, cured leather, woven plant fiber - Clothes (leather, fur, woven plants), likely in tatters - Bones/remains, of them and/or their prey

485.title: Various treasures
485.body: - A small wooden carving of some beast or chimera (Value 0) - ◇ Oversized, exquisitely knapped flint spear head ( crude , awkward , Value 1 to a collector) - ◇◇ Big wooden bowl and lid, sealed with beeswax, filled with dried medicinal herb (Value 2) - Mostly-buried skeleton of an elephant-sized beast, bones carved with glyphs and pictograms that tell an important story ( immobile , Value 2) - An acre of forest with 3d6 varieties of useful flora growing in careful harmony; 1d6-1 can be harvested at the moment.

486.title: A wooden mask
486.breadcrumb: Forest Folk › Artifacts › A wooden mask
486.body: fragile , magical Carved from pale elder wood in the shape of a bird's head, but with small horns and fangs. The inside is chiseled with glyphs like those that the Forest Folk used to make. Something interesting: You recognize glyphs for "beast," "spirit," "forest," and… "peace?" Or maybe "treaty." Something useful: When you wear the wooden mask , beasts of the Great Wood will do you no harm, though your companions have no such protection. Perversions of nature are unaffected. If you harm a beast of the Great Wood while wearing the mask , it loses all power and rapidly rots away.

487.title: Crystal knife
487.body: hand , crude , magical A blade of knapped quartz, with the hilt wrapped in glyph-covered leather. It gleams to those with the forest eyes. When you wield the crystal knife as a weapon , you can harm the manifest form of any spirit, ignoring armor and dealing +1d4 damage. If the 1d4 comes up a 4, though, the knife shatters into useless shards.

488.title: Artifacts of others
488.breadcrumb: Forest Folk › Artifacts › Artifacts of others
488.body: Held in reverence, used with caution, and/or hidden where they could do no harm. - Artifacts of the Green Lords - Items related to the spirits of the wild - Gifts of the Fae - Objects tied to death or the undying - Artifacts cursed by the Things Below

490.title: Cursed glyphs
490.body: They look like other glyphs the Forest Folk left behind, but pick or roll for both a trigger and an effect.

491.title: 1d6 trigger
491.body: - 1 Merely being in their presence - 2 Seeing them, even at a glance - 3 Passing a border that they define, or attempting to - 4 Attempting to read or comprehend them - 5 Touching whatever they're inscribed upon - 6 Erasing, breaking, or marring them

492.title: 1d6 effect
492.body: - 1 Afflicts a lingering, withering disease - 2 Traps/ensnares/binds - 3 Sickens/weakens/harms - 4 Instills a powerful emotion (fear, despair, rage, etc.) - 5 Draws the attention of certain spirits - 6 Befuddles/confuses/twists the senses

493.title: Opportunists
493.body: Encroaching on territory vacated by the Forest Folk, or growing now that they are no longer kept in check. - Crinwin - Beasts of the Great Wood The Great Wood - Hazardous growths and chimerae - Fae , released from old oaths and/or keen to claim new territory

494.title: Spirits
494.body: - Ghosts or revenants of Forest Folk who died badly, or with work unfinished - Spirits of the wild who dwelt happily alongside the Folk, or who were imprisoned by them - Emanations and spirits corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below), trapped but straining at their bindings.

495.title: Forge Lords
495.body: Forge Lords were Makers who bent fire and metal to their will. They built a sprawling civilization among the Huffel Peaks and on the shores of the sea beyond. They wrought wonders and traded with other Makers, in what is now Gordin's Delve . They grew wealthy, proud, and strong. A fire burned inside the Forge Lords. It fueled their magic, and also their passion and creativity, their love of beauty and craftsmanship. Their desire to do better, to be better. But this fire was also a curse. If left to burn unchecked—if they gave in to ambition, jealousy, and greed—it could consume them. Burn away their finer feelings, twist them into terrible, fire-belching beasts. To guard against this curse, the Forge Lords lauded loyalty and generosity. They observed strict rules of hospitality and decorum. For centuries, it worked. Their lands were hotbeds of politics and betrayal, but bloody-handed murder was rare. Only the most debased citizens lost themselves to the Change. Then came the Time of Cataclysm . Volcanoes belched forth lava and ash. The Forge Lord's principal city, Moratia Rubra, was destroyed. So were many of their mines, foundries, and estates. Their society plunged into chaos. And the Things Below stoked the flames of vengeance, greed, and paranoia. Rivalry gave way to murder. Murder gave way to slaughter. Only those who embraced the Change survived. And soon, the Forge Lords were gone. Only draventao remained.

496.title: Names
496.body: Portuguese- and Galician-sounding, but often with 4 or 5 syllables: Anacarbrugel, Barximare, Cagacola, Liainlosoi, Mancaoro, Olarela, Patomecie, Ronofoxur, Toloalare, Velanbria, Xeixabrun Often have titles like Dominar ("Master"), Ogran ("the Great").

497.title: Hooks
497.body: - Miners loot and sell treasure guarded by iron hounds , triggering a chain of grisly deaths. - Ustrina warn the PCs away from a Forge Lord ruin, but why? - A scoundrel finds a draventao 's lair Forge Lords, wants help looting it. - A merchant is secretly hauling a shipment of orichalcum to market. - Prospectors find a Forge Lord settlement buried and preserved in pumice.

499.title: 1d12 theme
499.body: - 1 Fire and heat, lava, smoke and embers, ash and cinders - 2 Industry: forges and furnaces, crucibles and kilns, foundries and fuel, mills and mines, and all that they consume/produce - 3 Artistry: inspiration, invention, the pursuit of excellence/beauty/truth - 4 Metal given life: clockwork constructs, spirit-vessels, prosthetics fused to flesh - 5 Weakness burnt away: to create, to transform, to destroy, to purify - 6 Ustrina servants/factions/rivalries/secrets/plots/loyalty/betrayal - 7 Passions enflamed: hunger, lust, envy, greed, obsession, paranoia, and wrath - 8 Cataclysm: eruptions, terror, slaughter, societal collapse, the descent into chaos - 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

500.title: Sites
500.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

501.title: 1d6 site
501.body: - 1-3 Lingering signs of their presence and influence (see below) - 4-6 Ruin

502.title: Lingering signs
502.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

503.title: 1d6 sign
503.body: - 1 Aftermath of destruction (next column), a scar on the world - 2 Traces of industry (next column) from long ago - 3 A fiery spirit (next page), bound to some useful purpose - 4 A monument or marker (next page) - 5 A stretch of the Makers' Roads , intact, broken, and/or buried in tuff - 6 A hidden hoard, filled with various treasures

504.title: Aftermath of destruction
504.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Sites › Aftermath of destruction
504.body: For example: - Chimneys and bits of wall, sticking out of rubble and tuff, charred and dusty. - A mountainside, melted and smooth like black glass. - A clearing above a long-burning coalseam fire: barren, buckled, and cracked, the soil charred white, the air foul to breathe and hot even in winter. - A mass grave of Ustrina, maybe a Forge Lord or two, buried rather than properly burned. - The bones of a great reptilian beast, with rusted plates (armor?) fused to the ribs and spine. The skull is caved in, the teeth pried loose and missing. Such places might very well be haunted and/or corrupted by the Things Below .

505.title: Traces of industry
505.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Sites › Traces of industry
505.body: For example: - A gouge in bedrock, like a pit or canyon with terraced walls—an open quarry from long ago. - A lake/mire/pool: chalky shores, metallic swirls, stunted life. - A slag-pile: pitted, fist-sized rocks (dull red and rough, or glassy blue and green), mostly barren, quite unstable. - The top of a mountain just missing, collapsed on itself or turned into a crater. - The caldera of a dormant volcano. Rusty, rune-carved spires dot the rim, and enormous pitted chains litter the area. - Fragments of an aqueduct, still adorned with beautiful ceramic tiles.

506.title: A fiery spirit
506.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Sites › A fiery spirit
506.body: For example: - A furnace, still warm from banked coals, kept smoldering for centuries by a groggy, mopey spirit. - A rune-etched marble slab set before a magma pool. Binds a spirit that manifests as a figure of molten rock. Helps with metalwork in exchange for gossip. - A crystalline matrix, home to a dozen small flames that have burnt for centuries without fuel. Opinionated blowhards, but quite clever and craving novelty. - A huge bronze statue of a boar, its mouth agape. The spirit within consumes the flammability of objects, making them fireproof. But the statue itself ignites with the slightest spark and burns like hot oil. - Small flame spirits, each bound into a different glass sphere set into a wall or fixture. Light up in the presence of people, chat amiably. - A cinderwraith and the bones it's bound to, trapped behind a masonry wall in some Forge Lord's cellar

507.title: Monument or marker
507.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Sites › Monument or marker
507.body: For example: - A bronze statue of a regal figure, once 60 feet tall, found toppled, dented, and buried in a valley. - Another huge bronze statue of a regal figure, still set upon its summit but half melted. - A portrait on a mountainside, a mosaic made from millions of glass beads. - A seamless tower of basalt, topped with an open-faced onion dome of brilliant brass, an eternal flame burning within. - A marble slab, big as a house. One side is covered with a bronze plaque, etched with tight-packed calligraphic runes, mostly illegible under all the lichen.

508.title: Ruin
508.body: Pick or roll for themes Forge Lords, structure, purpose, architectural elements, condition, and ustrina presence. Place it in an appropriate terrain (see Gordin’s Delve for Gordin's Delve, Huffel Peaks for the Huffel Peaks, or The Labyrinth for the Labyrinth). Populate it with lingering signs , artifacts , and/or dangers of the Forge Lords or the appropriate region.

509.title: 1d6 structure
509.body: - 1 A cluster of smaller buildings - 2 A single larger building, built around an open courtyard/garden/pool - 3 Large open space - 4 Warren-like structure, many connected rooms/buildings - 5 Tunnel(s)/vault(s) carved into mountain/bedrock - 6 Fortified complex containing a number of the above

510.title: 1d6 purpose
510.body: - 1 Dwelling (home, manor, barracks, apartments, etc.) - 2 Infrastructure (aqueduct, cistern, bridge, tunnel, fortification, etc.) - 3 Storage (vault, warehouse, treasury, granary, etc.) - 4 Production (smithy, studio, mine, etc.) - 5 Civic life (baths, market, gardens, monument, governance, etc.) - 6 Pleasure (gallery, restaurant, theater, arena, etc.) The PCs are in Gordin's Delve, seeking an audience with Mutra the Teeth. I think his compound is one of the Forge Lord ruins in town. I'm picturing something carved into the ridges that cradle Gordin's Delve, so I pick "tunnel(s)/vault(s)" for structure, "buried… but mostly intact" for condition, and "none to speak of " for Ustrina presence. I roll a 3 for theme (artistry), a 6 for purpose (pleasure), and a 1 for architectural elements (domes/arches/columns/arcades). I envision the following:

511.title: 1d6 architectural elements
511.body: - 1 Domes/arches/columns/arcades - 2 Ceramics and glasswork: mosaics, tile, urns, stained glass, mirrors - 3 Enamel/glaze/gilding/flowing runes - 4 Bricks/marble slabs/carved porphyry - 5 Statues/reliefs (bronze or marble) - 6 Furnaces/crucibles/chimneys/chains/gears Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants—people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

512.title: 1d6 condition
512.body: - 1 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable - 2 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away - 3-4 Buried, submerged, and/or overgrown, but mostly intact - 5-6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

513.title: 1d6 ustrina presence
513.body: - 1 A settlement, actively inhabited - 2 Two or more groups, in conflict - 3 Pilgrims/scavengers/wardens/etc. - 4 A settlement, unoccupied/abandoned - 5 Offerings/vandalism/excavations/etc. - 6 None to speak of If Ustrina are present, or have been, consider rolling 1d6 for a sense of their numbers. Mutra's compound was once the showroom for some Forge Lord's collected wealth. Outside, it's like the Treasury of Petra, in Jordan—columns before a recessed entrance and a looming arch. The arch leads to a vast chamber with a lofty domed ceiling and numerous empty plinths, each taller than a tall man. Mutra's people have strung screens between the plinths to split the chamber into barracks and storage and so forth. "Smaller" side chambers, carved further into the rock, hold Mutra's personal quarters—and his own not-so-modest collection.

514.title: Orichalcum
514.body: A mystical metal that flashes red with fire and burns away the impure, bane to the Things Below and perversions of nature. The Forge Lords knew its secrets—how to mine it, smelt it, alloy it, and work it—and it was treasured even in their day. Pick or roll the variety of any orichalcum the PCs encounter.

515.title: 1d12 variety
515.body: - 1 Nearly pure, unworked nuggets/ingots, full of potential (Value 2) - 2-3 Nearly pure, cast into runes (repels perversions of nature, protects against their magic, Value 2) - 4 Alloyed with copper and tin, powdered (burns perversions of nature; Value 1) - 5 Alloyed with gold, flaked, suspended in elixir (burns away poison/disease/corruption should the imbiber survive, Value 2) - 6 Alloyed with lead, powdered (dispels unclean magic, Value 2) - 7 Alloyed with magnesium, wrought into a charm (burns white-hot when touched by an oathbreaker, Value 2) - 8 Alloyed with iron, forged into a sphere (glows bright red near illusions, reveals them as false, Value 2) - 9-10 Alloyed with tin and arsenic, then cast into a weapon/shield/cuirass (+2 Value), counts as bronze whenever it matters - 11-12 Alloyed as needed and worked into some appropriate form, then imbued with magic related to fire/truth/purification Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size, quantity, and/or scale. Orichalcum often melts or vaporizes as it burns away impurity. Proper alloying helps prevent this, but even a well-made orichalcum spear might turn to slag as it strikes down a corrupted foe.

516.title: Anthracite
516.body: A lustrous, gray-black stone, a form of purified coal. Catches fire like dry wood, but burns hotter, longer, with very little smoke. The Forge Lords used it to fuel their furnaces and kilns, or even to just heat their homes. Some Ustrina know the secrets to making the stuff (cooking coal, infusing it with flammability leached from other objects), though they (probably?) lack the numbers for extensive coal mining. They occasionally trade anthracite in Gordin's Delve, but only small amounts. The Bosses or southern merchants would start wars to learn how it could be mass produced.

517.title: Other materials
517.body: Many Forge Lords knew how to make aetherium and invest it with special characteristics. Indeed, they likely taught the Tempest Lords a thing or two. A few Forge Lords could create makerglass , or at least shape it, but never with the skill and deftness of the Stone Lords. The Forge Lords prized black iron . It can be worked only with fire, strength, and skill—not with magic—and so only master smiths could work it. A few Forge Lords dabbled with red crystal , inspired by the Things Below or just arrogant enough to think they could control it.

519.title: Various treasures
519.body: - 1d6 glass vials ( fragile , Value 0), etched with a Forge Lord's mark - 2d4 lumps of anthracite (Value 1), each able to fuel a good cookfire for a night - A ◇ padded case with 4 clay spheres, each containing potent naphtha that burns hot & sticky when exposed to the air (damage d10, thrown , area , dangerous , ignores armor) - A gold mobius strip on a chain (Value 2) - An oversized ◇◇ orichalcum spearhead (Value 2), too big to use but a smith might recast it - A ◇ box holding a clockwork diorama; wind its key and it plays music, the masked figures dance and twirl ( fragile , magical ?, Value 3) - A flawless ◇ vase ( fragile , beautiful , Value 4), 2 feet tall with an iridescent glaze; reminds you of the fragility of life - A pile of coins, jewelry, art, and wonders ( immobile , Value 5), a draventao 's hoard Forge Lords See also: the curiosities and wonders that Ustrina sometimes bring to Gordin's Delve to trade.

520.title: Orichalcum spear
520.body: ◇, close , thrown , magical , beautiful , 1 piercing The haft needs replacing, but the spearhead itself is pristine, beautiful, sharp as hot flame. When you strike a perversion of nature with the spear , deal +1d4 damage. When you strike a perversion of nature and roll max damage (including any bonus dice), deal another +1d4 damage as the spearhead flares and melts into worthless slag.

521.title: An oversized ring
521.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Artifacts › An oversized ring
521.body: ◇, beautiful , magical , Value 4 A fist-sized signet, large even by the Makers' standards, made of orichalcum. It glows like a dying ember, but is cool to the touch. When anyone lies in the ring's presence, their tongue blisters and burns. The greater the lie, the more damage is done. Something interesting: This was the signet ring of a Forge Lord, one of great wealth and importance; the Ustrina often show keen interest in such things. Something useful: This was the signet ring of Omelor Xonane, a storied artificer. Many of their works were said to be keyed to this signet ring. Who knows what wonders it could unlock?

522.title: Two mirrors
522.body: ◇, beautiful , magical , Value 4 One is a flawless silvered ◇◇ mirror ( magical , beautiful , fragile , Value 4), 3 feet tall and set in a golden frame. Forge Lord runes adorn the frame, translated as: "To my beautiful Esé, my faithful, my rock." The other is a smaller, humble ◇ mirror ( magical , fragile , Value 2), only 12 inches tall in a frame of bronze. It bears the same inscription as the other mirror, but inverted. A small needle protrudes from the frame, stained dark. When you prick your finger and touch the smaller mirror , it shows what is reflected in the larger mirror until you break contact. Your blood soaks into the surface, leaving no stain.

523.title: Minor arcana
523.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Artifacts › Two mirrors › Minor arcana

524.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
524.body: - 1 Runes around a ruined hall - 2 A richly woven rug - 3 A metal man - 4 A prospector's tale - 5 A rusty steel blade - 6 A runic branding iron

525.title: Major arcana
525.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
525.body: These might very well be associated with the Forge Lords. - The Blood-quenched Sword - The Red Scepter

527.title: Entities
527.body: - Ustrina , of course - Fire spirits , bound to an oven/forge/furnace, likely hungry and weak - A fire vortex , set to guard a Forge Lord ruin - A family of caralandrao , scavenging for magic or nesting in a ruin - Fire drakes or magma worms , basking in the lingering heat of a Forge Lord's furnace - The ghosts of those who died in some cataclysm or violent purge

528.title: Hazards
528.body: - Unstable construction: weakened by fire, rust, and age - Piles of coal or anthracite: flammable, dusty, unstable - Slag heaps, unstable and ready to become a rockslide - Pools or flows of magma - Volcanic activity - Primordial flame , bound by ancient runes, terrible if unleashed

529.title: Smoke, fire, and heat
529.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Dangers › Smoke, fire, and heat
529.body: Most Forge Lord ruins are cold and ashen, with little left to burn. But some are home to piles of unused fuel (coal, anthracite, etc.) or sources of magical fire. - Show a warning: fuel ready to burn, heat pouring off magma or an ancient furnace, air rippling with heat shimmer - Scald them with intense heat (1d4 damage) - Have someone/thing catch fire - Fill the area with choking smoke (debilities) - Block a path with smoke/fire (1d6 damage, ignores armor) - Catch them in a burst of flames (1d8 damage, messy , ignores armor) - Engulf someone, set them on fire (1d10 damage, messy , ignores armor)

530.title: Cinderwraith
530.body: Solitary , large , spirit , undead , fiery , terrifying HP 23; Armor 1 (amorphous) Damage blistering flames 1d10+1 ( hand , grabby , messy , ignores armor) Special qualities fireproof, dormant without fresh air to "breathe," tethered to its bones Instinct to burn the living - Manifest a leering pillar of fire and blackened hands (harmed only by water or silver) - Melt silver or bronze, weaken steel or iron, ignite wood, char flesh - Engulf a living being, char both flesh and soul (deal damage with advantage, inflict debilities, harm even fireproof foes) The Forge Lords burned with an inner fire, a fire that threatened to consume them even when they lived. And when they died badly—slowly, desperately, betrayed and full of helpless rage—that fire could linger on. First it would burn away the Forge Lord's flesh, and then its spirit, and then its soul. And then it would wait in the bones, like heat in banked coals. Wait for fresh air to give it new life, and fresh souls to burn for fuel.

531.title: Metal constructs
531.body: The Forge Lords could breathe life into metal through clever workmanship, spirit-craft, and magic. Many of these wonders remain, though only a few still function and those might function poorly. Pick or roll a purpose and structure, the source of its animation, and its attribute, size, and condition. Allow these elements to inform its physical form and construction.

532.title: 1d12 purpose
532.body: - 1-2 Defense/violence/murder - 3-4 Labor/service/convenience - 5-6 Beauty/decoration/aesthetics - 7-8 Novelty/practice/experimentation - 9-10 Companionship/diplomacy/business - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

533.title: 1d12 structure
533.body: - 1-3 Clockwork, pistons, springs, gears - 4-6 Mundane, metal object (pail, mirror, trumpet, sword, plow, oven, chariot, etc.) - 7-8 Statue, representational (person, beast, creature) - 9-10 Statue, abstract/stylized (shapes, patterns, etc.) - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine Forge Lord constructs are animated by more than biology (+4 HP). Most have Armor 3 (made of metal) and lack vital organs (another +3 HP, +1 Armor). Clockwork constructs do have vital organs (basically), and can have Armor as low as 1.

534.title: 1d12 animation
534.body: - 1-2 Red crystal - 3-4 A spirit, possessing it - 5-6 Fire/heat, harnessed by runes - 7-8 Energy stored in aetherium / makerglass - 9-10 A spark of its creator's power - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

535.title: 1d12 attribute
535.body: - 1 Nothing of note, just animated metal - 2 Beautiful/musical/sublime - 3 Terrifying/unnerving/imposing - 4 Strong/durable/powerful - 5 Perceptive/wise/cunning/devious - 6 Fiery/destructive/vicious - 7 Multipart/group/horde - 8 Disguised/camouflaged/innocuous - 9 Fast/agile/mobile/flies - 10 Attuned to energy/elements/magic - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice Forge Lord constructs are often fireproof. Most are blind, sensing vibrations or the heat emitted from objects and creatures.

536.title: 1d12 size
536.body: - 1 Cat-sized or smaller ( tiny ) - 2-3 Like a human child ( small ) - 4-7 Human-sized - 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. ( large ) - 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger ( huge )

537.title: 1d12 condition
537.body: - 1-2 Sluggish/low power/inert - 3-4 Rusted/corroded/damaged - 5-6 Trapped/pinned/buried/bound - 7-8 Behaving erratically - 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice - 11-12 Fully functional

538.title: Bronze colossus
538.body: Solitary , huge , construct , plodding , implacable HP 31; Armor 4 (metal, lacks organs) Damage trample d10+5 ( hand , close , area , forceful ) or crushing hands d10+5 ( close , reach , forceful , grabby ) or hurled object d10+5 ( far , area , forceful , reload ) Special qualities fireproof, too big to hurt with most weapons, vulnerable to lightning Instinct to defend its creator's honor - Pretend to be "just" another statue - Come to life, screeching as it moves - Issue a booming challenge in a forgotten tongue - Block their path It stands nearly 60 feet tall, a muscular Forge Lord cast in bronze. Similar statues loom about, each one grand and imposing. This statue, though, is shot through with blueish veins. And it feels like it's watching you. Because it is. The colossus guards the entrance to its master's domain. If someone approaches honorably, speaking in the Forge Lord's tongue, it politely tells them to come again later, for its master is away. Intruders, blowhards, and thieves are dealt with forcibly. Something interesting: Those blueish veins are aetherium , and likely what animates the statue. Which, alas, means there's no single weak-point. Maybe you could harm this thing by drawing the lightning from it. Something useful: Or you could, you know, just blast it with lightning and overload it.

539.title: Clockwork spider
539.body: Horde , small , construct , creepy HP 7; Armor 2 (metal), 0 vs. crushing Damage many little pincers d4 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities fireproof, blind, tremorsense, active only for short periods Instinct to get in the way - Skitter towards them, ominously - "Watch" intently, responding subtly to every move - Try to take things from their hands - Defend itself if attacked - Follow orders given in the Forge Lord's tongue, poorly They're not really shaped like spiders, but that's what they make you think of. Picture a metal basket, holding gears and springs and a glowing central core. Articulated legs extend out, each ending in a little 3-clawed pincer. They squeak as they move, their many joints needing oil. They were made to be little servants, extra sets of hands to help around a house or forge. Even now, they're just trying to help.

540.title: Iron hound
540.body: Horde , construct , undead , corrupted , tireless , terrifying HP 10; Armor 4 (metal, lacks organs) Damage bite d6+2 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities fireproof, heat-sensing Instinct to punish and torment - Follow the trail of its master's property - Fix its prey with a soul-chilling stare - Once it's bitten down: crush bone, shred muscle, tear apart (+ messy , 3 piercing) There was a Forge Lord who prized his hounds as much as he prized his treasure. When one would die, he'd capture its spirit in red crystal and set them as the eyes in a crudely-wrought effigy. If anyone would be so foolish as to steal from him, he would loose his iron hounds to punish not just the thief but all whose hands had sullied his property. That Forge Lord is long gone, but his iron hounds remain. They've learned to leave their master's treasures lying about in plain sight, and to give thieves a nice long head start. For the more hands touch the stolen goods, the more victims they can claim.

541.title: Singing sword
541.body: Solitary , small , construct , lonely , dutiful HP 19; Armor 4 (quick, made of metal) Damage cut, thrust, slash d10 w/advantage ( hand , messy , 2 piercing) Special qualities flies, fireproof, blind, heat-sense, bound to its hall Instinct to defend its hall, to ease its loneliness - Make a strange, ethereal song - Strike a warning blow, deft but nonlethal - Respond enthusiastically to music - Block their path with a razor's edge This naked blade needs no wielder. It dances about the air, making music as it moves: every bob a hum; every slice a whistle. It was set here long ago, as guardian to this crumbling hall. For ages it has kept its vigil. Yet, this is not some mindless thing waiting to cut down foes. It remembers and yearns for the days in which this hall was filled with light and laughter and—joy of joys—music! And so it waits. In the dark. Singing songs to itself that have not been heard by any living mortal ear. It waits, dutiful, and worries that it has misremembered the music it once knew. It waits. In the dark. Alone.

542.title: Draventao
542.body: Solitary , large , hoarder , paranoid , terrifying , fierce HP 22; Armor 4 (scales, steel plates) Damage bite d12+3 w/ advantage ( close , reach , forceful , messy , grabby , 1 piercing) Special qualities fireproof, bestial intellect, slumbers for years at a time Instinct to protect itself and its hoard, from threats real or imagined - Lure or drive intruders into a trap - Attack with sudden, terrible ferocity that makes even stout hearts falter - Suck in air, stoking its inner flames - Breath sticky fire, d10+3 damage ( near , area , grabby , messy , reload , ignores armor) Picture a long and sinuous beast with serpentine scales, curled upon a pile of treasure, covered in a layer of long-cold soot or ash. Bits of rusted steel protrude from its flesh. Smoke drifts lazily from its nostrils. Charred and jagged teeth poke out from its sneering lips. An eye snaps open. It fixes you with a glare like a red-hot coal. Hard to believe that this thing, this beast, was once a Maker. But it was. It was. A draventao is what remains of a Forge Lord. It survived the Time of Cataclysm , when its rivals moved against it, when its allies betrayed it, when even its loved ones came for it in the dark. It survived by Changing, by letting its inner fire burn until its heart was a blackened coal of wrath. It shed its once-beautiful form. It forced its servants to bolt living metal onto its now-twisted flesh and bones. Then it ate those servants. Now, mostly, it sleeps. For years at a time, it sleeps. It broods upon its treasure heap, loathe to leave its secret, trap-strewn lair. Only hunger draws it forth. Hunger, or vengeance to be had upon some brazen thief. Pick or roll its particular strength, its metal augmentation, and what it remembers.

543.title: 1d6 particular strength
543.body: - 1 A steel-rending bite (3 piercing) - 2 Great size ( huge , +4 HP, +2 damage) - 3 A glance that befuddles ( reach , area ) - 4 Breath that poisons as well as burns - 5 Blood that burns like lit naphtha, d10+1 damage ( hand , area ) - 6 A venomous stinger in its tail, d12+3 damage ( reach , forceful , 1 piercing, weakens or paralyzes)

544.title: 1d6 augmentation
544.body: - 1 Aetherium wings (Special quality +flies) - 2 Clockwork claw with sword-like talons, 1d10+5 damage ( hand , close , messy , forceful , 1 piercing) - 3 Overlapping plates, protecting its belly (Armor 5 instead of 4) - 4 Nasty spikes all over, d12+3 damage ( close , area , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) - 5 Implanted bellows, stoking its breath (+2 damage) - 6 Roll twice

545.title: 1d6 what it remembers
545.breadcrumb: Forge Lords › Draventao › 1d6 what it remembers
545.body: - 1 A word that makes water boil and metal glow red hot ( near , reload ) - 2 The bosom friend who betrayed it - 3 The child it could not save - 4 The lover who died to protect it - 5 The name by which others called it - 6 The lullaby its mother sang to it

546.title: The Frozen Wastes
546.body: North of the Whitefang Mountains lies a vast steppe, a windswept sea of shortgrass, sedge, and lichen. No trees grow here. The landscape is broken only by rocky outcrops, low moraines, and kettle lakes. It rarely snows and almost never rains, but hail is somewhat common. Caribou and mammoths graze the plateau, preyed on by wolves and saber-toothed cats and terrible things that crawl forth from the lakes in summer. Bands of mammoth herders roam in warmer months. But for the most part, it's an empty and forlorn place. Farther north, the plateau gets even colder, even windier, the steppe giving way to tundra. Eventually, a mountain-like wall of dark ice blocks the way, stretching across the horizon. The glacier is the final working of the Rime Lords . It seals a rift in the world, and keeps the worst of the Howling Curse at bay. Mammoth herders come here to quarry dark ice, slowly chipping the glacier away.

548.title: Always
548.body: - A landscape so wide and empty you can see the horizon curve - The constant wind, whispering and howling, tugging and lashing - Patterns forming in the windswept grass or snow, or the growth of flowers, like runes that fade just before you can read them - The swell of anger, the urge to inflict violence on your companions, as your temper and patience fray

549.title: Spring
549.body: - Yellow and brown grass, splashed with pale violet flowers - Fields of standing water, just inches deep, reflecting the empty sky - Squelch and crackle of half-frozen soil underfoot

550.title: Summer
550.body: - Days that last forever, the sun never truly setting - Mix of bright green grass, red lichen, white flowers - Swarms of insects, spawned from muddy soil - Birds and ice drakes pecking at the soil or flocking to the sky

551.title: Autumn
551.body: - The world turned red and orange and golden - Long shadows in the low, low sun - Sharp cold edge to the wind - The restless foraging of small animals

552.title: Winter
552.body: - Endless night, the sun barely rising at noon - Thin carpet of frost and snow, shrubs and dead grass poking out - The ceaseless, gnawing wind and cold - The sense that you've made a terrible mistake in coming here

553.title: Traversing the Wastes
553.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Traversing the Wastes
553.body: The Frozen Wastes are inhospitable in a way that southerners can barely comprehend. On any expedition into the wastes: - You need a knowledgeable guide, like a mammoth herder , who knows the ways of this place - You need lots of firewood or dried dung, for you'll find no fuel and even in the summer the "nights" can be freezing - The way will be grueling (from cold and mud and bugs); you risk exhausting yourselves/your supplies - You need to watch out for predators, like wolves or mchebra-gris ( kleztigrs , basically, see The Steplands) - You need to watch out for kettle lakes, easily mistaken for ice or shallow standing water - You risk succumbing to the Howling Curse , which taints much of the Wastes

554.title: Questions
554.body: - What precious flower grows only in the Frozen Wastes, blooming for a few days each summer? Why is it so valuable? - What do you find most surprising about this place? - What do you find yourself missing the most, out here in vast and empty space? - What is \ \ \ doing, that you find so damned annoying? - If you had to kill \ \ \ , how would you do it? - If it came down to it, which of your companions would you eat first?

555.title: Hooks
555.body: - A villain that the PCs want dead flees north, into the Frozen Wastes - Only one thing can kill the Crombil , and it lies at the bottom of a kettle lake - The dark ice glacier that contains the Howling Curse has begun to melt and recede

556.title: Terrain
556.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

557.title: 1d12 terrain
557.body: - 1 Kettle lake, small but dozens or hundreds of feet deep - 2-3 Stretch of shallow standing water/ice - 4-6 Wide open expanse, just grass and shrub for miles - 7-8 Large patch of mud/frozen mud - 9 Gentle slope/low ridge/small rise - 10 Low moraine, a small hill of broken stones and loose soil - 11 Rocky outcrop, a dozen feet tall at most - 12 Single, notable tree If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

558.title: Discoveries
558.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d6).

559.title: 1d6 discovery
559.body: - 1 Change of terrain (previous column) and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 Useful or valuable flora - 4 Dark ice - 5 A ruin or artifact of the Rime Lords - 6 An encounter (see below)

560.title: 1d6 encounter
560.body: - 1 A mammoth herder encampment, now deserted - 2 Small animals (fox, hares, rodents, birds, etc.) - 3 A herd of wild mammoths or caribou , wary, easily spooked/provoked - 4 A spirit of the wild or perhaps a tulpa , curious or indifferent - 5 Mammoth herders , seen in the distance, avoiding you - 6 Mammoth herders , approaching warily

561.title: Mammoth herders
561.body: Here's a typical band: Size hamlet ( 15-30 souls) Population +0

562.title: Prosperity -1
562.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Mammoth herders › Prosperity -1
562.body: - Trade with (pick 1 or 2)... - ... other mammoth herders - ... a farming valley - ... a fortress-monastery

563.title: Resources
563.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Mammoth herders › Resources
563.body: - Mammoths (wool, ivory, bone, dung) - Sleds/sledges of bone, hide, timber - Hunting (wild mammoths, caribou, hare, fish) - Gathering (timber, ivory, bone, flint, herbs, dark ice )

564.title: Defenses +0
564.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Mammoth herders › Defenses +0
564.body: - Mammoth-mounted warriors - Hounds and hunting drakes - Spears/javelins tipped with dark ice, flint, bone, or bronze ( crude ) About half of each band are youngsters, with the rest being able-bodied adults. Elders rarely allow themselves to survive long enough to become a burden to the others. A band typically keeps a herd of 10 to 20 adult mammoths , plus a few young. They butcher their own beasts only when desperate, keeping them instead for wool, transport, and labor. A band spends the warmer months on the Frozen Wastes, but spend the colder months sheltering in the caves, ruins, and forests of the Whitefang Mountains . They'll trade with valley farms or fortress-monasteries in spring, before heading out into the Wastes, or in autumn, before they encamp for the winter. Something interesting: Mammoth herders tend to be impeccably polite, with drawn-out, formal greetings and elaborate, almost artistic ways of doing everything from lacing up a parka to butchering a beast. Something useful: These rituals help identify those afflicted with the Howling Curse , who lack in patience and good grace. Alas, this means that mammoth herders interpret frustration or bad manners as a sign of being accursed.

565.title: Names
565.body: Tibetan-inspired: Bhokhi, Cheyez, Dangyi, Gamyang, Lhing, Jongash, Nayan, Ningo, Nyayelar, Paing, Ramang, Samanz, Sewas, Tawaro, Tsaru, Tsetse, Wasen, Yenphi

567.title: Commonly available
567.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Trade & Barter › Commonly available
567.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available among mammoth herders—if the PCs are on friendly terms with a band, they can acquire these items without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Food, iron, glass, and wool are usually in demand. They have little to no use for finery. goods value ---------------------------------- ------- Ivory a bit (comb, pendant, etc.) 0 ◇ a substantial piece 1 a mammoth tusk (immobile) 2 cartload of mammoth tusks 3 Dark ice ( magical , see Rime Lords) a small piece (magical) 1 ◇ a large chunk (magical) 2 with special characteristics +1 a functional tool or weapon +2 <sup \ </sup +1 if sold/traded on to anyone other than mammoth herders livestock value ----------------------------------------- ------- Hound , follower ( keen-nosed , fierce , tireless ); HP 6; Damage d6 ( hand , grabby ); Instinct to chase; Cost a full belly 1 Hunting drake , follower ( tiny , sharp-eyed , swift-winged ); HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ); Instinct to test its limits; Cost hours/days of training 1 Mammoth ( huge , hardy , keen-nosed ); HP 15; Damage d6+5 ( reach , forceful , or hand , area , forceful ); Instinct to suffer no insult or threat; sickly in warmer climes 3

568.title: Special items
568.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Trade & Barter › Special items
568.body: The following might be acquired among mammoth herders, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. This is in addition to the special items on the Moves & Gear handout. When PCs Trade & Barter among a band of mammoth herders , they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage because it's a hamlet . On a 6-, they don't mark XP—this band simply doesn't have or want what the PCs are after/selling. goods value ----------------------------------- ------- Pottery 0 Bronze or iron items, of any sort 1

569.title: Services and favors
569.breadcrumb: The Frozen Wastes › Trade & Barter › Services and favors
569.body: PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by a band, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price. service/favor value -------------------------------------------- ------- A few nights of hospitality at their camp 0 Labor for a day or two 1 Accompanying the PCs to a place… they were heading towards anyhow 0 a few days out of their way 1 they have no business going 2 that they know to be cursed 3

571.title: Hazards
571.body: - Brutal cold and ceaseless wind, leading to hypothermia & frostbite - Hail storms—annoying, pelting (d4 damage), smashing (d6 damage) - Mud—sucking, cold, exhausting - Slippery footing - Still, reflective water hiding a sudden, plunging drop into a kettle - In summer: swarms of midges/ mosquitos—annoying, distracting, painful, diseased - Getting lost or misjudging time in the vast featureless terrain - Whispers and visions , pushing people towards bloodshed, cruelty, and savagery - Places tainted by the Howling Curse

572.title: Monsters
572.body: - Ice drakes , especially in warmer months - Kleztigrs , called "mchebragris" in the local tongue - Wolves - A frythanc , nesting in the Whitefangs and preying on caribou or even mammoths - Wraiths of those who died of starvation and want, or corrupted by the Howling Curse - Spirits of the wild - Tulpas , free-roaming or bound to something nearby - Kyakaara , perhaps what remains of a corrupted band of mammoth herders

573.title: Caribou
573.body: Horde , hardy HP 7; Armor 0 Damage antlers/hoofs d6 ( hand , close ) Instinct to stampede - Run back to the herd - Huddle close at signs of danger - Stampede, d6+4 damage ( hand , area , messy , forceful , 1 piercing)

574.title: Mammoth
574.body: Horde , huge , hardy HP 15; Armor 1 (fur/hide) Damage tusks d6+5 ( reach , forceful ) or trample d6+5 ( hand , area , forceful ) Instinct to suffer no insult or threat - Trumpet in warning - Toss someone or something aside - Charge, often abruptly

575.title: Mammoth herder
575.body: Horde , hardy , cautious , organized , clever HP 7; Armor 1 (thick hides & furs) Damage long spears d6 ( reach , crude ) or javelins with atlatls d6 ( far , reload , crude ) Instinct to put their people and their herd first - Greet someone from a distance, politely and formally - Remain calm as they prepare for violence - Work or fight in tandem with their mammoth(s), dogs, and hunting drakes

576.title: Gods and Religion
576.body: The village of Stonetop maintains a pavilion of the gods, an open-air structure with shrines to four main gods: - Aratis , the Lawkeeper Aratis, the Lawkeeper - Danu , the Earth Mother Danu, the Earth Mother - Helior , the Daybringer Helior, the Daybringer - Tor , the Rain-maker Tor, the Rain-maker, the Thunderhead, Slayer-of-Beasts Tor is the most popular god in Stonetop, and associated with the Stone itself, but all four gods receive their proper respect. Depending on the PCs in your game, some of the gods may be more important than others. Aratis, for example, will feature more prominently in a game with a Judge PC than a game without one. Other communities might worship some or all of these gods, perhaps by different names, perhaps with different aspects and traditions. Or they might have different gods entirely, gods unknown or unworshipped in Stonetop.

577.title: Questions
577.body: - Who tends the pavilion of the gods? - Are Stonetop's gods part of a coherent pantheon? Or a mix of gods from different cultures? - What little gods are revered by your family/in Stonetop/where you grew up? - What other gods are known in Stonetop? Do any have shrines in the pavilion? - Which god, if any, do you consider your patron?

578.title: Hooks
578.body: - Someone vandalizes one/all of the shrines in the pavilion. - The devotee of a foreign god arrives in Stonetop, preaching strange rites. - A saint or prophet of a new or forgotten god arises in Marshedge or Gordin's Delve , upsetting the balance of powers there. - Someone offends their household god(s); misfortune ensues.

579.title: True nature
579.body: "God" is not a distinct category of being. If a spirit or entity receives offerings and prayers, and responds in kind, then it's a god. The origin of any given god is for you and your table to decide. A god might be… - … one of the great spirits , there at the dawn of time, or birthed spontaneously from the fabric of existence, or made by the First Creators as a steward of the world; - … a Thing Below , or an emanation of such; - … a spirit of the wild , transformed through worship; - … the child of other gods, or of a god and a mortal; - … a mortal, ascended/transcended/reborn; - … a mortal, revered in death; - … a spirit born from humanity's beliefs; - … one of the above, but also part of a duality/triumvirate; or - … one of the above, but died and reborn.

580.title: Divine intervention
580.body: Gods shape the world in subtle ways: through synchronicity, dreams, and omens, or by acting in the spirit world. Even Tor, who sometimes walks the world, limits himself to testing mortals and bestowing gifts and curses. Every so often, though, a god will touch a mortal and imbue them with divine power. They then send that mortal forth as their instrument in the world. Such mortals become prophets or saints, heroes or villains, and help shape the world for generations to come. If you have the Blessed, the Judge, the Lightbearer, or the Storm-marked Heavy among your PCs, they might well be one of these god-touched mortals. Should the PCs wish a god to intervene on their behalf, they can petition them (see next page). Should the PCs offend a god, or oppose one, or otherwise draw its ire, write that god up as a threat (a magical entity ).

581.title: Religion
581.body: For most people in the World's End, religion is not about faith or morality. It's a practical matter. The gods are , and they influence the world. You perform rites and make offerings in order to gain their favor and avert their wrath.
