Even priests and the like aren't necessarily devoted to the gods they serve; they just know what the gods expect and how to read their signs. There are, however, some mortals who feel a profound connection to their god(s). Such devotees give offerings gladly. They aspire to emulate their god(s), to heed their teachings and spread their glory. Devotees might form fellowships with the like-minded, which in time can become institutions: formal priesthoods, mystery cults, temples, and the like. Such institutions can be powerful forces in the world. They might preserve knowledge, legitimize leaders, protect innocents, or otherwise stabilize societies. But they might also grow corrupt, fat on power and privilege, run by cynics rather than true devotees.

582.title: Petitioning the gods
582.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Religion › Petitioning the gods
582.body: Religion is an expected part of daily life. You help in the fields, you clean your house, and you give the gods their due. There's no extra benefit for performing the usual rites or offerings. But if you fail to do them, you invite disapproval from your neighbors and misfortune from the gods. PCs can seek the favor of the gods in order to Bolster . This involves time and effort, rites and offerings above and beyond what's typical and expected. Be sure to ask the PC for details! What god(s) do they petition? What sort of aid do they seek? What offerings do they make? Consider, too, the consequences. What other work goes undone? How do NPCs react? Etc. Should a PC seek a specific boon from the gods—a curse lifted, an injury healed, a curse laid upon a foe, an answer or an insight, etc.—then Make a Plan! Some example requirements include: - You must learn the proper rites/offerings - You must obtain a specific sacred object - You'll need the help of a particular spirit - You'll need to wait for an auspicious date/time - You must travel to a particular sacred site - The rites will take hours/day/weeks - You must make an appropriate sacrifice (Value 2 at least) - You risk angering the god(s) Choose requirements based on the PC's request, the god(s) they petition and how they are typically worshipped, the PC's relationship to the god(s), and how active the gods are in your particular game.

583.title: Little gods
583.body: These are the gods of households, of specific places or geographic features, of particular chores or crafts or trades. Many are just elemental spirits worshipped at a sacred site . But others are spirits closely tied to humanity. Little gods mostly just do their thing, quietly watching over their people or places, keeping things running or causing trouble, as is their wont. A little god can: - Reveal what it knows, from its own perspective, to those who can speak to it - Calm/annoy/frighten animals or children - Move/hide/reveal an unattended item - Express displeasure through accidents, misfortunes, neglect - Fend off other spirits Little gods manifest only to small children or at the beck and call of someone like a Blessed or spirit-talker . When they do manifest, treat them more like NPCs than monsters. Here are some example little gods. Feel free to modify them, expand on them, and ask the PCs questions about them.

584.title: Ancestral spirits
584.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › Ancestral spirits
584.body: Group , spirit , protective , conservative Instinct to protect their family's honor. Manifests in dreams, as drafty whispers. Expects an altar, prayers, incense/libations. Notes Like grandparents who don't quite get you.

585.title: Garden spirits
585.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › Garden spirits
585.body: Horde , spirit , coy , needy Instinct (roll 1d4) 1 = to languish; 2 = to run rampant; 3 = to show off; 4 = to give back. Manifests as flower-folk and little creatures. Expects first fruits, water, blood. Notes Many voices talking over each other.

586.title: God of the Cistern
586.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › God of the Cistern
586.body: Solitary , spirit , gloomy , patient Instinct to keep secrets. Manifests as an echo in the dark, as a misty figure. Expects water, snow, coins, wishes. Notes Desperately wants to know how various tales end(ed).

587.title: God of weaving
587.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › God of weaving
587.body: Solitary , spirit , exacting , insightful Instinct to reward diligence, to punish perfectionism. Manifests as a spider, as idle thoughts. Expects one perfect bit of fabric to dwell in, at least one minor flaw in everything else. Notes A trickster (tangles yarn, snaps your warp), sometimes a wise councilor.

588.title: God of whisky
588.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › God of whisky
588.body: Solitary , spirit , secretive , grandiose Instinct to take its sweet time. Manifests as a vaporous figure. Expects the head & tail of each batch. Notes Passionate about flavors, smells.

589.title: Hearth spirit
589.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › Hearth spirit
589.body: Solitary , spirit , friendly , nurturing Instinct to belong. Manifests as pops & whispers, as an impish little person. Expects warm greetings, friendly banter, little offerings cast into the flames. Notes Natters on quietly even if no one's present.

590.title: Household spirits
590.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › Household spirits
590.body: Group , spirit , helpful , mischievous Instinct to easily take offense. Manifests as gnomish little folk. Expects bowls of goat's milk, bits of bread. Notes Complain loudly to each other, grumble about leaving if things don't improve.

591.title: Threshold spirit
591.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Little gods › Threshold spirit
591.body: Solitary , spirit , protective , vigilant Instinct to dislike outsiders. Manifests as a grumpy little person. Expects sprinkles of salt, poured-out whisky, reverent touches. Notes Crosses arms, puffs up, harrumphs. See also the following elemental spirits : - Beast wardens (for livestock) - Fertility spirits - Meadow/prairie/grass spirits (for the fields) - Rock/stone spirits (for the Stone or the Ringwall) - Stream/creek spirit (for the Stream).

592.title: Other gods
592.body: To create a new god, choose or roll for 1 or 2 themes, who they are relevant to, their standing, and up to 3 observances.

593.title: 1d12 theme
593.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Other gods › 1d12 theme
593.body: - 1 Thresholds/change/trickery/chaos - 2 Competition/violence/murder/warfare - 3 Moon/stars/night/sleep/dreams/revelation - 4 Skill/crafts/learning/writing/wisdom - 5 Travel/trade/navigation/enterprise - 6 Luck/prosperity/fortune/fate - 7 Hearth/home/marriage/family/community/civilization - 8 Fertility/birth/vigor/sickness/death - 9 Agriculture/hunting/seasons/weather - 10 Music/art/beauty/passion/revelry - 11 Bravery/justice/oaths/protection - 12 Fire/rebirth/hope/light/sun

594.title: 1d12 relevant to
594.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Other gods › 1d12 relevant to
594.body: - 1-2 A specific household/family - 3-4 A particular village/band/clan - 5-6 A minority within a larger people (e.g. Manmarcher smiths) - 7-8 A whole people (e.g. Manmarchers) - 9-10 A minority across multiple peoples (e.g. thieves everywhere) - 11-12 Many different peoples A deity who is relevant to multiple peoples might have different standing and observances among each.

595.title: 1d12 standing
595.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Other gods › 1d12 standing
595.body: - 1-2 Obscure, forgotten, misunderstood - 3-5 Avoided, propitiated, appeased - 6 Revered in secret - 7-9 Invoked only when needed - 10-11 Given due respect and honor - 12 Adored, praised, heaped with gratitude

596.title: 1d12 observance
596.breadcrumb: Gods and Religion › Other gods › 1d12 observance
596.body: - 1 Charity/the care of \ \ / the cultivation of \ \ - 2 Dancing/intoxication/ecstatic practice - 3 Divination/augury/prophecy - 4 Elaborate prayers/ceremonies/gatherings - 5 Fashioning of icons/symbols/monuments - 6 Fasting/ascetic denial/extreme endurance - 7 Libations/offerings of material wealth (burnt, buried, etc.) - 8 Pilgrimage/hermitage - 9 Sacrifice (animal/blood/human) - 10 Taboo/proscribed behaviors - 11-12 Roll 1d10 again, twice, but one of them is done in secret From there, envision the god's specific sphere of influence, how it is depicted, its rites, its myths, and its true nature . To name your god, use one of these methods: - Pick a word associated with the god, translate it into an appropriate language, then change it a little ("threshold" "trothwy" in Welsh "Troth") - Pick a deity from actual mythology or popular culture, and change a letter or two ("Thor" "Tor"). Then give them one or more titles, kennings, or honorifics to reflect their portfolio. Minor gods might have only a title; "She Who Threads the Needle" might go by no other name than that. If this god will cause ongoing trouble for the PCs or the village, write it up as a threat (a magical entity ). The PCs are approaching Three Coven Lake , and I've decided that a group of Hillfolk called The White Hand patrol the area. Their god tells them to keep outsiders away and to keep the horrors of the lake at bay. I consider having them worship Helior, but decide to make up a new god instead. I start by picking a theme of "bravery/justice/oaths/protection," but also roll a second theme for flavor. I get a 12, focusing on "fire/rebirth". Then I roll a 6 for relevance ("minority within a larger people"), an 8 for standing ('invoked only when needed"), and 8 for observances ("pilgrimage/hermitage"). I could use a bit more, so I roll another observance: 5 ("fashioning of monuments"). I look at the possible true natures, ponder a bit, and settle on this: The White Hand worship Gyndorn of the Cleansing Flame, a hero of the Hillfolk who led an uprising against the Barrow Builders Barrow Builders , and whose name the Hillfolk invoke when facing unholy evil. Gyndorn charged her followers with eternal vigilance. She calls warriors and spirit-talkers from all tribes to pass through a sacred flame and be reborn as members of the White Hand. Members of the sect follow a circuit around the lakes that Gyndorn took herself, marking certain landmarks with white handprints each time they pass. And each band in the sect carries a consecrated flame with them as they go.

597.title: The Golden Oak
597.body: The Golden Oak On a clear day, sharp-eyed folk in Stonetop can spot the Golden Oak, gleaming at the horizon and dwarfing the trees around it. According to the tales of the Forest Folk , a hero buried at the roots of the Oak can find their way back from beyond the Last Door . But no one from Stonetop has ever made it there and back.

598.title: Questions
598.body: - When was Stonetop's last expedition to the Golden Oak? Who were they trying to save? - What other tales have you heard about the Oak? How much of them do you believe? - Who would have to die for you to risk a journey to the Golden Oak?

599.title: Getting there
599.body: Something interesting: The Golden Oak doesn't quite exist in this world, at least not anymore. It can only be reached through Fae pathways, at great risk and personal cost. When you travel towards the Golden Oak in the mundane world , you never reach it. Even if you were to fly above the trees, it would remain always out of reach, its bearing shifting. Something useful: To reach the Golden Oak, you must… - learn to walk the Fae paths (see way stones ); - follow the Fae paths to a specific set of waystones, those closest to the Oak; - find a secret crossing and face its guardian, set there by the Lady of Crows herself Death and the Undying; and - pay the Lady's due to make the crossing. When you pay the Lady's due , choose 1 as a group: - Present a marker of the Pale Hunter 's favor The Pale Hunter, honestly earned - Each of you must make a sacrifice. Cross off a move you possess; it is gone and you cannot regain it. (Followers cross off a tag of the GM's choice instead.) - One of your number passes willingly through the Last Door, their body gone, never to be seen again. When you make the crossing , you glimpse the Last Door and what lies beyond. Tell us what you see.

600.title: Setting the scene
600.breadcrumb: The Golden Oak › Setting the scene
600.body: The Golden Oak itself is enormous, so big as to defy any sense of scale. The bark is pale and luminous, and the glittering canopy is distant as the sky. - What detail do you notice that drives home the fact that everything here is huge and massive? - What here tells you this is a place of primal, unchecked vitality? - What do you notice about yourself, that tells you that something is making you more angry, savage, and violent?

601.title: The savage curse
601.breadcrumb: The Golden Oak › The savage curse
601.body: This was once a hub of the Green Lords ' civilization Names. When the Fae rebelled and turned on their masters, they claimed it for themselves. The surviving Lords laid a curse of primal savagery on the place. The rebel Fae abandoned it and the Lady of Crows sealed it away. When you are under the vast canopy of the Golden Oak … - … and you Persuade using anything but violence or threats , the best you can get is a 7-9. - … and you give in to the savagery in your heart , regain 2d8 HP and become larger, more muscular, more bestial. Gain advantage when you roll STR and disadvantage when you roll WIS until you leave this place. - … and you fight with no mercy, hesitation, or remorse , roll damage with advantage. - … and you seek to make something, heal someone, or repair something , roll +WIS: on a 10+ , you can work as long as you keep focused on your task; on a 7-9 , you make some progress, but are easily distracted, frustrated, or enraged.

602.title: Dangers
602.body: Pick 1, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

603.title: 1d6 danger
603.body: - 1 Enormous vermin—dog-sized, man-sized, horse-sized, or worse—each with some uncanny trait or mutation - 2 Gargantuan beasts, crashing about you, disinterested and unconcerned - 3 Specters of betrayed Green Lords, like roving storms of hate - 4 Hollow Fae , trapped here for ages, mad with soul-hunger - 5 Pockets of dangerous old magic, ready to ensnare, rend, or mutate - 6 An overwhelming urge to fight, to hurt, to kill. If they act on the impulse, they mark XP. If they resist, they are Defying Danger (probably with WIS).

604.title: Discoveries
604.body: Pick 1, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

605.title: 1d6 discovery
605.body: - 1 A small pocket of safety: a cave, an old ruin, a hollow in a massive tree - 2 The remains of a Green Lord or a rebel warrior, strangely well preserved - 3 A dead beast: enormous, half-eaten, teeming with vermin - 4 Useful or valuable flora , growing in preposterous proportions - 5 A mad hermit, trapped here and lost in time (perhaps from a previous expedition?) - 6 An artifact of... (roll d12) - 1-7 The Green Lords - 8-11 The Fae - 12 The Things Below

606.title: Treasures of the Golden Oak
606.breadcrumb: The Golden Oak › Treasures of the Golden Oak
606.body: - A living ◇ leaf from the Golden Oak can be made into a Goldleaf Cloak . - With proper tools and knowledge, the bark can be harvested to craft weapons or armor (2 armor, warm , cumbersome ) that is hard as iron and bane to undead or things from beyond the Last Door. - A ◇◇ golden acorn, harvested in autumn, can be planted elsewhere to grow a Golden Sapling (see sidebar).

607.title: Buried at the roots
607.breadcrumb: The Golden Oak › Buried at the roots
607.body: When you bury a corpse at the roots of the Golden Oak , close to the trunk, roll and add… - … +1 if the corpse has not yet started to rot; - … +1 if they died bravely in their prime; and - … +1 if their name is still well-known. On a 10+ , they return from beyond the Last Door when the sun next rises, whole and healthy though likely shaken; on a 7-9 , they do not yet rise, but maybe if you give it another night? On a 6- , they return, but something else comes with them, something dangerous and dark, something now tied to them in a deep and primal way.

608.title: . steading improvement .
608.breadcrumb: The Golden Oak › Buried at the roots › . steading improvement .

609.title: GOLDEN SAPLING
609.body: Requires all of the following, in order: - Retrieve an ◇◇ acorn from the boughs of the Golden Oak in late autumn - Plant the acorn in the fields near Stonetop, watering it with the lifeblood of a great virile beast, like an aurochs bull - Nurture and protect the sapling through 4 years of growth When you mark all the requirements , add "Golden Sapling" to the Resources list and automatically mark the Greater Harvest improvement. Henceforth, when the steading generates Surplus, even just 1, it generates +1 Surplus.

611.title: Prosperity +0
611.body: - Trade with... - ... Marshedge (wheat, flax, rope, textiles, herbs, glass) - ... Stonetop (barley, potatoes, furs, whisky)

612.title: Resources
612.body: - Mining (iron, copper, lead, tin, silver) - Trades (smithing, tinkering) - Timber - Goats - Aqueduct

613.title: Defenses -1
613.body: - Maker-ramparts - Unassailable position For centuries, it was just a pile of Forge Lord ruins Forge Lords at the end of the West Road , up in the Huffel Peaks . A few generations back, ambitious folk started hauling ore out of the Delves. Foundry got built, merchants showed up, and coin flowed in—along with a constant trickle of folks seeking opportunity, or with nowhere else to go. There's no town council, not even a militia to speak of. And unlike Stonetop, there ain't much sense of community. Some folks got friends, and allies, and reputations to lean on. But when it comes right down to it, sure seems like everyone's only looking out for themselves.

614.title: Questions
614.body: - When was the last round of violence between the Delvers and the Hillfolk? What did it cost you, personally? - Which of you have been here before? When? Why? - Who up here should you never, ever cross? - Who's the merchant who trades with Stonetop the most? - Who left Stonetop to come up here? What's the last you've heard of them? - What are they said to eat up here, besides goats and imported grain? - According to the stories you've heard, who the hell was Gordin?

615.title: Places
615.body: - The slopes near town , all sludge and mud and tree stumps, mining camps and goat herds - Sorrow's Gate , where the West Road ends, a sprawl of tents, no permanent structures on the pave stones - The Arms , two ridges that cradle the town, Maker-ruins clinging to their sides - Foundry , a brick slab squatted at the town's foot, belching smoke and slurry - The ancient aqueduct , still in use, feeding Foundry and turning its waterwheel - The Terraces , seven of them, crowded with shops, stables, tenements, alleys, and people. Make-do construction lashed to humbling ruins - The Swap , an open-air market for labor, second-hand goods, questionable food or pleasures, and the odd Delve-finds - A half-dozen walled-off compounds , claiming the best spots, built into Maker-ruins - The three Delves , gaping tunnels into the dark, miners coming and going

616.title: People
616.body: No one is from here originally. Even folks born here, their parents or grandparents came from somewhere else. For any NPC, pick or roll where they're from and what brought them. Pick a name based on their origins, maybe with an epithet like "the Knife," "Gaptooth," "Limper," "Boils," "the Yellow," or "Go-Between."

617.title: 1d12 where they're from
617.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › People › 1d12 where they're from
617.body: - 1-2 Lygos, or other points south - 3-5 Marshedge - 6-7 The Manmarch - 8 Stonetop - 9 The Steplands (Hillfolk) - 10 Barrier Pass (or beyond) - 11-12 Grew up here; reroll for parents

618.title: 1d12 what brought them
618.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › People › 1d12 what brought them
618.body: - 1-3 Seeking their fortune - 4-5 To join family/friends - 6-9 A bad turn, nowhere else to go - 10 On the run - 11 Happenstance, fate, dumb luck - 12 Dragged here against their will The Bosses each run big parts of the local economy; everyone in town relies on what they control. Their positions are far from stable, and when one topples, there's turmoil. Each is attended by 10-15 family, servants, and retainers. - Mutra the Teeth (mines, timber) - Odo Thriceborn (mines, housing) - Honest Draigh (trade with Marshedge, food) - Jahalim of the Keys (Foundry) - Smiling Ffransis (water, booze) Merchants bring goods and raw material in and out of Gordin's Delve. Most are backed by one of the Bosses, but one or two manage the careful dance of independence. ( 10 operations, or 25 souls total) The Keeps keep the town running, such that it does. They run the trades, the shops, the inns, the boarding houses, the hostelries, the dens of vice. They don't mine the Delves, they mine the miners. And the Bosses mine them . ( 50 souls, including family and apprentices) Miners make up the bulk of the town. Small crews work what claims they can, usually veins far down the Delves or dotting the slopes. They'll be gone for days on end. The Bosses' crews are bigger, working the best veins on day/night shifts. It's hard work, that barely pays enough to get by. ( 250 souls) Prospectors work alone or in small teams, seeking new claims and selling them to mining crews. They also sell Maker artifacts to the Bosses when they can, or else at the Swap for whatever they can get. Prospecting is terribly dangerous, but a way to make good coin fast. ( 5-10 souls at any given time) Smelters work at Foundry, processing raw ore into iron, copper, silver, lead, and tin. There's a handful of skilled smiths, too, but mostly it's hard, hot, dangerous work. Pay's decent, though, and you don't have to go down the Delves. ( 50 souls, working in shifts) Loggers ply the mountainsides for timber and haul it back to town, for construction or to fuel hearths and Foundry's furnace. The nearby slopes were stripped clean long ago, so nowadays loggers range farther out into the mountains. Hard, dangerous work that takes folks away from town for weeks at a time. ( 50 souls) The desperate can't find work or can't keep it, due to injury, infirmity, or temperament. They take what gigs they can, or they scavenge, or they die slowly out near Sorrow's Gate. Some turn to robbery and theft, but unless they're very good, they're beaten or killed by victims, mobs, or other cutthroats. And if you are very good at thuggery? Well, the Bosses probably have work for you. ( 100-200 souls, always in flux) Beyond that , another 100-150 souls do odd jobs: goat herding, butchering, hauling water, cleaning and cooking, working for the Keeps or the Bosses as staff or muscle or entertainment or a combination of all three. They often live with their employers, or their stock. There are very few full-time homemakers in Gordin's Delve; almost everybody works or hustles a little. Even kids are put to work as soon as possible. It's not a nice place, not at all.

620.title: Commonly available
620.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › Trade & Barter › Commonly available
620.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in Gordin's Delve—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky, furs, barley, potatoes) are always in demand. weapons & armor value --- --- Common weapons (iron or bronze) 0 Common weapons (steel, 1 piercing) 1 Weapons of war (iron or bronze) 1 Weapons of war (steel, 1 piercing) 2 ◇◇ Hauberk/cuirass/scale (2 armor, warm , clumsy ) 2 goods value ------------------------------------------------------------------- ----- ◇ Lantern (○○○○○ hours , reach , area ) 0 Small metal tool (chisel, file, shears, etc.) 0 ◇ Block & tackle 0 ◇ Metal tools (drill, prybar, saw, etc.) 0 ◇ Mirror, hand-held, polished bronze 1 Wheelbarrow 1 Cart (requires donkey/mule/horse) 2 Spare parts for cart/wagon (wheels, axles, struts, ○○○ uses, immobile ) 2 Cartload of timber ( immobile ) 2 Cartload of copper/lead/tin/iron 2 coin value --- --- ◇ Purse of coppers ( 10 handfuls) 0 Handful of silvers 1 ◇ Purse of silvers ( 10 handfuls) 2 food & lodging value --- --- Camping near Sorrow's Gate ( cold , risky ) free Food & drink for a small party 0 Provisions (2-12 uses) 0 Lodgings, decent (for a small party up to a week, food included) 1 Night of fine living (for one) 1

621.title: Special items
621.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › Trade & Barter › Special items
621.body: The following might be acquired in Gordin's Delve, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. When PCs Trade & Barter in Gordin's Delve, they use the local Prosperity (+0 by default) with advantage because it's a town . On a 6-, they don't mark XP; they must talk to one of the Bosses if they want to acquire/sell it. goods value --- --- A lock and key 1 Silver jewelry 1 A small silver mirror 2 An object of black iron Primordial Powers +2 An object of orichalcum (see Forge Lords) +2 food & lodging value --- --- Humble, cramped apartment to call your own 2 Modest house or shop 3

622.title: .   trade opportunities   .
622.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › Trade & Barter › .   trade opportunities   .
622.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Gordin's Delve might offer the following: - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus. - A cartload of timber or metal (Value 2), in exchange for help with a problem. - A cartload of timber (Value 2) every season for a year, in exchange for 1 Surplus now - A tradesperson (engineer, foreman, smith, etc.) willing to move to Stonetop for a price - An arcanum of the Stone Lords Stone Lords, Forge Lords Forge Lords, or Things Below The Things Below.

624.title: ASK AROUND
624.body: When you spend a purse of coppers and a few hours asking around town , say what you hope to learn and roll +CHA: on a 10+ , you get a clear and useful answer; on a 7-9 , you get an answer but pick 1: - It's incomplete, contradictory, or unclear, but the GM will say how you could learn more - It'll cost you another purse of coppers - You've drawn unwanted attention or gotten yourself embroiled in a situation of the GM's choosing

625.title: CAROUSE
625.body: When you spend a handful of silvers having a good time , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - You spread a rumor or tale far and wide - You make a loyal new friend - You learn something useful or juicy - You avoid getting swindled, tricked, or entangled in someone else's trouble

626.title: RECRUIT
626.body: When you put out word that you're hiring , say who you're looking for, what the job is, and what you're offering (the GM will veto if you're being unreasonable). Then roll and add… - … +1 if your offer is generous; - … +1 if the work conditions aren't terrible; - … +1 if you've got a solid reputation hereabouts. On a 10+ , you find someone, but pick 1; on a 7-9 , you find someone but pick 2. - They lack some of the skills you wanted - They suffer from some impairment or vice (GM's choice) - You draw unwanted attention in the process - They have debts or obligations that you need to clear up first services value --------------------------------------------------------------------- ----- Someone to keep an eye on things for a week 0 A bit of crude, one-sided violence 0 A guide into the slopes/Delves, for a day 0 A crew of miners/woodcutters, for a week 1 A prospector, to take you to a secret or dangerous part of the Delves 1 A trustworthy live-in servant, for a season 1 A bodyguard/muscle, for a few days 1 A targeted killing, discrete 1 An engineer/foreman, for a big project 2 An assassination, someone important 2

627.title: Secrets
627.body: - The Red Door is a well-known feature down in the First Delve , a 20-foot tall slab of makerglass , inlaid with hundreds of patterned tiles. Some are missing, allowing the tiles to be slid around. When the tiles are in the exact right spots, the Red Door will open. Hints to the correct arrangement are hidden in old mosaics throughout Gordin's Delve. - An established shopkeep (like a chandler, a publican, an innkeeper, maybe even a barber?) has a habit of killing folks who won't be missed. They're otherwise a pillar of the community. - Everyone knows there are grates in the terraces , carrying water through chutes and culverts to pour out below Sorrow's Gate. But there's more, a series of cramped tunnels and chutes that connect to some of the old ruins and even the Delves. The sewers are gross, infested with rats or worse, and just big enough to get lost in. - A cult of locals (some influential) have taken to tossing sacrifices into the underground lake at the bottom of the Second Delve : coin, flesh, living victims. They've received blessings from Daagon as a result. - Every few years, miners in the Third Delve will unearth a "door" made of orichalcum . Rarely, they rebury it. Usually, they pry it open, to find nothing but more rock beyond. A powerful earthquake always follows a few days after one of these doors is opened, but few have made the connection.

628.title: Hooks
628.body: - The PCs' favorite merchant is in trouble with one or more Bosses - An ambitious Keep is angling to become a new Boss - Miners, smelters, and loggers seek to organize and throw off the Bosses' yoke - A small mining crew has gone missing; a worried loved one asks for help - One of the Bosses was just killed by an underling, who's desperately trying to assume their position - Ustrina offer gold and silver for something unsavory (fresh blood, corpses, slaves, etc.) - Logging crews have gone missing, and the folks who went to check on them never came back. Are Hillfolk raiders to blame? Bandits? Or something worse?

630.title: Living conditions
630.body: - Hunger, if you can't afford food - Exposure to harsh mountain weather , if you can't afford shelter - Sickness and disease, from close quarters and poor hygiene - Mudslides , especially on the slopes - Workplace accidents (or "accidents") - General apathy from those who could help

631.title: Folks from elsewhere
631.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › Dangers › Folks from elsewhere
631.body: Such as: - Stonetop - Marshedge - The Manmarch , 350\) - Lygos or other points south - Barrier Pass or beyond - Hillfolk , cast out by their people - Ustrina , here to trade (probably)

632.title: Alley cutthroats
632.body: Group , stealthy , cautious , organized HP 6; Armor 0 Damage wicked knife d8 ( hand ) Instinct to prey on the unwary - Pick out a likely mark - Jump someone - Fight dirty - Leave no witnesses

633.title: Desperate souls
633.body: Group , craven , meek , opportunistic HP 6; Armor 0 Damage brick, makeshift club, rusty knife d6 ( hand , crude ) Instinct to do what they must - Do something unsavory, if there's food or coin in it - Tell someone what they think they want to hear - Demonstrate their humanity in a small—or sometimes enormous—way

634.title: Dawa Eyegouger
634.body: Solitary , brutal , fearless , drunkard HP 16; Armor 0 Damage knife or fingers d8 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing, advantage) Instinct to get this over with - Ignore pain, doubt - Explode into sudden violence - Fight dirty, mean, without remorse A goatherder from north of Barrier Pass, with orange irises and too-long fingers (marks of the Howling Curse ). She turned up in Gordin's Delve years back, and one of the Bosses put her to work as an enforcer. They keep her sauced and happy, until there's work to be done. Then they dry her out, point out the target, and stand back.

635.title: Laborers
635.body: Horde , hardy HP 7; Armor 0 Damage fists, boots d4 ( hand ) or pickax d6 ( close , messy , 1 piercing) Instinct to cling to what they've got - Make a show of toughness, solidarity - Dogpile someone ( grabby , forceful ) - Break and run when things go sideways

636.title: Shoddy construction
636.body: Gordin's Delve is a weird mix of piled stone and crude carpentry, lashed onto Maker-ruins that have stood for hundreds of years. It's all very slapdash and precarious. - Things creak, sway, and teeter ominously - A wall/support/floor gives way, putting someone in danger - Someone falls from a height/is buried under rubble - Something catches fire - A structure collapses, weakening others - A home/shop is destroyed, someone's savings/livelihood/family is lost

637.title: The Delves
637.body: The Arms feature three archways, each over 30 feet tall. Each arch leads to a tunnel of smooth stone that plunges into the mountain, eventually connecting to the Labyrinth . Side passages, chambers, and complexes branch off the main tunnels—some ancient, some excavated in the last 50 years. The First Delve opens where the two Arms meet. It stretches half a mile from the surface, then becomes a series of switchback tunnels, one after the other, down and down and down. The main tunnel ends with a cave-in, but side passages and chambers connect to the Labyrinth. Forge Lord carvings Forge Lords decorate the walls, and rich veins of copper and hematite (iron ore) have been found. The Second Delve opens on the righthand slope (facing uphill). The delve goes straight, sloping down for about 300 feet, then turns left at a landing. Another 300 feet, there's another landing, another left turn. The pattern repeats dozens of times, ending at a ledge that overlooks a subterranean lake. Miners brick up the end of the passage, but their walls rarely stand for long. Ancient side passages spoke outward from this delve; many have led to rich (though inconvenient) copper veins. The Third Delve opens on the lefthand Arm. The main tunnel forks and turns frequently, soon becoming one great big twisting mess of tunnels and chambers. Many of these lower tunnels connect with the Labyrinth. Hematite veins have been found at the end of many such tunnels, and turned into active mines.

638.title: Impressions
638.body: - Vast, arched tunnels, stretching well beyond your torchlight - Echoing sounds of picks on rock, carts rumbling, voices grumbling, your own footsteps - That sense of immense, merciless weight above you - A smooth, rippling texture to the main tunnel walls, less like they were carved and more like they were… melted? - Constant, warm breeze flowing up, like gentle breath - Crude, cramped scale of new excavations, propped up with timbers

639.title: Chance encounters
639.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › The Delves › Chance encounters
639.body: Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

640.title: 1d6 encounter
640.body: - 1 Cultist or worshiper of the Things Below The Things Below - 2 A shady, strange, or desperate character, out of place - 3-4 Mining crew, or part of one - 5 Someone alone, taking something to/from a mine - 6 Prospector, or (1 in 6 chance) some Ustrina Ustrina For disposition, choose or have someone roll:

641.title: 1d6 disposition
641.body: - 1 Planning treachery or betrayal - 2 Aggressively unfriendly, even hostile - 3 In dire need of assistance - 4-5 Wary, tight-lipped, suspicious - 6 Friendly enough

642.title: Discoveries
642.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

643.title: 1d6 discovery
643.body: - 1 signs of violence/disaster (below) - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 A mine, abandoned or destroyed (perhaps mysteriously) - 4 Possessions/gear, lost or discarded - 5 A vein of copper, hematite (iron ore), or galena (silver/lead ore) - 6 A ruin or artifact of the Forge Lords Forge Lords

644.title: 1d6 signs of violence/disaster
644.breadcrumb: Gordin's Delve › The Delves › 1d6 signs of violence/disaster
644.body: - 1 A scene of wanton carnage - 2 A dead monster from the Labyrinth The Labyrinth, bloody trail leading off - 3 One or more dead bodies - 4-5 A recent collapse, possibly with survivors - 6 Sounds of a fight, or a call for help

645.title: Dangers
645.body: - Utter darkness - Getting lost - Pockets of bad air: stale, poisonous, smoke- or dust-filled - Cave-ins, especially in more recent excavations - Laborers Gordin's Delve, with something to hide or protect - Ghosts (see Death and the Undying), victims of cave-ins or previous violence - Traps or wards protecting Forge Lord compounds Forge Lords - Monsters come up from the Labyrinth The Labyrinth

646.title: The Great Wood
646.body: Stonetop sits on a bluff overlooking the Great Wood, which stretches hundreds of miles to the east. Few villagers dare to even cross the Stream that flows near the base of the bluff. Hunters, trappers, and foragers go deeper, but even they venture a day or two into the forest at most. The terrain is hilly and rugged, laced with small streams and dotted with ponds. The trees are a mix of deciduous and evergreen. In some spots, the trees grow tightly, surrounded by thick brush so that one can barely walk. In others, massive old growth trees dominate, the space between them sparse and open.

648.title: Always
648.body: - Massive trunks, reaching up up up, some bigger around than houses - Pale sky glimpsed through the canopy - Trees bending, creaking, rustling in the wind - A skittering in the distance - A sense of smallness, of being swallowed up by something ancient and vast

649.title: Spring
649.body: - Feet slipping on mud, rotting leaves - Drip drip dripping of snowmelt, rain, mist - Pale buds and early blooms, blue and yellow and violet - Hopeful swell of birdsong

650.title: Summer
650.body: - Brilliant, overwhelming green - Drone of insects buzzing, birds chirping - Oppressive heat and humidity

651.title: Autumn
651.body: - A world aflame with reds and golds - Crunch of needles and leaves underfoot - Stark long shadows cast by a low sun - Sharp smell of cold, damp air

652.title: Winter
652.body: - A frosty coating, a deathly stillness - Shocking white of snow drifts - Trees like boney fingers - Surprising chirp and rustle of finches

653.title: The Compact
653.body: For generations, the people of Stonetop have kept their promise to the Forest Folk : they never cut down a living tree in the Great Wood. The villagers gather deadfall, but timber comes from elsewhere. - According to the elders, when did someone last break the compact? What happened to them? - Who's been pushing to start harvesting trees, since the Forest Folk seem to have vanished? What will happen if someone fells a living tree? That's for you to decide. Maybe… - … they suffer the Forest's Wrath ; - … they risk releasing an evil spirit, held captive by the tree's roots; - … the trees house the spirits of dead Forest Folk, and they've just created a specter ; or - … nothing—unless the Forest Folk return.

654.title: Questions
654.body: - Who in your family used to ply the Wood? How did they lose their nerve? - What did you see deep in the Wood that you've never told to another? - Who did you lose to the Wood? How? - Of all the stories you've heard of the Wood, which one scares you most?

655.title: Places
655.body: - The Golden Oak - The Maw - Red groves - The Stream - Vor Svetelik

656.title: Hooks
656.body: - A villager wanders off into the Wood for unknown reasons - A hunter stumbles home, having narrowly escaped some new danger - A southern scholar arrives in Stonetop, seeking a guide into the Great Wood - A hunter or trapper who the PCs care about hasn't returned home - Trappers are catching strange game: sickly, mutated, and wrong

657.title: . steading improvement .
657.breadcrumb: The Great Wood › Hooks › . steading improvement .

658.title: GREAT WOOD TIMBER
658.breadcrumb: The Great Wood › Hooks › . steading improvement . › GREAT WOOD TIMBER
658.body: Requires all of the following: - Proof that trees can be felled without dire consequence - A crew of brave lumberjacks - A skilled carpenter, working full time - Pulling Together once, over a course of a few weeks, to generate the first load of timber. When you mark all the requirements , increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Timber from the Great Wood" to the Resources list. Henceforth, the steading has a ready supply of timber for improvements and other projects, and it consumes 1 less Surplus every winter.

659.title: Terrain
659.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

660.title: 1d12 terrain
660.body: - 1 Pond, wetland, or lake - 2 Creek, stream, or river - 3 Rocky outcropping, cave(s) - 4-5 Clearing, meadow, sparse trees - 6-7 Dense thicket - 8-9 Swath of one type of tree - 10 Briars, thorns, nettles - 11 Slope, hill, ridge, ravine - 12 Single, notable tree If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

661.title: Discoveries
661.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

662.title: 1d6 discovery
662.body: - 1 A change of terrain (previous page) and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 game (see below) - 4 Useful or valuable flora - 5 A crinwin , Fae or spirit of the wild , curious or indifferent - 6 A point of interest (next column)

663.title: 1d6 game
663.body: - 1-3 Mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, fowl, rabbits, etc. - 4-5 Big and dangerous if provoked: boars , wisents , tal gerdwyr , etc. - 6 Deadly and easily provoked: cave bears , ceirwmawr , raselbaedd , etc.

664.title: 1d6 point of interest
664.breadcrumb: The Great Wood › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
664.body: - 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below or the Fomoraij - 2 A place haunted by death and the undying Death and the undying - 3 A place bearing traces of the Forest Folk - 4 A place sacred to the spirits of the wild - 5 A Fae domain or waystone - 6 A ruin or artifact of the Green Lords If it's been there for at least 10 years, it's likely marked by Forest Folk glyphs .

666.title: Hazards
666.body: - Getting lost - Treacherous terrain (loose footing, mudslides, sinkholes, deadfall, etc.) - Swarm (bees, wasps, midges, bats, etc.) - Tall tree to climb, and a reason to climb it

667.title: Monsters
667.body: - Crinwin - Fae - Spirits of the Wild - Chimerae and other remnants of the Green Lords - Servants of the Fomoraij - The ghosts or revenants of those who died in the Wood, like the Gwraig Wen - A copse of dool trees and its resident spirits Dool trees - The Pale Hunter and his hounds The Pale Hunter - The Crombil , should it wake - One of the many beasts that inhabit the Wood

668.title: Boar
668.body: Group , gluttonous HP 10; Armor 0 Damage gore d8 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Instinct to protect their territory - Relentlessly charge their victim, over and over - Eat just about anything - Ignore pain or injury

669.title: Butcherbird
669.body: Horde , tiny , organized HP 1; Armor 1 ( tiny ) Damage peck d6-2 ( hand , messy , 2 piercing) Instinct to feast on bloody, bloody meat - Grab on and stab stab stab - Fend off any who would steal the flock's meal - Scent blood from miles away About the size of a woodpecker, with black and wine-red plumage, flitting about in small flocks (2d6+4). Their laugh will make your blood run cold. But don't worry. They'll get your blood flowing again, real soon. Something interesting: Butcherbirds can smell fresh blood from miles away, and might descend on a hunter's kill to gorge. Something useful: They can smell the blood of their own, and shun it; smeared butcherbird blood is an excellent repellent.

670.title: Cave bear
670.body: Solitary , large , cautious HP 16; Armor 1 (fur, hide) Damage claws, bite, hug d10+4 ( hand , close , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Instinct to fill its belly, to protect its young - Rend, maul, crush - Move with surprising speed and grace - Sniff out food, or trouble Great big silver-brown things, eating anything they can dig up or catch. They mostly hibernate through winter, and get especially bold and ornery in autumn as they try to fatten up. They've been known to wake up and forage even in winter, though. They lair in big caves, usually two mates and maybe their cubs. Out and about, the bulls are usually found on their own; the sows on their own, or with their cubs. They're not usually aggressive towards people—unless surprised, extra hungry, or protecting the cubs. Then? Watch out.

671.title: Ceirwmawr
671.body: Solitary , large , terrifying , fearless HP 16; Armor 0 Damage hoof d10+1 ( close ) or antlers d10+3 ( reach , forceful ) Special quality awe-inspiring antlers Instinct to tolerate no disrespect - Toss aside a barrier or foe - Suffer harm without flinching - Make a show of strength Majestic. Proud. Antlers so large you can scarce believe how deftly it moves through the woods. They are the royalty of deer. It is said that only the Pale Hunter can give them chase, and that any mortal who dares to stalk them quickly finds the tables turned.

672.title: Questions
672.body: - What special property does folklore say the ceirwmawr's antlers possess? - What dire fate is said to befall those who slay a ceirwmawr without the Pale Hunter's permission?

673.title: Cougar
673.body: Solitary , stealthy , cautious , athletic HP 12; Armor 0 Damage bite d10 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities keen night vision and sense of smell Instinct to catch prey unawares - Move into position, silently and unseen - Pounce on prey, dragging them down ( forceful ) - Bite down and rake ( messy ) They don't usually hunt people. But when game is scarce, or some fool provokes them, they sometimes get a taste for human blood. Even then, they mostly leave groups alone, waiting for someone to fall behind or wander off. Mostly.

674.title: Cwisarff
674.body: Solitary , small , stealthy , cautious HP 10; Armor 0 Damage venomous bite d10 ( close ) Instinct to defend its current spot - Wait patiently to spring on prey - Hiss menacingly at a threat - Bite quickly and ready to strike again The vaunted "tree adder," most lethal serpent in the Wood, up to 6 feet long and dripping with venom. In warm months, they hunt and bask in the canopy or sunny clearings. In colder months, they hide in hollows and under logs, going into torpor in winter. They don't hunt people, but they don't fear them either. When you are bitten by a cwisarff, mark a debility in addition to taking damage. Until the poison is treated, you can't regain HP and are in danger of further damage and debility.

675.title: Cynddaraig
675.body: Solitary , large , terrifying , fearless HP 20; Armor 2 (scales) Damage three-inch long teeth d10+3 ( close , reach , grabby , messy , 3 piercing, advantage) Instinct to dominate its territory - Roar and unfurl its quills, making even brave warriors quail - Bite a limb clean off - To lose itself in primal fury The mighty "rage drake," almost 10 feet tall and 20 feet from nose to tip of tail. Its jaws are full of knives. Blood-red patterns surround its eyes. A frill of feathered quills sprouts from its neck and runs down its back. Maybe a half-dozen of them stalk the whole Great Wood, and one hasn't been spotted near Stonetop for generations (Tor be praised). Something interesting: They prey mostly on large game (wisents, tal gerdwyr, etc.). They'll attack people, but rarely hunt them. Something useful: They attack people to establish dominance, seeing us as rivals. An appropriate offering or show of obeisance might calm one enough for you to get the hell out of there.

676.title: Feathered drake
676.body: Group , stealthy , organized HP 6; Armor 1 (feathers, scales) Damage boney crest d8+2 ( close , forceful ), talon & teeth d8+2 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities flightless Instinct to spring on unwary prey - Burst out of the brush - Bash prey to the ground, senseless - Surround downed prey, and rend Bigger than a grown man, with a talon yea-long on each foot and that ridge on their heads they use to go crashing through the undergrowth. They might follow a party for days, waiting for them to let their guard down or for someone to fall behind. Then? Bam. Their feathers are much-sought by merchants. You can harvest a ◇ bundle of drake feathers ( fragile , Value 3) from a flock of 3-4 drakes.

677.title: Gylglyd vines
677.breadcrumb: The Great Wood › Feathered drake › Gylglyd vines
677.body: Damage strangling vines d6 ( hand , grabby ) Instinct to lure prey - Ensnare their senses with beauty - Lull their minds with euphoric scents - Slowly entwine them, unnoticed, and hold them fast Thick, velvety green vines that drape entire groves or clearings, speckled with flowers of iridescent blue and pink and yellow. The mind reels to see them, wanting to move closer. The flowers give off the sweetest scent, erasing all cares. But the wary might notice strange bulges in the hanging vines, or white bone beneath the carpet. Something interesting : Gylglyd flowers are prized by herbalists and apothecaries, but nearly impossible to cultivate. Something useful : A kerchief soaked in musk can counter the euphoric effect.

678.title: Hagr
678.body: Solitary , large , terrifying HP 16; Armor 1 (blubbery hide) Damage meaty hands d10+3 ( hand , close , grabby , forceful ) or tree trunk d10+5 ( reach , area , crude , forceful ) or hurled heavy thing d10+3 ( thrown , forceful ) Instinct to compulsively shape its environment - Fix someone with its evil eye, overwhelming them with fear - Pluck things from the ground as if weightless - Obsessively stack things to exacting standards - Throw a tantrum when things aren't just right Misshapen brutes with sagging flesh and one bulging eye so filled with hate it'll stop you dead in your tracks. They spend their days collecting fallen branches, looking for just the right one to add to their home of rotting logs. The others they pile neatly, reverently. Perhaps they were servants of the Green Lords, once bred to build, and now, long ages hence, building still.

679.title: Nailadd
679.body: Group , cunning , stealthy HP 6; Armor 2 (carapace) Damage venomous bite d8 ( hand ) Special qualities webs, prodigious leaps Instinct to capture prey and slowly consume it - Leap on prey and knock it prone - Inject a paralytic venom - Drag helpless prey into the canopy and cocoon it So far, these hound-sized spiders have thankfully avoided hunters and trappers in the Wood. They certainly haven't come near town. But they're out there, rest assured, and growing in numbers. Something interesting: Nailadd venom paralyzes prey, rather than killing it. Something useful: You can harvest the venom (d4 uses, dangerous , reload , Value 1) from an intact corpse. When you coat a blade in nailadd venom , it deals +1d4 extra damage and numbs the wounded area.

680.title: Raselbaedd
680.body: Solitary , large HP 20; Armor 4 (hide, unnatural resilience) Damage gore d10+5 ( hand , close , forceful , messy , 3 piercing) Special qualities iron hide, always hungry, easily provoked, afraid of fire Instinct to hold a grudge - Rip things apart: armor, weapons, flesh - Eat almost anything - Refuse to back off or let go, even when dying - Recover from even mortal wounds, over time When a boar lives long enough, and survives enough close calls, and dines year after year on the roots and nuts of ferro trees, it eventually becomes a raselbaedd. The size of a horse, with tusks like short spears and a hide that's crisscrossed with scars and that bristles with broken blades. When you deal 2 damage or less to a raselbaedd , before armor, your weapon chips or shatters on its hide. A raselbaedd's hide ( immobile , Value 2) can be harvested by a competent hunter with a fine steel blade (2 piercing, Value 2). It can then be fashioned into 1d4 raselbaedd cuirasses (2 armor, Value 3). Doing so requires: - An exceptional tanner - An upgraded workshop with fine steel tools (Value 3) - A season of labor per cuirass

681.title: Tal gerdwyr
681.body: Group , huge HP 14; Armor 2 (feathers, scales) Damage trample, kick d8+5 ( close , forceful ), tail sweep d8+5 ( reach , area , forceful ) Special quality extremely long neck Instinct to get annoyed - Bellow loudly - Smash down or through a barrier - Reach very, very high up The Forest Folk would sometimes tell stories of the "tall walkers," drakes with necks like trees and bodies like small hills, draped in feathers that look like vines and leaves. Supposedly, they roam deep in the forest, blithely chewing leaves from the canopy and singing mournful songs to each other.

682.title: Wisent
682.body: Group , large , cautious HP 10; Armor 0 Damage headbutt, gore, trample d8+3 ( hand , close , forceful ) Instinct to protect the herd - Put itself between a threat and the young - Stampede ( area , forceful , messy ) - Leap a surprising height and distance, even from a standstill Forest bison travel in herds of up to twenty, but only the bulls typically fight. When cornered, though, even the sows can be dangerous due to their mass and strength.

683.title: Wolf
683.body: Group , organized , cautious HP 6; Armor 0 Damage bite d8 ( hand , grabby ) Qualities keen sense of smell Instinct to bring down the weakest prey - Follow prey for miles on end - Surround, flank, and harry - Clamp down, eventually crushing bone

684.title: Green Lords
684.body: In the time of the Makers , the Green Lords ruled the Great Wood and what is now Ferrier's Fen . They grew things, bred and cultivated. They bent the spirits of wild to their will, and brought forth new forms of life—some of which would eventually become the Forest Folk . Yet while they mastered life itself, they feared death. They dreaded the bleak eternity waiting behind the Last Door . They bred the Fae out of spirit and flesh, and harnessed their power to craft false afterlives. And: it worked. Dozens of Green Lords had themselves mummified and entombed, dreaming forever of decadence and wonder. Some of the Fae, however, grew to resent their servitude. They plotted in secret and rose up against their masters. They gutted tombs, put the living to the sword, and sent the survivors running. The Green Lords, in panic and desperation, called on the Things Below and used their power to quash the rebellion. But this in turn drew the ire of other Makers. They worked a mighty magic that collapsed a vast swath of Green Lord territory, sinking it into a mire and killing thousands. The Fae rallied and besieged the survivors at Vor Svetelik . Their cause lost, the Green Lords unleashed a last great wave of horror before retreating into the bowels of the earth. There they have remained, growing ever more twisted and vile, becoming the Fomoraij .

685.title: Names
685.body: Slavic-sounding: Athanasi, Bogdana, Chedomir, Dozhena, Darobor, Jigorii, Kresimira, Mstislava, Nikalor, Radomira, Tikomar, Tomila, Vecheslav, Voitsek, Yaroslana, Yevena.

686.title: Hooks
686.body: - An earthquake or wildfire reveals a long-buried Green Lord ruin close to Stonetop. - A hazardous growth is aggressively spreading through the Great Wood - An old scholar or manuscript shows up, telling of a Green Lord relic that can greatly improve crops. - Some fool braves a Green Lord's tomb and returns with a valuable treasure, but they're pursued by mummified servants and infected with a horrible rotting disease.

688.title: 1d12 theme
688.body: - 1 Beasts or vermin: bred, changed, or harnessed - 2 Plants and growing things: grandiose and/or unusual - 3 Adaptation/growth/expansion/vitality - 4 Chimerae: creatures with disparate parts, features of other organisms - 5 Tombs, mummification, constructed afterlives - 6 The spirits of the wild or that which they embody, bound or put to use - 7 Fae servants/rebellion - 8 Corruption by the Things Below , and roll again - 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

689.title: Sites
689.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

690.title: 1d6 site
690.body: - 1-4 Lingering signs of their presence and influence (at right) - 5-6 A ruin

691.title: Lingering signs
691.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

692.title: 1d6 sign
692.body: - 1 An entity, imprisoned in a strange tree, rune-carved stone, or natural feature (next page) - 2 Strange plants (next page) - 3 A section of the Broken Roads , unearthed or still barely visible - 4 A spirit of the wild, bound to some useful purpose (next page) - 5 Useful or valuable flora growing in abundance - 6 A waystone

693.title: An entity, imprisoned
693.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Sites › An entity, imprisoned
693.body: For example: - A tree with a metallic sheen, its wood infused with iron. Its trunk spirals around itself, like a spool of yarn, a rebellious Fae trapped within. - A man-sized mass of blood-sucking insects—an entity of pain and disease—imprisoned in amber. - A mighty old elder tree, its bark like bone, oozing black sap from the deathless horror it holds bound in its roots. - A great hunk of stone, reminiscent of a ravenous, howling beast, wrapped in petrified vines and roots and mostly buried/submerged. - A Fae locked in the form of a tree, a snarl of defiance barely visible.

694.title: Strange plants
694.body: For example: - A grove of trees, planted in strangely regular patterns (neat rows, spirals, etc.) - Trees/vines, their trunks or stalks woven around each other to form walls, curtains, ceilings, etc. - Shrubs/briars in crude topiary shapes with no obvious tending - Grand sculptures of living wood that memorialize long-dead Green Lords - Moss, lichen, mold, or fungus that glows, or hums, or gives off heat, etc. - Plants made partially of impossible material (flowers/leaves of glass, bark infused with metal, a vine with thorns of bone, a petrified tree that blooms with normal flowers, etc.) - An enormous and ancient tree, festooned with gylglyd vines that protect some Green Lord artifact.

695.title: A spirit of the wild, bound
695.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Sites › A spirit of the wild, bound
695.body: For example: - A spirit of winter/hibernation bound to a grotto by vines growing in runic patterns. Other runes fill those who approach with dread. An ancient being lies in torpor within. - A clearing or meadow lush with an edible grain, tended by a fertility spirit bound to a menhir standing in the field's center. - An orchard of fruit trees, guarded by a birdwaig and her glasbren daughters Spirits of the Wild, a long family of spirits and trees bound by an ancient oath to the Green Lords. - A ring of five ancient willow trees, each with a rune-carved hollow, surrounding a stretch of mire. A spirit of reclamation and decay is bound within. Anything that enters the mire will soon be sucked into the muck, where it will decompose and dissolve at an alarming rate. - A huge stone altar on a treeless hilltop, exposed to the sun. A spirit of growth and vitality is bound within, fed by blood and bestowing gifts of strength. - An obelisk of milky white crystal, pushing out of the loamy soil, shot through with tendrils of vine that form looping runes of binding. The spirit bound within can pull forth maladies and afflictions from those who touch it, but the spirit is willful and the process is excruciating.

696.title: Ruins
696.body: Pick or roll for theme Green Lords, structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place it in an appropriate terrain (see Great Wood Timber for the Great Wood or Ferrier's Fen for Ferrier's Fen ). Populate it with lingering signs , artifacts and/or dangers of the Green Lords or the appropriate region.

697.title: 1d6 structure
697.body: - 1 Sprawling compound of "smaller" buildings - 2 Single, smaller building - 3 Earth-covered mound - 4 Ancient trees, rooms and structures built into hollows and on crooks - 5 Underground vault(s) - 6 Ziggurat/pyramid/dome

698.title: 1d6 purpose
698.body: - 1 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, etc.) - 2 Containment (fortress, prison, kennel, menagerie, etc.) - 3 Storage (cellar, vault, warehouse, library, collection, etc.) - 4 Production (workshop, nursery, factory, kitchen, abattoir, etc.) - 5 Esoteric (laboratory, shrine, surgery, etc.) - 6 Tomb (of a Green Lord, their ancestors, their servants, their creations, etc.)

699.title: 1d6 architectural elements
699.body: - 1 Huge slabs of smooth stone (white marble, granite, serpentine) carved with scrollwork and bas reliefs - 2 Moss and lichen, of striking colors, growing in decorative patterns - 3 Amber, agate, geodes, even unworked boulders of interesting shape - 4 Ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk sometimes fused with or growing from wood or stone - 5 Pillars, arches, or even walls of petrified wood, seemingly grown into shape - 6 Vines and tendrils (often petrified or metal-infused) clinging to surfaces in branching whorls, sigils, and runes Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants—people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

700.title: 1d6 condition
700.body: - 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, submerged, and/or overgrown; its purpose unrecognizable - 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, submerged, and/or overgrown - 5 Buried, submerged, and/or overgrown, but mostly intact - 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved See Vor Svetelik (the ruined city of the Green Lords, Vor Svetelik) for additional examples. Many Green Lord ruins feature (or are preserved by) Fae domains . The PCs are tracking nailadd (giant jumping spiders) through the Great Wood, and I think that they're heading for a Green Lord ruin. I roll a 6 for theme (the spirits of the wild, bound or put to use), a 6 for structure (ziggurat/pyramid/dome), a 4 for purpose (production), a 4 for architectural elements (ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk), and 3 for condition (crumbling, largely buried and overgrown). I ponder for a little, then come up with this: A crumbling ziggurat, half-collapsed and mostly covered with dirt, trees, and webs. Inside is a smaller ziggurat that bears a silk tapestry of the Mother-of-Spiders, a powerful elemental spirit. In days of yore, this place was filled with spiders of all sizes, spinning silk and weaving impossible fabrics for the Green Lords. Now, the spiders merely grow large, and hungry, and strangely intelligent.

702.title: Various treasures
702.body: - A ◇ mummified bird in a clay pot ( fragile , Value 0) - A number of ◇ corroded bronze hatchets of ancient make, with beautifully preserved wooden grips ( crude and Value 0 as-is; hand and Value 1 if restored) - A beautiful silk shirt , embroidered with vine-like patterns (Value 1) - Stacks of raw, unworked timber with a metallic sheen to the grain; incredibly strong but incredibly difficult to work ( immobile , Value 2) - A coat of scale armor for someone 20 feet tall. The scales are strange, organic, semi-translucent and iridescent around the edges. Thick, twisting vines grow from the armor where a body should be; nothing else grows nearby. The armor is impractical and creepy, but if extracted intact, it could fetch a decent price ( immobile , Value 2) to a collector. - An oversized ◇◇ drinking horn , gold and amber fused into the horn in impossible ways (Value 3) - A tall, thick, gnarled tree bearing large, delicious fruits in early autumn. Provides as many ◇ provisions (4 uses) as the PCs care to take, and could be cultivated into a rhoillyg orchard.

703.title: A large urn
703.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › A large urn
703.body: immobile , magical When the urn is unoccupied and a Fae looks inside , it is compelled to crawl in (it can always fit). If you then close the lid , the Fae is trapped and the urn slowly and painfully peels away its knowledge, essence, and sense of self. In time, nothing remains but a pile of pearls (Value 2).

705.title: RHOILLYG ORCHARD
705.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › . steading improvement . › RHOILLYG ORCHARD
705.body: Requires both: - A sack full of rhoillyg seeds - An herbalist of considerable skill and patience And either of these, to germinate the seeds: - A year or so of experimentation - Advice from a knowledgeable source And then each of these: - A full spring, to sprout and cultivate saplings - Pulling Together once, over the course of a summer, to clear and plant the orchard - Protecting the orchard through two more summers When you mark all the requirements , increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Rhoillyg Orchard" to both the Resources list and the steading map. Henceforth, the steading generates +1 Surplus in summer and another +1 Surplus when the autumn harvest is complete.

706.title: A vine in a rune-carved pot
706.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › A vine in a rune-carved pot
706.body: ◇, magical It's about as thick as your finger and maybe 30 feet long stretched out, with skinny little tendrils. It looks rather dried out, but moves slightly if you touch it or its pot. If you water it, it twitches excitedly. A plant spirit is bound to the vine and pot. If you befriend it and carry its pot, it will animate and act as a follower: grabby , clever , playful ; HP 3; Damage 0; Instinct to not let go; Cost affection, play time.

707.title: A fang-dagger
707.body: hand , magical , +1 damage An 8-inch fang, bound to a wooden hilt by fiber that never rots. A cruel-looking thing, with strange carvings along the blade. Something interesting: The fang looks like it came from a cynddaraig , and the markings are definitely some sort of spell related to blood and soil. Something useful: When you bury the fang-dagger and water the soil with blood , the ground buckles and bulges. A minute later, a full-grown cynddaraig bursts from the soil—angry, confused, and hungry. The spell grants no control over the beast and provides no way to dismiss it. The dagger is gone, now a tooth in the rage-drake's maw.

708.title: A big stone coffer
708.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › A big stone coffer
708.body: immobile , magical Carved from serpentine, 4 feet by 2 feet by 2, with a heavy stone lid. When found, it's likely half-full with soil. To those sensitive to such things, it thrums with the power of fertility and growth. When you add fresh soil to the coffer, plant seeds in it, and close the lid , the seeds will germinate and sprout overnight. Each seed will sprout only once, and the sprouts henceforth grow at a regular rate, regardless of whether they stay in the coffer or are planted elsewhere. With coordination, this could lead to a boom in Stonetop's gardens and +1 Surplus each summer. In the hands of a skilled herbalist, this can greatly improve an herb garden and allow the cultivation of rare or difficult plants. The herbalists of Marshedge would kill over this—or even start a war.

709.title: Small clay jars
709.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › Small clay jars
709.body: fragile , magical A number of earthenware jars, about the size of a pinecone, sealed with wax and seemingly empty. When opened (or broken) a fine cloud of dust puffs out. The dust is, in fact, chogor spores. When someone is exposed to chogor spores , the spores quickly start to grow on them in masses of vine-like ferns. They will—in a matter of seconds—ensnare and restrain their victim. After about an hour, the ferns die and rapidly dry out, but until then, it's like being bound up in twine.

710.title: Minor arcana
710.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › Small clay jars › Minor arcana

711.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
711.body: - 1 A strange skull and antlers - 2 A giant oak leaf - 3 A huge wooden sphere - 4 A stretched vellum - 5 A patch of rainbow moss - 6 A crumbling arch

712.title: Major arcana
712.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
712.body: These might very well be associated with the Green Lords. - The Twisted Spear - The Shield of the Wisent Witch

714.title: Bound Entities
714.body: - Spirits of the wild , forced or enticed to perform a particular task or to protect a particular place - Fae , loyal and still serving their embalmed masters (possibly bitter and questioning their fate) or rebellious and imprisoned for their crimes - Spirits and beasts corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below, either locked away by the Green Lords, or weaponized against the Fae - Plasmic horrors , which once served as blank canvases for fleshweaving

715.title: Chimerae
715.body: The Green Lords meddled wantonly with life, mixing creatures in impossible ways to suit need or whim. Some such creatures linger, breeding true or having survived for hundreds of years. Pick or roll for purpose, ability, size, and composition. Allow purpose, ability, and size to inform the elements of its composition, and what traits it has from each element.

716.title: 1d12 purpose
716.body: - 1-2 War/destruction/murder/hunting - 3-4 Defense/protection/companionship - 5-6 Experimentation/insight/curiosity - 7-8 Labor/industry/mundane tasks/convenience - 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice and combine

717.title: 1d12 ability
717.body: - 1 Burn/dissolve/destroy/drain - 2 Transform/mutate/change - 3 Control/influence minds or emotions - 4 Wield/produce/ignore elements or energy - 5 Poison/disease/infect - 6 Hide/mimic/disguise - 7 Entangle/trap/engulf/subdue - 8 Attack at range/over an area - 9 Prowess/mobility/speed - 10 Strength/toughness/durability - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

718.title: 1d12 size
718.body: - 1-2 Cat-sized or smaller ( tiny ) - 3-4 Like a human child ( small ) - 5-8 Human-sized - 9-11 Like a horse, a cart, etc. ( large ) - 12 Like an elephant, or bigger ( huge )

719.title: 1d12 composition
719.body: - 1-4 Beast + beast - 5-6 Beast + plant - 7-8 Beast + mineral - 9-11 Beast + roll again with 1d8 - 12 Person + roll again with 1d8

720.title: Hazardous growth
720.body: Pick or roll for form and peril, possibly rolling the Die of Fate for size/scope/scale. Maybe consider how it's useful/valuable as well.

721.title: 1d12 form
721.body: - 1-2 Invisible to the naked eye/spores/dust - 3-4 Mold/moss/lichen/slime - 5-6 Mushrooms/puffballs/stinkhorns/fungus - 7-8 Vines/briars/brambles/shoots - 9-10 Flowers/plants/ferns/grasses - 11-12 Tree(s)/shrubs/bushes/hedges

722.title: 1d12 peril
722.body: - 1 Crushes, strangles, cuts, stabs (possibly animate) - 2 Eats away, dissolves, absorbs (tissue or specific material) - 3 Grows incredibly quickly, overwhelms, ensnares - 4 Infects, colonizes, sickens, changes - 5 Poisons, weakens, subdues - 6 Causes dizziness, hallucinations, delusions - 7 Releases spores/pollen/etc. that irritate, obscure, agitate - 8 Draws attention (via noise, phosphorescence, scent, etc.) - 9-10 Roll 1d10 twice and combine - 11-12 Roll again with 1d10 but make it weird—elemental, psychic, spiritual, extremely specific, etc. See Vor Svetelik (ruined city of the Green Lords, Vor Svetelik) for specific examples.

723.title: The Unquiet Dead
723.breadcrumb: Green Lords › Dangers › The Unquiet Dead
723.body: - Ghosts of the Green Lords and their many servants, who died in battle or sudden cataclysm. - Specters of Green Lords who were murdered by trusted servants. - Wraiths of Green Lords who were mummified and entombed, only to have their false afterlives ruined by treachery and war. - Nemurvojak , the corpses of mortal servants, reanimated by demon-seed

724.title: Llamudwr
724.body: Solitary , large , construct , corrupted , cautious , stealthy HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage stinging tail d10+1 ( reach ), crunching bite d10+1 ( hand , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities clumsy when grounded, hates the sun Instinct to fill its belly and retreat beneath the water - Lurk unseen, pick a target, and wait until sunset - Leap out of the water with shocking speed - Follow prey from the sky, or hanging from trees - Strafe its prey and deliver a painful, debilitating sting - Chomp down, and then swallow whole Everyone knows to shun deep, still water, and the llamudwr (or "water leapers") are surely one reason why. Take the body of a frog, 6 or 7 feet long. Add another 8 or 9 feet of whipping tail, tipped with a venomous barb. Get rid of its legs, not even stumps, but give it bat wings, a span of nearly 20 feet unfurled. Hide it in a lake or fen, away from the sun's cruel light. And finally, make it hungry. Very, very hungry.

725.title: Mummified Green Lord
725.body: Solitary , large , spirit , undead , magical , cunning , cautious , organized , terrifying HP 24; Armor 4 (potent wards), 0 vs. fire Damage mighty blows d10+2 ( hand , close , forceful ) Special qualities tethered to canopic jars, can sense all that happens in its tomb Instinct to preserve its glorious afterlife - Animate its preserved corpse - Overawe anyone in its presence - Twist, mutate, or transform living things with a gesture - Wield nearby plants and beasts as extensions of itself - Regenerate its corpse, even if burned, unless its canopic jars destroyed They lie entombed, their corpses embalmed, their souls bound to the heavily-warded canopic jars that store their most-vital organs. Yet those souls linger forever in Fae-woven realms of glamour and delight. In these realms, the Green Lords are as gods. They are loathe to leave them, and rarely need to. But should their existence be truly threatened, they will return to this world of base flesh, inhabit their long-dead bodies, and punish any intruders with the savagery of the cruelest beasts.

726.title: Mummified servant
726.body: Horde , spirit , undead , organized , relentless HP 11; Armor 0 Damage choking grip d6 ( hand , grabby ), spear d6 ( close , 1 piercing) Special qualities takes +1d4 damage from fire, tethered to canopic jars Instinct to destroy intruders - Animate its preserved corpse - Surround intruders and hem them in, cutting off escape - Keep fighting with limbs that have been severed - Regenerate its corpse, unless burned or its canopic jars destroyed Some of the Green Lords were mummified and interred with their most faithful mortal servants. These servants get to enjoy their master's false afterlife, but when threats to the tomb arise, they are sent back to their corpses, to rise and crush any intruders they find.

727.title: Rhagedn
727.body: Horde , construct , stealthy HP 3; Armor 2 (woody hide) Damage talons, antlers d6+2 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities clumsy fliers Instinct to protect their nests - Shift into a better position while unobserved - Strafe their prey ( forceful ) and return to the skies - Roost on earth or stone or wood, sinking in talons, furling its wings, and becoming like woody stumps or boughs Hunched humanoid forms carved of wood, with great wings, taloned feet, and heads crowned with branch-like antlers. They were made by the Green Lords to be sentinels and guardians, to watch over their borders and their most important sites. Now, ages and generations later, they still watch, and wait, and protect their nests. Something interesting: The tales say that the song of a redbreasted bluebird will keep the rhagedn still, letting folk pass unmolested. Something useful: It just so happens that you know how to whistle the tune (whether or not you can whistle it well enough is questionable).

728.title: Swyn
728.body: Solitary , large , construct , corrupted , stealthy , magical , devious , cautious , hoarder HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), 2 vs. bronze (scales) Damage bite & constriction d10+3 ( hand , close , forceful , grabby ) Instinct to indulge its ego - Appear out of nowhere and lock eyes - Hold its prey fast with gaze and soothing words - Plant a suggestion in its victim's mind - Turn the weak-willed into fawning servants Swyn are great serpents with simian heads and hypnotic eyes. The Green Lords bred them out of desperation, borrowing secrets from the Things Below. They wanted spymasters and saboteurs. What they got was a race of petty, craven narcissists. Each swyn fancies itself the greatest, the most beautiful, the most magnificent of beings. They have exacting tastes, far more subtle and refined than any of their peers. But should you embarrass, defy, or simply bore one, expect to be brutally devoured. Few of these creatures survive, thankfully. The Fae hunt them and they shun others of their kind, jealous of territory and wealth. But every dozen years or so, some urge drives them all to the ruins of Vor Svetelik (page 468), entourage in tow, where they engage in ostentatious displays and mating rituals. There, amidst the sick white trees, the next brood of swyn are seeded. There, perhaps, these foul things might be wiped out once and for all. When the swyn compels you to act against your will , mark XP if you do what it wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.

729.title: Zrajedak
729.body: Solitary , large , construct , corrupted , devious , terrifying , stealthy HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 1 vs. bronze (hide) Damage maw full of iron teeth d10+3 ( hand , messy , grabby , 1 piercing, advantage), iron tail-quills d10+1 ( near , area , reload ) Special qualities keen sense of smell Instinct to torment its prey - Assume a form similar to that of its prey, but just a little off - Reveal its horrible, smiling maw - Cripple a victim and let it suffer - Chew right through a limb When the Fae rebelled, the Green Lords made living weapons to use against them. The zrajedak—or "treachery-eaters"—were one such weapon. They merged the body of a lion, the tail of a spiney drake, and the face of their mortal servants, and then infused it all with iron, and malice, and the cruel power of the Things Below. Some of these horrors are trapped by Fae magic. Others remain in stasis, yet to be unleashed on the world. But surely some few stalk the world still, for the Forest Folk would tell hushed tales of them. "Never trust someone who will not show you their teeth," they'd say, "for Zrajedak smiles only before he bites."

730.title: Helior, the Daybringer
730.body: Helior is one of the four main gods of Stonetop. He is the god of the sun and light, beacon of hope and mercy, the fiery foe of the dark. If one of your initial PCs has the Lightbearer playbook, then they will answer many of the questions in this section via their presence, their background, and the decisions they make during character creation and introductions. Defer to that PC as the expert on Helior in your game, but don't forget to ask other PCs for their opinions and experiences, too.

732.title: 1d12 theme
732.body: 1d12 theme ------ --------------------------- 1-2 The sun/day/light 3-4 Hope/courage/aspiration 5-6 Mercy/charity/kindness 7 Truth/revelation 8 Fire/burning/warmth 9 Glory/awe/majesty 10 Radiance/beauty/intensity 11 Refuge/sanctity/defense 12 Purification/cleansing/redemption

733.title: Questions
733.body: According to the tales: - How did Helior come to be (a god)? What's his origin story? - How did he obtain or create the White Flame? - Which god is his rival/ally/lover/kin? - Where does the sun/Helior go each night? - What causes eclipses? What do they mean or portend? - Who was famously martyred in Helior's name? Who killed them, and why? Use the answers to help decide what Helior's true nature is Gods and Religion.

734.title: Hooks
734.body: - A devotee of Helior ministers in a nearby town, fomenting dissent. - A child is born with a sun-shaped birthmark during a solar eclipse. - The spirit of the prior Lightbearer appears to a PC and assigns them a quest. - A Manmarcher chief acquires a relic of the prior Lightbearer and seeks to auction it off. - A charlatan or sorcerer claims the mantle of Lightbearer. - Spring fails to come; omens point to Helior being trapped or wounded.

735.title: Veneration
735.body: What is the worship of Helior like? It might be… (pick 1) - … ancient, widespread, and well-known; - … most common in Lygos and the South ; - … a new thing, still unheard of by many; - … an old thing, forgotten by most; or - … widely persecuted. And how is Helior properly worshipped?
