It might involve… (pick 1 or 2) - … solemn hymns; - … serene meditation; - … joyful song; - … ascetic denial; - … fervent dancing; - … formal ceremonies; - … drugs & intoxicants; and/or - … pain & sacrifice.

736.title: Consider or ask:
736.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Veneration › Consider or ask:
736.body: - Why does the worship of Helior involve the things it does? According to his devotees, why do these things please him so? - How do these practices change from community to community? - How are the solstices celebrated or observed? - If the worship of Helior is foreign/new/forgotten/persecuted: what other god or gods of the sun are worshipped around here? (See Gods and Religion for guidance)

737.title: Places of worship
737.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Places of worship
737.body: Helior has a shrine in Stonetop's pavilion of the gods. Outside of Stonetop: consider whether anyone in this community worships Helior, and where. For any given place of worship: - What is it? (A shrine, a hermitage, an orchard, a natural feature, etc.) - Where is it? (In a private home, a public building, outside of town, etc.) - How is Helior represented? - What symbols/trappings adorn it? - Which of his themes (previous page) are emphasized? - Who tends to it? What is this place of worship like? It might be… (pick 1) - … given a place of high honor, above all other gods; - … well-tended and given due respect; - … newly established/recently restored; - … fallen into disuse and disrepair; - … hidden away, where only the faithful know to look; - … some variation on the above; or - … something else entirely.

738.title: The Lightbearer
738.body: The Lightbearer is or was the appointed servant of Helior, wielder of his power and grace. There has only ever been one active at a time. If one of your PCs has the Lightbearer playbook, they're it. If no PC has the Lightbearer playbook, then consider or ask: - How many Lightbearers have there been? - Is a Lightbearer currently active in the world? - If so, where do they dwell and how well known are they? - If not, when did the last Lightbearer live? - What are/were they like, personally? - What great deeds are/were they known for? - Who are/were their greatest allies? Their greatest foes? - How is the mantle of Lightbearer said to pass or manifest? Should an NPC Lightbearer (or their spirit) oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (a wildcard if they still live, a magical entity if they cause trouble from beyond). Remember: NPCs don't have playbooks. An NPC Lightbearer might have tags or moves reminiscent of a PC Lightbearer, or not.

739.title: Devotees
739.body: Helior is a generous god, one who brings light and warmth to the world. He calls on humanity to do the same, to live lives of mercy, charity, and hope. Only a few devotees truly heed his call. In any given community, ask or consider: - How many devotees of Helior reside here? - Is there a formal cult or order, or just likeminded individuals? - Who is most notable among them? - How much influence do they have? - What charitable work(s) do they pursue? Who in the community resents them? - What contact do they have with devotees from other settlements? Should the Heliorite devotees of a community oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (like a wildcard , but maybe an institution ).

740.title: Entities of darkness
740.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Entities of darkness
740.body: The Lightbearer playbook has moves and invocations that affect creatures or spirits "of darkness." The Things Below definitely count, as do any corrupted beings. Spirits of night and dark places (like caves) probably count, too. Undead are almost certainly entities of darkness, but maybe there are exceptions? Ask the Lightbearer what they think makes something a creature or spirit of darkness. Consider what you know about the entity in question, and make a ruling. When in doubt, defer to the Die of Fate or the result of an appropriate move.

742.title: Various treasures
742.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Artifacts › Various treasures
742.body: - A vial of lemon-infused oil (Value 0) - A copper torc decorated with a stylized sun (Value 1) - A circle of bronze sigils two paces wide, inlaid into a stone floor; blessed by Helior, a ward against spirits of darkness ( magical , immobile , Value 2) - A lit ◇ oil lamp that consumes no oil; it will burn forever unless snuffed, blown out, or spilled ( magical , fragile , Value 2) - Holy relics (○○○ uses, Value 3 to a collector): if you have one in your inventory when you Invoke the Sun God, you can mark a use in lieu of choosing a consequence - A ◇ crown of white gold, that shines with holy light ( reach , area ) when worn by one with a pure soul ( magical , Value 4)

743.title: Minor arcana
743.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Artifacts › Minor arcana

744.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
744.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
744.body: - 1-2 A gold ring - 3-4 A gold butter lamp - 5-6 A rusty steel blade

746.title: Associated entities
746.breadcrumb: Helior, the Daybringer › Dangers › Associated entities
746.body: - An archon , set to guard a holy site (like the Lightbearer's tomb) - A southern assassin , paid to murder the Lightbearer or a problematic devotee - An elemental spirit of fire , consecrated by Helior, passionate and righteous

747.title: Pilgrims
747.body: Threat ( rabble ) Instinct to find succor and purpose - Grow in numbers: a steady trickle, a constant stream, a tide - Demand time, attention, consideration - Strain or consume resources - Get into trouble with the locals - Misinterpret teachings - Split into factions As a Lightbearer grows in power, word of their miracles will likely spread. Pilgrims are likely to follow. They seek out the Lightbearer for healing, direction, or purpose.

748.title: Fanatic
748.body: Group , organized , devoted HP 10; Armor 0 Damage weapons d8 (tags by weapon) Instinct to protect their beliefs - Make a passionate argument - Suffer for the cause - Do evil for the greater good A Lightbearer often attracts faithful followers, as do some of Helior's more charismatic devotees. Some of these followers might take things a little far.

749.title: Hillfolk
749.body: The Steplands are home to dozens of nomadic bands, collectively called the Hillfolk. The bands are tied together into a bewildering tapestry of kinship and intermarriage and treaty, rivalry and blood feud and long-standing grudge. Some Hillfolk roam the Flats in warmer months. Some bands trade with Stonetop, at least on occasion, signaling to them at Titan Bones . Others trade with Marshedge , and still others with southern merchants . They have little contact with the Manmarchers , and tend to treat them with suspicion. Their wariness towards the Manmarchers is nothing, though, compared to their contempt for Gordin's Delve . The Hillfolk view mining metal ores as sacrilege. They'll gladly steal from the Delvers or loot their corpses, but only the most debased Hillfolk trade peacefully with Gordin's Delve. And only the most shunned and shamed will actually move there and call the mining town home.

750.title: Names
750.body: Breton-inspired, but missing vowels and clipped-sounding: Adm, Blej, Cirl, Davth, Elst, Gwilm, Gwenl, Henri, Ines, Jenfir, Jown, Juda, Kiln, Laurl, Loic, Merrn, Maikl, Nanzl, Nolwn, Quent, Reegn, Ropr, Sabi, Stren, Yanz.

751.title: Questions
751.body: - Which of you knows the Hillfolk best? How so? - What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to (these) Hillfolk? - How is Hillfolk language different from Stonetop's? - What do the Hillfolk call themselves? What do they call each other? - What are groups of Hillfolk called? Bands? Rides? Herds? Families? Etc. - What affiliations do they have beyond their bands? Clans, nations, sects, etc? - What sparked the last open conflict between them and Gordin's Delve? - What, do you think, is the source of their taboo against mining? - What deity (if any) do they hold in highest regard? - How is their view of the gods different than Stonetop's? - What festival do (these) Hillfolk celebrate this time of year? - What game/pastime/food are (these) Hillfolk known for?

752.title: Hooks
752.body: - A band of Hillfolk has come under the sway of a hdour and moved into the Ruined Tower . - Ne'er-do-wells from Gordin's Delve kill some Hillfolk in the Flats; war looms. - Someone in town owes a blood debt to a Hillfolk band; they come calling to collect, with friends. - The ruin that the PCs seek lies in the territory of a particularly unfriendly Hillfolk band.

753.title: Lore
753.body: Hillfolk rarely discuss their ancestry with outsiders. They claim an ancient right to the Steplands, but fear the barrow mounds and many of the Stone Lord ruins. Something interesting: The Hillfolk's ancestors were human servants (slaves?) of the Stone Lords. They rebelled, calling on the power of the Things Below . Petty warlords and would-be sorcerer-kings—the Barrow Builders —dominated the Hillfolk for many years after the Stone Lords fell. Something useful: Many Hillfolk traditions stem from their time suffering under the Stone Lords and the Barrow Builders, and the efforts required to throw off the yoke of those rulers. What's one such tradition that you know of, and how can you use it to your advantage?

754.title: Nomads
754.body: Most Hillfolk bands are nomadic, following food and good grazing for their herds as the seasons change. Each band has traditional territory, including a winter camp with permanent shelter (caves, ruins, stone huts, wooden longhouses, etc.). A number of bands have territory that extends into the Flats or South Manmarch , but winter camps are always nestled in the Steplands. The Makers' Roads are considered communal territory, but otherwise infringing on a band's territory is cause for suspicion or bloodshed. Wise travelers hide their passage, or else bring tribute and actively seek to avoid offence. - How do (these) Hillfolk mark the boundaries of their territory? - What sort of temporary structures do (these) Hillfolk dwell in? (Tents? Yurts? Log houses? Something else?) - The Steplands are fertile enough—why don't (more) Hillfolk take up agriculture, do you think?

755.title: Hillfolk bands
755.body: Here's a typical band: Size hamlet ( 25 to 75 souls) Population +0

756.title: Prosperity -1
756.body: - Trade with other bands - Trade with (pick 1)... - ... Marshedge (rope, textiles, herbs, pottery) - ... Stonetop (whisky, potatoes, oats) - ... Lygos, etc. (salt, tools)

757.title: Resources
757.body: - Horses, sheep, goats, hounds - Wool, milk - Forage (fruits, nuts, veggies, honey) - Hunting (meat, fur, hides) - Gathering (timber, sap, flint)

758.title: Defenses +0
758.body: - Mounted warriors - Flint-tipped weapons ( crude ) About a third of each band are able-bodied adults; the rest are children or elders. Maybe half of the adults are riders, and the rest manage life back at camp. Older children typically manage herds, often with the help of hounds. Specialization is rare, and even homemakers and older children are capable fighters. - Smoke from multiple fires seen over the horizon - Bleating of sheep or goats, cluck of chickens, bark of dogs - Thickets and pens for keeping herds - Stern, suspicious faces and hands hovering near weapons If a PC is familiar with the(se) Hillfolk, ask them: - What is this group called? - What sets them apart from other Hillfolk? - Who are their staunchest allies? - Who are their bitterest rivals/enemies? - What resource do they have in (relative) abundance?

759.title: Naming bands
759.body: If you need inspiration for naming a Hillfolk band (or other group), pick a word from below (e.g. "Red band") or mix 2-3 (e.g. "Ash Pickers" or "Ochre Wolf Fang"). Arrow, Ash, Badger, Black, Blanket, Blue, Cloak, Crow, Drake, Eastern, Eye, Face, Fang, Feather, Ferret, Fist, Foot, Fur, Goat, Grass, Green, Hair, Hand, Herd(er), Hill, Hoof, Hound, Leaf, Lower, Moon, Northern, Ochre, Paw, Picker, Planter, Rain, Ram, Raven, Red, Rock, Run(ner), Seed, Shield, Shrew, Slayer, Snake, Snow, Soil, Southern, Spear, Staff, Sun, Tooth, Tree, Upper, Vulture, Watch(er), Weed, Western, Whisper(er), White, Wing, Woad, Wolf, Yellow.

760.title: Traits
760.body: Ask the PCs, or pick 1 or 2, or roll the Die of Fate.

761.title: 1d12 trait
761.body: - 1 Particularly large or small, prosperous or poor - 2 "Picked band," made of unrelated adults, few children among them - 3 Worship a particular deity, revere a particular spirit - 4 Famous member, now or in the past - 5 Keep dangerous beasts (boars, drakes, etc.) - 6 Ride something other than horses (aurochs, Huffel rams, big deer) - 7 Tend to be large/slender/tall/short/etc. - 8 Especially fierce/friendly/territorial/aggressive/political/etc. - 9 Extraordinarily large/fast/clever/fearless/etc. horses - 10 Produce more than their share of spirit-talkers/hdours/ gouzadn - 11 Guard a greatly feared and cursed ruin/barrow/area - 12 Not actually nomadic; they stay in cliff-dwellings all year

763.title: Commonly available
763.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available among the Hillfolk—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky, potatoes, oats) are always in demand. Note that items printed on the Inventory insert are crude if acquired from the Hillfolk, and any iron items will instead be stone, bone, wooden, or maybe hammered copper. Iron items are considered special items (see below). goods value ----------------------------------------- ------- Salt (a little box) 0 ◇ Adz (hand, crude) 0 ◇ Cudgel, wood (close, crude) 0 Cartload of timber (immobile) 2 livestock value Goat (sure-footed, curious, hungry); 1 HP 3; Damage d4 (hand); Instinct to explore; can butcher for 4 provisions (6 uses) Sheep (timid, hardy, wooly) 1 HP 3; Damage d4 (hand); Instinct to follow the herd; can butcher for 4 provisions (6 uses) Sheep hound, follower (keen-nosed, 1 herder, fierce) HP 6; Damage d6 (hand, grabby); Instinct to bully, threaten; Cost training Horse, follower (powerful, keen-nosed, 3 swift or hardy, large); HP 10; Damage d6+3 (hand, close, forceful); Instinct to panic; Cost care & grooming

764.title: . trade opportunities .
764.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), a band of Hillfolk might offer the following: - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus. - A cartload of timber or a dozen sheep/goats (Value 2), in exchange for help with a problem. - Horses (Value 3) traded for a similar amount of Surplus (Value 2), plus help with a problem. - Someone versed in training and caring for horses, willing to move to Stonetop - An arcanum of the Stone Lords , the Barrow Builders , the spirits of the wild , or the Things Below .

765.title: Special items
765.body: The following might be acquired among the Hillfolk, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. This is in addition to the special items on the Moves & Gear handout. When the PCs Trade & Barter among the Hillfolk , they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage for being a hamlet . On a 6- , they don't mark XP and this band doesn't have/want it—maybe they know of another band who might? goods value ------------------------------------ ------- Iron items from inventory insert 0 Makerglass (misshapen shard/chunk) 1 Makerglass (an item made of it) +2 livestock value ----------------------------------------- ------- Huffel ram (hardy, wooly, powerful, 2 grumpy); HP 6; Damage d6+2 (hand, forceful); Instinct to protect the herd; can butcher for 4 provisions (9 uses) Upland horse, follower? (powerful, 3 keen-nosed, agile, brave, large); HP 14; Damage d6+3 (hand, close, forceful); Instinct to suffer no fools; Cost firm training

766.title: Services and favors
766.breadcrumb: Hillfolk › Trade & Barter › Services and favors
766.body: PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the Hillfolk—even if they offer something of suitable Value. A group that's well-disposed to the PCs might grant these boons for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price. service/favor value -------------------------------------------- ------- Safe passage through their territory 1 A few nights' hospitality in their camp 1 A guide through their territory 2 A guide through another band's territory 3 A guide to a cursed/sacred/forbidden place 3

768.title: Hdour
768.body: Solitary , cautious , devious , hoarder , magical , spirit-wise HP 14; Armor 2 (thick hides, minor wards) Damage bronze knife d8 ( hand ) Instinct to ignore rules, boundaries, and taboos - Call up a spirit to attend them - Send spirits or beasts to harass, spy, or attack - Reveal some spell or trick prepared in advance - Unleash power they cannot control Spirit-talkers (next page) have great power, but use it with care and only for the benefit of their people. There are many rules that they follow, many things they will refuse to do. Because to step over the line, and to seek power for power's sake, and to use their power selfishly—that is the way of the sorcerer, the hdour. When the Hillfolk suspect a spirit-talker of sorcery, they exile them. In the worst cases, they bury them alive. Few are willing to deal a death-blow to a hdour, for that's a sure way to get oneself cursed. Hdour are often in possession of one or more arcana , which they do not hesitate to use. They are often attended by beasts, spirits of the wild , spirits of the dead and undying , and minions of the Things Below .

769.title: Hillfolk rider
769.body: Group , stealthy , organized , horse-wise HP 6; Armor 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield) Damage flint-tipped spear d8 ( close , thrown , crude ), cudgel d8 ( close , crude ), adz d8 ( hand , crude ), or bow d8 ( near ) Instinct to distrust outsiders - Ride a steed like an extension of themselves - Outmaneuver their prey - Take advantage of the terrain A typical band of Hillfolk has between 6 to 12 riders: adult hunters and warriors. They patrol their territories in twos and threes, seeking game and keeping an eye out for trespassers. The brash and the bold will challenge anyone they don't recognize. More cautious riders will follow unseen and take the outsiders' measure; whether they let them pass, or spook them into leaving, or murder them in their sleep… well that remains to be seen. When a Hillfolk rider and their horse attack a foe together , they deal 1d6+4 damage (the horse's 1d6+3, with +1 for the rider), combining tags from the attacks as makes sense.

770.title: Spirit-talker
770.body: Group , cautious , cunning , magical , spirit-wise HP 8; Armor 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield) Damage flint-tipped spear d8 ( close , thrown , crude ), club or adz d8 ( hand , crude ), or bow d8 ( near ) Special quality sensitive to the presence of active spirits Instinct to protect their people - Sharply rebuke a spirit or mortal - Commune with beasts, or the spirits of a place or thing - Take advantage of the terrain - Borrow and wield a friendly spirit's power Now and then, a young Hillfolk shows signs of being touched by the spirits. The signs often come after a sickness or brush with death. They hear voices that aren't there. They find themselves more comfortable with horse and hound than their fellow people. Sometimes, they lose the human tongue altogether and go feral, becoming a gouzadn . But when things go right—when they are guided by an elder, and possessed of great self-control they become a spirit-talker for their band.

771.title: Upland horse
771.body: Group , large , powerful , keen-nosed , agile , brave HP 14; Armor 0 Damage kick, trample d6+3 ( hand , close , forceful ) Instinct to suffer no fools - Sniff trouble on the wind - Navigate tricky terrain or obstacles - Remain calm, unmoved - Find their own way Hillfolk bands breed their horses for strength, agility, and courage. The best become steeds for Hillfolk riders. It takes a firm hand and a steady will to handle an Upland, but win one over and you've got a friend for life.

772.title: Huffel Peaks
772.body: This range runs along the western edge of the Flats and the Steplands , connecting with the Whitefang Mountains in the north. They're not as tall and menacing as the Whitefangs, but they are jagged things of granite and basalt, home to a number of volcanoes, and rich in minerals and ore.

774.title: Always
774.body: - Slabs of jagged stone: impassive, looming, somehow still surprising - Tall thin trees (aspen, pines, firs) in the lower slopes and valleys; windswept junipers higher up - The stone-on-stone clink and thunk as rocks tumble down a slope - A sense of being surrounded, cut off

775.title: Spring
775.body: - Snow clinging to slopes and peaks, slowly melting - Pale green on the tips of branches; meadows bursting with wildflowers

776.title: Summer
776.body: - Too-bright sky as you step out of the shade - Lips cracking in a hot, dry breeze - Hot sun beating down on you while, somehow, you're getting rained on

777.title: Autumn
777.body: - Slopes and meadows of yellow and gold - A bitter chill in the now-damp air - Air pressure dropping as a storm rolls in

778.title: Winter
778.body: - Black stone peeking out from white - Trees drooping under snow - A sense of stillness, despite the constant wind

779.title: The World's End
779.body: Beyond the Huffel Peaks lies a vast expanse of water. But what lies between the mountains and the sea? That's for you and your group to decide, and the answer might vary depending on which direction you go through the mountains. But perhaps one might find... - … mighty cliffs, pounded by relentless waves; - … a gentle slope, a bountiful lowland, vast sandy beaches, a mild clime; - … a chain of islands, some tiny, some quite large; - … a wine-dark sea, the civilized lands of the south to be found at its farthest shores; - … deserted ruins of the Tempest Lords , the Forge Lords or both; - … the Ustrina , in numbers far greater than anyone would suspect; - … an isolated people, their ancestors a mix of refugees from when the Makers fell; - … colonies from the civilized lands of the South , eking out a living; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

780.title: Questions
780.body: - Who did you once meet who claimed to have reached the far side of the Huffel Peaks? Do you believe their stories about an endless body of water? - What legendary treasure is said to be hidden in those peaks? - How long has it been since one of the volcanoes erupted violently? How did it affect Stonetop/Gordin's Delve/the Hillfolk? - You don't really think that there are dragons in the Huffel Peaks, do you?

782.title: Hooks
782.body: - Prospectors find veins of cinnabar near Gordin's Delve, and set about mining the lucrative, toxic stuff - A draventao chases a Hillfolk band from their winter camp in the mountains - Scouts and warbands of an unknown people start to appear in the Huffel Peaks - A massive volcanic eruption throws ash across the region, causes strange sunsets and weather, spreads panic and superstition, and perhaps sets loose a powerful emanation of the Things Below The Things Below

783.title: Terrain
783.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

784.title: 1d12 terrain
784.body: - 1 Lava/hot springs/geysers/mudpots/fumaroles - 2 Crater/caldera/lava tubes/volcanic formations - 3 Stream/river/waterfall/lake/boggy soil - 4 Travertines/basalt columns or terraces/fairy chimneys/arches - 5-7 Sheer rock face/bluff/summit/peak - 8-9 Steep slope/switchback trails/ridgeline - 10 Ravine/canyon/gorge - 11 Gentle slope/meadow/valley/pass - 12 Cave, cavern, grotto If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

785.title: Discoveries
785.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

786.title: 1d6 discovery
786.body: - 1 A change of terrain (previous column) and roll again. - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 An entrance/exit to the Labyrinth - 4 An encounter (next page) - 5 A point of interest (next page) - 6 A natural resource (next page)

787.title: 1d6 encounter
787.body: - 1 Thraulgwyn : come out of the Labyrinth for some reason - 2 Spirit of the wild : curious or indifferent - 3-4 Ustrina : travelling, scrounging, on patrol - 5 Miners/prospectors/loggers, likely based out of Gordin's Delve ): wary, on edge - 6 Hillfolk : herding, hunting, encamped, or on patrol

788.title: 1d6 point of interest
788.breadcrumb: Huffel Peaks › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
788.body: - 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below - 2 A place haunted by death and the undying Death and the Undying - 3 A ruin or artifact of the Stone Lords , maybe claimed by the Barrow Builders - 4-5 A ruin or artifact of the Forge Lords , perhaps inhabited by Ustrina - 6 A place sacred to the spirits of the wild

789.title: 1d6 resource
789.body: - 1 Game, but dangerous to hunt, like a cave bear , fire drakes , or goats on an impossible slope - 2-3 Game, not dangerous but prone to bolt: a goat or deer, quail, rabbits, etc. - 4 Useful or valuable flora - 5 A safe place to shelter/a shortcut/an unexpected path - 6 A vein of ore/mineral deposit

791.title: Hazards
791.body: - Getting lost (maze of mountains, canyons, passes, and valleys) - Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it - Steep slopes, unstable footing (narrow paths, wet stone, scree, thin waterlogged soil, mud, crumbling rock) - Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes - An unexpected obstacle/hazard: go forward and risk it, or backtrack and lose half a day? - Mudslides and rockslides - Altitude sickness , especially near the peaks

792.title: Harsh mountain weather
792.breadcrumb: Huffel Peaks › Dangers › Harsh mountain weather
792.body: - Baking sun, dry air, lack of water - Fog banks/low clouds, blocking vision and soaking everything - Buffeting wind, drowning out voices and keeping you off-balance - Powerful gusts, threatening to toss you aside - Rain and wind rolling up from below - Rain and sleet pelting you, soaking you, threatening to wash you away - Thunder or lightning: making you flinch/jerk/jolt, triggering rockslides - Brutal cold and ceaseless wind, leading to hypothermia & frostbite - Snow and ice burying a pass or trail, making narrow paths impossible - Snow and ice building up, forming an avalanche

793.title: Volcanic activity
793.body: - Superheated air (d4 damage) or poisonous vapors (d4 damage, ignores armor), especially in enclosed spaces - Searing hot stones (d6 damage) - Steam vents/geysers (d8 damage, may be forceful ) - Scalding hot mud/water (d8 damage, ignores armor) - Lava flows (d10+2 damage, grabby , messy , 3 piercing)

794.title: Earthquake
794.body: - The ground shakes briefly, alarming but not dangerous - Powerful tremors make footing unstable, knock rocks loose, cause animals to panic - Tremors persist: people lose their footing, large rocks tumble (d8 damage, forceful ), trails and paths are damaged - It's a big one: mudslides and rockslides , avalanches , whole rockfaces come tumbling down

795.title: People
795.body: - Hillfolk - Ustrina - Laborers (miners, woodcutters) from Gordin's Delve - Folk from Barrier Pass or the Whitefang Mountains - Some southern adventurer or antiquarian , ruin-hunting - Thraulgwyn , come up from the Labyrinth

796.title: Monsters
796.body: - Predators: cave bears , cougars , kleztigr , spitting drakes - Frythanc , nesting or hunting over a wide valley/plateau - A gouzadn , living far from people - The ghosts and revenants of those who died up here, recently or in ages past - Spirits of the wild - Constructs left by the Forge Lords or the Stone Lords - Magma worms near volcanic features - A draventao , out for a rare hunt or squatting in some ruin

797.title: Caralandrao
797.body: Horde , small , stealthy , hoarder HP 3; Armor 2 (shell) Damage pincers d4 with disadvantage ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities spider-climb, camouflaged shell Instinct to feed on magic - Sense mystic emanations from afar - Get close without being noticed - Skitter-skatter, dart and weave - Make off with something magical Are they crabs? Spiders? Hard to say. Whatever they are, they've got too many too-long limbs, shells that blend in with the rocky terrain, and an appetite for magical artifacts. They'll drag their prizes back to their nests to feed. They can suck the power right out of minor trinkets, but powerful arcana can feed a small colony indefinitely. And yet, they keep venturing out to find more.

798.title: Fire drake
798.body: Group , aggressive , tough HP 10; Armor 2 (scales) Damage bite d8 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities fireproof; caustic, burning blood Instinct to get riled up easily - Sound off in a deep, threatening chorus - Blast a jet of burning, caustic blood from its eyes, d8 damage ( near , messy , reload , ignores armor), but lose 1d4 HP - Shake and worry and toss aside a foe it has bitten, d8+2 damage ( hand , forceful ) Picture a horned lizard, but big as a large boar and with a mouth full of short jagged teeth. They're often encountered basking near hot springs or mudpots or other geothermal vents, soaking up the heat until they swell and glow like embers. Of course, once they notice you, they stop basking and start attacking. Something interesting: Fire drake blood is caustic and catches fire when exposed to air; a jar of it deals d6 damage ( thrown , area , dangerous , ignores armor, Value 1), but good luck harvesting it. Something useful: The trick is to tap a vein with a metal tube, and drain the blood directly into a wax-sealed jar. Just… be careful.

799.title: Frythanc fledgling
799.body: Group , stealthy HP 6; Armor 0 (scales) Damage talons d8+2 (hand, forceful), beak d8 with disadvantage (hand) Special qualities exceptional eyesight; terrible hearing Instinct to eat and grow - Cling to rock faces, camouflaged - Strafe their prey - Knock prey off a ledge The full-grown frythancs that hunt the Flats are terrors. Their young, thankfully, keep to the mountains. There, they hunt goats and sheep and the occasional unwary traveler. The fledglings aren't nearly as dangerous as their full-grown parents, but still, let's see how you do when birds with 12-foot wingspans try to tear you off some switchback trail.

800.title: The Labyrinth
800.body: Head into the Delves that give Gordin's Delve its name, and go down. Keep going. Take a side passage, one with no miners in it. Go further still. Eventually, the Maker-hewn tunnels and the rough-cut mine shafts give way to natural caverns and lava tubes, twisting and turning and running for miles. The Labyrinth. These tunnels stretch out beneath the Huffel Peaks , occasionally connecting to the surface. Perhaps they stretch further, to the Whitefangs or the Steplands or even to the Great Wood . The Ustrina come out of the Labyrinth when they visit Gordin's Delve to trade. But when asked, they claim it is not their home. Other things come out of those tunnels, too, to prey on miners in the Delves. Survivors tell tales full of outlandish horror. Chief among those tales are the thraulgwyn , the "pale demons," white-haired raiders wielding weapons of bone and volcanic glass. But the Labyrinth holds more than horrors. It holds riches, too. Ore and gems, seams of coal, and ancient wonders of the Forge Lords. Riches enough to spur the occasional expedition, ill-fated though it may be.

801.title: Impressions
801.body: - The vast and hostile darkness, a quiet that sucks at the soul - The humbling scale of the tunnels - Air heavy with the smell of earth, stone, and sulfur - Smooth walls, ribbed walls, knobby walls, cracked walls - Distant dripping, clinking stone, footsteps, whispers, screams

802.title: Questions
802.body: - Which of you has seen the Labyrinth in your dreams? What do you recall? - Who entered the Labyrinth years ago, never to be seen again? - What legendary Forge Lord site is said to be down there? - Of all the tales that Delvers tell, of things coming out of the Labyrinth, which disturbs you most? - What do you think the thraulgwyn, the pale demons, really are?

803.title: Hooks
803.body: - Thraulgwyn raid a mining crew and drag off captives. - A prospector can lead the PCs to the Portal Vault , but plans to feed them to her sister's wraith once there. - Ustrina offer a reward for retrieving a relic from a ruin in the Labyrinth. - An old map of the Labyrinth shows a path from Gordin's Delve to Moratia Rubra (the Forge Lords' old capitol). - More and more Delvers hear a voiceless call from the Labyrinth. Some heed it. - A draventao emerges from its lair in the Labyrinth and preys on loggers, miners, and such.

804.title: Navigating the Labyrinth
804.body: The Labyrinth is vast and sprawling. In theory, one could travel these tunnels from Gordin's Delve to the far end of the Huffel Peaks and the sea that lies beyond. It'd be madness, but one could try. On any expedition into the Labyrinth: - You'll need plenty of light sources and more supplies than you'd think - You need a knowledgeable guide or an accurate map - Even then, you risk getting lost - You must surmount any number of tight squeezes and difficult climbs - The way is perilous, plagued with danger

805.title: LOST IN THE DARK
805.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › LOST IN THE DARK
805.body: When you lose your way in the Labyrinth , each member of your party consumes 1d4-1 supplies. Then, roll 2d6 once for the party and if… - … you all had enough supplies, add +1; - … you are led by someone wise to the Labyrinth's ways, add +1; - … you are stumbling around blind, roll with disadvantage; and/or - … one of you has the Mental Map move (from the Ranger playbook), roll with advantage. On a 10 +, you find your way to a portable light source, an exit from the Labyrinth, or a familiar path—GM's choice as to which; on a 7-9 , you encounter thraulgwyn , a Forge Lord ruin , or an obstruction The Labyrinth—GM's choice, and afterwards you might need to make this move again; on a 6- , decide as a group whether you find yourselves in the Deeps , or stumble out of the Labyrinth days or weeks later, with all your debilities marked and only 1d8 HP remaining. If you choose the latter, each PC must pick 1: - You have suffered a permanent injury or impairment—what and how? - A follower of yours didn't make it out—who and why? - You lost a treasured (or at least vital) item—what and how? - Actually, you didn't make it out after all—why not?

806.title: Terrain
806.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). 1d12 terrain --- --- 1-2 Lava tube or magma worm tunnel 3-4 Braided/branching tunnels 5-6 Chamber, cavern, or alcove 7-8 Steps, terraces, ledges 9-10 Forge Lord construction, worked stone passage, or ruin 11-12 obstruction (see below), and roll 1d10 again 1d12 obstruction --- --- 1-2 A cave-in (dig through?) 3-4 A squeeze (wriggle through?) 5-6 A sheer rock face, like the earth just shifted (climb?) 7-8 A shaft/chimney (climb/squeeze?) 9-10 A flooded chamber (wade, swim, find an exit underwater?) 11 A chasm/rift, your path crossing it (jump, belay, or find a bridge?) 12 Intense heat, foul air, brittle crust, magma flows (good luck) For any given tunnel or chamber, pick or have someone roll for 1-3 features. Consider rolling 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

807.title: 1d12 feature
807.body: 1d12 feature --- --- 1 Stream/spring/pool/lake 2 Stalactites/sharkteeth/knobby, ball-like stone protrusions 3 Rubble/debris/boulders (possibly fused into other stone) 4 Dome shaped cupola overhead 5 Hexagonal columns, packed tight 6 Apron/tongue/slipbank/gutters 7 Flat, cracked, a hardened lava lake 8 Steep slope/awkward ridges/undulating or ropy floor 9 Intense heat/cold 10 Cramped/narrow/low ceilings 11 Slippery mud/scree/oil/ooze 12 Glowmoths, glimmercaps, red crystal , other light sources

808.title: Discoveries
808.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

809.title: 1d6 discovery
809.body: - 1 A change of terrain (previous page) and roll again. - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 An encounter with thraulgwyn (see below) - 4 Some other encounter (next column) - 5 An opportunity (next column) - 6 A point of interest 1d6

810.title: 1d6 thraulgwyn
810.body: - 1 A whole tribal band, on the move - 2-3 A gang on patrol, wary but curious - 4 One or two, under attack by a rival band - 5 Foragers, eager to invite guests home - 6 Two groups in a tense parley See The Labyrinth for more on the thraulgwyn.

811.title: 1d6 other encounter
811.body: - 1 A thrall The Things Below, terribly marked, living down here all alone - 2 A swarm of small, cave-dwelling vermin—cave spiders, rockrats, or bats The Steplands—alarming and distracting, but essentially harmless - 3 A ghost Death and the Undying, revenant Death and the Undying, or spirit of the wild Spirits of the Wild: curious or indifferent - 4 A metal construct Forge Lords, following ancient orders or awaiting activation - 5 Adventurers/prospectors from Gordin's Delve, wary, maybe lost - 6 Ustrina Ustrina: surface-bound, returning to the Portal Vault 1d6, or seeking something

812.title: 1d6 opportunity
812.body: - 1 A good place to rest/hide/ambush foes - 2 Potable water, edible fungi, cave fish, rockrats, etc. - 3 Portable light source: glimmercaps, red crystal The Things Below, glowing makerglass Various treasures, etc. - 4 A vein of coal/ore/copper/gold/gems - 5 An artifact of the Forge Lords Forge Lords, Barrow Builders Barrow Builders, or Things Below The Things Below - 6 Sign of their objective/quarry

813.title: 1d6 point of interest
813.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
813.body: - 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below , perhaps a shrine tended by thraulgwyn or cultists - 2 A haunted site - 3 A notable site (see below) - 4 A thraulgwyn settlement - 5 A Forge Lord site , perhaps inhabited by Ustrina - 6 A path up and out into the Huffel Peaks , or down into the Deeps

814.title: Notable sites
814.body: - Heat, choking air, and a resonant rumble give way to a magma lake, air a-shimmer with heat, toxic gas. - A vast subterranean lake, perhaps the same one that connects to the Second Delve . - The Hanging Forest , a long, wide tunnel full of skinny roots dangling from the surface. Full of crickets and things that eat them. - The Mirrorkeep , a Forge Lord outpost, largely intact, lined with reflective mosaics. Sits over a bottomless (?) pit. - The Dreaming Breath , a hot spring pool. Mold and fungi cake nearby stone. The waters restore vigor but cause stupefying euphoria. - The Red Glutton, a horned, 40' tall, toadlike statue with an ever-burning brazier and fist-sized rubies (Value 4) for eyes. Revered by thraulgwyn. - The locked doors of the Portal Vault (next column), cold and imposing.

815.title: The Portal Vault
815.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Discoveries › The Portal Vault
815.body: Five hours from the Delves (if you know the shortest route) stands a pair of double doors: 30' tall, bronze, covered in runic wards and reliefs of Forge Lord glories. A cunning lock seals them. Only six keys exist; the Ustrina hold five. Inside is a vast octagonal space, 60' from floor to angled ceiling. Six of its corners feature 20' tall niches, each housing a large metal construct that attacks any party that doesn't bear one of the keys. A slab of white stone, 30' tall and 3' thick, stands upright in the center of the vault. Each side is etched with a magic circle. It's a portal , connected to those in the Ruined Tower , Three Coven Lake , Vor Svetelik , and so forth. It is fully functional, and can be used via Opening the Way . The Ustrina use this place to travel to Gordin's Delve, and to return to their far-flung homes. No one sect controls the Vault, and they treat it as neutral ground. But that doesn't apply to non-Ustrina.

816.title: Thraulgwyn
816.body: Their hair is white, their skin pallid and painted with lime and mud and blood. Their pupils are wide, like gaping holes. They file their teeth sharp, and craft their weapons from obsidian, stone, and bone. They tan human flesh for leather. The folks of Gordin's Delve know them as thraulgwyn, "pale demons," terrors that emerge from the Labyrinth and drag off friends and loved ones. Something interesting: The thraulgwyn descended from Barrow Builders , from the followers of certain warlords and sorcerer-kings who fled into the Labyrinth when their subjects rose against them. They were already depraved disciples of the Things Below, and after generations of inbreeding, cave-dwelling, and cannibalism, they're barely human. Something useful: Except, that's not really true. They have language (a dialect of the Hillfolk tongue) and outsiders have made deals with them. They hate other tribes more than they hate surface-dwellers. And while they do eat the dead, most of their food is foraged, even cultivated. Tip: Cock your head. Dart your eyes. Hold a hand up to block the light. Try not to touch your teeth with your tongue.

817.title: Names
817.body: Breton- or Cornish-inspired (after the Barrow Builders), but clipped: Aln, Az, Benez, Clem, Con, Dav, Demel, Gryf, Gwun, Humra, Iarn, Ilcar, Ilcu, Kyt, Lum, Mar, Mera, Nez, Rud, San, Tek, Wurg. Tribal bands are named "Uskl-" (the People of) and a truncated Barrow Builder name Barrow Builders: Uskl-Azel, Uskl-Judak, Uskl-Enor, Uskl-Renza, Uskl-Wioc, etc.

818.title: Tribal bands
818.body: Here's a typical tribal band: Size hamlet ( 25 to 75 souls) Population -1 to +1

819.title: Resources
819.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Thraulgwyn › Tribal bands › Resources
819.body: - Gathering (fungus, fish, water) - Farming (fungus, grubs, bugs) - Scavenging (Forge Lord sites) - Raiding (neighboring tribes) - Cannibalism

820.title: Defenses +0
820.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Thraulgwyn › Tribal bands › Defenses +0
820.body: - Warlike culture - Bone clubs/spears tipped with obsidian - Corrupted gifts - Magical artifacts Each tribal band descends from a particular warlord or sorcerer-king of the Barrow Builders and the disciples who followed them into exile. And each band is likewise corrupted by a different one of the Things Below. For each tribal band, pick or create the entity they worship . Pick or roll for a gift and a mark that entity bestows on their people. Then pick or roll an asset and a quirk. Finally, identify 1d6 artifacts of the Barrow Builders, Forge Lords , or Things Below that they hold as sacred treasures. 1d6 gift --- --- 1 Eat flesh to regenerate wounds 2 Soundless movement 3 Immunity to poison, fire, and cold 4 Brutal strength (+2 damage, forceful ) 5 Throat sac, exhales poison gas (d4, close , area , reload , debilitating ) 6 Wordless communication

821.title: 1d6 mark
821.body: - 1 No eyes (they echolocate) - 2 Too-long limbs, fingers, necks - 3 Covered in weeping sores - 4 Grotesque/horrific behaviors (even for thraulgwyn) - 5 Subject to hallucinations - 6 Fear open flame See The Things Below for more gifts and marks.

822.title: 1d6 asset
822.body: - 1 Metal weapons, lovingly cared for - 2 Means to brew a paralytic poison - 3 An additional gift - 4 Greater numbers (a village , 150 souls) - 5 A pet emanation - 6 Still led by their deathless founder, and roll again

823.title: 1d6 quirk
823.body: - 1 Hubristic and overconfident - 2 Bear an additional mark - 3 Build structures out of bones - 4 Venture out to the surface at night - 5 Revere magma worms - 6 Intrigued by surface-dwellers

824.title: Trade & Barter
824.body: The PCs must be on civil terms with a group of thraulgwyn before any of this matters.

825.title: Commonly available
825.body: The following would be special items in Stonetop, but they are commonly available among the thraulgwyn. The PCs can acquire them here without rolling, but need to trade something of equal Value. Meat, clean water, and whisky are always in demand, as are durable goods and tools. But thraulgwyn have little use for coin or luxuries, and are suspicious of most other surface food. goods value ------------------------------------------ --------- Set of bone tools (needles, hooks, etc.) 0 Pouch of hallucinogenic spores (○ use) 0 ◇ Supplies (○○○ uses, crude , unsavory ) 0 Corpse-wax candle (last 1 hour, close ) 1 ◇ Bone or stone weapon ( crude ) 1 Glimmercap oil (○○○○ hours, hand , crude ) 1 Elaborately scrimshawed bone item +1 -1 if sold/traded outside the Labyrinth.

826.title: Special items
826.body: The following might be acquired from or traded to the thraulgwyn, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to do so. When PCs Trade & Barter among a band of thraulgwyn , they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage because it's a hamlet . On a 6- , they don't mark XP—this band simply doesn't have or want what the PCs are after/selling. goods value --- --- Red crystal "candle" ( hand , magical ) 1 Vial of paralytic powder (○ use, close ) 1 ◇ Meat provisions, don't ask (○○○○○ uses) 1 ◇◇ Human-hide armor (1 armor) 1 ◇ pouch of anthracite ( 10 lumps, each lasts 8 hours, close , area ) 2 ◇ Red crystal "torch" ( reach , area , magical ) 3 Pottery, wool, textiles, wooden items +1 Bronze, iron, or steel items/weapons +1 An object of black iron Primordial Powers +2 Scavenged makerglass Various treasures +2 -1 if sold/traded outside the Labyrinth. Thraulgwyn are always willing to accept people or corpses in trade: a living wretch or a dodgy corpse is Value 1, a healthy specimen is Value 2, a big healthy surface dweller might be Value 3.

827.title: Services and Favors
827.body: PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they will likely need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. service/favor value ------------------------------------------ --------- A raid against a rival tribe 0 Directions to a spot in the Labyrinth 1 A day's food and shelter 1 A guide for a few days 2 A temporary truce with a rival tribe 2 Directions to a forbidden or sacred site 3 Ritual to commune with a Thing Below 3

828.title: The Deeps
828.body: The Labyrinth is far from static. Earthquakes, volcanism, and burrowing magma worms all wreak havoc upon it. But you could map it. You could learn its ways and find your way back to a place you had been. But as one goes deeper, things change. The world becomes… softer. Less fixed. And beyond an uncertain point, one enters the Deeps. The Deeps shift when you aren't looking. They reflect your fears and your desires. They seek to trap you, to twist you, to consume you. For it is here, of all the places in the world, that one is closest to the Things Below. Why would anyone come here? Perhaps… - … they are called by the Things Below; - … they are lost and didn't mean to come; - … to rescue someone; - … to find an artifact of legend; - … to hide something away forever; and/or - … to do the impossible: to face and truly slay one of the Things Below.

829.title: Impressions
829.body: - Endless tunnels, going and going - Thoughts drifting as you trudge along, jerking awake - The darkness, deeper and absolute - When did you last eat, drink, or rest? - Wait, who are these people again? - Dreamlike acceptance of whatever weirdness you encounter

830.title: Arriving
830.body: When the PCs first arrive, describe their endless slog using the impressions above. Intersperse your description with questions like these: - What pleasant detail about the surface are you struggling to recall? - What do you fear you'll find here? - What nightmare keeps replaying in your mind? - Who'd be better off if you never made it home? - When did you start suspecting that \ \ isn't really who they say they are? After setting the mood, present a nightmare tableau (below). If the PCs escape it, they are then traversing the Deeps (next page).

831.title: Nightmare tableaux
831.body: Pick or roll for the terrain , including features, and combine with a theme of the Things Below . Pick or roll for the crux of the tableau and its instinct. Create a scene or situation that combines these elements, riffing on whatever internal conflicts the PCs are dealing with.

832.title: 1d6 crux
832.body: - 1 Another traveler, trapped here - 2 Details pulled from the PCs' minds - 3 Simulacra of known NPCs, convinced that they are real - 4 Simulacra of the PCs, convinced they are real - 5 An artifact of the Things Below, the tableau's heart - 6 Roll twice, combine

833.title: 1d6 instinct
833.body: - 1 To traumatize/punish them - 2 To weaken/hurt/degrade them - 3 To corrupt them The Things Below - 4 To turn them against each other - 5 To trap or ensnare them - 6 To coalesce into a full-fledged emanation Play out the scene with dreamlike logic but very real potential consequences for the PCs. Make liberal use of whispers and visions and unnatural phenomena . Should a simulacrum leave the tableau with the PCs, decide for yourself what happens; don't be afraid to get weird.

834.title: Traversing the Deeps
834.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Impressions › Traversing the Deeps
834.body: Time flows strangely down here. Visitors don't really need food or drink. Sleep is fleeting. Light sources might last indefinitely, only to snuff out at the worst moment. Distances are fluid, dreamlike. Those who wander without a purpose are sure to be trapped, or worse.

835.title: SEEK OUT
835.body: When you seek something in the Deeps , whoever leads the way rolls +WIS. Unless they bear a holy light, they roll with disadvantage. On a 10+ , pick 1 from the list below; on a 7-9 , encounter a danger, but gain +1 Progress; on a 6- , lose all Progress and encounter a danger. - Encounter a danger, but gain +2 Progress - You find a reprieve, a pocket of normalcy and a safe place to Make Camp When you encounter a danger , roll the Die of Fate to see what it is. The PCs get lost in the Labyrinth, one of their followers dying in the process. Now they've wandered into the Deeps, full of guilt and recrimination. For their first tableau, I roll a 7 for terrain ("steps, terraces, ledges") and an 11 for a feature ("slippery mud/scree/oil/ooze"). For a theme, I get 12: "Death/undeath/loss/grief… (skulls, tombs, pyres)." Looking at the crux list, I just pick "simulacra of known NPCs," and I roll a 5 for instinct ("to trap or ensnare them"). The tunnel opens to a cavern of everwider terraces, sloping down out of sight, covered with scree and rubble. But the scree isn't stone, it's bones and skulls, who knows how deep? If they slip, the bones cut them, drag them down, try to bury them. As they cross, that dead follower starts calling for help, trapped in the bone-rubble, and don't you dare leave me again, you bastards!

836.title: 1d6 danger
836.body: - 1 Tcaventes , wandering - 2 An emanation - 3 A huge magma worm - 4 A nightmare tableau (previous page) - 5 An ordeal: heat, cold, hunger, intrusive thoughts, false memories, etc. - 6 Someone else lost down here, maybe from a different time, paranoid or at least extremely wary When you escape or otherwise survive the danger and you hold 3+ Progress, you find your goal. If you continue to seek your current goal, keep your Progress and make this move again. If you choose a new goal, lose all Progress.

837.title: The Utterwell
837.body: At the very bottom of the Deeps stands a throne of black iron , the seat of Tcaventes, Shackle and Key . Behind the throne looms a black iron door. An orichalcum bolt holds the door fast. The bolt is bound with the Ineffable Word for "seal." The black iron door opens onto the shore of the Utterwell: a sunless sea, still and black as night. No light penetrates its depths, not even the light of Helior. Anyone or anything who enters the sea is gone, consumed by the Things Below. When you stand before the Utterwell and call one of the Things Below by name , it will emerge in all its terrible glory. Woe be upon you. And if the door is still open, woe be upon the world.

839.title: Hazards
839.body: - Getting lost - Running out of light - Cold, especially if they get wet - Heat, gas, volcanic activity - Treacherous footing, slopes, climbs - Getting stuck in a squeeze - An unexpected drop-off - Cave-ins, rock-falls - Earthquakes! - Panic in the face of the above

840.title: Monsters and such
840.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Dangers › Monsters and such
840.body: - Ghosts and revenants of those who never got out - Metal constructs , guarding something or on some task - Caralandrao , seeking magic - Fire drakes , near magma - A grochslon , near a stream, lake, or cave mouth - Explorers: adventurers , an antiquarian , a sorcerer , etc. - Ustrina , en route or in search of something - A draventao , making its lair down here - Corrupted beings : bats, rockrats, spirits, any of the above - Emanations of the Things Below, seeping out of the Deeps

841.title: Corruption, as one goes deeper
841.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Dangers › Corruption, as one goes deeper
841.body: - Disquiet and unease - Whispers and visions , coming from below - Unnatural phenomena , in or near the Deeps

842.title: Magma worm
842.body: Solitary , primordial , large , terrifying HP 19; Armor 4 (rocky plates, lacks vitals) Damage bite d10+3 ( hand , close , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) or searing touch d10 ( hand , area ) Special qualities blind; tremorsense; fireproof; superheated body; can move only forward, can't back up Instinct to devour minerals and metal - Eat through solid rock - Burst forth from a tunnel wall - Exude choking, sulfurous gas - Melt metal that pierces it Creatures of the first age, things of fire and stone. They're segmented, 3' thick and 20' long, covered in overlapping plates that glow red at the seams. The air heat-shimmers around them. They have no eyes, just a great mouth-opening and concentric rows of grinding teeth that melt and burrow through rock. Winding tunnels stretch behind them, lined with slag hardened into ropy stone. The worms described here are mere centuries old, the ones most likely to be encountered. Older and larger ones survive in the Deeps, up to 10' wide and over a 100' long ( huge , +6 HP, +2 damage and roll 1d12 instead of 1d10). In eons past, even larger worms carved out much of the Labyrinth.

843.title: Thraulgwyn raider
843.body: Group , corrupted , stealthy , organized , cautious HP 6; Armor 0 or 1 (human hide) Damage obsidian-tipped bone spear d8 ( close , thrown , crude ), femur d8 ( close , crude ), hair-rope net ( thrown , crude , grabby ), bite d6 ( hand ) Special qualities gifted and marked according to their tribe 1d6 Instinct to glorify their tribe - Use their gift - Move quietly through the dark - Lure prey into an ambush - Pounce with sudden ferocity - Flee if things go south They roam the tunnels in gangs of 3-5, playing cat and mouse with rival bands. Should they notice outsiders, they might try to ambush them, capture them, and eat them. But they might also greet them, offer to trade, try to use them against their foes. Some raiding parties are led by a champion ( leader , +2 HP, deals damage with advantage), who bears one of their tribal band's sacred artifacts.

844.title: Soithech
844.body: Solitary , corrupted , devious , magical , cautious HP 18; Armor 5 (resilience, human hide), 1 vs. bronze Damage obsidian knife d10 ( hand , crude ) Special qualities powerfully gifted and marked, as per their tribe 1d6 Instinct to maintain their status - Foresee an opportunity or dire event - Play parties against each other - Use their gift, or one of their tribe's sacred artifacts - Embody an aspect of their master Most thraulgwyn settlements are ruled by a soithech, a "vessel," who claims descent from the tribe's founder. They preach old ways and old hatreds, keeping their people in line with prophecies of glory and doom—some of which are even true. A soitech's power is not just political. They manifest their tribe's gift and mark with great intensity, and they can let the power of their patron Thing Below flow through them. When they do, they change to embody one of its aspects The Things Below and gain up to 3 additional gifts The Things Below. Adjust their stat block accordingly.

845.title: Tcaventes, Shackle and Key
845.breadcrumb: The Labyrinth › Tcaventes, Shackle and Key
845.body: Solitary , large , primordial , construct , magical , terrifying , corrupted HP 25; Armor 6 (hard, warded, lacks vitals), 4 vs. black iron Damage word of force d12+3 ( near , area , forceful ) or four fists d12+3 with advantage ( close , reach , forceful ) Special qualities ageless; immune to the elements; perceives thought and matter; omnilingual Instinct to hide evidence of their failure, even from themselves - Sense and neutralize threats with cold precision - Shift the environment to create or close an opening - Seal or unseal: a door, a binding, a mouth, a secret, etc. - Blaze suddenly with the blinding light of creation They are an archon , forged by the First Creators to stand vigil over the Utterwell and to act as a bulwark against the Things Below. They are Tcaventes, Shackle and Key. A flawless guardian. Or at least, they were. Over eons, even they succumbed to the whispers of the Things Below. The corruption leads to lapses of discipline that let their charges' influence seep out through the Deeps. It amplifies their guilt, which leads to denial strong enough to drive Tcaventes mad. Should the PCs encounter Tcaventes on their throne, they will find the archon focused, vigilant, and stern, with only a hint of their unravelling mind. But should the PCs encounter Tcaventes elsewhere in the Deeps, the archon will be wandering aimlessly, speaking to themselves in halting tones, struggling to hold a thought, in turns imperious and self-castigating. The PCs will need to walk a razor's edge to avoid drawing down their wrath.

846.title: Lygos and the South
846.body: Picture a sun-drenched land, a semi-arid climate. Roads stretch out between cities and oases. Farmers tend to orchards of figs, dates, and olives. Boundless fields of grain sway in gentle winds. Picture palaces, temples, and handsome estates. Priests, scholars, and bureaucrats bustle about. Politicians squabble over laws and votes. Merchants hawk goods from the far corners of the world. Dozens of languages can be heard in streets and taverns. Picture armies on the march in tight formation, spears and shields and helms a-glinting. Plumes of dust rise from thousands of stamping feet. Horses neigh and harnesses clatter. Camels bray. Elephants trumpet. Picture opulence and splendor, silks and feathers, spices piled high in lurid color. Picture hunger and desperation, urchins risking a hand to steal a loaf of bread. Picture the rich and powerful above mundane concern. Picture the poor and downtrodden consumed by nothing but.

847.title: Names
847.body: Inspired by Greek, Hebrew, Persian, and Arabic: Agatte, Aref, Alix, Baraz, Canan, Darya, Demetra, Elene, Elios, Fotios, Faruza, Golza, Iasos, Iona, Kyriakos, Marika, Maayan, Osher, Natasa, Nivola, Rinat, Stamat, Thecla, Zhaleh.

848.title: Questions
848.body: - Do they fear deep water down south? - Do they ply the seas or shun them? - What are the most common languages down there? - What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to Lygosi? To southerners in general? - What festival or holiday do they celebrate this time of year? - What game/pastime/food are Lygosi/southerners known for? - Do they worship \ \ ? How does that worship differ from up here in Stonetop? - Who (or what) do they worship that isn't well-known up here?

849.title: Personal questions
849.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › Questions › Personal questions
849.body: If a PC hails from the south, ask some or all of the following. - Are you from Lygos proper? Or some where else down south? - What little thing do you miss about life down there? - What do you miss the least? - What drove you to leave? - Who did you leave behind? - What southern practice do you still observe? - What was most surprising to you about life in these parts? - Are there other southerners in Stonetop? Who?

850.title: A storied past
850.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › A storied past
850.body: The arid south has long been a cradle of civilization. Ruins and ancient works dot the landscape and clutter cities. Who built them, and where did those people go? That's for you and your group to decide, but perhaps these structures were built by… - … a truly ancient people, predating even the Makers; - … the Tempest Lords , before their island capital was swallowed by the sea; - … the ancestors of the Stone Lords , who later migrated north; - … an offshoot of the Forge Lords , spreading out through a network of portals; - … some other group of Makers , unheard of up north; - … ancient humans—contemporaries of the Makers, perhaps even their rivals; - … offshoots of the Barrow Builders , who forged human civilization from blood and fire and sand; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

851.title: Hooks
851.body: - Catastrophe (war, plague, famine, etc.) sends refugees flooding north. - A deposed royal flees to the north incognito, pursued by assassins. - An old foe/rival/creditor of a PC (or an important NPC) comes calling. - A southern antiquarian arrives, looking to excavate a specific Maker-ruin (possibly one in Stonetop). - Members of a persecuted mystery cult arrive from down South; if Stonetop won't accept them, they take up residence in the Ruined Tower .

852.title: There and back again
852.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › There and back again
852.body: A typical trip to and from Lygos involves: - An entire season of travel (80-90 days), there and back. - Joining a merchant caravan for the trip there, and another one for the trip back (Value 2 each way). - While in Lygos, either… - … a purse of silvers (Value 2) to pay for room, board, and bribes; or - … a resident of Lygos willing to put you up and cover your costs. - The risk of misadventure, robbery, betrayal, or sundry other setbacks at every turn. Don't play out trips to Lygos in detail! Doing so would take dozens of sessions and could easily derail your campaign. Unless you and your group actively decide to leave Stonetop behind and play a very different sort of game, then you should handle PC trips to Lygos off-camera. Use love letters to gloss over such a journey. Work with the player(s) to identify: - What they hope to accomplish - How they plan to go about it - What valuables/resources they bring with them Think about what might go wrong, what might go well, and what obligations or simmering troubles they're leaving behind. Think about elements of the setting or character that you want to explore or highlight. Weave these together into a love letter for each PC who makes the journey, and present it to each player when their PC returns to Stonetop. If some PCs remained behind while the others went to Lygos, you can write love letters for the PCs who stayed behind, summarizing events back home. Or, you can play one or more sessions with just those players. Or you can replace the travelling PCs with temporary PCs for a few sessions. Work with your group to decide on the best approach.

853.title: Merchants
853.body: A few dozen caravans make their way up from Lygos every year. A typical merchant caravan is made up of: - 3-12 separate merchants, each with at least one wagon or cart - 2-4 helpers per merchant (often family members) - Horses, mules, donkeys, or other beasts of burden - Caravan guards , 6 per merchant Most caravans stop in in Marshedge to trade, but some enterprising souls will veer into the Steplands and trade with the Hillfolk directly. Others keep going north to one of the Manmarcher's hillforts . A rare few merchants make their way to Gordin's Delve , and some of those stop in Stonetop.

854.title: . trade opportunities .
854.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › Merchants › . trade opportunities .
854.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), a southern merchant might offer the following: - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus. - 1-4 Value 2 items or even a Value 3 item, in exchange for some creepy arcanum or relic of the past (might have repercussions) - A unit of exotic trade goods (Value 3) in exchange for a bundle of drake feathers , gylglyd flowers , a raselbaedd hide , etc. - A suit of armor or weapon of war, forged from black iron , as payment for helping them recover some lost or stolen goods (Value 3). - An arcanum of primordial power , from one of the Makers , or related to the Things Below .

856.title: Adventurer
856.body: Group , hoarder , devious , [pick 1 more] HP 8; Armor 1 to 3 (skill, armor, and/or shield) Damage whatever's at hand d8 ( hand , close , maybe forceful or messy ) Instinct to stir up trouble - Sniff out an opportunity, or reveal one - Escape danger in a way that endangers others - Act without scruple - Use a particular knack or skill They make their way north now and then: scoundrels, mercenaries, treasure-hunters… folk who just can't make a go of a normal life. They'll show up in Marshedge or Stonetop or Gordin's Delve, in search of some big score. Or running from the consequences of the last one. Or both.

857.title: Antiquarian
857.body: Solitary , hoarder , cautious , cunning , magical HP 12; Armor 0 Damage iron mattock d8 w/disadvantage ( close , messy , awkward ) or knife d6 w/disadvantage ( hand ) Special qualities often in possession of arcana (page 508\) and willing to use them Instinct to indulge their curiosity or to secure the next big score - Expound at length upon a topic - Open, touch, or tinker with something - Use a minor spell or artifact - Hang back and let the help face the danger Among the southern well-to-do, the glorious past is a point of pride. Wealthy households keep pet scholars on retainer, and sponsor the study of ruins and antiquities. There's a brisk trade in artifacts and relics. Collectors pay handsomely for items of true power. The south is full of ruins, but it's also teeming with people. The best sites have already been dug up. But up north, at the World's End? Why, one can barely swing a shovel without hitting some relic of the past, just waiting to be excavated. And studied. And sold.

858.title: Assassin
858.body: Solitary , stealthy , devious HP 12; Armor 1 (skill) Damage dagger 1d10 ( hand , 1 piercing) or garrote d8 ( hand , grabby , ignores armor) Instinct to finish the job (nothing personal) - Stalk their target at length - Pass unnoticed, in shadow or disguise - Poison someone - Slip away, try again later Only in "civilized" lands is there such demand for murder that it's an actual profession.

859.title: Caravan guard
859.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › Caravan guard
859.body: Horde , organized , cautious , alert HP 3; Armor 2 (shield, leather cuirass) Damage iron spear d6 (close, thrown) or dagger d6 (hand) Professional instinct to keep their ward(s) safe - Spot or suspect trouble - Take sensible precautions - Put themselves in harm's way Craven instinct to avoid real danger - Suggest a safer course of action - Make a show of force - Flee, surrender, or betray their client Much of the way between Lygos and Marshedge goes through trackless wilds, without the protection of the Makers' Roads . Bandits, beasts, and Hillfolk are all a threat, and plenty of potential clients would just as soon steal a merchant's goods as trade fairly for them. So, merchants hire guards. And when danger looms, they hope they've hired well.

860.title: Conquering army
860.breadcrumb: Lygos and the South › Caravan guard › Conquering army
860.body: The armies of the south are said to be as limitless as the stars in the sky, as countless as the leaves in the Great Wood. If they wanted, they could field legions whose soldiers outnumbered the entire population of the World's End. Thankfully, the southern nations have seen no value in invading the north. At least, not yet. What would prompt an invasion? Perhaps… - … a holy mandate to spread some southern faith; - … an emperor who has united the southern nations, and now turns toward new conquests; - … a new ruler in Lygos, obsessed with recovering the Makers' arts; - … a dearth of arable land, timber, salt, or just living space; - … an unquenchable thirst for slaves (or sacrifices); - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

861.title: Invasion
861.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to subjugate - ☐ A company (200-400 troops) establishes a foothold in the South Manmarch , proceeds to subjugate local Hillfolk - ☐ 2,000 soldiers march on Marshedge , their council surrenders, Brennan and the Claws flee - ☐ The army invades the North Manmarch , conquering a number of settlements, hillforts - ☐ Remaining Manmarch chiefs surrender; a company of soldiers besieges Gordin's Delve ; another marches on Stonetop and demands surrender - ☐ Impending doom: Stonetop is conquered and a garrison established Stakes Can the Hillfolk be united against the southerners? What about the Manmarchers? Will the PCs ally with Brennan?

862.title: Cavalry
862.body: Horde , large , organized , terrifying HP 10; Armor 2 (shield, iron scale) Damage iron-tipped long spear d6+4 ( reach , forceful ), or sword d6+4 ( close , forceful ), or bow d6 ( far ) Instinct to attack where the foe is weak - Circle around their enemy - Attack on the move - Form a wedge and charge, d6+4 w/ advantage ( reach , close , hand , area , forceful , messy , 1 piercing)

863.title: Infantry
863.body: Horde , organized HP 3; Armor 2 (shield, linen cuirass) Damage iron-tipped long spear d6 ( reach ), or short sword d6 ( hand , close ) Instinct to follow orders - Form a phalanx - Pin down or hem in their foe

864.title: Legionary
864.body: Horde , organized , hardy , disciplined ^07ec58 HP 7; Armor 3 (shield, iron scale) Damage iron short sword d6 ( hand , close ), or spear d6 ( close , thrown ) Instinct to conquer - Fight in tight-knit formation - Hold the line - Advance implacably ^invasion-legionary

865.title: Skirmisher
865.body: Horde , stealthy , organized HP 3; Armor 0 Damage long bow d6 ( far ), or sling d6 ( near , reload ), or short swords ( hand , close ) Instinct to avoid a fair fight - Unleash a volley, then retreat - Cover distance quickly - Attack a flank

866.title: The Makers
866.body: These lands were once ruled by giants, who stood two or three times the height of a full-grown human. They were powerful not only in stature but also in learning, wisdom, and magic. They established mighty civilizations and left many wonders in their wake. Five different groups of Makers helped to shape the region now called World's End. These included… - … the Forge Lords , passionate artisans who bent fire and metal to their will; - … the Green Lords , who bred and shaped all forms of life and bound the spirits of the wild to their will; - … the Rime Lords , who embraced the cold of the far north and practiced all manner of ascetic disciplines and esoteric arts; - … the Stone Lords , who carved cities out of bedrock, raised towering monuments, and sang stone and crystal and glass into impossible shapes; and - … the Tempest Lords , who harnessed the primal forces of sky and sea, who sailed forth to conquer much of the world. The Tempest Lords' empire was the oldest and greatest nation of Makers, and for a long time the others lived in their shadow. But hubris led to the empire's ruin, and allowed the other Makers to rise in power. There were rivalries between them, certainly, and border conflicts, but for centuries they largely lived in peace. They traded goods and ideas, even cooperated on grand projects like the Makers' Roads . The peace did not last forever. The Green Lords called on the Things Below to crush their rebellious servants, a crime for which the Stone Lords would not stand. They led their brethren against the now-corrupted Green Lords, shattered their lands and tried to wipe them from the earth. They succeeded, but at great cost. For the whispers of the Things Below had become a song. And over the decades, the Stone Lords' own human servants were themselves corrupted. In a flurry of violence, they broke the seals and turned against their masters, and ushered in the Time of Cataclysm . The power of the Things Below was loosed upon the world, and one-by-one the remaining Makers fell. That was a few centuries ago, at least. But the bones of those civilizations remain.

867.title: Hooks
867.body: - A still-living Maker is found, vitrified or caught in some Fae domain or otherwise held in stasis. - Someone unearths a stele, with a lengthy decree written in multiple Maker-scripts as well as an archaic but well-known—southern alphabet. - Two rival scholars are willing to kill in order to prove their theories about the Makers are correct. - Adventurers discover a ruin or tomb, clearly of the Makers, but bearing symbols and decorations unlike those of any of the known Makers. Perhaps there was a sixth type or nation of Makers?

869.title: Everyone knows:
869.body: - Giants built most of the ruins that dot the landscape and on which the settlements in the region were built - The Highway and the West Road were built by these Makers - There were different groups of Makers, dwelling in different regions - They worked powerful magic, some of which persists today

870.title: One might know:
870.breadcrumb: The Makers › Lore › One might know:
870.body: - The different types of Makers, where each dwelt, their most important themes - What happened to each group, broadly - The distinct features of each group's writing/architecture/artifacts - The special materials—like aetherium or makerglass —associated with each group, and their common properties - The names of a few individuals - Which peoples served/followed/were created by them - How to use/replicate some of their magic - How to read their writing The Makers

871.title: Very few know:
871.breadcrumb: The Makers › Lore › Very few know:
871.body: - The nuances of their politics, rivalries, history, and schisms - How to speak their languages - How to recreate their special materials - The underpinnings of their magic When a PC Knows Things about the Makers and gets a 7+, be sure to ask, "How do you know this?" If they're stuck for plausible answers, then give them suggestions: a folk tale, a children's song, secrets imparted by a wise old mentor, a tale heard from a merchant or an old prospector, some previous adventure, etc.

872.title: Questions
872.body: - In their art, how did these Makers depict themselves? How were they different than humans, beyond just their size? - Can Maker-ruins be found in Lygos and the South ? If so, how are they different than those often found around here? - Who is the authoritative scholar on the(se) Makers? When were they active? On what did they base their research? What did they clearly get wrong? - Which of the Makers' scripts are most like the common alphabet(s) used today? - How can you tell that these markings are magical, not mere writing?

873.title: Sites
873.body: Signs of these ancient civilizations litter the region, many of them surprisingly well-preserved. Some of these ruins are familiar and widely used today, but many are forgotten, avoided, or shunned. For while they contain many wonders of the past, they also hold ancient dangers. Plenty of them are haunted , or corrupted by the Things Below . Create a site based on the type(s) of Makers who left it. Pick the creators based on where the site is located, or roll if you're unsure.

874.title: 1d6 MAKERS & SITE LOCATIONS
874.breadcrumb: The Makers › Sites › 1d6 MAKERS & SITE LOCATIONS
874.body: - 1 The Forge Lords : - The Huffel Peaks Huffel Peaks - Gordin's Delve Gordin’s Delve - Western edge of the Flats The Flats - 2 The Green Lords : - The Great Wood The Great Wood - Ferrier's Fen Ferrier’s Fen - The Foothills The Foothills - 3 The Rime Lords : - The Whitefang Mountains The Whitefang Mountains - The Foothills The Foothills - 4 The Stone Lords : - The Steplands Spring - The South Manmarch South Manmarch - South arm of the Huffel Peaks Huffel Peaks - Southern edge of the Flats The Flats - 5 The Tempest Lords : - Along the Dread River - The Flats The Flats - The Ruined Tower The Ruined Tower - Anywhere you want (their sky-islands flew far and wide) - 6 Multiple Makers: - Along the Makers' Roads The Makers’ Roads - Along the Dread River - Along borders between regions

875.title: True nature
875.body: The Makers were large and physically powerful, adept with magic, quick of mind, and they brimmed with elemental might. Individuals varied greatly in both personal power and prestige. They were long-lived but far from immortal, and reproduced slowly (if at all). Their numbers, thus, were relatively few, and they surrounded themselves with slaves, servants, and disciples of smaller stature: humans, Fae , Forest Folk , Ustrina , etc. Beyond that, the nature of the Makers is for you to decide. Perhaps they were… - … among the first sentient beings, placed here by the primordial powers ; - … children of Danu , birthed from the elements themselves; - … humans who elevated themselves through magic and study; - … immigrants from across a trackless sea; - … travelers from another world or dimension; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely. The answer might well be different for each group of Makers. Maybe the Tempest Lords were among the first sentient beings and the other Makers were once their human subjects, who stole their power as the first empire waned and fell. Or maybe the Forge Lords came from another world, but the Stone Lords were born of the hills and mountains.

876.title: Runes & glyphs
876.body: Ancient writing often adorns Maker-ruins and artifacts. Most of these runes and glyphs are mundane (names, labels, memorials, directions, warnings, etc). But some Maker-marks brim with power. They bind spirits, channel energy, anchor magical effects. Many minor arcana (page 508\) feature such writing, which PCs must decipher in order to unlock the arcana's secrets. If a PC is literate and has some justification (such as the Well Versed move with "the Makers and their arts" as a topic), then they can Know Things to decipher Maker runes and glyphs: on a 10+ , tell them the full meaning; on a 7-9 , give them a vague sense of the text's meaning, or a few possible translations, and a way to learn more; on a 6- , maybe tell them what's required to translate the text, and/or interrupt the scene with trouble. Other PCs can Know Things about Maker runes and glyphs, but at best they can discern a few words and recognize the magical vs. the mundane. Focus instead on who could help them translate the runes, or who might find them valuable, or places and artifacts that bear similar markings, and what the PC knows about them. If the PCs can't immediately decipher the Markings, then maybe they could… - … show the writing to a learned elder back home; - … Spend a few weeks referencing their collection of Books and Scrolls (if they have one); - … confer with an antiquarian , probably in Gordin's Delve or Marshedge ; - … convince the monks up in Barrier Pass to help; - … seek aid from a spirit-talker or hdour ; - … make a deal with a spirit of the wild , Fae , Ustrina , etc.; - … find an intact Green Lord tomb and beseech its mummified occupant to help; or - … conjure the shade of a Maker or Barrow Builder , perhaps at the Crossroads , and bargain for their service.
