The Tempest Lords used ideographic glyphs (ideas, not sounds). The other Makers' writing was phonetic; just how distinct their alphabets were is up for you and your group to decide.

877.title: The Makers' Roads
877.body: The West Road stretches from Stonetop to Gordin's Delve . About 15 miles from town, it crosses the Highway, which runs from Barrier Pass all the way to Marshedge and beyond. The roads are wide ( 20 feet), grand, and humbling, clearly the work of the Makers . They have lasted for centuries, strangely well-preserved and free of growth. - Big slabs of tiled basalt, gently beveled, stretching to the horizon - Trickle of rainwater or snowmelt flowing down gutters that never clog - The unnerving way that dust, seeds, leaves, etc. refuse to land on the pavestones Tip: speak quietly, reverently, like you were in a church or temple among the faithful.

878.title: Questions
878.body: - Which of you have travelled the Highway or the West Road before? When and to where? - The magic of the roads—the wards against violence, evil, and decay—how do they make each of you feel? - When was the last time that you felt small, insignificant, or just very lonely?

879.title: The magic of the Roads
879.breadcrumb: The Makers' Roads › The magic of the Roads
879.body: The roads have lasted for centuries without maintenance. Predators shun them. Beings of darkness, chaos, and death are repelled, and generally cannot cross or set foot on them. When you attempt to commit violence upon the road , or to harm the road or anything upon it , you hesitate. If you will yourself to continue , lose 1d4 HP and roll +WIS: on a 10+ , go for it; on a 7-9 , you act but have disadvantage on any rolls to commit violence (including damage); on a 6- , you fail to act and mark dazed .

880.title: The Crossroads
880.body: The West Road crosses the Highway about 15 miles from town. A copse of dool trees grows nearby. Despite the wards, there's something unsettling about the Crossroads. Everyone feels it. No one likes to talk about it. - What about this place tells you that the veil between life and death is thin here? - Whose ghost is said to haunt the Flats near the Crossroads? Something interesting: One can call up the shades of the dead if they stand at the Crossroads under the new moon and call the departed's name. Something useful: When you stand at the Crossroads under the new moon and call the departed by name , roll and add… - … +1 if you are tied to them by blood; - … +1 if you bear a possession of theirs; and/or - … +1 if you offer food or drink that they loved in life. On a 10+ , their shade appears but pick 1; on a 7-9 , their shade appears but pick 2: - They are deranged, confused, muddled, unreasonable - They have no intention of returning peacefully to their rest - Something slips through the Last Door with them—a dool spirit , a gwyllgi , an unbound wraith , etc. For more on shades , see Death and the Undying.

881.title: The Broken Roads
881.breadcrumb: The Makers' Roads › The Broken Roads
881.body: Roads once stretched from the Crossroads to the Ruined Tower , and from Stonetop out into the Great Wood , and all throughout the Steplands . Terrible magic shattered the stones and scorched their runes. Most have long since been buried, but sometimes a cracked stone is unearthed by beast, rain, or quake. When you strike an entity of darkness, chaos, or death with a shattered road-stone ( hand , thrown , crude ), it takes 1d8 damage (ignores armor) but the stone then crumbles to dust.

882.title: Waysides
882.body: Every 25 miles or so, the roads expand to a broad circle, wide enough for travelers to camp without leaving the warded stones. Each wayside includes a well, a firepit, an obelisk marked with a single rune, and maybe more. Many feature a shrine to Aratis . - What, by tradition, do travelers leave behind each time they stop at a wayside? - What distinguishes this wayside from the others?

883.title: Fellow travelers
883.body: 1d6 traveler Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate. 1d6 Traveler ----- ------------------------------------------------------------------ 1 Some kook or shady character(s), looking for ruins or tombs to plunder 2 1d12 immigrants, likely en route to Gordin's Delve 3 Someone seeking help/escaping trouble/in pursuit of \ \ \ 4 One or more Hillfolk 5-6 A merchant and crew, quite likely someone the PCs know For disposition, choose or have someone roll: 1d6 Disposition ----- --------------------------------- 1 As hostile as the Roads allow 2 In dire need of assistance 3-4 Tight-lipped / unfriendly 5 Friendly enough 6 Bearing useful news or opportunities

884.title: The nosgalau
884.body: Veteran travelers warn of the nosgalau, the night lights that lure travelers from the roads. They don't always appear, and even when they do, not everyone hears their call. - Which of you have seen the nosgalau on a prior journey? - Who were you with? Which of you was lured off the road? - What do you think the nosgalau really are? Why do you think that? When you hear the call of the nosgalau , say whose voice you hear on the wind and why you feel compelled to join them. Then, roll +WIS: on a 10+ , say how you see through the deception, and you'll never hear their call again; on a 7-9 , either mark XP and follow the call, or tell us how you detect the lie this one time; on a 6- , you heed the call and go to the nosgalau, unless your allies restrain you. What befalls their victims is for you to decide, but it isn't good. Perhaps they… - … fall prey to some mundane danger, like a mire or pack drake ; - … vanish in the night (the grass twisted in strange patterns), only to reappear elsewhere, muddy and haunted-eyed, with patchy memories; or - … find themselves surrounded by faces from their past, concealing the nosgalau's true forms (all wisps and teeth, hatred and hunger).

885.title: Runic calligraphy
885.body: Should one overcome the magic of the roads and pry up a pavestone, they would find the bottom laced with impossibly intricate, dizzying runes. They impart the magic of the roads, but they are indecipherable unless one already knows the proper magic. To unlock the mysteries of this runic calligraphy, you must: - Master each of these minor arcana: - Peacebond - Preserving Runes - Sanctifying Mark - Acquire an intact pavestone - Spend 1 season and 1d4 handfuls of silver attempting to replicate the runes. Henceforth, when you make magic marks or runes, you can imbue multiple magical effects that you know. Such combined runes take no longer to craft than the most complex of the individual spells. Beyond that, work out the details with the GM.

886.title: . steading improvement .
886.breadcrumb: The Makers' Roads › Runic calligraphy › . steading improvement .

887.title: ROADBUILDING
887.body: Requires all of the following: - Unlock the secrets of runic calligraphy - Teach the runes to at least 2 full-time artisans of quick mind and steady hand - Secure a nigh-unlimited source of basalt blocks - Recruit a foreman of considerable skill, who has studied construction of the Roads - Recruit and maintain a crew of 50 skilled laborers and masons When you mark all the requirements , you can expand or repair the Makers' Roads. Every 25 miles of road requires Pulling Together , one season of labor, 2 Surplus to feed the laborers, and a purse of silvers for payment and supplies.

888.title: Marshedge
888.body: Size town ( 800 to 900 souls) Population +0 - Prosperity +2 - Trade with... - ... Lygos (fine goods, spices, etc.) - ... Manmarch (timber, salt, amber) - ... Gordin's Delve (metal, tools) - ... Stonetop (whisky, fur)

889.title: Resources
889.body: - Farming (wheat, hemp, wild rice, herbs) - Trades (textiles, pottery, glass, rope, medicine) - Forage (herbs, peat, clay) - Marketplace - Windmill

890.title: Defenses +1
890.body: - The Guard - Wooden palisade - Bendis root braziers The town of Marshedge lies along the Highway , on a rise overlooking Ferrier's Fen . Many consider Marshedgers insane to live so close to such a dangerous place, but long ago, some of them learned the ways of the Fen and of bendis root, the burning of which keeps unclean things at bay.

891.title: Questions
891.body: - Which of the Old Families (Ferrier, Hawtrey, Tricklebank, Eldershaw) have you dealt with most? How so? - What trouble did Brennan's Claws cause you, back in their bandit days? - Do they worship \ \ \ \ here? How? - Who do you look forward to seeing? - Who are you hoping to avoid? - What's the oddest rumor you've heard about the fen-walkers? - As you enter, what's the most striking difference between here and Stonetop?

892.title: Places
892.body: - The Hill , around which the town huddles, looming over Ferrier's Fen. Riddled with Maker -ruins The Makers. - The Palisade surrounds the town, with braziers of bendis root burning day and night. Four members of the Guard walk the ramparts. - The Sruth , a wide, shallow river that drains lazily past Marshedge and into the Fen. Lined with reeds and willows, the bed made of loose, sucking mud. - The Bridge , a wooden span across the Sruth where a stretch of road once lay, just wide enough for a wagon. A pair of Guards collect tolls at the gate house. - Low Town , hovels of the working folk, all around the Hill and Edgemarket. Wattle and daub construction, cramped gardens, dirty kids, lots of livestock. Guards don't patrol here. - Edgemarket , on a wayside near the middle of town, crowded with tents, wagons, merchants, and visitors. Surrounded by shops, a pair of pubs, and a few traveler-houses. Patrolled by a pair of Guards. - The Mire , a muddy slum north of the main palisade, poking into the Fen. Wood walkways stretch between bogs of rice and herbs. Two gates link it to Low Town, each manned by a Guard. - Dropoff , middle-class homes and shops all around the slope of the Hill. A pair of Guards are always on patrol. - High Town , a neighborhood of stone buildings with slate roofs atop the Hill. Where the Old Families live. Ringed by a second palisade, with a pair of Guards always watching the gate. - The Garrison , the Guard's headquarters, just outside High Town. A dozen or so Guards are stationed here at any time, off-duty but on-call. - Eastgate , where the Highway passes through the palisade and farmers come and go to the fields. A pair of Guards collect tolls from visitors. - Miller's Hill , a Maker-ruin just east of town, repurposed into a windmill. Everyone mumbles about how the place is haunted. It is. - The Fields , stretching away to the east and south, owned by the Old Families. Some outbuildings, plenty of livestock, but few inhabitants.

893.title: Ruins of the past
893.body: This was clearly a place of importance in the time of the Makers. Their ruins dot the Hill and extend deep below. Some of these tunnels are used as storehouses or mausoleums. Others are unexplored. What was this place, originally? That's for you to decide, but perhaps it was… - … a Stone Lord outpost, standing watch over Ferrier's Fen; - … the edge of a Green Lord city, all that's left after the Stone Lords sank much of their lands; - … a center of trade between Green Lords and Stone Lords, abandoned when war broke out between them; - … a Stone Lord manor, reclaimed by the Barrow Builders ; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely. Whatever you decide, consider writing it up as a site, with dangers and discoveries that might now be in the hands of the Old Families, the Guilds, and/or Brennan.

894.title: Names
894.body: Irish-inspired: Abben, Ailen, Brin, Brogan, Catlin, Coln, Daedre, Dermos, Ennin, Finnen, Gilor, Isbeal, Kiran, Lile, Lim, Mathuin, Mirne, Noren, Owan, Ragan, Renan, Seadha, Seann, Tierney, Ulliam Marshedge and Stonetop share a language but the dialects are quite distinct, implying some common but distant ancestry.

895.title: People
895.body: Brennan , onetime bandit leader of the Claws, now the town's marshal. The Council thought it'd be cheaper and easier to pay him to guard the town than to hunt him down and defeat him. The Guard , the town's well-drilled standing watch. Tensions exist between the ex-Claws, old-timer loyalists, and the new recruits. ( 40 guards total) The Old Families dwell in the stone manor houses of High Town and have a piece of every pie. ( 30 family members and another 40 servants) - The Ferriers have lost much clout in recent years. Their matriarch, Ianthe, is ailing but still sharp ( Instinct to maintain her family's dignity). - The Hawtrey patriarch, Muiris, is one of Brennan's main allies ( Instinct to put his family's fortunes above all else). - The Tricklebanks resent the Ferrier's prestige. Led by cagey old Caineach ( Instinct to sieze any opportunity). - The Eldershaws have a reputation for being tough yet honorable, but an untimely death has left young Ailbe in charge ( Instinct to put off decisions). The Guilds , comprised of weavers, potters, glassblowers, and herbalists. They generate much of the town's wealth, and chafe at the Old Families' power. ( 125 artisans, assistants, and families) The lesser trades —smiths, bakers, carpenters, thatchers, the miller, and others who keep the town running. ( 40 artisans, assistants, and families) Fen-walkers ply the wetlands, gathering herbs, hunting game, cutting peat and clay, and keeping tabs on the suarachan . They recruit from orphans and those with no prospects. Some have (and hide) weird mutations. ( 20 souls) The Council , Marshedge's governing body. Includes the head of each Old Family, the master of each Guild, the eldest of the fen-walkers, and Brennan (who has no vote, but pushes them around). Merchants are key to the town's prosperity. Mostly local, but a few have settled here from afar. ( 40 souls) Beggars & thieves gather in Edgemarket, along the Highway, and in Dropoff, seeking sympathy or easy marks. ( 20-40 souls, varies by day) Farmers & laborers make up the rest of town. Mostly tenant farmers, put-upon homemakers, and muscle for hire hoping to earn rent. ( 500 souls) Travelers come to trade at Edgemarket, to consult with herbalists, or to seek (or spend) their fortunes. They stay with local contacts, in their own wagons, or at one of a few traveler-houses. ( 25-100 souls, depending on the season)

896.title: Secrets
896.body: - The town charter gives fen-walkers the right to demand aid from any resident, and the responsibility to execute those found to be "corrupted by vile spirits, or congressing with such." - Every seven years, one of the Old Families delivers one of their children to the Willow Witches . A fen-walker leaves the child at one of the grand willow trees, never looking back. Rarely, such a child returns, years later, and greatly changed. - The miller's wife is a necromancer , with a number of ghosts bound in the basement. Brennan knows her secret and has taken to consulting her. - You don't just start begging or thieving in Marshedge; there's a guild to join, rules to follow, and fees to pay. Surely Brennan and the Guard are compensated for looking the other way.

897.title: Hooks
897.body: - A daughter of the Old Families tries to save her son from the Willow Witches. - A fen-walker discovers a ruin, home to a fell beast but also great treasure. - Idealists attempt to organize and get recognition for a Farmers Guild. - A High Town merchant has what the PCs seek; but seeks exclusive rights to trading Stonetop whisky in exchange. - A spurned Guild apprentice is sending dark spirits against their old master - A well-regarded loyalist of the old Guard has gone missing. His granddaughter asks the PCs for help.

899.title: Commonly available
899.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in Marshedge—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky and furs) are always in demand. goods value ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------- Candle (lasts 1 hour, close ) 0 ◇ Lantern (○○○○○ hours, reach , area ) 0 Glass vial 0 Common trade tools (for pottery, weaving, etc.; immobile ) 1 ◇ Wax tablet and stylus 0 Parchment, a few sheets 1 Ink, vial and quills 1 Bendis root (burnt fumes repel perversions of nature; lasts 1 hour, reach , area ) 0 ◇ Pouch full of bendis root ( 10 uses) 1 ◇ Salt or seasonings (a pouch full) 1 Exotic spices (a little box) 1 ◇◇ Bolt of hempen fabric 1 Finery, like a silk scarf, a silver comb, embroidered shirt, nice dress, etc. 1 ◇ Cloak, finely embroidered ( warm ) 1 Spare parts for a wagon/cart (axles, wheels, etc.; ○○○ uses, immobile ) 2 +1 Value if sold outside of Marshedge coin value ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------- ◇ Purse of coppers ( 10 handfuls) 0 Handful of silvers 1 ◇ Purse of silvers ( 10 handfuls) 2 food & lodging value ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------- Hospitality, of a friend or contact 0 Food & drink, for a small party 0 Lodgings, decent (for a small party up to a week, board included) 1 Night of fine living, for one 1

900.title: Special items
900.body: The following might be acquired in Marshedge, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. When PCs Trade & Barter in Marshedge, they use the local Prosperity (+2 by default), with advantage because it's a town . On a 6-, they don't mark XP but they'll need to Ask Around if they want to sell/acquire it. goods value --- --- ◇ Medicines, potent (○○○○ uses) 1 Useful flora Useful or valuable flora, 1d4 uses 1 or 2 Lens, like for a magnifying glass 1 Spectacles, custom made 2 Luxurious clothing, silk embroidery, fine lace, drakeskin boots, etc. 2 ◇◇ Bolt of silk (or other exotic fabric) 3 ◇ Regalia ( warm , clumsy ) an outfit of ostentatious clothing; ignore clumsy if you're used to it 3 property value --- --- Cottage or hovel, to call your own 2 House or shop, a modest place 3 Business, small but established, like an inn, a pub, or a smithy 4

901.title: .   trade opportunities   .
901.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Marshedge might offer the following: - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus - A wagon, mule, or horse, for 1 Surplus and help with a problem - A tradesperson (healer, herbalist, potter, glassblower, etc) willing to move to Stonetop for a price - An arcanum of the Stone Lords Stone Lords, Green Lords Names, or Fae Fae

903.title: ASK AROUND
903.body: When you spend a purse of coppers and a few hours asking around town , say what you hope to learn and roll +CHA: on a 10+ , you get a clear and useful answer; on a 7-9 , you get an answer but pick 1: - It's incomplete, contradictory, or unclear, ask the GM how you could learn more - It'll cost you another purse of coppers - You've drawn unwanted attention or gotten yourself embroiled in a situation of the GM's choosing

904.title: CAROUSE
904.body: When you spend a handful of silvers having a good time , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - You spread a rumor or tale far and wide - You make a loyal new friend - You learn something useful or juicy - You avoid getting swindled, tricked, or entangled in someone else's trouble

905.title: RECRUIT
905.body: When you put out word that you're hiring , say who you're looking for, what the job is, and what you're offering (the GM will veto if unreasonable). Then roll and add… - … +1 if your offer is generous; - … +1 if the work conditions are favorable; and - … +1 if your reputation around here is solid. On a 10+ , you find someone but pick 1; on a 7-9 , you find someone but pick 2. - They lack some of the skills you wanted - They're entangled, impaired, or annoying - You draw unwanted attention - They have debts or obligations that you need to clear up first services value ------------------------------------------ ------- A guide around town, for the day 0 Someone to watch things, for a day 0 A laborer's help, for a day 0 A crew of laborers, for a week 1 A bodyguard/muscle, for a few days 1 A consultation with a skilled healer 1 An engineer/foreman, for a big project 2 A skilled healer's care, for a few weeks 2 A fen-walker Ferrier's Fen to guide you... ... to a known, nearby spot 1 ... to a dangerous/unknown spot 2 ... to a place of almost certain death 3

907.title: Folks from elsewhere
907.body: - North Manmarch - South Manmarch - Hillfolk - Lygos or other points south Lygos and the South - Stonetop

908.title: Things from the fen
908.breadcrumb: Marshedge › Dangers › Things from the fen

909.title: Brennan
909.body: Solitary , organized , hoarder , cautious , devious HP 12; Armor 3 (brigandine, shield) Damage steel sword d8+1 ( close , 3 piercing) Instinct to maintain or increase his power - Identify and exploit a weakness - Choose the best person for the job - Hide his true strength or position - Out-think and out-maneuver

910.title: The Guard
910.body: Group , organized HP 6; Armor 2 (iron scales) Damage steel-tipped spear d8 ( close , thrown , 2 piercing) Ex-Claw instinct to lord over others. - Bully, threaten, intimidate - Cover each other's backs - Demand a bribe or accommodation Loyalist instinct to protect the town, no matter what. - Give fair warning before using force - Stand firm in the face of danger New recruit instinct to avoid danger or punishment. - Shout a warning/order - Hesitate - Look the other way

911.title: Fire
911.body: Much of Dropoff is susceptible to fire, and Low Town is basically a tinderbox. A building catches fire? Hoo boy. Instinct to consume and spread. - Push them back with a wave of heat (d4 damage) - Fill the area with choking smoke (debilities) - Collapse a burning structure (d8 damage, ignores armor, forceful ) - Engulf someone or thing in flames (d10 damage, ignores armor, messy ) When a building catches fire and it's not quickly contained , start the following: - ☐ The building is engulfed; cries of alarm - ☐ A nearby building or two also catch; the original building starts to collapse - ☐ Many buildings are ablaze; folks panic - ☐ Fire spreads in multiple directions; palisade catches fire; bedlam - ☐ Impending doom: Fire burns itself out, scores dead, homes and shops ruined, large parts of the palisade destroyed

912.title: Thieves
912.body: Group , devious , stealthy , cautious HP 6; Armor 0 Damage knife d6 (hand, disadvantage) Instinct to get theirs and get gone. - Grab something, slip away unnoticed - Vanish into a crowd or an alley - Spot an easy mark - Wriggle free When you go unwary into the bustle of Edgemarket or the crowded streets of Dropoff , roll +WIS: on a 10+ , you spot the pickpocket coming or catch them in the act; on a 7-9 , they make off with only trifles (1d4 uses of Supplies, a purse of coppers, a skin of whisky, etc.); on a 6- , whatever they got, you're going to miss it.

913.title: The Maw
913.body: There's a cave in the Great Wood , a few hours north of Stonetop. No underbrush grows within 30 feet of it, and bones litter the entrance. Trappers and hunters avoid it, and claim to have heard strange noises coming from its direction—especially at night.

914.title: Impressions
914.body: - An unnatural quiet, the forest holding its breath - A blighted patch, spreading out from the cave, where only grey lichen grows - Bones strewn about, broken & chewed - The black void of the cave mouth - Those strange noises from within Tip: speak softly, hold very still. Pause a little longer between words, sentences.

915.title: Questions
915.body: - Who among you have been here before? What brought you? - What do the noises from the Maw (supposedly) sound like? - What do you think is in there? Why?

916.title: Hooks
916.body: - A beloved animal goes missing; someone saw it near the Maw. - A hunter reports strange figures (or tracks) coming and going. - A sickness afflicts the town; the treatment is an herb that grows near the Maw, but that must be harvested under moonlight.

917.title: What could be in there?
917.breadcrumb: The Maw › What could be in there?
917.body: That's for you to decide, but perhaps it's... - … a hagr , afflicted with some withering curse; - … a brutal, gluttonous Fae , and an entrance to its domain ; - … an idol or emanation of the Things Below ; - … a passage to one of the Fomoraij 's Names twisted realms; - … a specter or a pack of wraiths , terrible and ancient and only partly bound; or - … something else entirely.

918.title: North Manmarch
918.body: The North Manmarch lies east of the Great Wood , north of the Highway and northeast of Marshedge . Its western edge is made up of rolling hills, giving way to a wide, sparsely-wooded prairie that slopes gently towards the east. Many springs bubble up from the hills and flow eastward in tiny streams that feed the Dread River . The region is heavily populated and small, well-armed hamlets dot the hills and plains.

920.title: Always
920.body: - A wide-open sky, a sense that you can see forever - Wagon-rut trails and well-worn foot paths stretching on and on - Longhouses with steep, thatched roofs - Smell of woodsmoke, dung, wet fur - Huge rams pulling plows, wagons - Scarred faces, hard glares, weapons at hand even while working fields

921.title: Spring
921.body: - Pale wildflowers bobbing in the wind - Damp smell of mud or fresh-tilled earth

922.title: Summer
922.body: - Baled hay, laid out to bake - Sun beating down on you, sweaty clothes sticking to you

923.title: Autumn
923.body: - Crop fields, neatly clipped after harvest - A startled deer, bounding away

924.title: Winter
924.body: - Tracks in the snow: paw prints, hoof prints, foot prints, sled trails, etc. - Twinkling stars on a cold night, sure sign of a coming storm

925.title: The Manmarchers
925.body: Thousands of people dwell here, spread out over hundreds of hamlets. Those hamlets cluster around a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements. These hillforts are home to chiefs who (ostensibly) protect the surrounding hamlets. Manmarchers say "east of the river" the way others say "once upon a time." They migrated from across the Dread River long ago, before the Barrow Builders called up the Things Below and made the waters unsafe.

926.title: Names
926.body: Germanic-inspired: Alfher, Bathhilde, Berkhard, Bertrim, Clothar, Dagmar, Elfrida, Ganter, Gerhild, Hartig, Hilde, Hiltrude, Hramn, Ludig, Luise, Meike, Modd, Sabrinne, Swanhilde, Ulrike, Urrsla, Weillem, Wiland, Wulfrim

927.title: Lore
927.body: Everyone has heard of the Manmarchers' warlike ways. They fight mostly among themselves, the violence fueled by bloodfeuds, treachery, and ambition. The area might see a decade or two of peace, but only after war leaves the best part of a generation dead or maimed. Something interesting: When one Manmarcher kills or harms another in times of peace, the victim's family is owed a wergild—coin, livestock, or other wealth. The victim's chief sets the wergild (usually Value 2, or Value 3 for a person of import). Failure by the accused or their kin to pay is grounds for blood-feud. Something useful: Scheming chiefs will justify violence by setting an unreasonable or highly specific wergild. Should the wergild be paid, though, the feud must be called off. A paid wergild is sacrosanct—a chief who ignores it invites a curse upon their people.

928.title: Questions
928.body: - How is the Manmarcher language different from that of Stonetop? - When did the last round of fighting start among the Manmarchers? Has it ended yet? - Who all did you lose to the violence? Which clan/family/chief is to blame? - Which hamlet or hillfort do you hail from? What was it notable for? - Do they worship \ \ \ there? How does it differ from what's done in Stonetop? - The Manmarchers fell trees from the Great Wood—did they break or just never have a compact with the Forest Folk? - How do Manmarchers treat Green Lord ruins and artifacts? Do they shun them? Treasure them? - What is this clan best known for? How can you tell them from other clans? - What tells you that this land has been shaped by human activity, even though there's not a settlement in sight?

929.title: Hooks
929.body: - Manmarchers waylay a merchant south of the Highway, stealing something important/dangerous/valuable. - News arrives that the kin of a Manmarcher PC (or important NPC) were butchered in the latest round of fighting. - People are vanishing from some of the western hamlets, and the local chief seems powerless to stop it. - Manmarcher refugees arrive in Stonetop, promising buried treasure to whomever avenges them. - An ambitious chief has conquered three of the hillforts, and is preparing to move on a fourth. If they succeed, they will likely unite the Manmarchers, and then what?

930.title: Terrain
930.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

931.title: 1d12 terrain
931.body: - 1 Spring/pond/creek/stream - 2 Ditch/wash/gully/ravine - 3 Copse/thicket/grove of trees - 4 Steep slope/rocky outcrop/large hill - 5 Gentle slope/low ridge/small hill - 6-7 Open meadow, grass waist-high or shorter - 8 Footpath/wagon ruts/trail - 9-10 Farmland/pasture - 11 Fence/low wall/embankment - 12 Hamlet/hillfort/isolated longhouse

932.title: Discoveries
932.body: For any one of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

933.title: 1d6 discovery
933.body: - 1 Change of terrain (previous column) and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 signs of violence (see below) - 4 Beasts: maybe game (deer, turkey, pheasant, etc.) or a grazing herd watched by a youth and some dogs - 5 Farmers: working the land, hunting, or heading somewhere - 6 Warriors: not hostile, certainly wary

934.title: 1d6 signs of violence
934.breadcrumb: North Manmarch › Discoveries › 1d6 signs of violence
934.body: - 1 A ghost Death and the Undying lingering near its murder, more confused than dangerous - 2 A burnt hamlet, thoroughly looted, its inhabitants slaughtered - 3 Dead bodies: burnt on a pyre, heads on spikes, swinging from trees, or left to rot - 4 Survivors of a raid, some injured, likely desperate - 5 Sounds of a fight, or calls for help - 6 The dead from a recent skirmish, un-looted except by scavengers

935.title: Hamlet
935.body: Here's a typical settlement: Size hamlet ( 25 to 75 souls) Population +0 Prosperity -1 - Trade with other hamlets

936.title: Resources (pick 2-3)
936.body: - Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool) - Farming (wheat, barley, oats, honey, mead) - Timber - Trapping (fur, meat) - Mining (amber, salt, and/or iron)

937.title: Defenses +1
937.body: - Warrior culture - Iron weapons, wooden shields - Protection from the local chief Hamlets consist of a longhouse for each family and their livestock, plus smaller outbuildings (sheds, cellars, etc.), clustered together. Inhabitants work the surrounding 1-2 miles of land. Each hamlet bears the name of its most notable resident, possibly truncated, followed by "-hall" (Dagmarhall, Swanhall, etc.).

938.title: Hillfort
938.body: There are about a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements spread across the North Manmarch, each 20-30 miles apart. Most are in the western hills, but a couple sit on the plains to the east, at the western edge of the woodlands that overlook the Dread River. Hillfort names might include: Wulfberg, Rabenberg, Eikelberg, Hokberg, Tiefberg, Baerinberg, Schutberg, etc. Size village ( 150 to 350 souls) Population -1 Prosperity +0 - Trade with other hillforts - Trade with Marshedge and/or Lygos (textile, spices, glass, herbs, finery)

939.title: Resources (pick 2-3)
939.body: - Taxes from surrounding hamlets - Trades: smithing, tanning, pottery - Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool) - Market (for surrounding hamlets) - Timber

940.title: Defenses +2
940.body: - A dozen or so professional warriors - Rotating recruits from the hamlets - Iron weapons, wooden shields - Series of earthen ramparts - Palisades Each hamlet is bound to one of the hillforts and its chief, through oaths or blood. The chief keeps a standing force of warriors, and is responsible for protecting their hamlets from other clans, or (when that's not possible) demanding wergild or exacting vengeance.

942.title: Commonly available
942.breadcrumb: North Manmarch › Trade & Barter › Commonly available
942.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in the North Manmarch—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop whisky is usually in demand. weapons of war value --- --- ◇ Mace or flail, iron ( close , forceful ) 1 ◇ Battleaxe, iron ( close , messy ) 1 ◇ Sword, iron ( close , +1 damage) 1 ◇ Warhammer ( close , 2 piercing) 1 armor value --- --- ◇ Cuirass, boiled leather (1 armor) 1 goods value --- --- Salt (a little box) 0 Skin of mead, common 0 ◇◇ Firkin of mead, common 0 ◇ Salt (a pouch full) 1 Bit of amber 1 Barrel of mead, common ( immobile ) 1 Cartload of timber 2 transport value --- --- Wheelbarrow 1 Cart or sleigh (requires donkey/horse/mule) 2 Wagon (requires horse/mule) 3 Spare parts for wagon/cart/sleigh (axles, wheels, etc.; ○○○ uses, immobile ) 2 livestock value --- --- Goat ( sure-footed , curious , hungry ) HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ); Instinct to explore; butcher for ◇ provisions (6 uses) 1 Pig ( keen-nosed , stubborn , gluttonous , clever ) HP 6; Damage d4 ( hand ); Instinct to eat anything; butcher for ◇ provisions (1d6+10 uses) 1

943.title: Special items
943.breadcrumb: North Manmarch › Trade & Barter › Special items
943.body: The following might be acquired in the North Manmarch, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. When PCs Trade & Barter in the North Manmarch , they use the local Prosperity (-1 and disadvantage for a hamlet, +0 in a hillfort, by default). On a 6-, they don't mark XP, and if they want to sell/acquire it, they'll need to talk to the local chief. goods value ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --------- Skin of mead, fine (○○ uses, share 1 use with someone to gain advantage to Persuade) 0 ◇◇ Firkin of mead, fine 1 Barrel of mead, fine ( immobile ) 2 Piece of amber, beautifully worked 1 Bit of silver and amber jewelry 2 Large chunk of amber 2 Chariot (requires horse/tonnerhorn) 2 livestock value Tonnerhorn ( hardy , woolly , powerful ) HP 7; Damage d6+2 ( hand , forceful ); Instinct to protect its herd; can pull loads like a small horse 2

944.title: Services and favors
944.breadcrumb: North Manmarch › Trade & Barter › Services and favors
944.body: The PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the locals—even if they offer something of equal value. A likely way to convince them is to bribe the local elder, or get permission/backing from the local chief. A hamlet that's well-disposed to the PCs might grant these favors for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price. service value ------------------------------------------- ------- A night's food and shelter 0 A guide to the next hamlet 0 A laborer's help for a day 0 A crew of laborers, for a week 1 An armed escort for a couple days 1 A warrior to join you on a nice simple raid 1 A small crew, for a week or two of action 2 Someone to hide you from vengeance for a few days 2 A crew of toughs for a season of action 3 A fitting gift for a well-off chief 3 A compelling bribe for a well-off chief 4

945.title: . trade opportunities .
945.breadcrumb: North Manmarch › Trade & Barter › . trade opportunities .
945.body: If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), the Manmarchers might offer the following: - A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus - A ◇ pouch full of amber (Value 3) in exchange for help getting vengeance against a rival clan or chief - A small herd of 6-12 tonnerhorn (Value 3 total), for 1 Surplus and help with a problem - An arcanum of the Green Lords , Stone Lords , or Things Below

947.title: Hazards
947.body: - Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/hail/rain/snow; lightning - Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes - Prairie fire on the plains - Mudslides and rockslides on the hills - Fire in a hamlet or hillfort

948.title: Monsters
948.body: - Predators: pack drakes , cougar , feathered drakes , or wolves , maybe even a kleztigr - Big ornery beasts: boar , cave bears , or thunder drakes - Chimerae of the Green Lords Green Lords, unearthed by recent logging or mining - Ghosts or revenants , lurking near old battlefields or places of bloody, one-sided slaughter - Emanations of the Things Below, especially near the Dread River - Spirits of the wild in the lonely, untouched places - Terrors come down from the Whitefang Mountains

949.title: Chief
949.body: Chiefs are the closest things the north has to rulers. The position might pass down to children, or be voted on by the hamlets, or picked from the chosen shields, or taken by cunning or force. Some chiefs are foolish or weak, but they do not last long. For any given chief, pick or roll 2-4 strengths and 1-3 flaws.

950.title: 1d12 strength
950.body: - 1 The backing of another chief - 2 A black, fearsome reputation - 3 A bloodline full of heroes - 4 Coffers dripping with silver and amber - 5 The courage to try new things - 6 Deep, deep cunning - 7 Many bloodthirsty warriors - 8 A pet sorcerer/spirit, or a powerful artifact - 9 A silver tongue, or regal presence, or both - 10 Strength-at-arms worthy of the songs - 11 An utter lack of scruples - 12 The well-earned loyalty of their followers

951.title: 1d12 flaw
951.body: - 1 Debts owed - 2 Difficult/bickering/ambitious underlings - 3 A reputation for faithlessness - 4 Few warriors who will answer their call - 5 Frailty, from age/sickness/corruption - 6 Many who curse their name - 7 The need to prove themselves - 8 Overconfidence; a belief in their own songs - 9 A rigid sense of honor - 10 Seething, venomous ambition - 11 A temper to rival Tor's - 12 Utter lack of a warrior's skills

952.title: Chosen shield
952.body: Group , organized , brave , vicious HP 6; Armor 3 (iron hauberk, shield) Damage iron battleaxe d8 ( close , messy , 1 piercing), spear d8 ( close , thrown , 1 piercing), knife d6 ( hand , 1 piercing) Instinct to suffer no insult or disrespect - Lash out with sudden, casual violence - Fight with grim purpose or reckless abandon - Pry apart an enemy's defenses The chosen shields are a chief's best: the most loyal, the bravest, the bloodiest. They're professional fighters, their chief's bodyguards, enforcers, and lieutenants. They sit closest to the chief by the fire, and share the best haunches of meat. They take a share, too, of their chief's loot and their chief's glory. The wise chief lets them have it, for a chief is nothing without their chosen shields. And when a chief is toppled, it's usually one of their chosen shields that does the toppling.

953.title: Manmarcher recruit
953.body: Horde , young HP 3; Armor 2 (thick hides, shield) Damage iron battleaxe d6 ( close , messy ), knife d6 ( hand ), or javelins d6 ( thrown ) Instinct to prove themselves, one way or the other - Boast and brag - Form a ragged shield wall - Hesitate - Act rashly Every able-bodied youth does their turn at their chief's hillfort. They spend a year or two drilling, marching, learning the spear, and standing in the shield wall. They sing songs, and talk big, and maybe even get their hands red. A few are picked for their chief's standing force, but most head home to their hamlets, prepared to defend their families and swell their chief's ranks when called. That's the idea, at least.

954.title: Manmarcher veteran
954.body: Horde , organized , cautious HP 3; Armor 2 (thick hides, shield) Damage iron battleaxe d6 ( close , messy ), knife d6 ( hand ), or javelins d6 ( thrown ) Instinct to protect their homes, their kin, their honor - Form/hold a shield wall - Pick their battles - Charge! No longer young, no longer eager or scared. Just grim folk with something to fight for, something to lose. They might not be as spry as once they were, but they're no strangers to the black business.

955.title: Tonnerhorn
955.body: Horde , hardy , powerful , grumpy , loyal HP 7; Armor 0 Damage butt/gore d6+2 ( hand , close , forceful ) Instinct to protect its herd - Relentlessly pull against a load or restraint - Grunt, huff, paw, or bellow to make its displeasure known - Smash into whatever offends it, repeatedly Tonnerhorns are great big sheep, as big and strong as a donkey and twice as ornery. The ewes have good-sized horns, but the rams? The rams' horns are massive, curved, and nigh-unbreakable. Which is good—for the rams at least—because they do love to just smack-smack-smack them into things. Manmarchers raise them as beasts of burden, lashing them to plows, carts, sleighs, or even chariots. They bottle-feed the lambs and as a result, tonnerhorns are fiercely loyal to those who raised them. They're all kinds of unfriendly towards anyone else.

956.title: War-chariot
956.body: Group , large , terrifying HP 13; Armor 1 (wood/iron construction) Damage trample and wheel-scythes d8+5 ( hand , close , forceful , messy , 1 piercing), or iron battleaxe d8+3 ( close , messy , forceful ), or long bow d8 ( far ) Special qualities pulled by two tonnerhorns; carries a driver, two warriors Instinct to rush inexorably forward - Ride in broad arcs around the battle, peppering foes - Ride down foes, filling them with dread - Crash spectacularly, d8+3 damage ( area , forceful ) Most violence in the North Manmarch is small-scale, intimate even. A nighttime raid on a steading. An ambush in the hills. A war-band's throats slit around their campfire. When chiefs do raise large forces, and meet on the open plains, well… they bring their very best.

957.title: The Pale Hunter
957.body: On certain nights, the Pale Hunter stalks the Great Wood with his ghostly hounds. When he does, even the bravest hunters stay in town. The Forest Folk spoke of him as something like a force of nature, less a god to worship than a storm to endure. Tales differ as to what happens should you cross the Hunter's path. He might ignore you, or call you to join him, or mark you as his quarry. But the tales all agree: impress him, and you'll be well rewarded. Get caught or fail to do your part—well, best not to think of it.

958.title: Hooks
958.body: - Someone's still out in the Woods, but the Pale Hunter roams tonight! - The Pale Hunter is said to bear an artifact the PCs want or need. - The PCs wish to rescue a soul that the hunter holds bound. - The PCs meet a traveler, secretly undead. The Hunter has his scent.

959.title: Lore
959.body: The Pale Hunter values worthy prey and worthy company: the fleet, the clever, the brave. But he always hunts something when he roams the Wood, even if it's beneath him. Everyone knows this. Something interesting: He forgoes all other prey to hunt the undead, pursuing them even outside the Great Wood and on nights he would not normally roam. Something useful: To set the Hunter upon an undead being, acquire an item that bears its spirit-scent. Burn it on a moonlit night, in a smokey fire of fresh-cut cedar boughs.

960.title: Questions
960.body: According to the tale(s) you've heard… - What is he? Spirit, ghost, or what? - How is he connected to the moon? - What exactly are his hounds? - What is his relationship with the Lady of Crows ? - What hero won his respect, and how? What reward did they earn? - What grisly fate befell the hero who dared to stand against him? - What observances did the Forest Folk use to appease him? And also… - What tales do they tell of him in Marshedge ? Or among the Manmarchers ? - Who never came home, and is said to have crossed his path? - Who claims to have seen him? When and where? What happened? What left them (you?) so unnerved?

961.title: On certain nights...
961.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › On certain nights...
961.body: When does the Pale Hunter roam? Work with the PCs to pick 1: - When the moon is high and full - When the moon is new, missing - On nights of a lunar eclipse - On moonlit nights in winter - On moonless nights in summer - On each season's first full moon - Some other nights you all agree on Why are these nights special? Why does he stalk the Wood then? If the PCs are in the Great Wood on such a night, choose or roll for the Pale Hunter's quarry:

962.title: 1d6 quarry
962.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › On certain nights... › 1d6 quarry
962.body: - 1-2 Nothing… yet. - 3-4 Paltry prey: a fox/rabbit/boar/deer, crinwin , a fool who crossed his path, etc. - 5 Worthy prey: a ceirwmawr , a raselbaedd , a beast warden , a Fae with whom he's made a bet, etc. - 6 Undead prey: a ghost or revenant , caught in the open, away from any dool trees . When the time is right, point to a looming danger (his horn blows, his hounds bay, his prey rushes past). Offer an opportunity (to run, to hide, to seek him out, etc.). Announce trouble (mist rises, his hounds appear). If the PCs cross the Hunter's path, what happens next depends on his current quarry, whether they hide or face him, how they comport themselves, whether there are undead among them, and the fickle whims of the dice.

964.title: The Pale Hunter
964.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › The Pale Hunter
964.body: Solitary , large , athletic , cunning , hoarder , magical , terrifying , tireless HP 26; Armor 4 (resilience) Damage silver spear d12+3 w/advantage ( close , reach , thrown , forceful , messy , 2 piercing) or silver antlers d12+5 w/advantage ( close , forceful ) or grapple d10+3 w/advantage ( hand , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities surrounded by mist; can grab/grapple undead spirits; keen senses; uncanny reflexes Instinct to revel in the hunt, to claim undead souls - Perform a feat of prowess - Disperse into mist, reform - Summon his spear to hand - Call up more hounds - Claim a dead or dying soul A looming, antlered figure, luminous white, his face in shadow. His spear is long and straight, tipped with a silver crescent. He is draped with trophies of past hunts, jangling ominously or strangely silent as suits his need. Tip: If you must speak, speak softly. Slowly. Just above a whisper. With frequent pauses. And a flat affect. When you deal damage to the Pale Hunter that he might dodge or roll with , roll with disadvantage. The Pale Hunter's true nature is for you to decide, but perhaps he is… - … the incarnation of a primordial entity of cycles and the moon; - … a spirit of the wild ; - … the ghost of a Green Lord , who hunts forever; - … a mighty Fae ; - … one of the Forest Folk , exalted and accursed; - … some variation on the above; or - … something else entirely. Adjust the Hunter's stat block to reflect his nature. An incarnation of cycles and the moon might be primordial , with a move like "Anticipate their actions." A Green Lord's ghost would have the spirit and undead tags, a tether somewhere in the Wood, and 0 Armor vs. silver.

965.title: Ghostly hounds
965.body: Horde , spirit , terrifying , fierce , organized HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks organs), harmed only by salt or as it bites Damage bite d6 w/advantage ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities bound to the Hunter; can roam only 1 mile from him Instinct to give chase - Manifest a form made of mist - Sniff out prey, or the dead - Call the pack and the Hunter - Harry, surround, drag down A score or so of these savage white dogs accompany him on any given night. But there are more. So many, many more. Are they undead souls he has claimed, vulnerable to silver? Fae hounds, hurt by iron? Manifestations of some primordial urge? That's all for you to say. The Call of the Hunt When the Pale Hunter races past , your soul thrills to the call of his horn and the baying of his hounds—mark XP if you join the hunt. If you fail to join the hunt or the Hunter casts you out , your instinct becomes "Doubt: To balk at danger, to second-guess yourself" and you cannot Burn Brightly. Both last until you succeed at some bold and reckless act. The Hunter's regard The Pale Hunter welcomes company but expects them to keep up and do their part. Start the following. Track the grim portents separately for each PC. - ☐ He grows annoyed - ☐ He expresses his disdain - ☐ He gives you one last chance - ☐ Impending doom: He casts you out, strikes you down, sets hounds on you, and/or marks you as quarry When a PC impresses the Pale Hunter , clear any marks they have accumulated.

966.title: Pursuit
966.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Ghostly hounds › Pursuit
966.body: When the Pale Hunter marks the PCs as quarry , he gives them a running start. Wait a bit, then start the following. If the PCs split up, track the grim portents separately for each. - ☐ They hear his horn in the distance - ☐ The baying of hounds grows near - ☐ Mist rises, the first hounds appear - ☐ The hounds surround/harry them - ☐ Impending doom: The Pale Hunter arrives, and moves to strike

967.title: Claimed
967.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Ghostly hounds › Claimed
967.body: When you are at 0 HP and the Pale Hunter takes a trophy from you , the Hunter claims your soul. What does he do with it? Perhaps he… - … delivers you to the Lady of Crows and the Last Door Death and the Undying; - … adds you to the ranks of his hounds; - … consumes your essence in silver fire; or - … does something different with each. If you escape his clutches , you must pass through the Last Door now or linger on in the world (gain the Ghost insert).

968.title: Running the Hunt
968.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt
968.body: The Pale Hunter and his hounds are tireless and relentless. To survive, their quarry must last until sunrise or reach the edge of the Great Wood. Run the hunt as a series of scenes and challenges. If you're unsure what sort of scene or challenge to present, ask a player to roll the Die of Fate, adding 1 on each subsequent roll (cumulative).

969.title: 1d6 scene/challenge
969.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › 1d6 scene/challenge
969.body: - 1 An open stretch, a sprint - 2 A choice of paths: one longer, one riskier - 3 An obstacle, hazard, or monster - 4 A difficult slog - 5 One side splits up, perhaps to outflank/trick/entrap the other - 6 A good place to hide - 7 A breather, as the Hunter and his hounds find the trail - 8 If part of the hunt, the quarry is in sight/reach; if the quarry, a place the hunter can't/won't go - 9 If part of the hunt, a chance for mercy, to let the quarry go; if the quarry, a chance to slip away or catch the Hunter off guard - 10 A final mad dash before dawn or to the edge of the Wood Change the terrain with each scene or challenge.

970.title: Evading the Hunter
970.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › Evading the Hunter
970.body: Some ploys that might work include… - … going someplace he won't follow (see below); - … crossing running water to throw off his hounds; - … masking one's scent with muskweed or some other pungent plant; - … distracting him with a ghost, or something that seems to be one; - … leading him into the path of a big, territorial creature—like a hagr or cynddaraig —something to slow him down; - … getting help from a Fae or a spirit of the wild ; or - … using magic to trap, ward, or hide from him and his hounds.

971.title: Places he won't follow
971.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › Places he won't follow
971.body: The Hunter's quarry might find refuge... - … near dool trees , which he seems unable to approach; - … in certain places marked by Forest Folk glyphs ; - … in one of the red groves , perhaps, depending on the Hunter's true nature; - … through a waystone , perhaps, on the Fae paths; - … in caves or other places too small for the Hunter to enter (though his hounds probably can); or - … near a powerfully corrupted site . Many of these places hold dangers of their own, and the Hunter is patient and cunning. He will surround them, wait, and/or seek to flush them out as makes sense.

972.title: Special move
972.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › Special move
972.body: PUSH ON! When you try to keep up with or ahead of the Hunter and his hounds , and you carry a normal or light load, roll +CON: on a 10+ , you do so, and even have a moment to think, catch your breath, or help an ally; on a 7-9 , you keep going but pick 1: - You're flagging; mark a debility - Drop down to a light load, or drop if your load is already light - You lose ground or the Hunter's regard; tell the GM to advance towards impending doom - You need an ally's help On a 6- , or if you carry a heavy load : pick 1 of the above and ask the GM what else goes wrong, or what new challenge you now face. Note: The Home on the Range and Trailblazer moves (from the Ranger playbook) apply to this move, too.

973.title: Rewards
973.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › Rewards
973.body: The Pale Hunter rewards those who join and impress him, and quarry who manage to escape. The reward might be: - A marker of his favor, which the Lady of Crows will take in lieu of a soul - One of his many trophies (and the soul it binds, perhaps) - An artifact or arcanum, something portable, hidden on his person - His blessing upon their blade(s), which now harm undead as if silver - An honest answer - A silvery horn (below)

974.title: Silvery horn
974.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Running the Hunt › Silvery horn
974.body: ◇, magical , loud When you blow the horn outside, at night, in the presence of the undead , the horn crumbles and a mist rises. The undead flee, and the Pale Hunter and his hounds soon appear to chase them.

975.title: Hunter of the Dead
975.breadcrumb: The Pale Hunter › Hunter of the Dead
975.body: Why does he hunt the dead? Perhaps… - … they are the worthiest of prey; - … it is part of his nature to do so; - … he owes the Lady of Crows a tithe; - … he seeks vengeance upon them; - … some variation of the above; or - … something else entirely. Regardless: he will chase undead beyond the Great Wood, and sometimes catches their scent even while he does not roam the world. He then appears 1d6-1 nights later, pursuing them 'til dawn. If such a quarry survives, its only reward is that it has thrown the Hunter off its scent.

976.title: Primordial Powers
976.body: The village of Stonetop sits upon ancient ruins, built centuries or even millennia ago by the Makers . But there are things in the world that were old when the Makers were young. And there are things older still, older even than the world, bigger than the world. Strange and alien things. Primordial things and primordial powers, from the very dawn of time.

977.title: Themes
977.body: Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

978.title: 1d12 theme
978.body: - 1 Vastness, enormity, incomprehensibility, the primordial void - 2 Truth, purity, quintessence, the raw stuff of creation - 3 Fundamental forces: order, chaos, time, space, gravity, light, death, etc. - 4 The spirit world: consciousness, symbols and thought, the connection of all things - 5 True names, words of power, speech that transcends language - 6 Celestial bodies: their movements, their mysteries, their influence on the world - 7 Paradox: destiny and free will, causal loops, infinity, mutually exclusive truths - 8 Natural forces and the shaping of the word: slow and subtle, violent and cataclysmic - 9 The binding of the Things Below - 10 Mythic times and mythic deeds, the birth and death of gods and legends - 11 Long-forgotten peoples/beings, from before the rise of the Makers - 12 Portals, possibilities, other worlds, beings/things out of time and space

979.title: Hooks
979.body: - A grand conjunction of the stars approaches, signaling momentous change. - A meteorite crashes not far from Stonetop. - A scholar returns from an ancient monument, preaching gibberish that infects the minds of others. - A ritual can be performed only at a site of primordial power. - Centuries ago, the spirit of War renounced their role and duties and incarnated as a reclusive shepherd, to live a simple life. - A sleeping titan—big as a mountain, covered in millennia of soil, debris, and growth—starts to stir. - A clan of refugees from no known region emerges from the portal below the Ruined Tower .

980.title: Questions
980.body: - The Pale Hunter is associated with "the" moon, but are there other moons? What are they associated with? - What tends to make you feel insignificant and small? - Do you believe in fate? Or do we chart our own destinies? - What strange place did you once visit that was clearly touched by ancient, eldritch powers?

981.title: Cosmology
981.body: Many aspects of the setting's cosmology are intentionally left vague, for you and your group to flesh out as needed. Here are a few things to ask about or consider: - How mythic is your cosmos? Like, is the sun a star, orbited by the world? Or does Helior drive his chariot across the sky? Or what? - Do other worlds float in the void? Can they be reached via the spirit world or portals ? - What lies beyond the Last Door ? Is the afterlife the same for all, or do different fates await each soul? - What exactly is the relationship between the First Creators, the great spirits, the gods of forgotten peoples, and the gods worshipped today? - Did life evolve over eons? Or did the First Creators populate the world with much of the flora and fauna we know today? Or what? - Where did humanity come from? Do we predate the Makers or were we made by them? The answers to these questions will depend on how your players answer questions about other topics, and decisions you make about them. In particular, see Aratis , Danu , Helior , Tor , gods and religion (page 178\), death and the undying , the Makers , Lygos and the south , and Stonetop itself (page 12\).

982.title: The First Creators
982.body: They were the first minds to know themselves, the first beings to give themselves names. They were born of the cosmic void, of light and possibility and the churning chaos of nothingness and all. They spoke reality into being, set down its fundamental laws. They made the world and bound the Things Below . The First Creators are unlikely to feature directly in your game. Mostly, they serve as background, an excuse for why things are the way they are. Some might linger as what we now call gods (page 178), but as a rule they no longer actively shape the world. Perhaps they have forgotten it entirely.

983.title: The Things Below
983.body: Beings of darkness, chaos, and corruption, older even than the First Creators. They are jealous and hateful and full of infinite malice, and so the First Creators bound them in the depths of the earth. Though imprisoned, they still influence the world. The Green Lords called on them when the Fae rebelled. The servants of the Stone Lords succumbed to their whispers and ushered in the Time of Cataclysm . The Barrow Builders courted them openly and worshipped them as gods. Their influence has lessened in the current age , but it lingers still.

984.title: Great spirits
984.body: Impossibly potent entities, numinous and grand. The First Creators made some as experiments, some to be companions, some to serve as stewards of the world. Other great spirits arose spontaneously, birthed from the cosmos or the beliefs of mortal beings. Many of these great spirits are aloof, alien, and disinterested in the world (or at least, in this world). Others are active, but shape the world in subtle ways. Their numbers might well include the Lady of Crows , and some or all of the major gods (page 178). To create a great spirit for your game, decide whether it's worshipped as a god. If it is, create it using the procedure for other gods . Otherwise, pick or roll 2 primordial themes Primordial Powers and imagine the entity suggested by the combination. Consider why it goes unworshipped—is it unknown, uncaring, alien, or what? Should one of the great spirits become active in your game, write it up as a threat (a magical entity ). Even benevolent beings of such cosmic power can't help but cause trouble.

985.title: Incarnations
985.body: The great spirits sometimes manifest directly in the world. Such incarnations are likely to become figures of myth and legend. For examples, see: - The Willow Witches - The Nine-fingered stranger if you assume that Tor is a great spirit - The Pale Hunter , if you decide he's a primordial entity To create an incarnation, first establish what great spirit it's a manifestation of. Then choose or roll for why it incarnated, its manifestation, and 1-3 constraints. Write it up as a monster, and possibly as a threat, with all manner of powerful moves—such beings are not bound by the normal laws of reality. See the examples (previous column) for inspiration. Be cautious about introducing incarnations into your game. They should make the world feel rich and mysterious and help you punctuate the PCs' lives with adventure; they should not overshadow the PCs, or serve as a convenient contrivance. Their involvement should always feel dangerous, fraught, and complicated.

986.title: 1d12 why it incarnated
986.breadcrumb: Primordial Powers › Great spirits › 1d12 why it incarnated
986.body: - 1-2 As a punishment/penance/escape - 3-4 Because potent magic summoned it - 5-6 In pursuit of some agenda - 7-8 Out of curiosity/boredom/despair - 9-10 Through fate or cosmic accident - 11-12 To learn, to experience the world

987.title: 1d12 manifestation
987.body: - 1-2 A seemingly normal person - 3-4 A seemingly natural beast - 5-6 A spirit or other mysterious being - 7-8 A clearly supernatural person - 9-10 A monster or supernatural beast, possibly a titan - 11-12 Roll 1d10 again, but it manifests as a group or multitude of the result

988.title: 1d12 constraint
988.body: - 1 Doesn't realize what it is - 2 Is subservient to another - 3-4 Has a tether - 5-7 Is bound by certain inviolate rules - 8 Can incarnate only briefly, in or at certain circumstances/times - 9 Is subject to mortal urges/needs/emotions/weakness - 10 Refuses to get involved - 11 Is hunted by other supernatural beings - 12 Can't use its power without dire consequences, for itself/the world The PCs are searching the bowels of the Ruined Tower for the bronze helm that serves as a casing for the Mindgem . In the next session, they're likely to find the oubliette and on a whim, I decide that one of the cells holds an imprisoned incarnation. I don't have much more than that in mind, so I roll two primordial themes. I get a 4 ("the spirit world: consciousness, symbols and thought, the connection of all things") and a 12 ("portals, possibilities…"). That seems like an entity who breaks down barriers between minds, dreams, and reality. From there, I roll a 5 for why it incarnated ("in pursuit of some agenda"), a 4 for its manifestation ("a seemingly natural beast") and a 3 for a constraint ("a tether"). I think that it incarnated to make dreams and desires come true. I pick an additional constraint: "It can't use its powers without dire consequences." It came to grant wishes, but in truth it spreads chaos—which is why Stormcatcher locked it away. THE NIGHTINGALE Solitary, tiny, primordial, magical, beautiful HP 19; ARMOR 5 (size, resilience), 1 vs. black iron (size) DAMAGE ear-piercing screech d12-2 w/ disadvantage (near, area, loud, ignores armor) SPECIAL QUALITIES ageless; reads minds; tethered to a particular ever-blooming rose bush somewhere in the Foothills INSTINCT to grant unasked-for wishes - Entrance with its beautiful song - Bring one's deepest desires to mind - Manifest those desires in the world, however unrealstic, consequences be damned - Flit away, go elsewhere If the PCs set it loose, I'll write it up as a threat (an affliction ) that spreads chaos throughout the region.

989.title: Archons
989.body: Archons are constructed spirits , made by the First Creators or one of the great spirits as guardians and enforcers of the cosmic order. Start with the base archon stat block below. Pick or roll for their purpose, their manifestation, and up to 3 powers. Adjust the stat block to reflect these choices or results, writing at least one move to reflect its purpose and one to reflect it manifesting (unless its manifestation is a construct/animate statue).

990.title: Base archon
990.body: Solitary , large , primordial , spirit , construct , magical , terrifying HP 23; Armor 5 (resilience, lacks vitals), 1 vs. black iron Damage physical blows d12+3 w/advantage ( close , reach , forceful ) Special qualities ageless, immune to the elements, perceives thought and matter, omnilingual Instinct per its purpose

991.title: 1d12 purpose
991.body: 1d12 Purpose ------ ------------------------------ 1 To seek out and destroy 2 To find, judge, and punish 3-4 To capture/retrieve 5-6 To warn/castigate/correct 7-12 To stand watch and protect

992.title: 1d12 manifestation
992.body: - 1-2 Pure energy/light/fire: harmed only by black iron - 3-4 A construct/animate statue: - spirit , Armor 6, or 4 vs. black iron - 5-6 A mass of vapor/dust/ash/soil/etc.: takes half damage except from black iron - 7-8 A ripple in reality: + stealthy , harmed only by black iron - 9-10 Someone plucked from the mind of whoever it interacts with - 11-12 An impossible entity, surreal and weird and awe-inspiring

993.title: 1d12 powers
993.body: - 1 Smites at range ( far or near , area ) - 2 Manipulates the environment - 3 Teleports, or close enough - 4 Pierces deception, compels truth - 5 Stuns/deafens/blinds/hobbles foes - 6 Marks/curses/binds targets - 7 Compels mortals/lesser spirits to \ \ - 8 Foresees the future, likely outcomes - 9 Harms weapons used against them - 10 Dispels/disenchants magical effects - 11 Quells emotions, instills peace - 12 Something special, unique, thematic Archons have unique names with unlikely combinations of sounds, with majestic and enigmatic titles. For example: Qrenithar, Dawn Herald; Klythorim, Arrow of Time; Xezaltran the Inevitable; Jharvakus, the Silent Witness; Phrysthalin, Bearer of Scales; and Lyvontis, the Judge Incorruptible. For an example, see Tcaventes, Shackle and Key . The Seeker acquires the Ineffable Words arcanum Ineffable Words and eventually unlocks their mysteries, only to then mark a consequence that has an "eternal, ancient being of Order" seek them out and reprimand them. That sounds like an archon to me! I pick a purpose of "To warn/castigate/correct," and roll a 2 for its manifestation ("pure energy/light/fire"). For powers, I roll a 3 ("teleports, or close enough") and pick both "stuns/deafens/blinds/hobbles" (it's made of light!) and "dispels/disenchants magic." I ponder a little, and write up the following:

994.title: LIVROTHOS, CLEANSING FLAME
994.body: Solitary, large, primordial, spirit, construct, magical, terrifying HP 23, ARMOR 1 (lacks vitals), harmed only by black iron DAMAGE white flame d12+3 w/advantage (hand, close, forceful, messy, 3 piercing) SPECIAL QUALITIES: ageless, immune to the elements, perceives thought and matter, omnilingual INSTINCT: to correct mistakes - Detect magic run amok - Appear as a column of white flame, painful to look upon - Vanish, maybe appear elsewhere - Burn magic away from the world

995.title: Titans
995.body: Legends speak of a time when beasts and beings of impossible scale roamed the world. The god Tor is said to have slain three such beasts, and everyone knows about the skeleton on the side of Titan Bones . Perhaps the First Creators placed these titans in the world. Perhaps they evolved amid the early world's riot of vitality and life. Some could have been incarnations of the great spirits, or emanations of the Things Below. Regardless, titans no longer roam the world like they once did. The Crombil might be one, but it wakes only once every few decades. The Suileach might be another, but it is confined to the Dread River. The hidden lands around the Golden Oak are full of gargantuan beasts, but whether they are truly titans is up to you. There might be other such titans, hidden away in remote corners of the world, sleeping the centuries away, waiting to rise once more.

996.title: Sites
996.body: There are places in the world marked or marred by primordial power. If such a site needs to be established, combine the terrain in which it's located with a marker.

997.title: 1d12 marker
997.body: - 1 A veil, hiding it from the world - 2-3 Dramatic geology (a peak, a crater, a rift, a geyser, etc.) - 4 Desolation/ radiation - 5 Remains of a titan - 6-7 Megalith(s)/petroglyphs/runes/cave paintings/etc. - 8-9 Ruin(s) of the Makers , built near or around it - 10 Mysterious structure(s), placed by primordial entities - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice and combine Once the site itself is established, pick or roll for a theme Primordial Powers, an origin, and 1-3 features that make it interesting.

998.title: 1d12 origin
998.body: - 1-2 The intent of a (the) great spirit(s) - 3-4 A confluence of geomantic/cosmic/celestial forces - 5-6 A conflict between mythic beings - 7-8 The death of a primordial entity - 9-10 The presence of a primordial artifact - 11-12 The working (successful or not) of a potent spell or artifice

999.title: 1d12 features
999.body: - 1 An archon , watching over it or what it contains - 2 A trapped primordial entity or emanation - 3 An abundance of certain spirits - 4 The shade of a dead god or primordial entity, easily called up - 5 Anomalies (time loops or fluxes, spatial warps, weird gravity, thoughts projected out loud, etc.) - 6 A thin barrier with the spirit world - 7 A portal , or a place with the potential for one - 8 A font of power; a good place to work particular types of magic - 9 Visions/omens/prophecies/insights, sometimes bestowed on visitors - 10 Useful or valuable flora , something weird, found only here - 11 The raw stuff of creation: black iron , water of life, primordial flame , etc. - 12 One or more artifacts of primordial power Artifacts If such a site lies near areas that are or were once populated, consider the locals' attitudes towards it (reverence, awe, terror, avoidance, etc.), and what additional signs they might have left. The Seasons Change and the PCs get "interesting news." I want to give them a lead on the Ineffable Words Ineffable Words , to be found in a primordial site in the Whitefang Mountains . I roll a 4 on the Whitefangs terrain table ("waterlogged soil, frozen or muddy") and a 9 for a marker ("ruin of the Makers"). In the Whitefangs, that's probably a Rime Lord ruin. I roll a 10 for theme ("mythic times, the birth and death of gods and legends") and an 8 for origins ("death of a primordial entity"). For features, I roll a 4 ("the shade of a dead god…"), and pick "one or more artifacts of primordial power" (the Ineffable Words). I create the Rime Lord ruin using the tables on Rime Lords and the dead god using the steps on Gods and Religion I picture a high place, where a demon killed Nangbu—a goddess of writing, wisdom, and prophecy. The Rime Lords carved a temple into the slope, to honor her and commune with her shade, but avalanches buried the temple. What remains is an alpine meadow with a few chunks of Maker-carved stone sticking out of muddy soil. There's a stillness to the air, in which one can sometimes hear a whispering voice. For their interesting news, I think the PCs will acquire a scroll describing this temple of Nangbu, and how the dead god's shade dwells there and bestows gifts of wisdom and prophecy upon petitioners (the Ineffable Words).

1000.title: Other sites of primordial power
1000.breadcrumb: Primordial Powers › Sites › Other sites of primordial power
1000.body: - A small lake: cold, acidic, devoid of life. During thick mists, it connects to other times, places, or realities. - A hill overlooking a river, where an emanation is impaled on a rod of black iron. An archon hides the hill and its prisoner from the world. - The base of a rusty metal tower, 6' wide, rising over a lush meadow that teems with nature spirits. The bones of a titan are buried below. - A fallen megalith, half-sunk in peat and mire. When the stars align, a mortal here can ask one question and get a total, overwhelming answer. - A lonely peak where, if one is not careful, their spirit will be drawn from their body and up into the void. - An underground arch marked with runes, opening to a long straight hall. The hallway is a loop; you always return to where you started. - Plenty of corrupted sites - Certain chambers below the Ruined Tower . - The Golden Oak . - The Utterwell . - Titan Bones , perhaps. - Maybe Tor's Fist ? - Possibly the Stone itself.

1001.title: Portals
1001.body: There are places throughout the world that touch each other on a fundamental level, despite being hundreds of miles apart. The Makers knew how to create portals in such places, allowing for instantaneous travel between them. One might find such a portal… - … beneath the Ruined Tower - … in the Labyrinth - … hidden away near Three Coven Lake - … among the ruins of Vor Svetelik - … up on Tor's Fist , perhaps - … maybe in certain ruins down in Lygos and the South - … anywhere else you think makes sense. Portals can be activated via Opening the Way , which requires inscribing the sigil sequence of one portal within the sigils of another. Should the PCs learn to use portals, they might wish to create new ones. To do so, they must: - Locate a suitable site of primordial power - Transport a slab of pure white stone (Value 2 or 3, depending on size) to the site - Discern the correct sigil sequence for this site - Have an exceptional stonecutter spend a season (Value 2) carving the sigils into the stone - Risk damage to the stone or a flawed portal The Makers' portals were safe and stable, but flawed or damaged portals are terribly dangerous. One might explode, tear travelers apart, and/or open a rift in the world. See the cracked portal of Vor Svetelik for an example.

1002.title: Black iron
1002.body: A few rare veins of this metal exist in the world, but mostly it's retrieved from meteorites. It is completely, utterly immune to magic. - Fae treat it like iron, but worse—its touch unravels their magic and they take +1d6 damage ( messy , ignores armor) from it - Spirits can't touch it, perhaps can't even perceive it - It can damage even insubstantial forms of manifest spirits , but does no true harm to the spirit itself - It damages or destroys any open portal it touches, going nowhere - Spells, rituals, and other magic cannot affect it, pierce it, detect it, or perceive things enclosed by it When the PCs encounter some, pick or roll for its form:

1003.title: 1d12 form
1003.body: - 1-2 Ore/lump/meteorite - 3-4 Raw material (spool/ingot/rod/etc.) - 5-6 Charm/talisman/jewelry/symbol - 7-8 Tool (nail/hammer/tongs/etc.) - 9-10 Weapon/shield/armor - 11-12 Construction (door/plating/throne/etc.), likely immobile Pick or roll for its size and its Value to those who understand what it is. For worked items made of black iron, add their base Value to what's listed below. For example, a sword (Value 1) made of black iron would be Value 3.

1004.title: 1d12 size
1004.body: - 1-4 A small object/amount (Value 1) - 5-8 A ◇ object/amount (Value 2) - 9-11 A ◇◇ object/amount (Value 2) - 12 An immobile object/amount (Value 3 at least) Black iron is strong and hard like steel and worked much the same way. It's a little on the heavy side, and doesn't hold quite as keen an edge. Black iron weapons don't have " x piercing," but a black iron warhammer would still have 2 piercing.

1006.title: Various treasures
1006.body: - ◇ Rocks with fossilized sea shells (Value 0), found far from any water - A ◇ stack of tin plates, with punched-out stencils; arranged correctly, they lay out the sigils of a specific portal (Value 1, or 3 to the right buyer) - A ◇ pouch of odd, white coins bearing odd symbols, much lighter and harder than you'd expect (Value 1, maybe?) - A… spearhead (?), wedged deep into bedrock, shining with eternal starlight ( hand , magical , beautiful , Value 2) - A ◇ silver chalice (Value 2), dented and tarnished, engraved with images of monsters being trapped in a globe - An 8' statue of an unknown goddess bearing stars and moons in her many hands ( immobile , fragile , Value 2) - A gem of blue, crystallized flame; cool to the touch but melts metal and starts fires given time ( dangerous , Value 3) - A ◇◇ black iron hauberk (2 armor, warm , cumbersome , Value 4), utterly immune to magic - An abandoned quarry of pure white stone, like you need for a portal; flooded but still full of good material A bejeweled helm ◇, magical , loud , dangerous , 1d4+1 uses Bronze, battered, and adorned with seven fittings, 1d4+1 of which bear purple gems that hum ominously. When you don the helm , you sense the gems' power and how to use them but can hear nothing over their incessant noise. When you unleash a gem's power , pick a target you can see and roll +WIS: on a 10+ , your target freezes in time and space for as long as you wear the helm and maintain focus; on a 7-9 , you must focus for a few seconds, but then the effect kicks in as per a 10+. While your target remains frozen , the gem hums louder and louder, painfully so. When the effect ends , the gem crumbles to dust. When you try to maintain focus , roll +CON: on a 10+ , you're fine for now; on a 7-9 , either mark a debility or let the effect end; on a 6- , mark a debility and either let the effect end or ask the GM how time and space start to go haywire around you. While frozen, the target (and anything on their person) is inviolate—they cannot be harmed, moved, or otherwise interacted with. They rejoin the timeline as if no time had passed for them, with whatever momentum they previously had. A sealed amphora ◇◇, fragile , magical , 3 uses A ceramic, two-armed jug with a wax seal. Inside is water of life, the quintessence of water, so pure that it washes away all impurity, corruption, poison, or disease from any living thing. It becomes sullied and loses its potency when used, or if left unsealed for more than a minute or so. The amphora itself is a perfect vessel: as long as it is sealed, no substance within will rot, putrefy, or otherwise spoil.

1007.title: A fossilized horn
1007.breadcrumb: Primordial Powers › Artifacts › A fossilized horn
1007.body: ◇, magical A spiral thing, maybe 2' long, the remnant of some long-gone incarnation . Those sensitive to the spirit world can sense its lingering, radiant power. When you bear the fossilized horn , you have advantage on any move to impress, intimidate, or threaten a spirit (though it grants no special ability to communicate with spirits in the first place). Should you roll a 6- on any such roll, the spirit can choose to destroy the horn and end its effect, in addition to whatever else the GM says.

1008.title: Minor arcana
1008.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1009.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1009.body: - 1 A time-worn missive - 2 A… key? - 3 A crumbling arch - 4 A silvery signet ring - 5 An oversized crown - 6 A metal puzzle box

1010.title: Major arcana
1010.body: The following are (or could be) associated with primordial powers. - The Ineffable Words - Storm Markings - Noruba's Ice Sphere

1012.title: Natural forces
1012.body: - Storms, violent weather, lightning , flooding - Avalanches , rockslides and mudslides - Prairie fire run amok - Volcanic activity - Earthquakes - Radiation

1013.title: Fate
