1013.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to push towards the inevitable There are those whose fates are heralded by prophecy, omen, and vision. And if you know your fate, maybe, just maybe, you can change it. - Reveal that recent events were foretold long ago - Reveal things yet to come - Use coincidence to push them towards their fate - Show how attempts to avert fate actually brings it to pass - Offer an opportunity to change their fate, but tell them the costs/consequences of doing so

1014.title: Incomprehensible knowledge Threat (affliction)
1014.breadcrumb: Primordial Powers › Dangers › Incomprehensible knowledge Threat (affliction)
1014.body: Instinct to unravel any sense of safety/self When one delves into primordial mysteries, one must grapple with alien and unsettling ideas. Dwell on them too long, and you find your mind turning against itself. - ☐ You fidget, fixate, have trouble sleeping or relaxing - ☐ Change your instinct to… (pick 1) - ☐ Paranoia: To invent dangers - ☐ Compulsion: To obsessively repeat certain actions - ☐ Apathy: To let things escalate - ☐ Henceforth, when the GM compels you to act on your instinct, mark XP if you do and Defy Danger if you don't - ☐ Impending doom: Retire your PC; they become a threat (a wildcard )

1015.title: Primordial flame
1015.body: Primordial , terrifying , magical Damage 1d12 w/advantage ( hand , messy , ignores armor) - Radiate blistering heat, 1d10 damage ( reach , area ) - Start normal fires nearby - Reduce what it touches to ash A tongue of perfect blue fire, somehow brought into the world. It is likely found held in stasis by some potent magic or artifice. For while it consumes and destroys all it touches, it cannot last long in contact with base reality.

1016.title: Rift
1016.body: When primordial power runs amok, it can damage reality itself. Such rifts might manifest as places where: - Time runs slowly/quickly/not at all - Things emerge from other times/places/realities - Natural laws like light, heat, gravity, and death behave strangely - The world breaks down into chemics/crystals/raw elements - New lifeforms/consciousnesses arise - Fundamentals , voidblights , or other dangerous spirits enter the world - The Things Below spread their influence (see corrupted sites ) Most rifts are temporary, soon sealed by archons or other primordial entities. But if one persists for any length of time, write it up as a threat (a MacGuffin , Instinct to unravel reality).

1017.title: Fundamental
1017.body: Solitary , spirit , primordial HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks vitals), harmed only by black iron Damage destructive touch 1d10 w/advantage ( hand , grabby , ignores armor) Instinct see below - Manifest in the proper conditions - Communicate poorly - Burden with incomprehensible knowledge - Refuse to cooperate - Manipulate/manifest its element, locally and dramatically These are spirits of fundamental forces, far removed from mortal concerns. They are active only near sites of primordial power and uncontrolled rifts , or when called forth by powerful, dangerous magic. Example fundamentals include:

1018.title: Time
1018.body: Manifests as a form of flowing sand Instinct to plod forward

1019.title: Gravity
1019.body: Manifests as a voice from below Instinct to be greedy, selfish

1020.title: Fate
1020.body: Manifests as a weaver or a spider Instinct to be coy

1021.title: Light
1021.body: Manifests as a prismatic pattern Instinct to overwhelm

1022.title: Void
1022.body: Manifests as a hole in reality Instinct to get confused/annoyed

1023.title: Order
1023.body: Manifests as an unmoving crystalline form Instinct to arrange, normalize, preserve

1024.title: Chaos
1024.body: Manifests as a bewildering series of impressions Instinct to undermine, to surprise

1025.title: Voidblight
1025.body: Horde , spirit , primordial , stealthy , hungry , cautious HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks vitals) Damage spirit-rending bite d6+2 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities can harm only spirits and mortals sensitive to the spirit world; harmed only by other spirits or magic attacks Instinct to eat and spawn - Follow the "scent" of a rift or a spirit-walker - Mimic a spirit it has eaten - Pounce on unsuspecting prey - After a good meal: spawn another of its kind To those who can perceive them, they seem like many-limbed salamanders made of starfilled night. Their broad, flat, eyeless heads split open into rows of glittering teeth. They are alien things, spiritual scavengers and predators. They haunt forgotten corners of the spirit world, the dead worldmotes that float through the cosmic void. Mostly, they wait and hunger. But if they catch the scent of a traveler, or worse, a rift into our world, they will follow it. Treat an incursion of voidblights as a threat (type: beasts ) with the following grim portents: - ☐ A few local spirits are killed; others are spooked; sensitive mortals are unnerved - ☐ "Softer" local spirits die or flee; plants fail, animals grow sickly, misfortune abounds; voidblights attack sensitive mortals - ☐ Almost all local spirits flee or are eaten; local systems collapse and die - ☐ Impending doom : voidblights spread into new colonies, all over the region and soon the world Stakes : Will any of the PCs' enemies join them to face this threat? How will the environment be permanently changed? What will happen if the voidblights are corrupted by the Things Below?

1026.title: Other primordial entities
1026.breadcrumb: Primordial Powers › Voidblight › Other primordial entities
1026.body: - The Things Below - Great spirits , possibly including the Lady of Crows and some or all of the major gods (page 178\) - Incarnations , including the Willow Witches and perhaps the Nine-fingered stranger and/or the Pale Hunter - Archons , including Tcaventes, Shackle and Key - Titans , perhaps including the Crombil and/or the Suileach

1027.title: Red Groves
1027.body: Half a day east of Stonetop is a copse of trees that turn blood red (leaves, bark, and all) at the autumnal equinox. By spring, the color has returned to normal. The Forest Folk warned the villagers to avoid this place. They never said why. - An iron tang in the air - The scrutiny of the forest upon you, heavy like a blanket - Words of warning, faintly heard in the breeze, in the rustling leaves, in the insect hum and birdsong. - Spring/Summer: bright green shoots/leaves bursting from black bark and mud - Autumn/Winter: overwhelming, glossy red Tip: faintly whisper things like "turn back" or "not welcome" or "go away" and then pretend you didn't say anything

1028.title: Lore
1028.body: Something interesting: A dozen such groves dot the Great Wood. Some redden in the fall, others in spring. Some, like the one near Stonetop, are large and old; others are just a few young trees. Something useful: When you enter a red grove , you draw the forest's wrath unless… - … you come naked and empty handed, having been ritually purified; - … you bear a dying spirit, wishing to pass on; or - … you are cloaked in subtle magic. Redwood serves as a conduit for spirits of the wild . Some of the Forest Folks' most powerful totems are made of redwood. Alas, harvesting redwood without the permission of the forest spirits is sure to draw their primal, relentless ire.

1029.title: Why are they so special?
1029.breadcrumb: Red Groves › Why are they so special?
1029.body: That's for you to decide. Maybe... - … they are where spirits of the wild are born and where they come to die; - … they each imprison some terrible evil; - … they each mark the grave of a champion of Danu ; or - … it's different for each grove.

1030.title: Hooks
1030.body: - A dying glasbren seeks a mortal to convey her to the Red Grove. - The Red Grove is corrupted by the Things Below . Its spirits are confused and unwell, and the Grove will soon start spawning horrors. - A villager steals a branch of redwood, and is cursed with the forest's wrath.

1031.title: Inhabitants
1031.body: Any spirit of the wild might be found here, but each grove hosts at least a few brennauwed and is protected by coedwaig . Butcherbirds frequent the Groves while they are red.

1032.title: The Forest's Wrath
1032.breadcrumb: Red Groves › Inhabitants › The Forest's Wrath
1032.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to punish the perpetrator and those who protect them The greater the transgression, the more quickly the curse escalates and the greater the penance required. - ☐ Nightmares, ire from beasts and birds, plants tend to wither and die - ☐ Friends and family are affected, too; birds and small beasts harass them in town - ☐ The whole village is affected; sleep is scarce, nerves frayed, the Wood is hostile - ☐ Saplings sprout in town overnight, or when folk aren't looking - ☐ Impending doom : Crops fail, livestock revolt, homes collapse, the granary rots

1033.title: Rime Lords
1033.body: In the utmost north, the Rime Lords made their domain. They spread through the Whitefang Mountains The Whitefang Mountains and the tundra beyond, embracing the ice and the cold and the terrible howling emptiness. The Rime Lords developed all manner of ascetic disciplines and esoteric arts. They inured their bodies to the cold. They sent their minds wandering through spirit realms. They shaped matter and energy with their will, imposed stillness on the world, froze pockets of reality in timeless stasis. Disciples came from the farm-valleys and the mammoth herders, to study at the feet of these living gods. Their teachings were harsh, rigorous, and demanding. But for a time, monasteries dotted the north and the ways of the Rime Lords flourished. But then the servants of the Stone Lords Stone Lords rebelled and set loose the Things Below The Things Below. Something cracked open at the very roof of the world, and the voice of Y'aaw'kara (the Howling Wind, the Flayer of Flesh) was unleashed. Bloodthirst and madness spread. Some Rime Lords fell to the Howling Curse . Others were brought low by mortal mobs run rampant. The thirteen survivors agreed that something must be done. They each retreated to a place of power, sent forth their minds, and together sealed the rift beneath a glacier of dark ice , quieting Y'aaw'kara's voice. The Howling Curse dulled to a constant whisper. But the last thirteen Rime Lords had sacrificed themselves. Their bodies remain encased in ice, their minds working the magic intended to keep Y'aaw'kara's voice at bay for eternity.

1034.title: Names
1034.body: Sanskrit-inspired: Akativet, Artarapishi, Dahishika, Goranat, Kanishanu, Mamaranu, Marash, Nilani, Prayanavi, Ralananan, Ranaji, Shayand, Supnam, Vitatana Often combined with a title of "Gar" ("teacher") or appellations of "Div" ("the holy") or Kav ("the wise"). For example "Gar Akativet," "Kanishanu Div," or "Ranaji Kav."

1035.title: Hooks
1035.body: - A rogue has some dark ice to trade, and knows where to find more - Timber-cutters in the Foothills disturb a Rime Lord-era ruin - Prospectors from Gordin's Delve find one of the last thirteen Rime Lords, still in stasis, encased in ice - A new glacier starts to form in the Whitefang Mountains , spreading into the Great Wood and upsetting the spirits there

1037.title: 1d12 theme
1037.body: - 1 Ice: shaped into grand and intricate designs - 2 The all-pervasive cold: bone-chilling, shivering, numbing, desperate - 3 Stasis/stillness/silence/permanence/preservation - 4 Ascetic practice: the ruthless perfection of mind/body/will - 5 Chants/gestures/rituals: to focus the will, to tame reality/chaos/entropy - 6 The mind set free: spirit-sight, spirit-walking, thought-crafting - 7 Writing and records: the preservation of the past - 8 Mortal servants/disciples, with their sects, schisms, and rivalries - 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

1038.title: Sites
1038.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1039.title: 1d6 site
1039.body: - 1-3 lingering signs of their presence and influence (see below) - 4-6 A ruin

1040.title: Lingering signs
1040.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1041.title: 1d6 sign
1041.body: - 1 Something preserved in ice/cold/stasis (next column) - 2 An entity, pacified by the Rime Lords or their disciples (next column) - 3 Fragments of old construction (roads, stairs, walls, foundations, chimneys, etc.), built by their disciples, barely visible after centuries of wind, snow, and ice - 4 A thoughtform (next page) - 5 A monument, or marker (next page) - 6 A formation of dark ice

1042.title: Something preserved
1042.body: For example: - Mortal disciples, frozen in a remote cave, mummified by dry, cold air - An entire valley—fields, houses, livestock, and villagers—encased in ice - A trove of scrolls in some dry mountain cave, chronicling the fall of the Rime Lords and the plight of their surviving disciples - A meadow in spring bloom, trapped in stasis, the grass and flowers never fading, never growing - One of the last thirteen Rime Lords, alive but in stasis, their spirit perpetuating the glacier that keeps Y'aaw'kara contained

1043.title: An entity, pacified
1043.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Sites › An entity, pacified
1043.body: For example: - Some monster, encased in dark ice - A spirit of the wild or Fae , itself a disciple of the Rime Lords, found meditating - A specter , compelled to perform an endless task (counting the snowflakes on a mountain, emptying a river with a thimble, etc.) - An emanation of the Things Below The Things Below, terrible and powerful, held in stasis by the will of an ageless, meditating adept - A feral Rime Lord , held in a stasis field and ringed by glyphs, its wolfen eyes open and hate-filled

1044.title: Thoughtform
1044.body: Objects, sensations, or vignettes of manifest will, formed of base matter or energy, like how a spirit manifests a body. Not real, but real enough. - A grand tree, growing in a lightless cave. Its bark and wood are shaped of stone, but its petals/leaves/fruits crumble to dust and water when shed. - A bubble of stillness on a wind-swept summit, boasting an elaborately decorated altar. The altar is flimsy as frost, dissolving if touched. - A chant that fills a valley, echoing endlessly from the mountainsides. - A spring in a low valley, the water never freezing. Drinking vessels are found at the water's edge, impossibly beautiful, but melting into water if removed.

1045.title: A monument, or marker
1045.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Sites › A monument, or marker
1045.body: For example: - An enormous, stylized statue of a Rime Lord, carved from the mountainside - A vast cave, its walls plastered over, bearing a vivid painting of a Rime Lord radiating power and mortals/spirits/demons bowing before it - A column of dark ice, tall as a tower, embossed with symbols and glyphs - A 50-foot statue of a Rime Lord atop a mountain, made of pebbles held impossibly in place

1046.title: A ruin
1046.body: Pick or roll for themes Rime Lords, structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see The Whitefang Mountains for the Whitefang Mountains , for the Foothills ). Populate it with lingering signs , artifacts and/or dangers of the Rime Lords or the appropriate region.

1047.title: 1d6 structure
1047.body: - 1 Terraced compound of smaller buildings - 2 Single, block-like building - 3 Large, open space - 4 Natural cavern(s), partially worked/carved - 5 Chambers dug into the stone itself - 6 Dome/hall/tower

1048.title: 1d6 purpose
1048.body: - 1 Dwelling (home, dormitory, barracks, etc.) - 2 Infrastructure (stepwell, aqueduct, road, bridge, etc.) - 3 Storage (cellar, vault, library, granary, cemetery, etc.) - 4 Production (workshop, kitchen, farm, abattoir, mine, etc.) - 5 Fortification (wall, rampart, gatehouse, tower, etc.) - 6 Study/practice (shrine, meditation chamber, prayer hall, training hall, etc.) The PCs have sent a logging crew up to the Foothills, but there's trouble! I think they stumbled on a Rime Lord ruin and upset whatever dwells there. I roll on the terrain table and get "ravine/gorge/gully/canyon." I get a theme of "mortal servants/disciples, with their sects/schisms/rivalries." For structure I get "chambers dug into the stone itself," and the purpose is "storage (cellar, vault, library, granary, cemetery, etc.)." It features "elaborate decorations" and is "buried, submerged and/or overgrown, but mostly intact." I ponder and come up with this: A series of tombs, carved into the side of a ravine, containing the remains of a Rime Lord's mortal disciples. The lower tombs are obscured by brush and debris, but the upper tombs are still visible, their entrances carved with guardians and ice-crystal patterns. I suspect someone from the logging crew stumbled in here and upset a guardian spirit.

1049.title: 1d6 architectural elements
1049.body: - 1 Trapezoidal building(s), prominent eaves, windows on upper stories - 2 Elaborate decorations: geometric/crystalline patterns, glyphs, carvings of spirits/monsters/teachers - 3 Walls/floors/ceilings of dark ice, perhaps in impossible shapes - 4 Statues formed of ice/snow/sand/gravel, held in stasis - 5 Large, empty, spacious places - 6 Sound and light behaving strangely (endlessly echoing/reflecting, emanating from nowhere, strangely muffled/dimmed, changing/warping, etc.) Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants—people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

1050.title: 1d6 condition
1050.body: - 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable - 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away - 5 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact - 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

1051.title: Dark ice
1051.body: Purplish ice, which stays cold and frozen except in the hottest furnace. The Rime Lords could form and shape it at will. Some of their disciples learned the trick, too, but the art has largely been lost. Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

1052.title: 1d12 form
1052.body: - 1-3 Fragment (chip/shard/chunk/etc.), from something larger - 4-5 Enigmatic shape (cube/sphere/spiral/etc.), its purpose unclear - 6-7 Mundane object (tool/utensil/container/etc.), though perhaps not easily recognized as such - 8-9 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/tiles/torc/amulet/ring/etc.) - 10-12 Construction (door/stairway/arch/bricks/walls/etc.), likely immobile Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

1053.title: 1d12 characteristic
1053.body: - 1 Breathtakingly beautiful - 2 Dims light/color in its presence - 3 Absorbs/baffles sound - 4 Exceptionally hard, roll 1d6 for degree (otherwise, hard as mundane ice) - 5 Presents a tangible barrier to spirits, even when unmanifest - 6 Pieces persist when removed, instead of melting like normal ice - 7-8 Roll 1d6 twice and combine - 9-12 Just (just) a bit of never-melting ice Consider rolling 1d6 to inform how pristine the dark ice is.

1055.title: Various treasures
1055.body: - A ◇◇ drum, wood and stretched skin, impossibly preserved ( loud , fragile ) - A 1-inch cube of dark ice ( magical , Value 1) - A ◇ 2-foot length of chain, the links made of stone-hard dark ice, bronze clasps at either end ( magical , Value 1) - A thumb-sized topaz, set in bronze on a… belt (?); fills you with dread to look at it ( magical , terrifying , Value 2) - ◇◇ Giant-sized robes of strange silk that's almost impossible to cut/tear; quickly frays and unravels with even minor damage ( warm , clumsy , Value 2) - A deep, round ◇ brass bowl, strangely resonant, the outer edges adorned with sapphires (Value 3) - A child-sized golden statue ( immobile , magical , Value 4) of a meditating Rime Lord, the head radiating cold light

1056.title: A column of ice
1056.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Artifacts › A column of ice
1056.body: magical , immobile Amidst a high, lonely meadow stands a pillar of dark purple ice, 12 feet tall and 2 feet thick. It rests on a circular slab of rune-etched stone, a dozen paces across, untouched by time or nature. There's a stillness to the place, a tension in the air. Something interesting: The runes appear to be some sort of spell, waiting to be triggered; it'd take days of study to puzzle out the command word (and you risk triggering it accidentally). Something useful: You can determine the command word with a few hours of study (less if you're versed in Rime Lord script). When you speak the command word near the column , the dark ice expands in a flash, encasing the stone slab and anything on it. The ice traps even spirits (manifest or not). Mortal prisoners soon perish, their souls ensnared. The dark ice never melts and resists all but the mightiest magic and blows.

1057.title: A dark ice knife
1057.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Artifacts › A dark ice knife
1057.body: hand , magical , crude A hilt of bone, with a spike of dark ice tapering to a sharp point. The ice is hard as stone, and not especially sharp. When you stab an undead entity with the knife , ask the GM whether it is driven by obsession, hunger, and/or vengeance. If the answer is "yes," then deal +1d4 damage (ignores armor), and leave the entity briefly disoriented. Should this reduce the entity to 0 HP, the dark ice knife melts away along with whatever bound the entity to this world.

1058.title: A small black sphere
1058.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Artifacts › A small black sphere
1058.body: magical , indestructible , immobile Found in or near some Rime Lord ruin, this… thing… appears to be a perfect sphere of lead, maybe 2 inches across. It hovers motionless in the air. No amount of physical force can mar it, nor move it from its relative position. When you focus all your will upon the black sphere , roll +WIS: on a 10+ , you can slowly move the sphere with your mind, so long as you keep your attention fixed upon the task; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - Mark a debility and move the sphere as per a 10+ - Move the sphere, but only a few inches - Fling the sphere in a random vector (if it matters: d8 damage, forceful , messy )

1059.title: Minor arcana
1059.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Artifacts › A small black sphere › Minor arcana

1060.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1060.body: - 1 An oversized codex - 2 A sealed cave - 3 A timeless vault - 4 A length of prayer beads - 5 A beautiful scroll - 6 A gold butter lamp

1061.title: Major arcana
1061.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
1061.body: These might very well be associated with the Rime Lords or their disciples. - Noruba's Ice Sphere - Rune-laden Scales

1062.title: Tulpa
1062.body: A tulpa is a constructed spirit , created by the powerful will of another. The Rime Lords crafted many such entities, which linger on in their creators' wake. A tulpa has the spirit and construct tags, with a move that reflects them manifesting or possessing a physical form. They are animated by more than biology (+4 HP) and usually lack vital organs (+3 HP, +1 Armor). Pick or roll a purpose, size, ability, tether, and disposition. Use these elements to inform how it manifests and behaves.

1063.title: 1d12 purpose
1063.body: - 1-2 Battle/sparring/protection - 3-4 Companionship/advice/diplomacy/subterfuge - 5-6 Labor/mundane tasks/convenience - 7-8 Curiosity/instruction/assistance/practice - 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

1064.title: 1d12 size
1064.body: - 1 Cat-sized or smaller ( tiny ) - 2-3 Like a human child ( small ) - 4-7 Human-sized - 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. ( large ) - 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger ( huge )

1065.title: 1d12 ability
1065.body: - 1 Possession (people and/or things) - 2 Emotional influence/control - 3 Elemental manipulation (ice/wind/water/etc.) - 4 Energy manipulation (light/heat/cold/darkness/etc.) - 5 Invisibility/stealth/observation/tracking - 6 Depths of knowledge/lore/experience - 7 Mind-reading/aura-sense/true-seeing - 8 Illusions/false senses/thoughtforms - 9 Strength/speed/prowess/mobility - 10 Dangerous physical attack(s) - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

1066.title: 1d12 tether
1066.body: - 1-2 Immobile object: unable to leave its presence - 3-4 Small, portable object: unable to manipulate it or leave its presence - 5-6 Sculpture or construct: can animate it, but cannot leave its presence - 7-8 Building or complex: can freely move about within, but cannot leave - 9-10 Place or object (roll 1d6 for size/scale): can travel but must periodically return - 11 Living creature/person: can leave their presence for short periods - 12 Untethered: free-roaming, independent

1067.title: 1d12 disposition
1067.body: - 1 Friendly/polite/helpful - 2 Apathetic/bored/lonely - 3 Curious/playful/fickle - 4 Resentful/duty-bound/compelled - 5 Proud/dedicated/honorable - 6 Confused/erratic/distraught - 7 Boastful/aggressive/hostile - 8 Malicious/cruel/devious - 9 Timid/shy/meek - 10 Focused/obsessive/anxious - 11 Roll 1d10, but make it extreme - 12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

1068.title: Gingkara
1068.body: Horde , spirit , construct , organized HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks organs) or 4 vs. stabbing (just bones) Damage bony fingers d4 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities tethered to one specific bone Instinct to keep their master's house in order - Animate its particular skeleton - Grab on and pull them down with numbers - Reassemble themselves (regaining 1d4 HP), unless their tether is shattered Some of the simplest tulpas to create, each of these spirits is bound to a skeleton that's been stripped of flesh and intricately carved. The Rime Lords and their disciples used them as domestic servants, tending to material needs and serving as guardians when needed. Gingkara still attempt to preserve their master's homes, for what else should they do with their existence?

1069.title: Khyagseng
1069.body: Group , spirit , construct , resolute , observant , fierce HP 13; Armor 2 (icy "skin," lacks organs) Damage icy jaws and paws d8+2 with advantage ( hand , close , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities tethered to its pedestal; body made of dark ice; cold-proof Instinct to repel intruders - See through illusions and deceptions - Offer a rumbling (though polite) warning - Repair itself while on its pedestal These tulpas inhabit dark-ice sculptures of three-eyed, stylized lions—muscular, proud, fierce—standing atop decorated pedestals. Always found in pairs, guarding doorways or thresholds. They dutifully challenge any who approach without an invitation from their master. Never mind that their masters are all long gone. Some of the grander Rime Lord ruins boast large khyagseng made of dark ice that is strong as stone (+8 HP, +1 Armor, +1 damage). These greater guardians can also unleash a thunderous roar, deafening and terrifying, likely to trigger avalanches.

1070.title: Mkhalang
1070.body: Solitary , huge , spirit , construct , cautious , devious HP 27; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage trample d8+3 with disadvantage ( hand , close ) or ice-tusks d8+7 with disadvantage ( reach , forceful , messy , crude , 1 piercing) Special qualities tethered to its dome but far-ranging; perfect memory; unable to lie Instinct to amuse itself at the expense of others - Fly quickly across the skies - Manifest a mammoth-like form of freezing mist and ice (harmed only by salt, fire, heat) - Suck the strength and warmth from anyone they trample - Reveal just enough to cause trouble The Rime Lords created these mighty spirits to act as emissaries and spies. A few still remain, tethered to the meditation-halls in which they were made but roaming the Whitefangs in search of novelty and diversion. They have grown bored in their masters' absence, and from boredom has grown a subtle cruelty. The people of the Whitefangs treat them with respect, for they are still the emissaries of the Rime Lords. But they also know better than to trust their counsel.

1071.title: Shaksa
1071.body: Solitary , spirit , construct , hoarder , magical , terrifying HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage antler of jagged bone d10+2 ( close , messy , 1 piercing) or stone hooves d8 ( hand ) Special qualities tethered to its cemetery, can only leave to track looters/vandals Instinct to preserve the sanctity of the dead - Fill an intruder with dread - Manifest a form of bone and gravedirt (unharmed by stabbing; vulnerable to rain/running water) - Track a looter or vandal, unerringly The Rime Lords and their disciples created shaksa to guard over cemeteries and charnel grounds. They usually watch, unseen and unmanifest. A shaksa manifests only within its cemetery, appearing as a powerful goat made of rock and soil, with a human (?) skull for a head, three eyes of floating light, and a single great antler of jagged bone. Once manifest, it can leave the cemetery in pursuit of looters or vandals. Should this form be destroyed, the spirit returns to the cemetery and cannot remanifest until the next sunset.

1073.title: Entities
1073.body: - Spirits of the wild , following the Rime Lord's teachings and/or acting as guardians - Spirits, beasts, and people corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below: encased in dark ice, bound by glyphs, and/or trapped in stasis - Wraiths of the Rime Lord's disciples, who failed to tame their body's needs and succumbed to eternal hunger - Ghosts of the Rime Lord's disciples, who pushed themselves too far and cannot accept their failure

1074.title: Hazards
1074.body: - Altitude sickness - Avalanches - Hypothermia & frostbite - Ice: slippery, jagged, unstable, very cold - Pools of liquid air, so cold it burns (d8 damage, messy , ignores armor) - Pockets of frozen time, ensnaring any who enter; or fragile and holding something dangerous in place - Thoughtforms composed of dense particulate matter on the verge of collapse or hiding some danger

1075.title: Deadly cold
1075.body: Rime Lord ruins sometimes include areas that are much, much colder than even the surrounding mountains. Dangerously, lethally cold. - Show a warning: a subtle change in light/sound, breath or smoke condensing/freezing, hoarfrost - Weigh them down with frost - Have gear stick/freeze/break - Inflict hypothermia & frostbite , quickly and with little warning

1076.title: Adept
1076.body: Solitary , cautious , cunning , magical HP 16; Armor 2 (toughness, skill) Damage freezing touch or aura d8 ( hand or reach , area , ignores armor) Special qualities cold-proof; spirit sight; no longer ages; needs little food/drink/sleep; powerful will

1077.title: Instinct see below
1077.breadcrumb: Rime Lords › Adept › Instinct see below
1077.body: - Send their mind forth into the spirit world, their bodies in stasis - Reveal a teaching/technique of the Rime Lords - 1-4 additional moves (next page) The Rime Lords taught that the self could be perfected. That the needs and frailties of the body could be purged. That the mind could free itself of the flesh and know the unseen world of spirits and fundamental forces. That the will could conquer all. The goal was nothing short of immortality, the preservation of the perfect self. For most of the Rime Lords' mortal disciples, these teachings did little more than strengthen the body and clear the mind. But for some—the adepts—these teachings unlocked a fragment of their masters' power. Rivalries were fierce among the adepts even when the Rime Lords walked the land. When the last Rime Lords disappeared, these rivalries flourished into schisms, sects, and violent purges. Some of the adepts fell. Others went into hiding. Still others passed their knowledge on, building schools and monasteries around them. To create an adept, start with the stats above. Pick or roll for an instinct and 1-4 moves to represent their capabilities.

1078.title: 1d12 instinct
1078.body: - 1-2 To atone - 3-4 To avoid disruption or distraction - 5-6 To drive their students towards perfection - 7-8 To prove their superiority - 9-10 To tame chaos - 11-12 To test themselves or their students

1079.title: 1d12 move
1079.body: - 1 <sup ä</sup As a spirit: animate a large mass of ice ( large , +8 HP, +2 armor, +3 damage, forceful ) - 2 <sup ä</sup As a spirit: manifest a body from snow/frost/mist (+4 HP, +1 armor, unharmed by stabbing or cutting) - 3 <sup ä</sup Calm/diminish/suppress the elements - 4 <sup ä</sup Conjure ice/dark ice ( near , area , grabby ), but lose 1d4 HP - 5 <sup ä</sup Craft thoughtforms from base matter/light/energy - 6 <sup ä</sup Create a minor tulpa ( tiny , 8 HP, 1 armor, 1d4 damage), or a more potent one with some sacrifice - 7 <sup ä</sup Dismiss/suppress magic with a touch - 8 <sup ä</sup Exert great force with their mind ( near , forceful ) - 9 <sup ä</sup Impose a compulsion/command/taboo upon a spirit - 10 <sup ä</sup Slow/stop time in an area - 11 <sup ä</sup Calm/suppress emotions - 12 <sup ä</sup Absorb intense heat/energy/power/force unharmed

1080.title: False adept
1080.body: Solitary , spirit , undead , magical , devious , organized HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage life-draining touch d8 ( hand , ignores armor) Special qualities tethered to some object; telepathic; powerful will Instinct to amass more followers, more power - Attract followers with promises of greatness, easily achieved - Eat away at someone's emotions/memories/will - Possess a follower (or one's corpse) - Siphon a follower's lifeforce to mimic some art of the Rime Lords Some who practice the Rime Lord's teachings push themselves beyond their limits. Their bodies fail but they become rage-filled specters or ever-hungry wraiths . The Rime Lords had many techniques for dealing with such abominations. Imagine, though, an adept who saw their body and their life as a resource to expend rather than a liability to overcome. One who burned health and vitality to empower their practice. One who saw vast, unused potential in those around them. Such an adept might deliberately sever their mind and spirit from their body and gather a herd of followers (victims) around them, to serve as fuel for their mighty arts.

1081.title: Rime Lord
1081.body: Solitary , large , cautious , cunning , magical , organized , terrifying HP 26; Armor 5 (potent wards, skill) Damage conjured ice d12+3 ( any range , area , grabby , forceful ) or heat-drain d12+1 ( reach , ignores armor) Special qualities spirit sight; telepathic; cold-proof; ageless; no need to eat/drink/sleep/breathe; aura of bitter cold Instinct to seek perfection (in themselves, in their surroundings, in others) - Overawe those in its presence - Act simultaneously in both the physical and spirit worlds - Impose its mighty will on the world: stilling emotions, freezing time, dismissing magic, reshaping reality, etc. - Create a tulpa or thoughtform Only thirteen Rime Lords survive; each remains in stasis in some key place of power. Their spirits are merged in an eternal dance and song, continuously weaving the dark ice glacier that keeps the voice of Y'aaw'kara bound and prevents madness from spreading unchecked across the world. Pray, o mortal. Pray that they continue their vigil. Pray that their wills do not falter. Pray that no interloper is so foolish as to disturb them. Pray for your family, your friends, and all that you know. Pray that they do not wake.

1082.title: The Ruined Tower
1082.body: A long day's march from Stonetop, a wide, low hill rises from the Flats . A pile of earth and stone squats atop that hill. Metal spires reach out of the pile, piercing the sky. If you squint, you can see the rubble for what it is: a broken tower, once grand in scale, long ago brought low. Everyone from Stonetop knows about the Ruined Tower and the giant-sized tunnels beneath it. Adventuresome folks sometimes think to explore them. Most of the time, they get spooked and don't go far. But those who've delved deeper have come back with wondrous things. Or never come back at all.

1083.title: Stormcatcher's tower
1083.body: Before the tower fell, this was home to Stormcatcher, last (?) of the Tempest Lords . The tower was like the mast of some mighty ship, catching wind and lightning, holding their power in the Storm Orb , fueling her many magics. For centuries, Stormcatcher's tower stood betwixt the realms of the other Makers . They would trade with her, and seek her council or her aid. But though the Makers' Roads ran to her very gate, she stood apart from them all. Yet she did not live alone. A bustling community grew up around her tower. Laborers, artisans, and scholars. Farmers and domestic help. Soldiers, even. Generations of families. Refugees seeking new homes. Ambassadors and outcasts from the other Makers. Seekers, misfits, and castaways from distant lands. When the Time of Cataclysm came when the Stone Lords' slaves rebelled and unleashed the Things Below , when the Rime Lords' disciples turned savage, when the Forge Lords' cities were buried by ash and war—Stormcatcher's tower endured. A bastion of civilization. An island of safety. A refuge. It could not last. Cultists of Og'molok the Faceless Horde swarmed the ramparts, driving the defenders back. Og'molok itself burst forth from below as a wave of skittering horrors. Stormcatcher unleashed a mighty spell that turned the attackers into glass. But that same spell shattered the Storm Orb. It laid waste to the tower and broke a stretch of the Makers' Roads. It wrought Stormcatcher's doom. Many of the tower's residents fled through a portal beneath the tower. But most were slaughtered by the attackers, or vitrified along with them, or caught in the Storm Orb's explosion. A slim few survivors remained and tried to rebuild, but they soon fell prey to the horrors now loose upon the world. In the centuries since, many have tried to claim the ruin, or plunder its treasures and its secrets. But all have failed, for one reason or another. And the Ruined Tower remains.

1085.title: Everyone from Stonetop knows:
1085.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Lore › Everyone from Stonetop knows:
1085.body: - Where the tower is - About the giant-sized tunnels below - That some brave (foolish) folk have explored them, and found terrible and wondrous things

1086.title: One might know:
1086.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Lore › One might know:
1086.body: - A tale or two about folks who've explored here and what they found - Details about the ruins above ground, or some of the upper tunnels - That the tower is a ruin of the Tempest Lords - That it was the last of the Makers' strongholds to fall - That pieces of the tower can be found strewn all around the Flats - That the outer ruins, the tower itself, and the upper tunnels have all been heavily looted, but still yield the occasional trinket

1087.title: Very few know:
1087.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Lore › Very few know:
1087.body: - Stormcatcher's story - Her relationship with the other Makers - How exactly the tower fell - Reliable details about the depths - Anything at all about Og'molok, the Faceless Horde

1088.title: Questions
1088.body: - Which one of you has braved these ruins before? What kept you from going very far? - What wondrous trinket back in Stonetop supposedly came from here? Who has it now, and what do they use it for? - What marvel is said to lie below the Ruined Tower? From whom did you hear such tales? - When the nightmares come and you dream of the tower's fall, are you one of the doomed defenders? Or part of the slavering horde?

1089.title: Hooks
1089.body: - Bandits squat in the ruins, and find an artifact that makes them extra dangerous. - A sorcerer is raising her demonic child in the tower's depths. - A drunk wanderer claims to have explored the depths, but his friends didn't make it out. - A southern scholar plans to delve the tower and seeks local help. - Frequent earthquakes coincide with terrible nightmares, especially for those touched by violence. The nightmares feature the tower, its fall, or the tunnels below.

1090.title: Getting there
1090.body: To get there from Stonetop, take the West Road 3-4 hours to the Crossroads . From there, it's 5-6 hours southwest across the Flats . - It'll take at least a day each way, and a corresponding amount of supplies - You'll need to bring light sources (if you intend to explore the tunnels) - You need to watch out for nosgalau and other dangers of the Flats - If there have been heavy snows, you must wait until the thaw, or… - You'll need snowshoes, and… - The way will still be grueling; you risk exhausting yourselves and/or your gear

1091.title: Areas
1091.body: - Overgrown berm , about 15-20' tall, encircling the tower 3/4 mile out. Huge dressed stones poke out, similar to the Old Wall . - Outer ruins between the berm and the tower. Once a thriving town, now just a jumble of ruins mostly swallowed by the prairie. - A shallow crater , stretching 400' from the tower's northeast edge. Grass/soil/mud/standing water cover glassy, slaglike bedrock. - The tower itself : 300' across. Northeast third is mostly rubble, but to the south and west a few floors remain, with walls 200' tall. - The upper tunnels start 100' below the surface. Heavily looted, very haunted, mostly maintained by a ghostly Forge Lord and his patchwork constructs . - The depths are even further down, a warren of chambers, halls, stairs, chutes, and burrows, all in various states of repair. Full of infrastructure, artifacts, experiments, prisons, and vitrified horrors.

1092.title: Aboveground
1092.body: The Ruined Tower sits atop a wide, low, lightly wooded hill. From a distance, it's just a landmark. As one gets closer, it becomes clear just how massive the tower once was.

1094.title: Anywhere
1094.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Impressions › Anywhere
1094.body: - Southerly wind, ever-blowing - Squeak and shriek of metal spires when the wind picks up - Metallic barks and chirps from high up, the snap of a metal cable - The looming scale of the thing, big as the landscape, hard to take in - Smell of ozone, even on a clear day - A fluting warble; a mocking laugh, a flash of bright blue feathers

1095.title: Outer ruins
1095.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Impressions › Outer ruins
1095.body: - Piles of earth dotting the land, tufts of grass and brush growing on them - The uneven, rugged terrain, making it so you can never catch your stride - Occasional bits of broken pottery or metal, centuries-old, just lying there, turned up from the dirt

1096.title: Inside a building or the tower
1096.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Impressions › Inside a building or the tower
1096.body: - Cave-like darkness, coolness, and stillness as they step inside - Faded mosaics/carvings, obscured by time, vandalism, erosion, lichen - Dim sunbeams, full of floating dust - Wind whistling in through cracks in walls/ceilings; water dripping through those cracks in the rain

1097.title: Terrain
1097.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1098.title: 1d12 terrain
1098.body: - 1 Barren patch of sand/dust/glass - 2 Mud/standing water/deep snow - 3 Ditch, gully, or embankment; the outline of buried ruins - 4 Exposed wall(s), crumbling and covered in moss/lichen - 5-6 Stretch of grass, 1d6+2 feet tall - 7 Shrubs, thicket, tree(s)—maybe even dool trees - 8 Huge stone slab, partly buried; a fallen piece of the tower - 9-10 Pile of dirt/stone and a nearby pit; an excavation or burrow - 11-12 building (see below), at least somewhat intact

1099.title: 1d12 building
1099.body: - 1-8 From before the tower's fall; pick or roll for its purpose - 9-11 Built after the tower's fall; pick or roll for its purpose - 12 A barrow roll 1d8 for size)

1100.title: 1d12 purpose
1100.body: - 1-2 Home/barracks/living space - 3-4 Kitchen/laundry/bath/latrine - 5-6 Gathering/meetings/civic life - 7-8 Storage/cellar/stable/tomb - 9-10 Work/production/creation - 11-12 Esoterica/experimentation

1101.title: The Tower itself
1101.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › The Tower itself
1101.body: The base of the tower is 300' across; rubble and piled-up soil spread out even farther. The south and west sections still stand tall, rising 200' from the hilltop. Spires: Four metal spires support the tower's walls, one at each cardinal direction: massive, rusted, straining towards the sky. The south and west spires each stretch another 200' or so above the walls ( 400' tall total). A length of cable dangles from the tip of the south spire. Rubble: The northeast third of the tower is just rubble, thick walls, and soil piled 30' high, topped with grass and shrubs. The rubble is riddled with trenches, pits, old excavations, buried rooms, burrows. Ground floor: The south and west thirds of the tower are intact at ground level, containing huge vaulted chambers ( 40' ceilings), including: - A parlor with tall narrow windows. Stylized friezes (faded, vandalized, full of bird nests) show scenes of the Tempest Lord empire. - The baths , a deep pool full of vile, stagnant water. The walls are busted open, the pipes looted. - The great hall , mostly intact, with a giant stone table. The ceiling is enchanted to show the night sky. Human-scale servant passages/rooms parallel the larger chambers and halls. Inlays: Rooms inside the tower once had aetherium inlays all along their walls and ceilings, but those were gouged away long ago by looters and thunder drakes . Main stairwell: A circular shaft at the tower's center, 30' wide. Goes up to the second floor, down 100' to the upper tunnels. Human-scale stairs spiral along the outer wall, far too small for Makers. Did they fly ? Upper floors: The second floor is 50' up and partly open to the sky, covering the southwest half of the tower. Accessible via the stairwell, holes in ceilings, or climbing the outer walls. A few intact rooms remain, where blue magpies stash shinies and build elaborate mosaics. The third and fourth floors are another 40' up each, accessible only via tricky climbs. Unstable, with only a few "rooms," all of which are at least partly open to the air. Magpie nests abound.

1102.title: Creating rooms
1102.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Creating rooms
1102.body: For any specific room that you establish, pick or roll for a purpose (previous page) and combine it with a Tempest Lord theme Tempest Lords. Imagine the room before the fall. Now, how was it affected by the attack? By the Storm Orb exploding? By centuries of looting, vandalism, weathering, and neglect? Who or what has lived here since? What details remains to tell this story? What discoveries are waiting to be found?

1103.title: Discoveries
1103.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1104.title: 1d6 discovery
1104.body: ^discovery-p338 - 1 Change of terrain The Ruined Tower and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 remnants of the past (see below) - 4 An encounter (next column) - 5 Useful or valuable flora , growing among the ruins - 6 Something valuable (next column)

1105.title: 1d6 remnants of the past
1105.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › 1d6 remnants of the past
1105.body: - 1 Exposed stonework, marred by cataclysmic violence (melted, shattered, etc.) - 2 Bits of ancient daily life (pots, tools, utensils, etc.), probably broken, possibly giant-sized - 3 Signs of squatters/explorers: bones, litter, graffiti, excavation, crude construction, etc. - 4 Vitrified remains - 5 Ancient writing or artwork, faded/obscured but still legible ( immobile , fragile , Value 0-2) - 6 A building/room, undisturbed for centuries, with treasure! 1d6 you'd otherwise find only in the depths

1106.title: 1d6 encounter
1106.body: - 1 A pyped , hiding from the sun or out at night, digging old bones to sup on the marrow - 2 Blue magpies , digging for shinies, foraging, or gossiping, maybe mocking/pranking the PCs - 3 Critter(s)—fox, skunk, stoat, hares, prairie dogs, starlings, vultures, etc.—curious, annoying, maybe just cute - 4 Larger animal(s)—deer, a ground sloth, wild sheep, maybe some aurochs —grazing near the ruins - 5 Someone else, exploring/making camp, wary but not hostile: some Hillfolk , adventurers and an antiquarian , cultists , a sorcerer , etc. - 6 A hidden entrance to the upper tunnels or the even the depths (a caved-in ceiling, a burrow, a chute, a secret door, etc.)

1107.title: 1d6 valuable
1107.body: - 1 Big ◇◇ bits of metal (Value 1): melted/cracked/corroded/rusted - 2 A gold ingot (Value 2), once jewelry, fused into a lump - 3 A piece of makerglass , overlooked by prior looters - 4 A shard of the Storm Orb (next page) - 5 A half-buried plaque , or some other artifact of the Tempest Lords. - 6 An artifact of the Barrow Builders, evidence that they were here, too

1108.title: Shards of the Storm Orb
1108.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Shards of the Storm Orb
1108.body: It was a filigree sphere of aetherium Tempest Lords, maybe 50' across. It floated near the tower, a battery and a focus for tremendous elemental power. Stormcatcher's final spell caused the Orb to explode. Shards were flung across the Flats The Flats, into the tower itself, and deep into the soil—to someday be unearthed by erosion, earthquakes, or burrowing beasts. If the PCs encounter a shard, pick or roll for its size and signs. 1d6 Size ------- ------------------------------ 1 Small (Value 0) 2 ◇ (Value 1) 3-4 ◇◇ (Value 2) 5 ◇◇ ( cumbersome , Value 2) 6 Immobile (Value 3 or more)

1109.title: 1d6 signs
1109.body: - 1 Hums/whines, painfully loud to those who can sense spirits - 2 Vibrations make odd patterns in soil, prevent growth - 3 Floats in a pocket of air, loosely covered by rock/soil - 4 Makes skin tingle, hair stand up - 5 Nearby soil/stone is blue-white, plants/lichen glow - 6 Roll twice Shards of the Storm Orb are unstable aetherium ( magical , dangerous ). If damaged or overloaded, they might explode or violently discharge energy. But they are also etched with arcane runes. Find a big enough piece, or assemble enough smaller ones, and you might unlock some of the Tempest Lords' secrets.

1110.title: Vitrified remains
1110.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Vitrified remains
1110.body: Stormcatcher's last spell turned anyone it touched into lumps of jagged glass. It was directed at Og'molok The Ruined Tower and the skittering horrors The Ruined Tower, but many of the attacking cultists were caught by the spell as well. So were some of the tower's residents. Above ground, vitrified remains are mostly people (attackers or residents), with a few domesticated animals. Most are jagged lumps of black-grey glass, only vaguely recognizable as what they once were, and/or broken by time or vandalism. These are covered in sharp edges (1d6 damage, messy , 1 piercing). A few of these vitrified remains, though, are perfect glass statues, the fear or frenzy of their final moments frozen on their faces ( immobile , fragile , Value 3 to the right buyer). Vitrification Tempest Lords can be reversed, but it's up to you to decide how.

1112.title: Hazards
1112.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Dangers › Hazards
1112.body: - Old construction, giving way under foot or collapsing on someone - A difficult climb or some missing stairs, a reason to risk it - Earthquakes and tremors - Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an ankle or worse - Nasty weather: heat, cold, wind, sleet, hail, rain, snow, thunder (not so much lightning, as the spires draw it all) - Prairie fire among the outer ruins - Unstable spells, aetherium , perilous stone

1113.title: Monsters and such
1113.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Aboveground › Dangers › Monsters and such
1113.body: - The nosgalau , at night - Thunder drakes , digging for metal, especially aetherium - Any of the other beasts of the Flats - An encampment of Hillfolk , bandits , cultists , etc. - Looters/explorers: adventurers , an antiquarian , a hdour , a sorcerer , etc. - Ghosts or revenants of those who died when the tower fell, or from some later misadventure - A pyped , digging up old bones where they can

1114.title: Blue magpie
1114.body: Horde , tiny , devious , organized , hoarder , magical HP 1; Armor 1 (size) Damage peck d4-2 w/disadvantage (hand) Special qualities smart as a child (at least); can speak Tempest Lord (understood by all) Instinct to amuse/impress its friends - Pretend to be just a dumb bird - Mimic sounds or voices - Mock or trick someone - Steal something - Vanish with a hrummm and reappear nearby ( reload ) Like big crows with white-and-azure plumage. Their songs are piping and musical, playful and complex, sprinkled with actual voices and other unlikely sounds. A few dozen of them nest in the tower and the surrounding ruins. Smaller mischiefs nest in the Flats. Stormcatcher kept blue magpies as pets, and their descendants have passed down much oral lore. Sneak up on them, and you might overhear magpies telling each other tales that southern scholars would kill to know. Magpies find humans boorish, but fun to mess with, and they have such neat stuff. They love shiny objects, especially glass and fragments of larger things, and spend years making elaborate mosaics or piecing puzzles together.

1115.title: Belowground
1115.body: Most of the rooms and halls below the tower are spacious, vaulted to 30'. Doorways are 25' tall and mostly 10' wide. There are a few human-scale rooms, but not many.

1117.title: Throughout
1117.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Impressions › Throughout
1117.body: - A sense of smallness, of being swallowed by the dark - Walls and ceilings beyond the limit of your light - Distant sounds: dripping, rattling clanging, moaning (?), footfalls, skittering - The thought of all that stone and earth above you

1118.title: The upper tunnels
1118.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Impressions › The upper tunnels
1118.body: - Surprisingly little dust, mud, debris - Paths worn smooth, as from the tread of countless feet - Faded gouges in flagstones, like heavy, sharp things were dragged across them, long long ago - Carefully stacked rubble, plugging holes in walls, blocking off passages - The sense that you're not alone, that you're being watched

1119.title: The depths
1119.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Impressions › The depths
1119.body: - Kicked-up dust, catching in your throat, making eyes water - The looming menace of a vitrified horror, the skip in your heart before you realize it's just dead glass - A thrum or whine, felt in your gut, your teeth, the back of your eyes - Wait, haven't we been here before?

1120.title: Aetherium inlays
1120.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Aetherium inlays
1120.body: The four metal spires extend down through the bedrock. They run through a number of underground rooms and passages, as freestanding pillars or embedded in walls. Most rooms in and below the tower bear aetherium inlays, spreading out from the metal spires. The inlays distribute power and anchor certain spells. Or at least, they used to. The aetherium has been stripped from most of the upper tunnels, either carefully salvaged by patchwork constructs or gouged out by looters. More inlays survive in the depths, but even there, many have been marred by star-moles or the claws of skittering horrors , or burnt out and reduced to crumbly chalk. Where intact, inlays glow and pulse in response to winds above ground. When lightning strikes a spire, the aetherium flashes a bright blue-white. Some inlays are stable, but others are erratic and prone to discharge (1d8 damage, forceful , maybe loud ).

1121.title: Sites
1121.body: - Central chamber at the base of the main stairwell . Big, well-kept, drains in floor, multiple exits. - Forge and nearby rooms, haunted by Ferocedes , packed with tools/parts and constructs in various states of repair. - Aetherium crucible , near the forge. Dusty, unused, broken beyond Ferocedes's ability to fix with what's currently available to him. - Barracks for soldiers who guarded the Portal (next column). Haunted by evacuees who hid and died there. - Latrine near the barracks, with rattling, moaning, clanging pipes and still-working (!) showers. - Large chamber , out of the way, the entrance blocked by piled stones. Packed full of vitrified horrors by Ferocedes's constructs. - Frozen room , a chokepoint filled with dark ice , encasing dozens of skittering horrors and an Ustrina who served as Stormcatcher's steward. - Oubliette , a dead-end hall with multiple cells/vaults. Each holds or held a dangerous artifact or entity in stasis. Some are torn open. The rest are guarded by wards, devices, constructs, magic. - Bottommost chamber , 400' across, partly flooded. Holds the feet of all four spires, a massive toroidal coil, hundreds of vitrified horrors , and the trapped manifestation of Og'molok, the Faceless Horde .

1122.title: The Portal
1122.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › The Portal
1122.body: In the upper tunnels, one can find a pair of double-doors: locked and warded, barricaded from within, with countless gouges clawed across their surface. Beyond those doors: an austere room with a dozen vitrified people, blown-out lightning sconces , a makerglass window to the next room, and yet another locked door. Beyond that: a once-stately sitting room (the furniture used for the barricade), a few more vitrified evacuees. Another door, this one ajar. One last room. The far wall is a solid white slab, etched with a magic circle. It's a portal , connected to those in the Labyrinth , Three Coven Lake , Vor Svetelik , and so forth. It is fully functional, and can be used via Opening the Way . Layers of wards are etched in the floor, directed towards the portal, guarding against things coming out.

1123.title: Rooms & passages
1123.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Rooms & passages
1123.body: When you need to describe a room or passage, pick a shape and one or more features, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1124.title: 1d12 shape
1124.body: 1d12 Shape ------- ---------------------------------- 1-3 Hallway, relatively narrow 4-5 Square/rectangle/block 6-7 Intersection 8 Round/sphere/dome/bowl 9 Hexagon/octagon/etc. 10 Irregular (L-shape, blob, etc.) 11-12 Cluster: roll d10, 1d4+1 times

1125.title: 1d12 feature
1125.body: - 1 Obstruction: locked door, rubble, cave-in, barricade, many vitrified horrors , etc. - 2 Fungus/lichen/slime/vermin - 3 Drips/leaks/mud/standing water - 4 Pipes, valves, pumps - 5 Human-scale construction - 6 Stairs/ramp/chute/platforms - 7 Grandiose scale, a massive room - 8 Unplanned exit/passage (burrow, collapsed construction, etc.) - 9 Extensive and/or notably intact aetherium inlays ; perhaps a metal pillar (one of the four spires ) - 10 A wonder (see below) - 11 Roll 1d10 twice - 12 Roll 1d10 three times Consider rolling 1d6 for the scope or condition of any room/passage/feature. Fill it with discoveries and/or dangers as you see fit.

1126.title: Wonders
1126.body: Stormcatcher kept many experiments, curiosities, and dangerous phenomena. Pick 1, roll 1d12, or make up your own.

1127.title: 1d12 wonder
1127.body: - 1 A fountain, its hidden pumps powered by aetherium. - 2 Nexus of pipes, pumps, boilers. By a stairwell, kept up by patchwork constructs . - 3 Deep pit, smooth walls, a jellied horror trapped below. Used as a midden pit. - 4 A huge stone slab, with fittings that make hot, steady flame on demand. - 5 Room full of plants ( exotic , useful , valuable , dangerous ). Lit by a makerglass sun, watered by pipes, tended by a fertility spirit. - 6 Huge orrery. Bits are melted, fused, burnt out; "stars" stuck in a potent cosmic alignment. - 7 Knee-high dust, shapes itself into whatever an occupant thinks of. Crumbles if removed from room. - 8 Freestanding arch. Pass through and you vanish. Exit 5 seconds later, seems instantaneous to you. - 9 Tight-packed aetherium inlays. Hazy images show what anyone in the room is about to do. - 10 Sliding tiles in walls/ceiling/floor change gravity's direction and/or intensity in the room. - 11 Airless room with small floating black sphere, annihilates any matter it touches. - 12 Black lightning crackles between two prongs, 3' apart. Fine glass dust all around. A vitrifying bolt , just barely contained.

1128.title: Discoveries
1128.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1129.title: 1d6 discovery
1129.body: - 1 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 2-3 A vignette of the fall of the tower (see below): a vitrified tableau, remains, physical evidence, psychic imprints, writing, etc. - 4 An encounter (next column) - 5 An opportunity (next page) - 6 treasure! (next page)

1130.title: 1d6 vignette of the fall
1130.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › 1d6 vignette of the fall
1130.body: - 1 A wanton slaughter - 2 A desperate flight - 3 A game of cat and mouse - 4 A grim last stand - 5 A heroic sacrifice - 6 A cruel betrayal

1131.title: 1d6 encounter
1131.body: - 1 A dangerous entity: imprisoned, bound, in stasis - 2 A pyped , trawling the depths for bones to sup on - 3 The ghost of someone who died in the attack, still running, trying to escape - 4 Star-moles : digging, foraging, or being hunted - 5 Uncanny fungus/lichen/vermin (glowing, humming, feeding on ghosts, etc.), maybe dangerous but not outright hostile - 6 A patchwork construct , on some task for Ferocedes

1132.title: 1d6 opportunity
1132.body: - 1 Something Ferocedes would want: a construct's core , a part for the aetherium crucible , etc. - 2 A map/path to a place they seek - 3 A hidden door/room/passage (secret, buried, or obscured) - 4 A good/safe place to rest - 5 Lore about the portal: a time-worn missive , sigils of other portals, a treatise on the axiom of local contagion, etc. - 6 A key (or similar), a way to bypass an obstacle elsewhere

1133.title: 1d6 treasure!
1133.body: - 1 Cache of metal ingots, tools, parts, etc. ( immobile , Value 2 or 3) - 2 Some… (roll 1d6 or pick) - 1 … dark ice - 2 … makerglass - 3 … orichalcum - 4 … black iron - 5-6 … aetherium - 3-4 An artifact of the Tempest Lords - 5 An artifact from another group of Makers : a gift, a trophy, an object of study, or a danger locked away - 6 An artifact of primordial power or the Things Below , kept for study and/or safekeeping

1135.title: The environment
1135.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Dangers › The environment
1135.body: - Getting lost in the maze of tunnels - Torches running low, time passing faster than you think - Unstable construction , caused by cataclysm, time, earthquakes, excavation, star-moles - Open stairwells, worn-smooth stairs, a lack of railings, a nasty fall - The exhausting trudge back up hundreds of stairs - Floors wet from rain, snow melt, or leaky pipes, slick and/or muddy - Weird growths/bugs: filling the air, shrieking if disturbed, absorbing raw power, spitting it back out - Earthquakes or tremors, even worse down here than up above

1136.title: Ancient wonders
1136.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Dangers › Ancient wonders
1136.body: - Damaged aetherium inlays , erratic and unstable (1d8 damage, forceful , maybe loud ) - Pressurized pipes, spewing steam or scalding water if ruptured (1d8 damage, area ) - Metal bulkheads, rigged to drop and contain runaway spells/experiments/prisoners - Raw chemics, neatly stowed, spilled, seeping - Machines, running erratically, fully able to rend flesh and bone - Broken laws of reality, and whatever that does to mind/body/soul - Radiation , hopefully contained, probably not - Smoke, fire, and heat in the haunted forge - Perilous stone : stolen, imported, borrowed, replicated

1137.title: Corruption, as one goes deeper
1137.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Dangers › Corruption, as one goes deeper
1137.body: - Disquiet and unease , especially near masses of vitrified horrors - Whispers and visions , coming from the horrors, or from below, or from everywhere at once - Unnatural phenomena , as one approaches the bottommost chamber and what remains of Og'molok .

1138.title: Vitrified horrors
1138.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Belowground › Vitrified horrors
1138.body: Skittering horrors , fused into black, jagged glass by Stormcatcher's last spell. There are more and more as you go deeper. In some places, they clog stairs or passages, making it difficult to squeeze by without your gear or your flesh getting shredded (1d4 to 1d8 damage, messy , 1 piercing).

1139.title: Lightning sconce
1139.body: Magical , construct , immobile HP 1; Armor 4 (tiny, metal fittings, made of crystal) Damage lightning 1d6+2 ( near , forceful , reload , ignores armor) A glowing gem, set in a fixture of aetherium. Found in pairs, or sets of up to six. Each set is keyed to something (a pass phrase, a hand signal, a color of robes, a style of locket, etc.). Get too close without the key, and zzzolt , they start blasting.

1140.title: Various entities
1140.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Lightning sconce › Various entities
1140.body: - Ghosts or revenants of looters or residents - Pypeds seeking bones - Metal constructs , lost to Ferocedes, or else greatly prized - Elemental vortices , guarding something - Cultists , a thrall , and/or a sorcerer , eking out a life - A deathless one , here to claim the tower's secrets, now trapped - An archon or spirit of the wild , bound and possibly put to use.

1141.title: Star-mole
1141.body: Horde , cautious , devious , organized , hardy HP 7; Armor 0 Damage caustic spit or blood d6 w/disadvantage ( hand , close , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities blind; tremorsense; bioluminescent Instinct to lure predators into traps - Soften stone with its spit, then dig through it - Spurt caustic blood when hurt - Scamper away, hide, act as bait - Trigger a collapse or cave-in Big, naked mole-rats, like 5' long, with saggy skin, glowing freckles, and clusters of wriggling feelers on their noses. They eat vermin and fungus that grow near leaking pipes. They also tunnel through bedrock, collapse passages, and expose sealed-off rooms. Every so often, they wake a skittering horror from its torpor. This spells doom for the colony, unless they can trap it or cut off access to the nest. They'll treat the PCs as a similar threat.

1142.title: Ferocedes Ogran, ghostly Forge Lord
1142.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Ferocedes Ogran, ghostly Forge Lord
1142.body: Solitary , large , spirit , undead , magical , organized , obsessive , absentminded HP 23; Armor 4 (metal, amorphous) Damage pummel d12+3 ( close , reach , forceful ) or barrage of metal d10+1 ( near , area , messy , forceful , reload , 1 piercing) Special qualities tethered to his forge, and can't venture far from it; speaks Tempest Lord (understood by all) Instinct to preserve/restore the tower, to ignore harsh truths - Manifest a cloud of ember and ash, a voice of crackling flame - Ask what they've brought him, if that one task is done yet, etc. - Fail to understand their speech - Grow annoyed, angry, violent - Manipulate metal, telekinetically - Repair metal, or shape it anew - Animate a body formed of metal tools, parts, and scrap In life, he was a brilliant but awkward Maker. Rivals sabotaged him, and he left his people in shame. Stormcatcher took him in, trusted him, gave him purpose and for that, Ferocedes was rabidly loyal. Loyal unto death, and beyond. He died without noticing it. He had no time to treat the wounds he had suffered. Stormcatcher needed his skills. Needed his creations. Needed him. So Ferocedes was already a ghost when Stormcatcher cast her final spell. And in the centuries since, he has fixated on his task: to reclaim, rebuild, and repair, little by little, with what tools he has, and preserve the memory of his friend.

1143.title: Patchwork construct
1143.body: Group , construct , cautious HP 14; Armor 3 (metal) Damage tool at hand d8 ( hand or close ) Special qualities must recharge every few days; dog-like intellect; blind; tremorsense Instinct to complete its given task - Size up an obstacle or task - Reveal/use a tool, feature, or attachment for its current task - Plod away, raise the alarm Ferocedes once had dozens of constructs to defend and maintain the tower. But the years have not been kind. Only ten or so remain, and one or two are usually offline for modifications or repairs. Each one has an ◇ aetherium core ( magical , fragile , Value 2 to the right buyer). Ferocedes can repair minor damage to a core, and/or install it in a new body, but he can't make new ones without the aetherium crucible . Each construct is generally humanoid, though most lack a "head" and all are made of mismatched parts. Pick or roll a current task for each. Consider picking or rolling for an attribute Forge Lords and adjusting its stat block accordingly.

1144.title: 1d6 current task
1144.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Patchwork construct › 1d6 current task
1144.body: - 1 Patrolling/guarding/defending - 2 Masonry/repairing walls - 3 Plumbing/other delicate work - 4 Sweeping/cleaning/pest control - 5 Hauling/storing/reorganizing - 6 Seeking/retrieving/inspecting

1145.title: Skittering horror
1145.body: Horde , emanation , terrifying , sadistic , aggressive HP 7; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage scythe-like limbs d6+2 w/advantage ( hand , close , messy, 2 piercing except vs. bronze) Special qualities spider-climb; blind; senses life (heat, heartbeats, breathing, movement, etc.); disoriented by intense noise/heat Instinct to hurt, terrorize, kill - Investigate sounds/signs of life - Chitter, trill, loudly drone - Call others to the hunt - Attack with shocking fury - Savage a fallen foe 6 feet long, with segmented bodies and too many legs. Scythe-like forelimbs. Feathery antennae that caress the air and quiver when they sense prey. No eyes, just red crystal pulsing within its skull. No mouth, for it never eats and lives on hate and malice. Each is a manifestation of Og'molok's cruel will. Born of blood, given shape, sent skittering up to slaughter. And slaughter they did, until Stormcatcher's final spell turned them all into jagged lumps of glass. Or, almost all of them. A few dozen avoided the vitrifying spell. They linger on, in a state of torpor until they sense a living, feeling being. Then they wake. Then they hunt. Then they slaughter once more.

1146.title: Og'molok, the Faceless Horde
1146.breadcrumb: The Ruined Tower › Og'molok, the Faceless Horde
1146.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Instinct to revel in slaughter, pain, and terror Themes cruelty/torture/violence and destruction/chaos/ruin - Send terrible dreams/visions to those touched by violence - Urge bloodshed, cruelty, rage - Shake the earth in a fit In the bottommost chamber of the Ruined Tower is a cone of jagged black glass, a pile of vitrified horrors that reaches almost to the ceiling. This is where Og'molok burst into the world as a fountain of blood, coalescing into a skittering swarm. This is where it met its doom, fused into a pillar of red crystal , encased in a pile of slag. Og'molok, of course, is not truly dead. Just bound, neutered, diminished. It still corrupts the lowest levels beneath the tower, still calls to those who might hear. It is trapped, and it cannot escape. Not yet. Not without help. - ☐ Someone sets out to free it - ☐ They learn how to release it from its vitrified state (see Tempest Lords) - ☐ They acquire what they need - ☐ They begin the process - ☐ Impending doom : Og'molok and its swarm are loosed upon the world

1147.title: South Manmarch
1147.body: If you descend from the Steplands to the southeast, you'll find yourself on a rolling prairie. The ground slopes gently down toward the east, to the banks of the Dread River . Travelers and merchants aplenty cross these plains. They come north, from Lygos and the civilized south , heading north to the Highway and from there to Marshedge . Or they head the other way, bearing herbs and textiles, metals and gems, back to the southern cities. Or they come from beyond the Highway, from the North Manmarch , to hunt or trade or raid. But for all those who pass through the South Manmarch, few call it home. Sure, the Hillfolk graze their herds here in the warmer months. And some new settlement is always popping up. But the land is dotted with burnt-out homesteads and ruined forts: a mute testament to those who have tried to settle here before. Tried, and failed.

1149.title: Always
1149.body: - The eternal, unresponsive sky - Shallow, rocky soil broken by outcrops of white limestone and gray shale - Smoke from a lone homestead, overlooking a watercourse - A stream or dry gully, winding lazily down toward the Dread River - An abandoned, collapsed longhouse - A Hillfolk horseband, watching from higher ground

1150.title: Spring
1150.body: - A mottled carpet of new grass and wildflowers—yellow, pale green, lilac - Pocket wetlands, home to nesting ducks recently returned from the south - A great cloud of birds, bursting forth from the brush

1151.title: Summer
1151.body: - Squinting into a blinding sky - The fervid and unrelenting chorus of summer insects, shifting in tone and rhythm with time of day - Tallgrass in full flush, waist-high and rippling in a southerly wind. - A mob of white-tailed deer or mighty elk, grazing on a long slope

1152.title: Autumn
1152.body: - Grassheads gone rust-colored, stalks still green - A streamside stand of cottonwoods, glowing gold in the dusk light

1153.title: Winter
1153.body: - Days of scouring, southwesterly wind - Powdery snowdrifts, stretching leeward from ripples in the landscape - Patches of dead grass, showing orange through the snow

1154.title: A long-disputed land
1154.body: Before the Cataclysm the Dread River formed the eastern border of Stone Lord territory. After the Stone Lords fell, the Barrow Builders fought each other back and forth across this expanse, grinding themselves down, like shards of shale abraded to dust over time. Now, the South Manmarch is the border between the civilized south and the hinterlands of the World's End. It's a lawless place, full of dangerous beasts and even more dangerous people. Nomadic Hillfolk claim much of the region, and bandits are a constant threat. And yet, the lure of the prairie is great. Every year or so, someone tries to carve out a piece of for themselves. A new settlement might be: - A family from Marshedge or the North Manmarch, starting their own farm - An ambitious Lygosi merchant, investing in a new trading post or caravansary - Some would-be chief or warlord from the Manmarchers , building a new hamlet or hillfort - The conquering armies of the South Lygos and the South, establishing a foothold Such settlements rarely last. They fall prey to natural disaster or roving beasts, to rivals or bandits, or to the certain wrath of the Hillfolk who claim these lands as theirs. Or perhaps, if you believe the stories, there's something darker afoot. Perhaps these lands really are haunted. Haunted by the numerous souls who've died here. Or by foul things, slithering out of the Dread River. Perhaps the land is cursed, and no settlement can ever survive. Perhaps.

1155.title: The Flintwash
1155.body: Draining out of the Steplands and into the Dread River, this watercourse draws thickets, copses of cottonwood, wildlife, and wanderers to its gentle banks. The Hillfolk call it Maengwalc in their own tongue—"Flintwash," after the stony plateau from which its headwaters spring. Hundreds of rills and brooks feed the Flintwash, and in its lower reaches, it's quite broad and difficult to cross. Yet ways do exist. How might a traveler get from one side to the other? Ask a PC who would know, but perhaps there is… - … an old bridge from the time of the Makers ; - … a newer bridge, built at great expense by some southern merchant or noble; - … a shallow ford; wide, rocky and usually safe; - … a ferry, run by enterprising souls; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

1156.title: Questions
1156.body: - Who did you know who tried to settle in the South Manmarch? What fate befell them? - What bandit crew is currently the most feared? Who leads them, and what makes them so dangerous? - At what landmark do merchant caravans typically encamp? Why do they stop there? - What landmark does everyone who travels these lands know to avoid? Why?

1157.title: Hooks
1157.body: - Someone has seized the Flintwash crossing, and is charging exorbitant tolls - An ambitious chief from the North Manmarch is sending settlers south of the Highway - The PCs eagerly await a caravan, but it gets waylaid by bandits - A ruin in the region becomes a place of pilgrimage, drawing the faithful of Helior/Aratis from all over

1158.title: Terrain
1158.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12) once or twice.

1159.title: 1d12 terrain
1159.body: - 1 Ash field/barrens/sand/rocky stretch - 2 Burrow/dugout/warren - 3 Sodden soil/mud/pocket wetland - 4 Spring/pond/creek/stream/river - 5-6 Open meadow, waist-high grass - 7-8 Shortgrass prairie, vast and open - 9 Footpath/wagon ruts/trail - 10 Tree(s), savanna, thickets - 11 Gentle slope/low ridge/small hill - 12 Steep slope/rocky outcrop/large hill If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

1160.title: Discoveries
1160.body: For any one of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1161.title: 1d6 discovery
1161.body: - 1 Change of terrain (previous page) and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 Useful or valuable flora - 4 beast (see below) - 5 An encounter (see below) - 6 A point of interest (next column)

1162.title: 1d6 beast
1162.body: - 1-3 Mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, hares, prairie dogs, birds, etc. - 4-5 A herd: aurochs , goats, sheep, wild horses , etc. - 6 Something big and majestic: a brawzbedr , a giant ibis, a horned drake , a thunder drake , etc.

1163.title: 1d6 encounter
1163.body: - 1 Hillfolk : encamped, herding, hunting, on patrol, come to trade, or maybe on a raid - 2 Merchant caravan Lygos and the South, coming or going - 3 Bandits , encamped, scouting, returning from a raid - 4 Settlers, hoping to carve out a home here - 5 Someone in dire straits; roll 1d4 again to see who - 6 Spirit of the wild , curious or indifferent

1164.title: 1d6 point of interest
1164.breadcrumb: South Manmarch › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
1164.body: - 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below - 2 A tomb, site, or artifact of the Barrow Builders - 3 A battlefield or remains (below) - 4 A ruin or fortification of the Stone Lords - 5 A place sacred to the spirits of the wild - 6 A new settlement

1165.title: 1d12 battlefield or remains
1165.breadcrumb: South Manmarch › Discoveries › 1d12 battlefield or remains
1165.body: - 1 Truly ancient: from the time of the Makers - 2 From the Time of Cataclysm , when humanity rebelled and woke the Things Below - 3-4 From a conflict among the Barrow Builders - 5-7 From a previous attempt at settlement: burnt out, collapsed, overgrown - 8-10 Recent: Hillfolk, bandits, travelers, settlers, beasts - 11-12 Very recent: the buzzards and crows still feasting Many such places are haunted by the dead.

1167.title: Hazards
1167.body: - Baking sun, no shade for miles - Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/hail/rain/snow; lightning - Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes - Lack of shelter, water, or wood for a long-burning fire (try dried aurochs dung, if you can find it) - Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an ankle or worse - Prairie fire

1168.title: Monsters
