1168.body: - Predators: cougars , kleztigr , pack drakes , or wolves - Big ornery beasts: aurochs , boar , or a thunder drake - Spirits of the wild - Ghosts or revenants , lurking near ancient battlefields or ruined settlements

1169.title: People
1169.body: - Hillfolk , if they're of a mind to do violence - North Manmarchers , heading south or defending a new settlement - Merchants from Marshedge , heading south or coming home - Southern merchants and their caravan guards, plus those traveling with them, like adventurers or an antiquarian - Armies of the south , coming north to exact vengeance, secure a resource, establish a foothold

1170.title: Horned drake
1170.body: Solitary , huge , hardy HP 24; Armor 3 (stony hide) Damage trampling charge d10+5 ( hand , area , forceful ) or horn gore d10+5 ( close , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Instinct to protect their young - Issue a honking call - Stand its ground, still as a rock - Lurch suddenly into a charge - Absorb raw elemental power (regaining 1d8 HP) The behemoth of the southern grasslands is striking for its great size, the quartz-like plates of its segmented hide, and its fearsome horns. The horns vary greatly from beast to beast, ranging from a single spike on its snout or forehead to a rack of five or six curved points edging the flanges of its massive skull. An intimidating beast, even at rest. To the Hillfolk, the downing of a d'lkornek ("thorn-dragon") is a hero's feat, to be followed by a week of harvest and celebration at the kill site. Something interesting : Some scholars hold that the Stone Lords imbued horned drakes with elemental qualities. Specimens of their hide are commonly mistaken for dolostone or some other mineral, and Hillfolk prize their horns (Value 3). Something useful: The horns of a d'lkornek act like power-crystals , but larger and with great capacity. They are extremely dangerous to harvest; a wise butcher slowly bleeds away their energy over the course of days. Adult horned drakes might be accompanied by their young ( group , large , hardy ; HP 14; Damage d8+3; Instinct to get curious, to play fight), whom they aggressively defend.

1171.title: Bandit
1171.body: Horde , stealthy , cautious , devious HP 3; Armor up to 2 (hides, shields) Damage spear d6 ( close , thrown ) or sling d6 ( near , awkward , reload ) Instinct to survive at the expense of others - Block passage, issue a blunt demand - Ambush under cover of darkness - Ransom the best, kill the rest The Makers' Roads limit banditry throughout most the World's End. Not so in the South Manmarch. Here, merchants travel without the safety of warded pavestones. And those merchants draw bandits like dung draws flies. They're motley crews, these bandits. Rogues from Marshedge. Deserters from the armies of North and South. Exiled Hillfolk. Scoundrels. Miscreants. Anyone who's willing to get their hands red, really.

1172.title: Bandit chief
1172.body: Group , leader , stealthy , cautious , devious , organized HP 8; Armor 3 (hides, shield, skill) Damage spear d8 ( close , thrown , 1 piercing) or bow d8 ( near ) Instinct to live their best life beyond the reach of the law - Exude a brutal charisma - Demonstrate great skill at arms - Brook no disagreement Every group demands a boss; the exiled and forsaken moreso. What makes this leader stand apart? (pick 2 or 3) - Infamy, from deeds real or fabricated - Fearlessness, borne of a veteran's confidence or amateur's stupidity - Fragile alliance with a Hillfolk band - A spirit-of-the-wild familiar Spirits of the wild - A sidekick, in the form of a sorcerer or spirit-talker

1173.title: Spirits of the Wild
1173.body: The physical world—this mortal realm of base matter and flesh—is but one reality. There is another, hidden reality. Arguably a truer reality. Here, spirits dwell. The spirit realm is filled with entities, especially those of the natural world. Each beast, each tree, each gust of wind or flickering flame—they all have spirits. So do the soil, the rocks, the mountains and the streams. Most of these spirits are quiet, slumbering or barely conscious or operating on a scale so alien as to be impenetrable to human minds. But some mortals can speak to them nonetheless, and goad them into action. Sometimes, these sleepers shift and mumble and stretch of their own accord. The most active spirits are those tethered to physical objects, or those associated with beasts or other "animate" things—things full of life and energy. These are the spirits that mortals encounter most often.

1174.title: Themes
1174.body: Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

1175.title: 1d12 theme
1175.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Themes › 1d12 theme
1175.body: - 1 Trees/plants/flora/growing things - 2 Beasts/vermin/fauna - 3 Air/wind/weather/fire - 4 Spring/birth/fertility/sex/healing - 5 Summer/heat/growth/vitality - 6 Autumn/harvest/sustenance/plenty - 7 Winter/cold/hunger/sleep/death - 8 Savagery/the hunt/dominance - 9 Nurture/shelter/provision - 10 The land/soil/stone/terrain - 11 Blight/decay/reclamation/destruction - 12 Water/streams/ponds/lakes (possibly corrupted by the Things Below

1176.title: Hooks
1176.body: - A villager (or visitor) has been cursed by a powerful elemental spirit, maybe in a way that puts others in danger. - A child hears voices and sees spirits; maybe they're crippled with fear, or maybe they recklessly ask spirits for petty favors. - Something that the PCs want can be found in in a sacred site, one that's known to house unfriendly, powerful spirits. - Someone has unwittingly brought a spirit's tether into the village, and it starts wreaking havoc. - A PC learns that a particular spirit knows an important secret, something they desperately want to learn.

1177.title: Regarding spirits
1177.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Regarding spirits
1177.body: The following apply not just to spirits of the wild, but also to ghosts , dool spirits , some primordial powers , the Things Below , and little gods .

1178.title: Manifestation
1178.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Regarding spirits › Manifestation
1178.body: Spirits lack physical form, existing in the realm of thought and symbol. They can perceive and influence the mortal realm in subtle ways, but to directly affect it, a spirit must manifest or possess a physical form. Each spirit manifests differently. Some shape a body from crude matter. Others appear as intangibles: cold, shadow, moonlight, etc. Some can only "ride" living beings. Others can animate beings that were once alive, or vessels specifically prepared to hold them. A rare few can manifest bodies of pure spirit-stuff. Spirits manifest or possess forms that make symbolic sense. A fire spirit won't possess water; it might manifest as a flame, or animate a cloud of cinders, or possess a hot-headed youth.

1179.title: Tethers
1179.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Regarding spirits › Tethers
1179.body: Some spirits are bound to a thing in the physical world. The tether might occur naturally (like a brennauwed and its tree), by choice or mutual benefit (like an altar where mortals leave offerings for the spirt), or via trickery, force, or coercion. A tether anchors and empowers its spirit, granting it a stronger connection to the physical world. If harmed or weakened, the spirit can retreat to its tether to rejuvenate. But a tether is not just a boon; it's also a vulnerability. Harming the tether harms the spirit. Destroying the tether destroys the spirit—or at least weakens, diminishes, and greatly changes it. Some tethers don't just anchor their spirits: they bind and imprison them. A spirit so bound can't leave its tether's presence. The most powerful bindings seal their spirits away, greatly restricting their perceptions and ability to influence the world. But no binding is perfect, and a spirit so bound might tempt or trick others into releasing it.

1180.title: Fighting Spirits
1180.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Regarding spirits › Fighting Spirits
1180.body: One can fight a spirit only when it manifests, and even then they can be difficult to harm. Manifest forms often have lots of HP and might be vulnerable to only certain materials or attacks. Even if one can harm a manifestation, they're not really harming the spirit. Reducing it to 0 HP ends the manifestation. This weakens the spirit, forcing it to retreat to its tether (if it has one). But it doesn't truly harm it. To truly harm a spirit, one must use potent magic, damage or destroy its tether, or fight it directly in the spirit realm. A spirit reduced to 0 HP in one of these ways is actually destroyed—or at least so thoroughly diminished that it won't be a problem for many human lifetimes. You might want to track a spirit's "manifest" HP vs. "true" HP. If the PCs hurt a manifest spirit with something that could truly harm it, reduce both sets of HP.

1181.title: Communion
1181.body: One might wish to commune with a spirit to seek knowledge or request aid. The Blessed in particular is able to sense active spirits, speak with them, and force even dormant spirits to manifest and hear them out. Some NPCs and monsters have similar powers. Most people, though, are unaware of the spirits around them. Spirits of the wild tend to ignore individual humans. The best way for most mortals to commune with a spirit is to enter a sacred site (carefully, respectfully) and make an offering that gets the spirit's attention. One could also intentionally provoke the spirit—getting it to manifest—but that rarely ends well. One might also seek out a spirit in its own realm. This requires shifting one's consciousness to the spirit realm, which might involve… - … meditation; - … imbibing drugs/alcohol; - … exhaustion or deprivation; - … percussion, music, dancing; - … a guide or teacher; and/or - … a magic item or spell. Once a mortal's consciousness is in the spirit realm, they must find the spirit(s) in question, get their attention, and convince it to help. None of these are easy. None of these are safe. If a PC attempts such a journey, Chart a Course with them through the spirit world. Good requirements and challenges include… - … they need a knowledgeable guide or detailed instructions; - … they risk getting lost; - … the way will be grueling, they risk exhaustion; and/or - … they must watch out for specters , wraiths , or other ghosts ; dool spirits ; fundamentals ; voidblights ; spirits corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below; or just elemental spirits that are territorial, or violent, or powerful and careless. Depending on the spirit that they seek, they might also need to surmount/brave/cross a hazardous stretch of the spirit realm and/or risk drawing the attention of certain spirits. For example, if they sought out the spirit of the Golden Oak , you might tell them... - … they risk drawing the attention of the crossing's guardian; and/or - … they need to brave the secret crossing itself, which borders the Last Door and the realm of the dead.

1182.title: Elemental spirits
1182.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits
1182.body: These spirits mostly just do their thing, with little concern for humanity unless some mortal offends them or seeks them out for aid. An elemental spirit can: - Reveal what it knows, from its own perspective - Spread word to other spirits - Bestow a blessing or curse, tied to its element - Shift, move, or direct that which falls in its domain Elemental spirits rarely manifest. When they do, they often possess or animate the element itself, or appear as ghostly figures somehow reflecting their element. They're more likely to be NPCs than monsters . Here are examples; feel free to modify and expand on them. Maybe you picture cave spirits differently than this. Maybe a decorated limestone cave has different tags, instincts, and notes than a cave-bear's den. Or maybe this particular cave's spirit is different than other cave spirits. Consider asking a character (who would know) what certain types of spirits are usually like, and use what they give you.

1183.title: Beast warden
1183.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Beast warden
1183.body: Solitary , spirit , beast-like , protective Instinct to see its wards thrive, prosper Notes A different spirit watching over each type of beast.

1184.title: Cave spirit
1184.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Cave spirit
1184.body: Solitary , spirit , mysterious , possessive , coy Instinct to hold its secrets tightly Notes Drops hints about what's inside; hates the light.

1185.title: Cold spirit
1185.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Cold spirit
1185.body: Solitary , spirit , quiet , patient , bitter Instinct to slow things down, to preserve Notes Whispery voice; finds warmth and exuberance distasteful.

1186.title: Fertility spirit
1186.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Fertility spirit
1186.body: Group , spirit , earthy , sensual , positive Instinct to encourage growth Notes Giggles, touchy; frustrated by prudery, inhibition, restraint.

1187.title: Fire spirit
1187.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Fire spirit
1187.body: Group , spirit , needy , intense , aggressive Instinct to consume Notes Cackles gleefully; fears water, smothering; always hungry.

1188.title: Log spirit
1188.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Log spirit
1188.body: Solitary , spirit , sickly , cheerful , giving Instinct to not mind, really Notes Ho-hum, it's okay; falls apart under serious pressure.

1189.title: Meadow/prairie/grass spirit
1189.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Meadow/prairie/grass spirit
1189.body: Horde , spirit , lazy , bashful , chatty Instinct to gossip amongst themselves Notes Like easily embarrassed teens; "Shh, don't tell them!"

1190.title: Mist/fog spirit
1190.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Mist/fog spirit
1190.body: Solitary , spirit , presumptuous , hazy Instinct to get intimate, blur boundaries Notes Hushed, dripping voice; spaced-out; touchy-feely.

1191.title: Mud spirit
1191.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Mud spirit
1191.body: Group , spirit , cheerful , grimy , clingy Instinct to make a mess of things Notes Genuinely doesn't get why you're upset or frustrated.

1192.title: Peat/bog/fen spirit
1192.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Peat/bog/fen spirit
1192.body: Solitary , spirit , lazy , jealous , gluttonous Instinct to take whatever it can Notes Deep froggy voice; licks "lips," makes baseless accusations.

1193.title: Pond spirit
1193.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Pond spirit
1193.body: Solitary , spirit , placid , watchful , callous Instinct to remain uninvolved Notes Sighs frequently; ignores them, unless they provoke it.

1194.title: Rain spirit
1194.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Rain spirit
1194.body: Solitary , spirit , moody , overbearing Instinct to give, but to be easily offended Notes Tut-tuts; wants to be needed, appreciated; passive-aggressive.

1195.title: Rock/stone/boulder/hill spirit
1195.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Rock/stone/boulder/hill spirit
1195.body: Group or solitary , spirit , reliable , chill Instinct to remain unperturbed Notes Speaks very slowly; friendly; no urgency; "Relax, man."

1196.title: Sapling spirit
1196.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Sapling spirit
1196.body: Group , spirit , earnest , chipper Instinct to seek what it needs to grow Notes Wants attention, makes big promises.

1197.title: Snow spirit
1197.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Snow spirit
1197.body: Solitary , spirit , secretive , aloof , quiet Instinct to conceal things Notes Prim and proper; talks around the issue, always.

1198.title: Storm spirit
1198.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Storm spirit
1198.body: Solitary , spirit , brash , arrogant , potent Instinct to test any who stand before it Notes Big, rumbling voice; intense but distracted, has places to be.

1199.title: Stream/creek spirit
1199.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Stream/creek spirit
1199.body: Solitary , spirit , bubbly , restless , forgetful Instinct to offer surprises, to wash away Notes Rarely stops talking, fidgeting; repeats self.

1200.title: Trail spirit
1200.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Trail spirit
1200.body: Solitary , spirit , inviting , yielding Instinct to reveal Notes Agitated, nervous voice; draws attention to those it helps

1201.title: Tree spirit
1201.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Tree spirit
1201.body: Solitary , spirit , proud , stubborn , stoic Instinct to take its time Notes Speaks slowly; clings to hurts, grudges, kindnesses.

1202.title: Wind spirit
1202.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Elemental spirits › Wind spirit
1202.body: Horde , spirit , restless , temperamental Instinct to get distracted Notes Howls, moans, whispers; hates all limits, confinement.

1203.title: Sacred sites
1203.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Sacred sites
1203.body: These are places where spirits of the wild are tethered, or where they tend to be active. Pick or roll for terrain based on the region, theme Spirits of the Wild to determine the nature of the spirit(s) present, a marker, and the spirits' activity and disposition. Consider rolling the Die of Fate to determine the spirit or spirits' relative power.

1204.title: 1d12 marker
1204.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Sacred sites › 1d12 marker
1204.body: - 1 Nothing you can put your finger on, just the sense of being watched - 2-4 A place that resonates strongly with the spirit's theme - 5-6 A remarkable natural feature, particularly large/beautiful/ancient/etc. - 7-8 An unusual feature, probably natural but strange or out of place - 9 An idol or altar, ancient and crumbling/buried/submerged - 10 An idol or altar, made by the locals - 11-12 Roll twice with a d10, combine

1205.title: 1d12 activity
1205.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Sacred sites › 1d12 activity
1205.body: - 1 Missing, dissipated, greatly weakened - 2 Imprisoned, probably seeking release - 3-4 Slumbering, waiting for something - 5-6 Sporadic, often slumbering or away - 7-9 Alert, aware, unlikely to manifest - 10-12 Alert, aware, manifesting freely

1206.title: 1d12 disposition
1206.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Sacred sites › 1d12 disposition
1206.body: - 1-2 Dangerous, possibly corrupted by the Things Below - 3-4 Vengeful, grumpy, protective, angry at intrusion - 5-6 Plaintive, needful, wronged, demanding - 7-8 Aloof, shy, dismissive, has their own concerns - 9-10 Curious, playful, mischievous, friendly - 11-12 Content, pleasant, helpful, beneficent The PCs are in the Steplands, heading towards Three Coven Lake, and they've just run afoul of some Hillfolk. They escaped, but only because the Hillfolk turned back from the cave they hid in, like they were scared to enter. I think this is a sacred site. I roll a 3 for theme (air/wind/weather/fire) and a 4 for the marker (place that resonates with the theme). I'm picturing one of those eternal flames, where a methane pocket seeps out and burns forever. I get 10 for activity (alert, aware, manifesting freely) and roll a 12 for disposition (content, pleasant, helpful, beneficent) which is odd given the Hillfolk's reaction. So: a cave lit by a flame with no apparent source, issuing from a crack in the earth. The flame houses an old, powerful, talkative spirit that's been burning since the days of the Makers. It blames the Hillfolk for their ancestors' role in calling up the Things Below and toppling the Stone Lords, so it's hostile towards them. It'll be right helpful to the Blessed and her friends!

1208.title: Length of braided leather
1208.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › Length of braided leather
1208.body: magical 3 feet long, frayed on one end, and crusty with flaking... something. The braids entwine 2d4 beads of redwood. Each bead contains some harm: a wound (1d10 damage, messy , ignores armor), a sickness, a poison, a delusion. The whole thing binds a spirit of blight. When you unweave the braid and let a redwood bead drop upon the earth , the spirit manifests as a warm, damp wind. Name someone in your presence; the spirit inflicts upon them whatever harm was stored in the bead. If you do not name someone in your presence, the spirit will choose someone at random (maybe even you).

1209.title: A tightly-woven net
1209.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A tightly-woven net
1209.body: close , thrown , magical , crude Woven from Flats-grass by a Hillfolk sorcerer or witch. Hundreds of minor root-spirits are tethered to the strands. When you cast the net over a spirit , it is ensnared—even if its form is intangible or could flow through the net. The spirit can't escape on its own as long as the net is intact. Don't try to use this on fire spirits—they'll burn right through it.

1210.title: A fur-lined bedroll
1210.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A fur-lined bedroll
1210.body: ◇, magical This bedroll is decorated with Forest Folk beadwork and lined with the softest rabbit fur. It tethers a nurturing, protective spirit, one that is quiet and comforting. When you Make Camp in the fur-lined bedroll , you regain 1d6 (extra) HP and gain advantage on your next roll.

1211.title: A stone cup
1211.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A stone cup
1211.body: magical A lump of limestone, big as a large man's fist, sheared off smooth at one end and hollowed out. Water slowly seeps from the interior wall, filling the cup in about half an hour. The water is hard, mineral-rich but potable, and just a little cursed. When you drink water produced by the cup , you'll have troubling dreams the next time you sleep—dreams of suffocation and endless wet caverns and huge uncaring things stirring in the lightless depths. Roll 1d6: on a 1 , you wake up screaming and will get no rest this evening; on a 2-6 , the dreams are merely unsettling.

1212.title: A cracked femur
1212.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A cracked femur
1212.body: ◇, magical This leg bone is always cold and covered in rime. It's the bone of a witch who broke her leg and froze to death. It houses not a ghost, but a potent spirit of winter and cold. When you first grip the bone , roll +CON: on a 10+ , the spirit acknowledges you as master and you can wield the bone; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - Drop the bone; you can never wield it - Endure as the spirit sucks away your heat—take 2d4 damage (ignores armor) and mark weakened —but you then master the spirit and can wield the bone. On a 6- , the spirit sucks away your heat—take 2d4 damage (ignores armor) and mark weakened —and you can never wield the bone. When you wield the bone as a weapon ( hand , crude , magical , ignores armor) it deals 2d4 damage instead of your normal damage, sucking the warmth from your foe.

1213.title: A crown of flowers
1213.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A crown of flowers
1213.body: fragile , magical A wreath of living vines, sprouting flowers of great variety and beauty. A woman who dons the crown becomes greatly attuned to the fertility of everything around her, and the whispering spirits of growing things. She also becomes aware of the rites of spring. When a woman wears the crown and performs the rites of spring —dancing around an oak tree, making offerings to the spirits, and joining with her lover in the light of the full moon—then her steading rolls with advantage to generate Surplus in both summer and autumn. Also, if the woman wearing the crown so chooses, she will conceive and bear a healthy child, regardless of any barriers.

1214.title: A forked stick of redwood
1214.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A forked stick of redwood
1214.body: ◇, magical A stout stick about 2 feet long, the bark removed, with fine woodworm trails making entirely-too-regular patterns. If you hold it gently, with a hand on each tine of the fork, you can feel it tugging, pulling you gently in certain directions. When you dowse with the redwood stick , it will guide you to the nearest site sacred to the spirits of the wild. With practice, you can estimate the distance and even the disposition of the spirits that dwell there.

1215.title: A hunk of amber
1215.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A hunk of amber
1215.body: ◇, magical , Value 2 It's about the size of two fists, with a mass of blood-sucking insects floating in its center. It tethers an ambitious spirit of insects, blight, and disease. Treat it as a threat (type: magical entity , Instinct to foster worship) with the following moves: - Demand a blood sacrifice, poured over the amber - Shield someone from sickness, vermin, and poison - Blight an enemy with sickness, vermin, or both

1216.title: Minor arcana
1216.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › A hunk of amber › Minor arcana

1217.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1217.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
1217.body: - 1 A path in the woods - 2 A cloak, richly embroidered - 3 A beaded satchel - 4 A cracked flute - 5 A wolf pelt - 6 A patch of rainbow moss

1218.title: Major arcana
1218.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
1218.body: These could be associated with the spirits of the wild. - The Redwood Effigy - The Twisted Spear - The Shield of the Wisent Witch

1219.title: The Brennauwed
1219.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › The Brennauwed
1219.body: The Tall Ladies, Daughters-of-Seed, the Slow Sisters: these are all titles given to the Brennauwed, powerful tree spirits and, if you believe them, kin to Danu herself. Each is tethered to a single tree. The younger ones can roam, but the older a Brennauwed is, the more bound she is to her tree.

1220.title: Glasbren
1220.body: Solitary , spirit , clever , magical , stealthy , beautiful HP 19; Armor 2 (bark, no vital organs) Damage raking fingers d8 ( hand , disadvantage) Special qualities tethered to its tree Instinct to stretch and grow and experience life - Travel freely between young trees - Spy on someone or something interesting - Manifest a beautiful form of moving wood (unharmed by stabbing, vulnerable to fire) - Call on the forest and its creatures for aid - Spread word through the Great Wood Youngest of the Brennauwed, a glasbren's tree is barely more than a sapling. The world is full of wonder and life, and they would see it all. From time to time, a comely mortal will catch a glasbren's eye. If the mortal proves kind and curious and full of life, it might reveal itself and shyly pursue a friendship—or more. When you give your heart or loyalty to a glasbren , your instinct becomes "Devotion: act in the glasbren's interests." The glasbren becomes a follower (Cost: wonder and excitement; holds 2 Loyalty to start). When a season passes without you spending time with the glasbren , alone at her tree, she loses all Loyalty and you mark miserable . You cannot clear this debility without spending time with her. When you spend time with the glasbren , alone with her at her tree, roll +CHA: on a 7+ , clear all your debilities and ask the GM to reveal a secret about the Great Wood or the things that can be found there; but on a 7-9 , also pick 1: - Days or weeks pass without either of you realizing it - You offend or upset her; she loses any Loyalty she held and she isn't speaking to you right now (tell us what you did wrong) - Her "family" would have words with you, right now

1221.title: Birdwaig
1221.body: Solitary , large , spirit , cautious , clever , magical HP 23; Armor 3 (woody, no vital organs) Damage crushing branches d10+3 ( close , reach , forceful ), choking vines/roots d10+1 ( reach , grabby ) Special qualities tethered to its tree Instinct to care for the forest, especially the trees - Wield the plants around her like a weapon - Possess a nearby tree (vulnerable to fire, all but impossible to harm with most weapons) - Purge or bind an unnatural taint - Grant a secret or a boon to a friend of the forest As they mature, Brennauwed grow more sedentary, manifesting only via their own tree or possessing other trees within a few miles. A birdwaig has a motherly regard for all natural things in her territory, and will turn the very forest against any that seek to do it harm.

1222.title: Hennain
1222.body: Solitary , huge , spirit , cautious , clever , magical , ancient , terrifying HP 31; Armor 4 (woody, no vital organs) Damage walloping branches d12+5 ( close , reach , area , forceful ) Special qualities tethered to its tree Instinct to take the long, long view - Remain unmoved - See through any tree in the forest - Animate her own tree (almost impossible to harm with weapons, or even fire) - Call upon the power of Danu herself - Lay a lasting curse on a defiler Old grandmothers of the Wood, the hennain are few and ancient and mighty. They watch, and they wait, and they endure. It takes a dire threat indeed to rouse one, and even then it acts in slow and inscrutable ways. For a hennain cares not one bit for any given creature or tree; she cares only for the slow turn of the forest itself.

1224.title: Andalau
1224.body: Horde , tiny , spirit , stealthy , mischievous HP 8; Armor 2 (tiny, agile) Damage none Instinct to play and frolic - Manifest as a fluttering gust of wind (harmed only by lead or salt) - Flit things about (dust, leaves, etc.) - Annoy or spook someone - Deliver a whispery message There are places where the wind dances constantly, fluttering and flitting, and here the andalau dwell. They are more prone to mischief than violence. Win their favor, and you might convince them to help you—especially if it involves going somewhere new or playing pranks. Just don't be surprised when their attention flits elsewhere.

1225.title: Ceffylwraig
1225.body: Solitary , large , spirit , swift , tireless , cautious , magical , beautiful HP 24; Armor 0 Damage kicking hoofs d8+3 ( hand , close , forceful , disadvantage) Instinct to be fiercely independent - Appear as a majestic horse among the herd - Slip free of any restraint, disappearing into the wind if need be - Guide the herd like it was a single, living entity - Curse someone with wild, unbridled emotions A herd of wild horses has a life, nay, a spirit all its own, a spirit as free and fickle as the wind. Take care, you who would poach wild horses for your own purpose, lest you find your heart turned against you! Something interesting: "Ceffylwraig" means "horse wife," but she sees herself more as queen than wife: proud and haughty, disdainful of the weak and the slow, the herd her subjects more than her children. Something useful: A ceffylwraig is scornful and suspicious of praise or fancy words, but can be appeased by thoughtful gifts, offered with appropriate respect from proud women of noble bearing. - What sort of gift do the ceffylwraig most value? - What sort of gift will do in a pinch?

1226.title: Coedwaig
1226.body: Horde , tiny , spirit , magical HP 5; Armor 2 (bark, no vital organs) Damage nasty thorns d6 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities thorns all over, to touch them is to bleed Instinct to viciously defend its ward - Possess thorny plants or vines and shape them into a body - Cause vicious thorns and spines to sprout from nearby plants - Dig in deep, make them bleed ( messy ) Each thorn on a plant is a sword, brandished boldly against a world that would defile its flowers, steal its fruit, and devour its delicate buds. What, then, are the coedwaig? Only Danu's swords, brandished by the Mother herself in defense of her most beautiful and vulnerable children.

1227.title: Fadyn
1227.body: Solitary , spirit , magical , stealthy , devious , audacious , cautious , beautiful HP 16; Armor 1 (reflexes) Damage bite, scratches, or knife d6 ( hand , disadvantage) Special qualities tethered to a fox skull Instinct to make mischief - Manifest as a fox ( small ) or a charming stranger - Be all manner of charming and ingratiating - Steal something when no one's looking - Make something seem like something else Every child has heard stories of the fox-spirit, the trickster, the fadyn. In some tales, a fadyn makes fools of the cruel or the proud. In others, they steal chickens from the slow-witted. And in still others, they seduce a beautiful human, disappearing when bored or discovered. Truth be told, there's little pattern to who a fadyn torments or fancies. They are fickle, fickle things. Something interesting: When a fadyn takes the form of a person, it always has some fox-like feature (a tail, feet, hands, ears) that it strives to hide. It is easily embarrassed by this feature, and flees if it's discovered. Something useful: Fadyn are in fact the spirits of old, cagey foxes who passed away, and they're tethered to their old skulls. One can't travel far from its skull, and often carries it hidden on its manifest person or buries it near the community that it torments. If you want to destroy a fadyn, or bind it to your service, getting your hands on its skull is the best way to do so.

1228.title: Grwgnach
1228.body: Solitary, huge, spirit, magical, mesmerizing HP 27; Armor 2 (reflexes, swarm) Damage whirlwind of pecks, claws, buffets d8+3 ( hand , area , disadvantage) Instinct to absorb individual identity - Manifest in a murmuration of starlings (nigh impossible to harm with personal weapons) - Strip a swath of land clean of seed, saplings, insects - Overwhelm with sound and the weight of numbers When the sun sets, starlings flock in absurd numbers: thousands upon thousands dancing and wheeling in the sky, like a chittering thunderhead, like a cloud of living smoke. Farmers do what they can to keep starlings far from town, and not just because they eat precious seeds and bury the land in droppings. They do so for fear of the grwgnach. The grwgnach is a spirit of the mob, of the many coming together to become something more. It's not a king or a queen of the starlings. Rather, it's what a million starlings become when each bird loses itself to the flock. When you gaze rapt upon a murmuration of starlings , tell us of a time when you felt small and insignificant, or when you lost yourself in a crowd. Then, roll +WIS: on a 10+, you feel the pull of the grwgnach but shake it off; on a 7-9 , your mind is briefly adrift among a thousand-thousand others—mark dazed but also ask the GM one question about the region, something that at least one starling might know; on a 6- , your mind is sucked into the grwgnach—mark dazed and you stand rapt until the flock disperses or someone removes you, physically. Also, until you Level Up, your instinct becomes "Birdlike: To act strangely, like a bird."

1229.title: The Halfeyd
1229.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Grwgnach › The Halfeyd
1229.body: Spirit , magical , fickle , sadistic , terrifying Instinct to terrorize its prey - Manifest as eyes in the dark, watching - Mark someone as prey - Set all manner of hunting beasts on their trail - Put ambush predators in their path The hunters and trappers of Stonetop know. So do the Hillfolk. The Manmarchers know, and Fen-walkers, too. When you make a kill, you leave an offering. An offering for the Halfeyd. Fail to do so, and you might find yourself as prey—for everything. Something interesting: If the Halfeyd marks you, you'll be hounded for 3 days and 3 nights—even by small predators who would never normally hunt you. Something useful: A spirit of running water (like that of a stream) can wash away the Halfeyd's mark.

1230.title: Nerth serpent
1230.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Nerth serpent
1230.body: Solitary , large , spirit , fearless HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), or 0 against bare hands Damage bite and constrict d10+3 ( hand , close , reach , forceful , grabby ) Special qualities tethered to the Nerth Tree Instinct to test the might of supplicants - Politely offer a chance to back down - Restrain an opponent's limb - Whisssper a foe's own doubts in their ear The Nerth Tree grows in the Great Wood. It can only be found by one mortal each midsummer, by following a particular path in the woods . The roots are littered with bones of former supplicants. Hanging from the tree: a great serpent. Crush it and eats its heart, and you will gain its vitality. But good luck with that.

1231.title: Prenysbyrd
1231.body: Solitary , large , spirit , magical , stubborn , terrifying HP 23; Armor 2 (woody, lacks organs) Damage club-like fists d10+3 ( close , forceful ) Instinct to punish trespassers - Blame one for the sins of another - Fill minds with leering images and unreasoning dread - Form a body of fallen trees, leaves, rotting wood (vulnerable to fire) - Smother a prone foe with soil, leaves, wood (d8+1 damage, near , grabby ) What becomes of a dead tree's spirit? Most linger as simple log-spirits, weakening as the log rots to mulch. But when generations of trees grow and die in the same place, they might become something more. Something that defends the grove, and its current trees, and all the trees that are yet to come.

1232.title: Troelloff
1232.breadcrumb: Spirits of the Wild › Prenysbyrd › Troelloff
1232.body: Spirit , magical , playful , cruel Instinct to confuse, frustrate, delay - Show warnings left by others: spiral carvings, many-arrowed icons, etc. - Inflict a brief, intense vertigo - Make them lose their way - Show a landmark they've passed before - Obscure trails, way-signs, and markings Some places never want you to leave: deep woods, twisting caverns, rugged hills that all look same. Such places give rise to troelloff, maze-spirits, who delight in mischief and feed on the desperation of the lost. Something interesting: Where a troelloff dwells, there's always a true path. It can't harass anyone who stays on this path, but good luck finding it. Something useful: The path is never obvious, but it's always marked, by a particular flower, a pattern in the moss, a vein in the stone, etc.

1233.title: The Steplands
1233.body: The Steplands are a rugged hill-country south of the Flats . To the north, they touch Ferrier's Fen and the Great Wood . To the east, they give way to the South Manmarch . To the west, they climb higher and higher, like a stairway to the Huffel Peaks . The region is a karst—a plateau of limestone and quartzite and granite, carved away by ages of runoff and dissolved by groundwater, leaving a jumble of hills and valleys and rocky cliffs, all shot through with caves, sinkholes, and streams. The land seems wild and untamed to outsiders, but in truth it is home to dozens of small nomadic bands, the Hillfolk .

1235.title: Always
1235.body: - Bare rock, host to lichen, moss, and stubborn grass/shrubs - Cloud-shadows drifting over hillsides - The feeling of being surrounded, closed in by the valleys - The burning in your legs as you climb another damn hill - The grandeur when you reach a summit: vista on vista marching, hills on hills, slope beyond slope

1236.title: Spring
1236.body: - Snow still clinging to south-facing slopes, never touched by sun - Sudden warmth as you step out of a shadow; chill as you step into one - Gurgle of water downhill, through ancient culverts under the Highway

1237.title: Summer
1237.body: - Emerald meadows, grass waving in wind, splashes of wildflower color - Flutter of birds taking flight, surprised when you round a bend - Blessed shade as you pass beneath some trees or into the shadow of a hill

1238.title: Autumn
1238.body: - Fog in the valleys, the hills like islands in clouds - Tickle on your face, from mist or an early snow - Mournful songs of cranes or owls or other large birds, echoing from far away

1239.title: Winter
1239.body: - White drifts half-covering the hills, rocks still poking through - Bitter winds on your face as you crest a hill or trudge through a pass - The breath of your companions, white clouds hanging in the air - The sense of stillness and quiet, like the world sleeping

1240.title: A rugged land
1240.body: By all rights, the Steplands should be lush and fertile, teeming with farms and pastures. The same storms that soak the Flats roll through these hills, and snowmelt from the Huffel Peaks feeds many streams and aquifers. And yet: the region is rocky, almost barren in places. The slopes boast meadows of sedge and shortgrass and thickets of skinny pine, but the soil is thin and prone to erosion and mudslides. Cliffs and outcrops abound, dotted with shrubs and gnarled trees and even succulents normally found only in the south. There's life a-plenty, sure—enough to support the roaming bands of Hillfolk and their herds—but one would be hard-pressed to make a go of farming. Why is the land like this? That's for you and your table to decide, but perhaps… - … it's a natural result of the thirsty bedrock, the ceaseless southerly wind, the grazing of beasts; - … the Stone Lords' magic still infuses the land, repelling soil and seed, fossilizing roots, devouring minerals; - … battles between Stone Lords and the Barrow Builders blasted the land, and only the hardiest spirits of the wild still dwell here; - … the Things Below sleep fitfully here, breathing in through lakes and sinkholes, sucking at the lifeforce of the soil; - … some combination of the above; or - … something completely different.

1241.title: Questions
1241.body: - What do the Hillfolk call the Steplands? - Which of you once explored the caves that dot these lands? What did you find there that haunts you still? - What treasure or wonder is said to be hidden in the Steplands? - What landmark, visible from the Highway, have you always wanted to explore?

1242.title: Places
1242.body: - Blackwater Lake - The Makers' Roads , specifically the Highway - Three Coven Lake

1243.title: Hooks
1243.body: - Harsh weather threatens PCs on the Highway; a nearby cave might provide shelter - A Hillfolk band is plagued by a bloodthirsty gouzadn , but a concerned parent begs for their lost child's life to be spared - A brawzbedr naps on the Highway, blocking a merchant's wagon - Someone the PCs care about went to explore a cave and never came out

1244.title: Terrain
1244.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1245.title: 1d12 terrain
1245.body: 1d12 Terrain ------- --------------------------------------------------------------------- 1 Creek, gulley, stream, river 2 Stream, disappearing into cave, sinkhole, or fractured bedrock 3 Sinkhole, crevasse, gorge 4 Barren patch (sand, scree, rocks) 5 Copse of pines, gnarled shrubs 6 Stretch of Broken Roads 7 Gentle slope, meadow 8-9 Steep slope, treacherous, unstable 10 Sheer rock face, bluff, rocky outcrop 11-12 Cave

1246.title: Discoveries
1246.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1247.title: 1d6 discovery
1247.body: - 1 A change of terrain (previous column) and roll again. - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 Useful or valuable flora - 4 A point of interest (next page) - 5 A hillfolk encounter (next page) - 6 Some other encounter (next page) 1d6 Point of interest --- --- 1 A place corrupted by the Things Below 2 A place haunted by death or the undying 3 A site or artifact of the Barrow Builders 4 A Hillfolk encampment or sacred place, occupied or not 5 A place sacred to the spirits of the wild 6 A ruin or artifact of the Stone Lords 1d6 Hillfolk encounter --- --- 1 Pair or trio of riders, not immediately hostile 2 Herd of goats or sheep, tended by teen(s) and hound(s) 3 Signs of their passing/encampment/borders 4 Foragers, not far from camp 5 A whole band, on the move 6 A meeting between two or more bands For more about Hillfolk bands, see Hillfolk. 1d6 Other encounter --- --- 1 Deadly beast, not actively hostile but easily provoked: a brawzbedr , cave bear , thunder drake , etc. 2-3 Potential game, but big and dangerous if provoked: boars , aurochs , etc. 4-5 Game, mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, fowl, rabbits, etc. 6 Spirit of the wild , curious or indifferent

1249.title: Hazards
1249.body: - Getting lost (maze of hills, valleys, streams; trees blocking views) - Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it - Steep slope, unstable footing (scree, mud, fallen needles, crumbling rock) - Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes

1250.title: Mudslide
1250.body: Start with a slope built up with soil. Peel away the vegetation through over-grazing, or fire, or drought, or too much rain entirely. Add a sudden heavy rain or some fools walking across it. Hey, look, a mudslide! - Point out the lack of ground cover, the sodden soil, the weight of waiting earth - It'll take time/effort/risk to avoid the unstable slope - The hillside shifts and buckles, underfoot or overhead - People, stones, and trees are swept away or buried, d6+2 damage ( area , forceful , grabby , ignores armor) - Trap them, crush them, deprive them of air - Leave behind a slick, treacherous, muddy swath

1251.title: Rockslide
1251.body: Some slopes are often covered with scree or strewn with boulders, fist-sized, head-sized, some as big as horses. Mostly they stay put. Mostly. - A loose stone clatters downhill, skitterscatter, billowing dust, but over soon enough - A smaller rock smacks them, d6 damage - A bigger stone teeters, starts to slip - A whole field of boulders gives way - Clunk, clunk, smash, d8+2 damage ( area , forceful ) - Bury them under tons of rock and dirt (Death's Door)

1252.title: Bat swarm
1252.body: Stepland caves are often home to thousands upon thousands of bats. They're nothing to fear individually. But a swarm of them, when you stand in their way? Oh dear. - Squeaks, chitters, and flapping are heard in the distance - The sounds get closer, louder, and louder still - Overwhelm them with sound and scratches and fluttering wings, d6 damage ( area )

1253.title: Hillfolk
1253.body: - A pair or trio of Hillfolk riders , hunting or on patrol - A half-dozen riders or more, trailing the PCs - A hdour and/or their attendant spirits, beasts, demons, or followers

1254.title: Monsters
1254.body: - Ornery beasts, all riled up: aurochs , boar , cave bears , or thunder drakes . - Predators: cougar , feathered drakes , pack drakes , wolves - Spirits of the wild - Deathless ones , barrow wights , or phantom riders , lurking in their barrows or set free of them - The Ghostly Legion , on the hunt or marching mortals to their cave - Other ghosts or revenants , victims of tragedy or violence - One of the other dangerous beasts that roam the Steplands

1255.title: Brawzbedr
1255.body: Solitary , huge , stubborn , grumpy HP 24; Armor 3 (thick hide, boney nodules) Damage tree-rending claws d10+7 w/ disadvantage ( reach , close , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities poor vision and hearing; excellent sense of smell Instinct to overreact - Not really notice something - Tear up trees like a child tears up grass - Swat at something that annoys it - Flail about in a rage (+ area ), then get tired Think of something like a badger or a sloth, but big as a house: 20 feet long, 9 feet at the shoulder, forearms nearly 12 feet long when you include those shovel-like claws. Mostly they just lumber about the Steplands, looking for their favorite trees to tear up and chew through, sometimes clearing whole slopes. Just about everything gives them a wide berth, when they can. Even apex predators like a kleztigr don't think they're worth the trouble. You'd think that they'd be easy to spot and avoid, and mostly you'd be right. But sometimes you'll round a bend or clear a slope and there one'll be, just plodding along or munching on a log or taking a nap, and you'd best try to circle around, quiet-like.

1256.title: Gouzadn
1256.body: Solitary , magical , cautious , stealthy , shapeshifter HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. certain sacred wood Damage tooth and claw d10 ( hand , grabby ), hoof and antler d10+2 ( close , forceful ), rending strength d10+2 ( hand , forceful , messy ) Special qualities confused and frustrated by human speech, host to dozens of beast-spirits Instinct to see people as either threat or prey - Assume the aspect(s) of one or more beasts - Do something impossible for a human, easy for a beast - Suffer through a terrible wound unfazed, healing with a few days' rest - Ask beasts or spirits (even quiet ones) for information or favors When a young Hillfolk shows signs of being touched by spirits, their band had best seek out a spirit-talker to mentor the child. Without such guidance, these youths might forsake their humanity, forget their language, and eventually bind spirits of the wild to their very flesh. A gouzadn shuns people, living with only spirits for company. They're more likely to flee and hide than fight when people encroach on their territory. An aggressive or unwary party, though, might find their numbers picked off one-by-one in the dark or when separated. And when that happens, a gouzadn might get a taste of killing people, maybe a taste for their flesh. Such monsters haunt the tales and nightmares of the Hillfolk. Gouzadn gain their spirits by bonding with a beast, then consuming its flesh in a special, intuitive ritual. They might be reasoned with by the Blessed or someone else who speaks the Spirit Tongue. They might even be re-taught language, and convinced to divest their flesh of its surplus spirits. But most will be loath to do so; at best, they'll insist on keeping these spirits with them in a sacred pouch or similar vessel. Something interesting: Tales tell of using "sacred wood" to put down a gouzadn. Maybe a Hillfolk elder could tell you the specific type of wood to use? Something useful: To kill this gouzadn, you'll need a weapon made of... (roll the Die of Fate)

1257.title: 1d6 sacred wood
1257.body: - 1 Redwood - 2 Heartwood of lightning-struck ash - 3 Elder wood, blackened in sacrificial fire - 4 Oak, etched with words of grounding and renewal - 5 Yew, carved with a sorcerer's bone - 6 Willow, the tree felled by an innocent, the wood carved by one stooped with regrets

1258.title: Grochslon
1258.body: Solitary , patient , stealthy HP 12; Armor 0 Damage outer jaws d10 ( close , grabby ), inner jaws d10+2 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities blind; senses prey by scent, hearing, vibrations Instinct to feed on the unwary, then flee - Lurk unseen in a dark, sheltered spot - Spring forward without warning, chomping down - Hold tight with outer jaws while tearing away with the inner - Squirm through a narrow opening Pale, eyeless things that spawn in the lightless lakes below the Steplands. Like a great big pale salamander or snake with two stubby, muscular legs. As they grow, they find food down there sparse, and make their way to the surface. Mostly they stick to cave mouths, but sometimes—sometimes—they get bold enough to leave their sheltered holes of rock, and they find other places to lurk, places where you'd never expect them.

1259.title: Kleztigr
1259.body: Solitary , large , cagey , stealthy , agile , powerful HP 16; Armor 1 (reflexes) Damage huge saber-like fangs d10+3 w/ advantage ( hand , grabby , messy , 1 piercing), claws or pounce d10+1 w/ advantage ( close ) Instinct to bring down a good, solid meal - Follow prey for hours, even days - Pounce on prey from hiding ( reach , forceful , grabby ) - Inflict a deadly, bleeding wound Like a cougar but twice as big, with 8-inch fangs hanging out of their mouths. Hillfolk tales claim they once were much more common, pets of the Stone Lords even. Now they are (thankfully) rare. Something interesting: Some Hillfolk bands "adopt" a kleztigr, making offerings of meat or young goats, sheep, or even horses. The kletztigr follows the band about, protecting them from outsiders. Something useful: Such kleztigr won't harm anyone wearing some item or clothing distinctive to "their" band (but they might expect a present).

1260.title: Spitting drake
1260.body: Group , stealthy , organized HP 6; Armor 2 (pebbly scales) Damage vice-like jaws d8 ( hand , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities chameleon skin, excellent climbers Instinct to pin down a good meal, then swarm it - Lay an ambush, where they can surround prey - Spit a head-sized gob of sticky mucus ( near , grabby , reload ) - Sprint much more quickly than you'd expect They're about the size of a big wolf, but lower and squatter, with arms out to the sides like lizards and a big old frill that pops up when they're startled or bearing down on prey. They favor canyons, defiles, or overlooks where they can bask in the sun but have a good angle to ambush passersby. Something interesting: Their mucus adheres quickly to rock, pinning prey in place. Even if it doesn't pin prey down, it gloms on and slows them up. It'll dry out and start to flake away after a few hours. Something useful: Strong spirits (yes, like whisky) quickly dissolve the mucus. Also, you can identify their territory by the pale chalky stains their mucus leaves on stone.

1261.title: Caves
1261.body: The Steplands are lousy with caves, carved out over centuries by the slow patient work of water, or more dramatically by the magic of the Stone Lords.

1262.title: Impressions
1262.body: - Instinctive hunch of your shoulders under a low stone ceiling - Prickly, humid air, thick and still and cloying - Slow, quiet drip… drip… drip… in the distance - Darkness so thick and deep, it could swallow you - Every sense heightened: rough stone underfoot, the breath of your companions, the damp mineral smell, the way your eyes strain in flickering light

1263.title: Structure
1263.body: Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1264.title: 1d6 structure
1264.body: - 1 Small, contained, only a few chambers - 2 Long, narrow, meandering - 3 Twisting, interlaced tunnels; multiple chambers - 4 A few distinct sets of chambers, connected by long twisting tunnels - 5 Few small passages to a larger, massive chamber - 6 Large and open main tunnel, smaller side passages and chambers For any given opening, passage, or chamber, choose or have someone roll for clearance, footing, water, and decoration. For each, consider rolling 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

1265.title: 1d6 clearance
1265.body: - 1 Basically a crack, too small for grown humans without excavation - 2 A squeeze—most folk can wriggle through without gear on - 3-4 Small, cramped—might need to duck, crawl, go single file - 5 Roomy, comfortable to walk through - 6 Yawning, vast, lots of space

1266.title: 1d6 footing
1266.body: - 1 Steep slope, vertical ascent/descent - 2 Slippery—water, mud, scree, guano, etc. - 3 Rocky, debris-filled, unstable - 4 Uneven, ridged, potholes, awkward - 5 Rolling, undulating, twisting - 6 Surprisingly smooth, level

1267.title: 1d6 water
1267.body: - 1 Submerged - 2 Partially flooded - 3 A stream or rivulet, flowing steadily - 4-6 Just a trickle, drips, or seepage; maybe not even that

1268.title: 1d6 decoration
1268.body: - 1 Stalactite(s) or soda straws, clinging tight to the ceiling - 2 Stalagmite(s), growing from the floor - 3 Column(s), where stalactites and stalagmites have grown together - 4 Crystals, crystalline growths, "honeycomb" patterns - 5 Flowstone, rimstone, "draperies," "shields" - 6 Fossils

1269.title: Inhabitants, past or present
1269.breadcrumb: The Steplands › Impressions › Inhabitants, past or present
1269.body: Choose or have someone roll, up to 3 times. What story does this tell?

1270.title: 1d6 inhabitant
1270.body: - 1 Thralls or victims of the Things Below The Things Below - 2 Barrow Builders Barrow Builders or their victims - 3 Truly ancient primordial powers Primordial powers - 4 natural beast or spirit (see below) - 5 Stone Lords Stone Lords or their servants - 6 Hillfolk Hillfolk or those they have cast out

1271.title: 1d6 natural beast or spirit
1271.breadcrumb: The Steplands › Impressions › 1d6 natural beast or spirit
1271.body: - 1 Bats. So many bats The Steplands - 2 Fish, salamanders, olms, crawfish—pale blind things dwelling in dark waters - 3 Cave bears The Great Wood, a kleztigr The Steplands, or similar large beast, making its den here - 4 Grochslon The Steplands, or some other subterranean predator - 5 Spirit, bound to or happily dwelling in this sacred site Sacred sites - 6 A malicious spirit, like a troelloff Spirits of the Wild

1273.title: 1d6 discovery
1273.body: - 1 Connection to another cave system - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3-5 Signs of a (prior) inhabitant (previous column), like a site, artifact(s), or remains - 6 A relatively good place to rest/hide

1274.title: Dangers
1274.body: - Cold, especially if they've gotten wet - Heat and humidity, especially if they carry heavy loads - Getting lost - Cave-ins, rock-falls - Getting stuck in a squeeze - Running out of/losing your light - Fear, panic, or confusion in the face of any of the above

1275.title: Stone Lords
1275.body: In the time of the Makers , the Stone Lords ruled the Steplands , the southern stretch of the Huffel Peaks , and the South Manmarch . They carved cities out of the hills. They raised towering monuments. They sang stone, crystal, and glass into impossible shapes. The Stone Lords' empire was grand, but it was not all glory. It was built on the backs of countless human servants. And while the Stone Lords were not especially cruel, neither were they kind. They imposed order on their world, by whatever means necessary. For, better than the other Makers, the Stone Lords knew the Things Below . Knew the insidious songs that echoed in the deep. Knew the chaos promised. Knew that they must never, ever be freed. And so, when the Green Lords called on the Things Below to put down their own servants' rebellion, the Stone Lords turned on their brethren. They shattered the Green Lords' lands and hunted down the survivors. Yet: the seals had cracked. The Things Below sang all the louder. The Stone Lords themselves stood resolute, but their servants did not. Corruption seeped through their society like water finding cracks in a poorly laid foundation. The end came swiftly. Sorcerers turned the city of Dulenn into a massive sinkhole, creating what is now Blackwater Lake . Seals were broken across the land and horrors slithered up through the tunnels. Mobs turned on their former masters. One by one, the domains and cities of the Stone Lords fell into ruin. The Time of Cataclysm had arrived.

1276.title: Names
1276.body: Breton- and Cornish-inspired, but with extra vowels for a drawn-out sound: Aenedellion, Caedour, Daenizel, Eelowain, Gaeille, Iaves, Kaeteal, Loiawen, Mearauwd, Neassa, Oaneaz, Paerrain, Rozainne, Saennaen, Taenguey, Wainoac, Yainniac Combine with prefixes like "Aalz" ("the great"), "Skol" ("master"), or "Loaji" ("apprentice"), etc.

1277.title: Hooks
1277.body: - Someone claims to have found a Stone Lord ruin abundant with precious gems - A petrified prisoner of the Stone Lords was recently reanimated, and must now make sense of this new age - While seeking shelter from the elements, the PCs stumble on a Stone Lord ruin - Hillfolk need help dealing with dangerous stone constructs that are running amok

1279.title: 1d12 theme
1279.body: - 1 Rock and stone: excavated and shaped into grandiose structures - 2 Crystals/gems/ makerglass : decorative, practical, or thrumming with power - 3 Songs/resonance/vibrations/echoes: to empower, shape, transform, or destroy - 4 Seals/bindings/things trapped in stone: creatures/spirits/ideas/memories/free will - 5 Constructs/stone given life, or an approximation thereof - 6 Authority/order/hierarchy/tradition - 7 Human servants (slaves?): their corruption by the Things Below , their revolt, the Barrow Builders they became - 8 Bonds broken/seals opened/collapse and chaos/the Things Below set loose - 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

1280.title: Sites
1280.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1281.title: 1d6 site
1281.body: - 1-3 Lingering signs of their presence and influence (see below) - 4-6 A ruin

1282.title: Lingering signs
1282.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1283.title: 1d6 sign
1283.body: - 1 Something trapped in stone, crystal, or makerglass (next column) - 2 Strange geologies (next page) - 3-4 Fragments of old construction (stairs, walls, foundations, chimneys), all that's left of once-grand structures - 5 A stretch of the Makers' Roads , intact or broken but unearthed/barely visible - 6 A monument, marker, or enigma (next page)

1284.title: Something trapped
1284.body: For example: - Someone trapped in a stalagmite, their grasping hands, anguished face, and mournful moans just barely discernible - A hollow, seamless cube of rune-carved white stone, 6 feet per side, bitterly cold; a dozen specters are sealed within, impotent and mad - A stretch of shining black flagstones, showing reflections of people long past; those who open their minds see a parade of memories, clear as day - A construct with a crack in its "head," possessed by an evil spirit; bound in a circle of deeply-cut runes - A labyrinth of stone walls, the walkways forming a rune of binding, some sort of demon trapped in the center

1285.title: Strange geologies
1285.body: For example: - A large grotto, unnaturally smooth and precise, like part of a sphere just vanished from the stone - A gorge cutting perpendicularly through a valley, the walls lined with glass - A tunnel or valley, shaped so that the smallest sounds echo echo echo - A cliff face, the striations perfectly smooth and parallel, the different strata strikingly different colors or types of stone - A slope entirely made of smooth, rounded stone; like an old lava flow, but chert, granite, or limestone - An enormous rock formation, shot through with dozens of small holes that catch the wind and sing a haunting melody.

1286.title: Monument, marker, or enigma
1286.breadcrumb: Stone Lords › Sites › Monument, marker, or enigma
1286.body: For example: - A crater of bare stone, 100 paces across, with a lozenge of black stone floating in the center, thrumming - A statue carved from a mountainside, fragments of its stone sword littering the valley below - A pedestal bearing two vast and trunkless legs of stone; hints of an inscription, now unreadable - Giant stone heads arrayed upon a slope, their faces faded yet still disapproving - A pylon of glass, 100 feet tall at least (and who knows how deeply set into the earth), faintly humming, faintly glowing - Great lines gouged into the earth, running for miles; meaningless from the surface, but making enormous runes when seen from on high - A large room in some cave or ruin, the charred bones of a giant at the center; bits of blackened bone/tooth/carapace litter the edge; hundreds of fist-sized shards of crystal are embedded in the walls - A chamber with dozens of glass cylinders, fixed vertically against a wall, each of differing length and width—some sort of instrument, perhaps?

1287.title: Ruin
1287.body: Pick or roll for themes Stone Lords, structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see Huffel Peaks for the Huffel Peaks , for the Steplands , or South Manmarch for the South Manmarch ). Populate it with lingering signs , artifacts and/or dangers of the Stone Lords Stone Lords or the appropriate region.

1288.title: 1d6 structure
1288.body: - 1 Tightly packed, human-sized structures/rooms - 2 Single, bunker-like building - 3 Large, open space - 4 Tower, rising/jutting out from a hill/mountain - 5 Chambers/tunnels dug into the sides of cliffs/hills/mountains - 6 Grandiose building of impossible construction

1289.title: 1d6 purpose
1289.body: - 1 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, prison, etc.) - 2 Infrastructure (aqueduct, rampart, road, bridge, etc.) - 3 Storage (vault, warehouse, library, museum, etc.) - 4 Production (workshop, factory, mine, farm, etc.) - 5 Esoteric (laboratory, experiment, power source, etc.) - 6 Civic life (agora, theater, shrine, monument, tomb, etc.)

1290.title: 1d6 architectural elements
1290.body: - 1 Great slabs of marble/granite/limestone/etc., far too big for humans to lift - 2 Seamless stone structures/tunnels, like they were poured or molded that way - 3 Runes carved into stone and shaped out of walls, roads, tunnels, etc. - 4 Crystals/makerglass: catching light/wind, humming/glowing, chiming/gonging - 5 Stones/crystals that emanate emotion/mood/thoughts/memories - 6 Grandiosity of scale, big enough to humble even the Makers Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants—people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

1291.title: 1d6 condition
1291.body: - 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable - 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away - 5 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact - 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved Many Stone Lord ruins were reclaimed by the Barrow Builders at some point. See Blackwater Lake and Three Coven Lake . The PCs cut north from Three Coven Lake and seek out a place to camp. I decide that they find a Stone Lord ruin. I roll 6 for theme (authority/order/hierarchy/tradition), a 5 for structure (chambers dug into the sides of hills), a 6 for purpose (civic life), a 3 for architectural elements (crystals/makerglass), and 4 for condition (buried or hidden, mostly intact). I imagine some sort of belltower, once used to signal the hour and give order to the day. What remains is the hint of stone stairs leading up a rocky outcrop. Near the top, a partially collapsed tunnel opens into a smooth-walled, domed chamber. A makerglass chime hangs from the ceiling, just a few inches in diameter but a hundred feet tall or more, dropping down a shaft barely wider than it is. If tapped, the chime rings painfully loudly. If struck firmly, it resonates with a thunderous, deafening noise. I think there's also a great big bronze maul still in the chamber, and that a kleztigr The Steplands has been using this place as a den.

1292.title: Makerglass
1292.body: Cut or blown into wondrous shapes, in many colors and textures, unbreakable and unworkable by mortal means. Often found near Stone Lord ruins, and highly prized. Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

1293.title: 1d12 form
1293.body: - 1-2 Enigmatic shape (chip/shard/chunk/block/sphere/etc.), its purpose unclear - 3-4 Mundane object (tool/utensil/container/lantern/etc.), though perhaps not easily recognized as such - 5-6 Object of war (weapon/armor/shield/helm/etc.) - 7-8 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/tiles/torc/amulet/ring/etc.) - 9-10 Musical instrument (bell/chime/flute/horn/etc.) - 11-12 Construction (door/stairway/arch/bricks/walls/etc.), likely immobile Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

1294.title: 1d12 characteristic
1294.body: - 1-2 Breathtakingly beautiful - 3-4 Glows/shines (roll 1d6 for scale) - 5 Sings/hums/thrums (roll 1d6 for scale) - 6 Absorbs something (heat/sound/light/energy/vibration/etc.) - 7 Roll 1d6 twice - 8-12 Just (just) an indestructible object made of glass

1296.title: Various treasures
1296.body: - A severed, three-fingered ◇ hand of a stone construct (Value 0), cleverly articulated but inert - A single makerglass arrowhead ( indestructible , 2 piercing, Value 1) - A ◇ makerglass jar with a threaded lid, seals airtight ( indestructible , Value 2) - A ◇◇ stack of engraved ceramic tiles that fit together like a jigsaw puzzle; properly assembled, the engravings form a potent ring of runes for binding demons (Value 1, or Value 3 to the right person) - A ring of petrified wood, set with a shining diamond (?) (Value 2, beautiful ) - A huge bronze gong ( immobile , Value 2), carved along the edge with runes of amplification - A ◇ makerglass sword ( close , messy , indestructible , beautiful , +1 damage, 2 piercing, Value 3) - A fist-sized ruby ( beautiful , Value 4); if placed in a fire or otherwise heated, it hums beautiful, haunting notes

1297.title: A chalky potion
1297.breadcrumb: Stone Lords › Artifacts › A chalky potion
1297.body: magical , fragile , slow 1d4 glass vials containing thick, gritty, whitish fluid. The stopper bears a mark of transformation, flesh, and stone. When you drink the whole potion while touching stone , you transform (over a matter of minutes) into a mass of stone, similar to that which you touch. You retain awareness of your surroundings, blind but with excellent hearing. When you attempt to end your lithic vigil , roll +CON: on a 10+ , you return to your original form, rested and invigorated—regain all your HP and clear all your debilities; on a 7-9 , the transformation back is slow and arduous, mark a debility; on a 6- , you are stuck until someone pours another dose of the potion over you or communes with your spirit and helps you try again.

1298.title: Power-crystal
1298.body: ◇, magical , dangerous A chunk of crystal the size of a clenched fist. If charged, it thrums or crackles with power. If depleted, it fills you with a sense of emptiness and longing. When you brandish the depleted powercrystal at a source of elemental energy , roll +CON: on a 10+ , the crystal absorbs the energy fully and as safely as possible (it is now charged); on a 7-9 , the crystal absorbs the energy (it's now charged) but pick 1: - You are harmed by the coursing energy, taking damage as the GM sees fit - The power stored in the crystal is extremely unstable, likely to discharge without warning - The crystal cracks; henceforth, treat a 10+ on this move as a 7-9, and you can't pick this option again. In the hands of a skilled arcanist, a charged power-crystal can fuel or empower a magical effect (though this is dangerous). In the hands of the incautious, the reckless, or the desperate, it can split or crack, leading to a violent explosion.

1299.title: A scarred slab of marble
1299.breadcrumb: Stone Lords › Artifacts › A scarred slab of marble
1299.body: immobile , magical A slab of once-beautiful stone, some 30 feet tall and about half again as wide, standing in a vaulted chamber, surrounded by deeply etched runes that speak of union and protection, consumption and sacrifice. The stone bears a multitude of chips, gouges, and cracks. Dust and debris surround it. When you pry a chip of stone from the marble and swallow it whole, while standing within the ring of runes , your flesh becomes like stone and your DEX becomes -2 until the effect ends. The next three times you would suffer physical harm, the marble slab suffers the harm instead. The effect ends after the third blow.

1301.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1301.breadcrumb: Stone Lords › Minor arcana › 1d6 minor arcanum
1301.body: - 1 A giant's dormitory - 2 Rune-etched pillars - 3 A timeless vault - 4 A sealed cave - 5 A makerglass chime - 6 A fine ceramic urn

1302.title: Major arcana
1302.body: These might very well be associated with the Stone Lords. - The Mindgem - Whispering Rocks

1304.title: Bound Entities
1304.body: - Spirits of the wild , especially elementals associated with earth, stone, and terrain, yoked by runecraft to serve the Stone Lord's needs - Spirits and beasts corrupted by the Things Below : petrified, encased in stone, or sealed away behind layers of runes - Would-be sorcerer-kings , petrified or otherwise imprisoned before they could cause too much trouble - Ghosts , mindlessly performing duties they were bound to perform in life, unaware that they could want anything else - Primordial entities , imprisoned by elaborate workings of crystal and rune, bound to serve or else locked away for safekeeping

1305.title: Unstable construction
1305.body: The Stone Lords' work has stood for centuries, and they were the cleverest architects to ever walk this world. But the problem with grand, clever architecture is that when it fails, it fails spectacularly. - A wall/floor/building is precariously perched, or a pillar/support/buttress supports an impossible load - Something creaks/teeters/sways/groans - Cracks form/debris tumbles/something starts to tip - Age-old construction starts to fall like dominoes (run!) - All is reduced to rubble, and dust, and death

1306.title: Perilous stone
1306.body: Pick or roll for form, original purpose, and peril, possibly rolling the Die of Fate for scope/scale. Consider how it fit into the daily lives of the Stone Lords or their servants—what they intended it for, how they avoided it, etc.

1307.title: 1d12 form
1307.body: - 1-2 Mechanism/construction - 3-5 Rune(s): engraved in stone, worked into tile, formed out of architecture - 6-8 Crystal(s)/gem(s)/glass - 9-11 Strange stone - 12 Makerglass

1308.title: 1d12 original purpose
1308.body: - 1-2 Convenience/storage/infrastructure - 3-4 Entertainment/art/pleasure - 5-6 Violence/war/defense - 7-8 Production/power/industry - 9-10 Esoteric/research/worship - 11-12 Control/order/authority

1309.title: 1d12 peril
1309.body: - 1 Discharges energy - 2 Crushes/smashes/grinds - 3 Captures/imprisons/isolates/separates - 4 Forbids/punishes certain actions - 5 Drains energy/emotion/memory - 6 Overwhelms senses/emotions - 7 Lures/entices/deceives - 8 Draws attention - 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice - 11-12 Roll 1d10, but make it grandiose—it doesn't just capture, it petrifies; it doesn't just grind, it reduces something to dust, etc.

1310.title: Some examples:
1310.body: - Peacebond Peacebond runes, worked into roads, halls, public places - A glossy cube of blue-black stone that steals memories from those who touch it, playing them back for only the strongest of wills - A stone vault that might slip closed if folks are careless, no way to open it from the inside - A human-sized crystal, cracked, absorbing telluric energies, occasionally discharging them as bolts of lightning - A pile of shimmery sand; when someone touches it, it absorbs an imprint of their mind and forms itself into a simulacrum of them (convinced that it is the original)

1311.title: Construct
1311.body: The Stone Lords were masters not only of shaping stone, but also of animating it and giving it the semblance of life. Many such creations remain, though only a few still function and many of those function poorly. Pick or roll a purpose, ability, size, power, sapience, and condition. Allow these elements to inform its physical form and construction.

1312.title: 1d12 purpose
1312.body: - 1-2 War/destruction/violence/coercion - 3-4 Defense/protection/companionship - 5-6 Experimentation/curiosity/practice - 7-8 Construction/labor/convenience - 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

1313.title: 1d12 ability
1313.body: - 1 Nothing of note, just animated stone - 2 Mental influence/control - 3 Energy/elemental manipulation - 4 Cunning/alertness/special senses - 5 Disguise/camouflage/stealth - 6 Restraints/snares/subdual - 7 Ranged/area attack - 8 Transformation/multipart form/swarm - 9 Prowess/mobility/speed - 10 Destructive/vicious weaponry - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice Stone Lord constructs are typically blind but able to navigate by sound and sensing vibrations. Almost all are highly durable (+4 HP) and animated by more than biology (another +4 HP), with at least 3 Armor.

1314.title: 1d12 size
1314.body: - 1 Cat-sized or smaller ( tiny ) - 2-3 Like a human child ( small ) - 4-7 Human-sized - 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. ( large ) - 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger ( huge )

1315.title: 1d12 power
1315.body: - 1-3 Requires the presence of external source - 4-8 Stores power, but must return "home" to recharge - 9-11 Self-charging, but limited range/activity - 12 Self-powered/indefinite charge Power sources might include: singing crystals that broadcast energy; ingots of aetherium ; veins of special stone transmitting power through floors; crystals that absorb telluric currents/vibrations; spirits bound in makerglass; etc.

1316.title: 1d12 sapience
1316.body: - 1-3 Puppet: requires active control by outside intellect - 4-6 Bug-like: can follow limited range of programs/tasks - 7-10 Beast-like: can take basic direction, complete some complex tasks independently - 11 Automaton: can handle complex tasks, learn; lacks true emotion/creativity - 12 Person: fully sapient, potentially quite intelligent Ask yourself: where does this construct's sapience come from? Clever scripting? Spirits bound to stone? The will drained from living servants? Something else?

1317.title: 1d12 condition
1317.body: - 1-2 No power/power source failing - 3-4 Broken/impaired limb(s) or chassis - 5-6 Trapped/pinned/buried - 7-8 Behaving erratically - 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice - 11-12 Fully functional

1318.title: Lithic servant
1318.body: Horde , construct , implacable HP 11; Armor 3 (made of stone) Damage stone fists d6 ( hand , grabby ) Special qualities externally powered; bug-like intellect; blind; tremorsense Instinct to follow its last orders - Act with eternal patience and endurance - Act with no sense of consequence - Hurt someone unintentionally while performing its tasks Imagine one of those artists' mannequins, but full-sized and made of polished rock. Two fingers and a thumb on each hand. Faceless, expressionless, making a stone-on-stone grinding noise as they move. They're most often found inert and covered in dust, waiting for some energy source—like a Stone Lord's song or a thrumming power-crystal—to give them "life." They'll then follow any orders given to them in the Stone Lord's tongue. A few of these servants are still active, powered by inexhaustible sources in ancient halls. Their joints are worn smooth, their feet and hands ground down to nubs. Some crawl on broken limbs, attempting to complete tasks that will never end.

1319.title: Stone sentinel
1319.body: Solitary , large , construct , implacable HP 24; Armor 3 (made of stone) Damage crushing fists d10+3 ( hand , close , grabby , messy , forceful , 3 piercing) Special qualities can animate for only a few minutes at a time; dog-like mind; blind; tremorsense Instinct to protect its ward - Emit an ominous hum, a warning as it powers up - Move with shocking, unexpected speed (but lose 1d4 HP) - Erupt in flurry of violence ( area , +2 damage, lose 2d4 HP) Articulated statues, squat and wide, with huge stone hands, set to watch forever over some ruin, passage, or treasure. Their stone "minds" are full of vigilance, and violence. Each sentinel bears a power-crystal in its chest, slowly absorbing vibrations through earth and stone. A few days of charging is enough to animate the construct for only a few minutes—which is usually more than enough time for it to repel (or crush) an invader.

1320.title: Mosaic swarm
1320.body: Solitary , large , construct , amorphous , stealthy HP 24; Armor 3 (made of thousands of ceramic tiles) Damage jagged fists of ceramic d10+3 ( hand , close , forceful ) Special qualities externally powered; bug-like mind; blind; tremorsense; unharmed by piercing weapons; half damage from cutting weapons; vulnerable to attacks that are blunt and forceful or area Instinct to destroy intruders - Wait for intruders to get close, rattling softly - Explode into a cloud of whirling ceramic tiles, d8+1 damage ( area , messy , 1 piercing) - Reform into a vaguely humanoid mass Stone Lords adorned their halls with elaborate tilework and mosaics. Most of them were decorative. Most of them. Something interesting : Either some spirit is possessing these tiles, or this is some sort of construct. If it's a spirit, there must be a tether. If it's a Stone Lord construct, well, the individual tiles are too small to power or direct themselves.
