Something useful : The mosaic's frame remained on the wall, and it's glowing blue—aetherium runes! Destroy the runes, and the construct should fall apart.

1321.title: Pacifier
1321.body: Group , small , construct , amorphous HP 10; Armor 4 (just a stone sphere) Damage stupefying pulse of sound d6 ( reach , area , reload , ignores armor) Special qualities must recharge after a few hours; limited autonomy; blind; tremorsense; has trouble with stairs; vulnerable to blunt, forceful attacks Instinct to keep mortals docile - Sense and follow strong emotions - Hum a soothing, pacifying sound ( near , area ) - Sound the alarm, call for reinforcements They're basically hollow stone spheres, maybe scratched and dinged but otherwise featureless. Often found in their little alcoves, charging. The Stone Lords would deploy them among the rowdier servants to keep things running smoothly. When you are in range of a pacifier's hum , your emotions lose their edge. When you rouse yourself to act violently , urgently, or passionately, roll +WIS. If you are in range of multiple pacifiers humming in unison , you have disadvantage. On a 10+ , you shake off the effect; on a 7-9 , you shake off the effect but pick 1. - Your mind is addled; mark dazed - Your freedom is short-lived; the effect returns if you pause for any reason - You get carried away or take things too far, resulting in a consequence of the GM's choice

1322.title: The Stream
1322.body: A few minutes from Stonetop, near the base of the bluff, in the Great Wood (page 200\). Wide, shallow, swift, lined with small stones. Folks come here to wash and fetch water, but rarely alone. It's not deep, but it's still a body of water. - The current's constant rush and murmur - Flat plain on either side, gravelly, dotted with saplings, weeds, wildflowers - Vague sense of dread, of being watched - Spring : cold snowmelt swelling the Stream - Summer : dragonflies, lightning bugs - Spring/Autumn : leaves in current - Winter : eerie stillness, icy surface Tip: raise your voice a little, like you're talking over background sound

1323.title: Questions
1323.body: - What little ritual do villagers perform every time they cross the Stream? Why? - How far have you followed the Stream? Which way? What strange sight did you see on the way? - Which of you have encountered the Quiet Twins, the ghostly children who haunt the Stream? - What's the scariest, darkest story you've ever heard about the Stream? Who told it to you? - Who do you think the Quiet Twins were? Why?

1324.title: Hooks
1324.body: - A dangerous beast takes to watering near town. - The Stream runs dry, despite ample rains - Something mysterious (dangerous?) is deposited by the current. - Haunting songs are heard on misty days; hot-blooded youths are intent on finding the source (an entrancing Fae, like Blush-of-Dawn ). - The Quiet Twins appear more frequently, urgently trying to warn of… something.

1326.title: The Quiet Twins
1326.body: Group , small , spirit , undead , cautious , unnerving HP 13; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage icy touch d6 ( hand , disadvantage, ignores armor) Special qualities mute, bound to the Stream Instinct to warn of some danger (real or not) - Watch what happens at the Stream (seen or unseen) - Manifest a ghostly form of mist, cold air (harmed only by silver or salt) - Mouth and gesture in silent terror Tip: shrink into your seat, to make yourself smaller, bug eyed, while mouthing gibberish. A pair of ghostly children who haunt the Stream. Sightings are uncommon, but common enough.

1327.title: Tempest Lords
1327.body: Tempest Lords They were, perhaps, the first of the Makers . They charted the seas and the stars. They leashed the elements to their will. They built mighty ships and flying castles, sailed forth from their island home and conquered. All who stood against them were reduced to ash and glass and slag. The pride of the Tempest Lords was warranted, but their hubris led to their fall. They sought to tear their entire homeland free from the earth, to set it floating above the world they ruled. They failed. Calamity followed. The seas boiled and belched forth horrors. Fire rained from the sky. Their island capital sank beneath the waves, and the spine of their empire was shattered. Civil war erupted. Colonies rebelled. The Tempest Lords who survived were scattered to the winds. Some tried to restore the empire, but met violent ends at the hands of rivals or would-be subjects. Soon, only a few Tempest Lords remained, enigmas even among other Makers. One of these survivors, Stormcatcher, made her way to the the Flats , beyond even the most far-flung of the empire's old outposts. There, between four other nations of Makers, she built her tower. And when the horrors of the Things Below were set loose upon the world, Stormcatcher's tower—what folks now call the Ruined Tower —was the last of the Makers' enclaves to fall.

1328.title: Language and names
1328.body: Tempest Lord writing was ideogrammatic (characters represent ideas, not sounds), and they spoke a primordial language—all who heard it understood it as their native tongue. As such, the names of Tempest Lords are rendered as descriptions, titles, or honorifics. Example names: Brinedrinker, Eyes-Like-Lightning, Fardweller, Foehammer, Lightning-Is-Her-Spear, His-Smile-Is-a-Harbor, Mind-Like-Fire, Moonspeaker, Oarbreaker, Rainchaser, Soother-Of-Waves, Sparkspitter, Squalltalker, Starfinder, Taller-Than-Masts, Voice-of-Thunder, Wavecutter, Waymaker, Windwalker When other Makers spoke or wrote of them, they used their own tongue. Windwalker was called Vetrodokha by the Green Lords and Avaelkerzhaer by the Stone Lords .

1329.title: Hooks
1329.body: - A terrible storm sweeps through, a sky-island floating in its eye - A scholar believes that Stonetop hides a trove of Tempest Lord artifacts - Cultists seek an orrery with the power to change the stars, to hasten a conjunction that will free their master - Bandits unearth a Tempest Lord weapon and threaten Stonetop with it - A hdour seeks to revive a vitrified Maker

1331.title: 1d12 theme
1331.body: - 1 Elemental forces and primordial power : wielded and harnessed, perhaps imperfectly - 2 Storms: thunder and lightning, wrath and fury, power unleashed - 3 The heavens: weather and clouds, sky-islands, stars and celestial bodies - 4 Sailing: seas and ships, navigation and exploration - 5 Machines and devices, aetherium and stored energy - 6 Curiosity/experimentation/invention/hubris - 7 Loss of control: unexpected consequences/berserk rage/cataclysmic failure/experiments run amok - 8 An empire collapsed: conquering armies, colonies, plunder, lost glory, resentment - 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

1332.title: Sites
1332.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1333.title: 1d6 site
1333.body: - 1-3 Lingering signs of their presence (see below) - 4-6 A ruin

1334.title: Lingering signs
1334.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1335.title: 1d6 sign
1335.body: - 1 A place bearing scars of unchecked power (next column) - 2 Fragments of a ship or sky-island (next page) - 3-4 Enigmatic structures (next page) - 5 An elemental spirit of sky, storm, weather, etc.; bound and tethered - 6 Ruins of other Makers or the Barrow Builders : blasted, shattered, vitrified

1336.title: Scars of unchecked power
1336.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Sites › Scars of unchecked power
1336.body: For example: - A crater, lousy with fulgurites and shards of glass - A fractal, branching pattern etched in a bare expanse of stone - A rune-etched menhir or large hunk of makerglass , near which some fundamental law or force (time, gravity, space, light, etc.) is warped - A barren stretch of glass pebbles, strangely colored and always warm - The vitrified remains of a Maker, a monster, a small army, etc. Crude glass shapes hinting at what they once were, flash-frozen in time Places scarred by such power might very well be radioactive , haunted sites , and/or corrupted by the Things Below .

1337.title: Fragments of a ship or sky-island
1337.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Sites › Fragments of a ship or sky-island
1337.body: For example: - A ship's hull, mostly buried but somehow still intact - A torn sail of aetherium mesh, strapped to a rusty metal cross - Strange objects, strewn about and long-buried: a crumpled brass cylinder, an etched metal disk, an oversized ship's wheel, a rudder, etc. - A rocky outcrop, the stone alien to the surrounding terrain, tilted at an odd angle - A cracked slab of granite, big as the granary and shot through with aetherium, floating a few feet above the ground and spinning listlessly - Slabs of worked stone: broken, buried, and scattered, like a child's toy dashed from impossible heights

1338.title: Enigmatic structures
1338.body: For example: - Iron spires, like hollow rusted trees, placed in a line across the landscape - A black cube the size of a house; when lightning strikes, the earth lights up for miles with glowing lines that fade over the next few minutes - Stone plinths along a ridge line, some of them topped with 6-foot-tall sculptures, like tulips, spinning in the breeze - Copper cylinders, each maybe 8 feet tall and caked in verdigris; mostly buried in white and chalky soil - Megaliths, etched with ideographs that describe the motions of the stars - A bronze plaque upon a stone slab, the only notable feature for miles; untouched by lichen; deeply etched ideographs begin with the phrase, "This is not a place of honor"

1339.title: A ruin
1339.body: Pick or roll for themes Tempest Lords, structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see The Flats for the Flats and South Manmarch for the South Manmarch , but Tempest Lords ranged far and wide). Populate it with lingering signs , artifacts and/or dangers of the Tempest Lords or the appropriate region.

1340.title: 1d12 structure
1340.body: - 1-2 Cluster of smaller buildings around some central feature - 3 Single, larger building (block, dome, ziggurat, etc.) - 4 Bunker/vault carved into the stone - 5 Platform/amphitheater/terrace(s) - 6 Tower/spire - 7-9 Sky-island, crashed/grounded (and roll d6 again) - 10-11 Sky-island, afloat but anchored/fixed over a particular place (and roll d6 again) - 12 Sky-island, free-floating across the landscape (and roll d6 again)

1341.title: 1d12 purpose
1341.body: - 1-2 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, etc.) - 3-4 Infrastructure (cistern, sewer, lighthouse, harbor, etc.) - 5-6 Storage (vault, batteries, warehouse, library, museum, etc.) - 7-8 Production (generator, workshop, shipworks, factory, farm, etc.) - 9-10 Study/Experimentation (laboratory, observatory, orrery, etc.) - 11-12 Enigma (magical device, artistic endeavor, etc.)

1342.title: 1d12 architectural elements
1342.body: - 1-2 Metal beams/cables/lattices/trusses - 3-4 Open spaces: shafts, atriums, vaulted ceilings, overlooks, windows, skylights - 5-6 Lines of power: aetherium inlays , runes, conduits, nodes, currents - 7-8 Stylized mosaics/statuary: geometric patterns, fractals, celestial bodies, angelic beings, ships - 9-10 Brass fixtures, pipes and pumps, water and steam, gears and flywheels - 11-12 Verticality: standing stones, menhirs, obelisks, towers, spires, masts Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants—people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

1343.title: 1d12 condition
1343.body: - 1-4 Shattered and laid waste by time, disaster, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable - 5-8 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away - 9-10 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact - 11-12 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved For more examples, see the Ruined Tower . One of the PCs is heading to Titan Bones, and I'd like some sense of what's out there. I think it holds a Tempest Lord ruin, probably once associated with Stormcatcher and the now-Ruined Tower The Ruined Tower . I roll a 3 for theme ("the heavens: weather and clouds..."), a 4 for structure ("bunker/vault"), a 3 for purpose ("infrastructure"), and an 11 for architectural elementals ("verticality"). I then roll a 5 for condition ("largely buried... hidden"). I ponder a bit and come up with this: Stormcatcher erected a spire on Titan Bones, like a lighthouse, topped with an aetherium weathervane that modulated the weather. When the Stone Lords' rebellious servants attacked, the tower's custodian took the weathervane and hid beneath the spire. The spire fell and the custodian was trapped. All that remains are scattered stones and a circular foundation. And beneath centuries of dirt and rubble, there's a locked trapdoor. It reveals a spiral staircase, a deep shaft, an intact cellar, a skeleton, and an aetherium weathervane.

1344.title: Aetherium
1344.body: An alloy of copper and lightning. Pure aetherium is light, soft, and ductile (similar to tin), but the Tempest Lords and Forge Lords could further alloy it or manipulate it with magic to have a wide range of properties and uses. Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

1345.title: 1d12 form
1345.body: - 1-3 Raw material (ingot/spool of wire/rod/plate/lamé/shard/etc.) - 4-5 Enigmatic shape (torus/orb/tube/hoop/runes/etc.), its purpose unclear - 6-7 Mundane object (tool/vessel/lantern/etc.), though perhaps not recognized as such - 8-9 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/inlay/torc/amulet/ring/etc.) - 10 Clothing/armor (robe/tunic/apron/cuirass/brigandine/etc.) - 11 Weapon (spear/arrow/sword/maul/shield/trident/wand/etc.) - 12 Construction (archway/truss/beam/door/plating/pipes/etc.), likely immobile Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

1346.title: 1d12 characteristic
1346.body: - 1 Breathtakingly beautiful - 2 Hard and strong as bronze - 3 Absorbs/stores/discharges/transforms an elemental force - 4 Suppresses/repels/directs/impedes a particular element/force - 5 Vibrates in ways that interact with spirits/magic - 6 Extremely light/levitates/defies or reverses gravity - 7 Attunes to another item/locale, for communication/detection - 8 Roll 1d8 twice - 9 Unstable, prone to discharge/failure ( dangerous ) and roll d8 again - 10 Relatively soft/ductile/malleable ( crude ) - 11-12 Roll again with 1d8, but it becomes unstable and/or soft (as above) after any serious use

1347.title: Making aetherium
1347.body: A clever arcanist might rediscover the process of making aetherium. To do so, you must… - Study a largely-intact aetherium crucible for a few weeks - Study both active and depleted aetherium for a few weeks - Consult a knowledgeable metalsmith (or be one yourself) - Acquire the appropriate chemics (Value 0) and a sizeable mass of copper (Value 1, immobile ) - Devise some way to introduce lightning to molten copper - Risk injury, fire, destruction, and failure When you meet all the requirements , you can transform a ◇ purse of coppers (Value 0) and the appropriate chemics (Value 0) into an ◇ ingot or casting of pure aetherium ( magical , crude , possibly dangerous, Value 1 to those-who-know). The process is risky; you are Defying Danger each time you undertake it. To imbue aetherium with one of the characteristics above, you'll need access to a trove of Tempest Lord or Forge Lord lore, at least. Beyond that, Make a Plan with the GM.

1349.title: AETHERIUM CRUCIBLE
1349.body: Requires all of the following: - Learning the process of making aetherium (at left) - Learning to imbue aetherium with at least one special characteristic - A reliable means of capturing, storing, and directing lightning that strikes the Stone - Securing a steady supply of the chemics needed to make aetherium - Securing a steady supply of copper - An exceptional smith who knows the secrets of aetherium When you mark all the requirements , add "Aetherium Crucible" to the Resources list and the map. As long as you trade aetherium to the outside world, increase Prosperity by 1. Henceforth, small amounts of raw aetherium ( magical , Value 1) are commonly available in Stonetop, and you have advantage to Trade & Barter for the aetherium items with special characteristics (+2 Value).

1351.title: Various treasures
1351.body: - A small lump of aetherium (Value 0 to most, Value 1 to those-who-know) - An ◇ oversized aetherium spear-head, dull and soft ( awkward , crude , Value 1) - A big brass locket, with a needle inside that points towards a specific Tempest Lord site (Value 2) - A few ◇◇ bolts of canvas, the material unfamiliar but very sturdy (Value 2) - A thunderstorm trapped in a big ◇◇ makerglass bottle, capped with aetherium ( dangerous , Value 3) - A few tarnished silver coins from the Tempest Lords' empire (Value 1 to most, Value 3 to the right collector) - A ◇ codex of thin, metallic sheets, stamped with star charts and Tempest Lord ideographs (Value 3) - A wall-sized bronze plaque, showing a map of the entire Tempest Lord empire ( immobile , Value 4)

1352.title: Aetherium urn
1352.body: ◇, magical , dangerous The lid is held fast by no apparent means. It takes some effort to pry open and eagerly snaps back into place. When the urn is open and empty , it draws raw elemental force into it like a lodestone draws iron. With the lid in place, the force is safely stored. If the urn is damaged or dented (not hard), any stored force escapes, violently.

1353.title: Bronze ring
1353.body: ◇, magical , fireproof , ○○○ hours About 8 inches in diameter, an inch thick throughout, scorched and smeared with ash. A button protrudes, with a Tempest Lord glyph that might mean "stubborn." When you find the ring , it has enough power to run for 1d4-1 hours. When you press the button , the ring fixes in place, defying gravity. It simply will not budge. The effect ends if the ring is bent or broken, or when it runs out of power. There's no obvious way to end the effect early. When the ring runs out of power , the button pops out and the effect ends. When you heat the ring in a forge or kiln for about eight hours, it gains an hour of use (up to 3 hours).

1354.title: Floating sash
1354.body: magical A length of bluish-white lamé, maybe 6 inches wide by 6 feet long, snaking about in the air. Like a belt, but for someone far larger than you. When you wrap the sash securely around your waist , it buoys you against falls—you never fall faster than if you had fallen your own height.

1355.title: Silvery tuning fork
1355.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Artifacts › Silvery tuning fork
1355.body: magical , loud When you strike this tuning fork against a hard surface , it emits a loud tone for about a minute. Nearby ( reach , area ) magical effects and creatures become visible: shaking, vibrating, distorted. The noise draws the attention of any spirits or magically attuned beings for miles around.

1356.title: Storm-cloud potion
1356.body: magical , fragile A glass vial of dark-grey liquid, roiling and shot through with sparks. When you drink a storm-cloud potion , lightning courses through you. Take 1d10 damage (ignores armor) and mark a debility. Should you survive, hold 3 Storm. While you hold any Storm , you cannot feel pain or fear, tell friend from foe, speak coherently, or exercise patience or care. Add your Storm to your Armor, and your melee and thrown attacks do +Storm damage (+ loud , + forceful ). When you reduce someone or something to 0 HP , lose 1 Storm.

1357.title: Minor arcana
1357.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Artifacts › Storm-cloud potion › Minor arcana

1358.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1358.body: - 1 A half-buried plaque - 2 A cloak, richly embroidered - 3 A corroded spearhead - 4 A copper-banded staff - 5 An odd conveyance - 6 A bow with no string

1359.title: Major arcana
1359.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum › Major arcana
1359.body: These might very well be associated with the Tempest Lords. - The Azure Hand - Ineffable Words - Storm Markings

1361.title: Monsters and entities
1361.breadcrumb: Tempest Lords › Dangers › Monsters and entities
1361.body: - A thunder drake or luglfsk , drawn to aetherium - Ghosts or revenants , victims of the Tempest Lord's experiments, callousness, or wrath - An archon , ensuring some Tempest Lord artifact goes unused - An elemental spirit or a fundamental , tethered to a device or artifice - A primordial entity or something corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below, vitrified and held in stasis.

1362.title: Radiation
1362.body: Sometimes, the Tempest Lords unleashed such terrible power that it poisoned all it touched: soil, bone, stone, metal. Poisoned them all so deeply that now, still, centuries and centuries later, those things poison all that draws near. Such poisonous places are often corrupted by the Things Below . But even when they are not, they are dangerous. - Point out flora or fauna, stunted and strange - Make their skin itch, make them feel unwell - Inflict a debility that refuses to go away - Have symptoms appear (or return) hours or days after exposure - Have symptoms linger or worsen, perhaps for months PCs or NPCs exposed to such radiation are prone to developing terrible, wasting illness, months or years later. Write this up as a threat (an affliction ).

1363.title: Vitrification
1363.body: A few Tempest Lords could call up a bolt of power so raw and potent that it would turn whatever it touched into glass. It was dangerous for even them to use, but no foe could stand against it. It's possible that some device still exists that can unleash this sort of power. If so, treat it as a threat (a MacGuffin ; Instinct to tempt people into using it). It would require vast amounts of energy and involve extraordinary risks. When you are struck by a vitrifying bolt , reduce your HP to 0 and roll +CON: on a 10+ , you merely suffer terrible burns and will soon be at Death's Door; on a 7-9 , you and your possessions are turned to jagged glass, but you might be restored; on a 6- , you die but your corpse, your soul, and your possessions are also turned into jagged glass—should the vitrification be reversed, it's as though you rolled a 6- on Death's Door. Vitrification can be reversed, but you must decide how it's done. Perhaps it involves: - … vast amounts of power and great risk; - … a sympathetic link to the victim, or whatever vitrified them in the first place; - … the intervention of a powerful god Gods and religion or primordial entity Primordial powers; - … spilling the life-blood of your kin upon the jagged glass; - … a spell of transference, where someone else becomes vitrified instead; - … a spiritwalker releasing the vitrified soul, and guiding it to a suitable vessel; - … something different for each victim, or for each source of vitrification; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

1364.title: Elemental vortex
1364.body: Picture a filigree sphere of aetherium, maybe 6 inches across, a glowing mote swirling at its center. This device-called a matrix-binds an elemental spirit Spirits of the Wild and absorbs a particular force, with which the spirit is able to manifest a swirling vortex. A spirit bound to a matrix must follow any orders given in the Tempest Lord's primordial language. As few alive can give them such orders, any matrices encountered will likely be acting on orders from centuries ago. Power and size: Each matrix absorbs a particular type of power, and current HP of its vortex reflects its current power. As a vortex gains or loses HP, its size, armor, and damage change as well. At 0 HP, it dissipates and its matrix falls to the ground, inert until it absorbs more HP. Pick or roll to determine a vortex's starting size, HP, Armor, and Damage.

1365.title: 1d12 hp stats
1365.body: 1d12 HP Stats ------- ---------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1-4 1 to 5 tiny ; Armor 1; Damage d8-2 ( hand ) 5-7 6 to 11 small ; Armor 2; Damage d8 ( hand ) 8-9 12 to 18 Armor 2; Damage d10 ( hand , close ) 10-11 19 to 23 large ; Armor 3; Damage d10+3 ( hand , close , reach , forceful ) 12 24 to 28 huge ; Armor 3; Damage d10+5 ( hand , close , reach , area , forceful ) Attacking the matrix: A matrix itself has only 3 HP. If reduced to 0 HP, the spirit within escapes, dealing the vortex's current damage (+ area , one range tag greater than normal). When a PC tries to strike the matrix inside an active vortex , the vortex's Armor applies. Also, tell them the requirements and/or consequences. such as: - You'll have disadvantage to Clash/Let Fly/deal damage - You'll need to create or wait for an opening, where the matrix is exposed - You'll need to get in close/let it engulf you - You'll need a longer/heavier/ magical weapon - You'll risk losing/ruining your weapon - You'll suffer its attack, no matter what Pick options based on the nature of the vortex, the PC's weapon, and the rest of their fictional positioning. The larger the vortex, the harder it is to strike the matrix.

1366.title: Earth vortex
1366.body: Solitary , size by HP, spirit , construct , amorphous , grumpy , clumsy HP varies; Armor by HP, +1 (made of rock) Damage smash (damage and tags by HP) Special qualities half damage from stabbing or slashing weapons (unless magical ) Instinct to topple and undermine - Manifest a churning mass out of nearby earth, sand, and rocks - Pin a smaller foe (+ grabby ) - Grind a pinned foe, dealing damage w/advantage (+ messy ) - Ruin a sturdy object or structure (costs 1d4 HP) - Shake the earth (+ area , + forceful , one range greater than normal; costs 1d4 HP) An earth matrix absorbs power from pressure, blunt force, and seismic activity. It gains 1d4 HP from a week buried under earth or stone, from being at the bottom of a rockslide or cave-in, or from touching the ground during a major earthquake (not one it created). When you strike an earth matrix with a bludgeoning attack , it takes no damage. If the blow was forceful and its vortex has 10 HP or less, it gains 1 HP. (The vortex itself has no such immunities, smash away!)

1367.title: Fire vortex
1367.body: Solitary , size by HP, spirit , construct , amorphous , intense , aggressive HP varies; Armor by HP Damage flames & fire (damage and tags by HP, + dangerous ) Special qualities fireproof; harmed only by magical attacks, water, or smothering Instinct to consume - Set nearby combustibles alight - Manifest a vortex out of nearby fire, cinder, smoke, and ash - Extinguish fires, absorbing their power ( near , area ) - Engulf a smaller foe (+ messy , +ignores armor) - Fling flames (+ near ; costs 1d4 HP) - Leap across a barrier (but lose 1d4 HP) A fire vortex absorbs power from heat and flames. It gains 1d4 HP by spending a day in an oven or an hour in a forge or kiln, or by absorbing a raging fire. When you target a fire vortex or its matrix with magical fire or heat , it takes no damage. If the vortex has 18 HP or less, it gains 1d4 HP instead.

1368.title: Lightning vortex
1368.body: Solitary , size by HP, spirit , construct , amorphous , brash , arrogant HP varies; Armor by HP Damage zap (damage and tags by HP) Special qualities flies; harmed only by magical attacks Instinct to lash out - Manifest a crackling pillar of lightning - Change position, incredibly fast (costs 1 HP) - Stun someone that they zap (costs 1d4 HP) - Fling a lightning bolt (+ near , + forceful , + loud , ignores armor; costs 1d4 HP) - Tear the lightning from unattended aetherium, gaining 1d4 HP A lightning vortex absorbs power from, well, lightning. When it is struck by lightning (mundane or magical ), it gains HP instead of taking damage.

1369.title: Water vortex
1369.body: Solitary , size by HP, spirit , construct , amorphous , relentless , callous HP varies; Armor by HP Damage pummel (damage and tags by HP) Special qualities harmed only by magical weapons, cold, or forceful blunt attacks Instinct to adjust, flow, and adapt - Manifest a churning wave out of water it touches - Vanish into/burst out of a body of water - Engulf a smaller foe (+ grabby , see below) - Surge past foes, dragging them off their feet (+ area , + forceful , costs 1d4 HP) - Move across a porous surface (costs 1d4 HP) A water matrix absorbs power from currents, tides, waves, and falling water. It gains 1d4 HP from a few days in a flowing stream, a few hours in a rushing river, or a few minutes in rapids or a major waterfall. When you are engulfed by a water vortex , hold 1d4 Breath. When you make a move while engulfed , and that move involves a roll, spend 1 Breath or mark a debility after rolling. If you can't do either, you drown and are at Death's Door.

1370.title: Wind vortex
1370.body: Solitary , size by HP, spirit , construct , amorphous , restless , fickle HP varies; Armor by HP Damage lashing grit/debris (damage and tags by HP, but w/disadvantage) Special qualities flies, harmed only by magical weapons, salt, or lead Instinct to play - Manifest a vortex of churning wind, grit, and debris - Engulf a smaller foe (+ grabby ) - Carry an engulfed foe aloft (costs 1d4 HP) - Fling objects (+near; costs 1d4 HP) - Flee when hurt, frightened, or bored A wind matrix absorbs power from moving air. It gains 1d4 HP from weeks exposed to normal weather, an hour in high winds, or a minute or so in a tornado. When you strike a wind vortex with a salt or lead , it takes 1d4 damage from a handful, 2d4 damage from a ◇ item, and 3d4 damage from a ◇◇ item or amount.

1371.title: The Things Below
1371.body: The Things Below They are older than the world. They are primordial entities of darkness, chaos, and corruption. When light first filled the void, they were huddled in the shadows. When matter coalesced, they were there, chewing at its edges. When the First Creators thought "I am me" and named themselves, Those Who Dwelt in Darkness sought their ruin. They whispered lies and stoked egos. They urged the Creators to war amongst themselves. To lay waste to their creations. To let chaos reign. But the First Creators saw the lies for what they were. They saw the danger posed by the Eldest of Evils. But they saw, too, the utility of such deception. And so, the Creators laid a trap. They came together to forge the world itself: matter and energy, land and sea and sky, life and death. They set in motion a system so vast and intricate and wondrous that They Who Whispered could not help but be jealous, could not help but try to destroy it. When the Terrible Ones moved to destroy the world, the First Creators sprung their trap. They folded the world around the Things That Knew Only Hate, sealed them away in rock and stone, bound them in the earth itself. The world— this world, what would become our world—became their tether and their prison. And they, so shackled, became the Things Below.

1372.title: The Time of Cataclysm
1372.breadcrumb: The Things Below › The Time of Cataclysm
1372.body: No prison is perfect. With the slow turn of ages, the bindings weakened. The Things Below whispered once again, and those whispers found ears. The Stone Lords learned firsthand the chaos that those whispers could unleash. They found the cracks in the world-prison and laid great seals upon them. For a time, the whispers were quiet. But the Green Lords remembered those whispers and what they promised, and called upon the Things Below to crush the traitorous Fae . The Green Lords were ultimately defeated, but the real damage had been done: the bindings were weakened. The whispers of the Things Below had returned, had become a song. The Stone Lords' own servants heard that song. Sorcerers arose among them, gathered followings, then cults. In secret, they laid their plans and spread their lies. When the time was right, the sorcerers broke the seals and ushered in the Time of Cataclysm. The Stone Lords' cities sank. Mobs of their servants turned against them. In the north, a dark and terrible howling arose and the Rime Lords sacrificed themselves to stop it. A wave of horrors burrowed up from the Flats , bringing the last of the Tempest Lords low Tempest Lords. The Things Below stoked the jealousies and fears of the Forge Lords , turning them against each other. The time of the Makers was over.

1373.title: The current age
1373.breadcrumb: The Things Below › The current age
1373.body: In the aftermath of the Makers' fall, the would-be sorcerer-kings of the Barrow Builders came to dominate the World's End. For generations, the region—and perhaps the wider world—knew only horror, and suffering, and war. And yet: the power of the Things Below waned. For centuries now, they have lain mostly quiet in the earth. What brought those dark times to an end? That's for you and your table to decide, but perhaps… - … the gods themselves Gods and religion intervened; - … mortal heroes worked a great binding, empowered by great sacrifice; - … the brave and the desperate rose up across the land, toppling sorcerers and banishing horrors one-by-one; - … the world began to heal itself and renew its bindings; - … the Things Below—sated on destruction and woe—simply tired and withdrew; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely. Whatever caused the Things Below to fall back, their retreat was incomplete. Some places remain touched by their influence, some creatures stained to the heart. Objects of power linger, corrupting the unwitting, and some of these—like the sorcerers of old—dance in the dark to a nightmarish tune. Of late, within the past generation or so, tremors have coursed through different quarters of the World's End. Do prisoners in the depths strain once more against their shackles?

1374.title: Hooks
1374.body: - A local unwittingly offers sacrifice to a corrupted spirit, gaining power; the spirit asks for (and offers) more - A conundrum vexes the PCs; consulting a black menhir might help - Crinwin have started to worship at a corrupted site becoming corrupted in turn - An antiquarian is secretly a thrall to a Thing Below, and seeks to unleash a more powerful servant - A spot in the fields around Stonetop goes bad, festering like an infected boil, corrupted by a discarded artifact

1376.title: Everyone knows:
1376.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Lore › Everyone knows:
1376.body: - Evil things are bound deep within the earth - Sometimes, they send forth demons to wreak havoc upon the world - Deep water is the most likely place for such horrors to emerge - Marshedge keeps such horrors at bay by constantly burning bendis root

1377.title: One might know:
1377.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Lore › Everyone knows: › One might know:
1377.body: - About the Time of Cataclysm and what came after - The name, titles, aspects, powers, or minions of a given Thing Below - How their power corrupts places, artifacts, people, beasts, and spirits - How to bind, cleanse, or ward off their taint/power/minions - That unclean things recoil from bendis root smoke, but truly fierce or willful ones can overcome it - That bronze is bane to the Things Below, and that orichalcum is even more effective - The difference between corrupted beings and emanations

1378.title: Very few know:
1378.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Lore › Everyone knows: › Very few know:
1378.body: - Of their true nature - How they came to be imprisoned - Any way to truly destroy one

1379.title: Questions
1379.body: - What frightening tale from childhood featured the Things Below? - What little thing do villagers do to ward off their attention or influence? - When did you first feel their presence? What happened? What still haunts you about that experience to this day? - What place nearby is said to be tainted by their influence? What sign marks it as such? - How do the locals speak of them? In hushed tones? In dire sermons? Somewhere in between? - When was the last time the village dealt with a demon, cult, or sorcerer?

1380.title: Demons
1380.body: In truth, there's no such thing as demons. Or rather, "demon" is not a meaningful term. It's often used to refer to emanations of or beings corrupted by the Things Below. But folks might use the term to apply to any number of supernatural terrors. If it's hostile, magical, and has (or can take) a physical form, someone is likely to call it a demon.

1381.title: Themes
1381.body: When you introduce a Thing—or more likely, when you introduce a site, artifact, or danger associated with one—choose or roll 2 or more prompts from the theme table here. Combine the results and see where they take you. Alternatively, pick one of the established Things Below , and use its themes.

1382.title: 1d12 theme
1382.body: - 1 Darkness/cold/despair: the swallowing of light, warmth, and hope (obsidian, hoarfrost) - 2 Denial/shame/secrets/deceit: the rejection of truth, forgiveness, trust (pearls, eyes, masks) - 3 Delusion/delirium/nightmare: the loss of self, the breaking of minds and wills (quicksilver, mirrors, reflections) - 4 Hunger/need/addiction: lack and desperation, the howling want (wind, fangs, meat) - 5 Cruelty/torture/violence/rage: the urge to hurt, kill, and dominate (red crystal, spilled blood) - 6 Destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance: the tower crumbling; anxiety and loss (fire, ash, dust) - 7 Wounds/injury/pain: the rending of flesh, the breaking of bone (blood, bones, claws, blades) - 8 Corruption/disease/decay: the loss of strength, wholeness, wellness (vermin, poison, acid, filth) - 9 Gluttony/greed/jealousy: the need for more, ever more (mouths, serpents, gems, swarms) - 10 Confinement/suffocation/pressure/abuse: the crushing weight of helplessness (chains, water, earth) - 11 Mutation/transformation/roiling flesh: the malleability of the body (wax, clay, slime, tentacles) - 12 Death/undeath/loss/grief: the end of all things, the refusal to let go (skulls, tombs, pyres)

1383.title: Creating a Thing
1383.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Creating a Thing
1383.body: As it becomes necessary to flesh out a Thing Below, combine its themes (previous page) with 2 or more aspects to inspire your descriptions of it. Choose or roll an instinct, or invent one of your own. If a Thing Below becomes an ongoing source of problems for the PCs (and it probably will), then give it a name and one or more grandiose titles. Write it up as a threat (a magical entity ).

1384.title: 1d12 aspect
1384.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Creating a Thing › 1d12 aspect
1384.body: - 1 Chitin/shell/carapace/armor - 2 An elder/a crone/a stooped old man - 3 Fire/light/haze/cloud/vortex/void - 4 Horns/wings/tail(s)/hooves - 5 The inanimate, animated/an amalgamation of things - 6 Many-limbed/many-headed/many-eyed/many-bodied - 7 Moisture/tentacles/slime/ooze - 8 A predator/a warrior/a warlord/a ruler - 9 Scales/sinew/suppleness - 10 Skulls/bones/corpses/death - 11 Vastness/immensity - 12 A youth/a child/an infant Remember that the Things Below are not bound by constraints of biology, physics, or reality. Make their aspects impossible and weird, things of fever-dream and drug-fueled vision.

1385.title: 1d12 instinct
1385.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Creating a Thing › 1d12 instinct
1385.body: - 1 To stoke conflict, confusion, distrust - 2 To erode hope/faith/honor/self-image - 3 To hide/bury/smother things or ideas - 4 To deprive others of what they need - 5 To inflict harm, cruelly and unnecessarily - 6 To desecrate/mutilate/ruin things of value - 7 To shock/terrify/horrify others - 8 To wantonly devour and consume - 9 To befoul and corrupt beauty/strength/dignity - 10 To fight and hurt and kill and rage and hurt some more - 11 To dominate, punish, and crush - 12 To simply destroy, nothing more and nothing less Tweak the instinct you choose to reflect your growing understanding of this particular Thing Below.

1386.title: Names & titles
1386.body: The true names of the Things Below are nigh impossible for humans to comprehend, much less pronounce. What names we have for them are crude approximations. Still, they work well enough to call upon their power. To name a Thing Below, try one of these: - Pick an unpleasant word or concept, translate it into a language you and your players don't know ("hunger" "Hlad" in Czech) - Take the name of a supernatural entity from pop culture, literature, or another game; change it a little or a lot ("Dagon" "Daagon"). - Combine short, unlikely sounds, often with apostrophes to denote short pauses or inflection ("Hec'tumel") - Some combination of the above Then, give each Thing one or more evocative titles, keeping in mind the themes and aspects already established. Look to the lists below for inspiration, altering word forms to suit (i.e., "suppurate" might becomes Suppurating, Suppuration, or Suppurator). Article/preposition: the, a, an, who, which, that, of, in, on, through, with, without, o Verb: bargain, bleed, break, bring, burn, burrow, bury, butcher, chew, consume, crawl, crush, dance, deceive, deny, desire, despoil, destroy, eat, end, engulf, feed, fester, flense, freeze, haunt, hide, hoard, howl, hunger, keep, know, lack, laugh, lie, melt, offer, promise, rage, rend, roil, rot, scatter, scurry, seep, shape, shatter, sing, slaughter, sleep, slough, smother, snuff, spoil, starve, steal, strain, suppurate, swallow, swarm, topple, unravel, wallow, want, weave, whisper, wither, etc. Adjective: all-consuming, awful, black, blackest, broken, crimson, cruel, dark, dead, desperate, eldest, false, forty, endless, first, forgotten, forlorn, gray, great, hidden, hundred, hungry, implacable, inexorable, lost, mad, many, pale, pallid, red, rusty, secret, terrible, thousand, unkind, unliving, vast, wrathful, yellow Role: child, daughter, father, heir, herald, king, lady, last, lord, master, mistress, mother, prince, princess, thief, queen, son, tyrant, etc.

1387.title: Example names
1387.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Names & titles › Example names
1387.body: Use these as a starting point to develop your own Things. - Ha'z-Ra'iel, Herald of Grief, Thief of a Thousand Hopes - Na'ieraak, the Liar in Shadows, He Who Freezes the Heart - Unhlef'k, the Nightmare that Eats, the Dream with Teeth - Ta'l Themyin, Burning Child, Cruel Infant, Prince of Rage - H'nisat, the Suppuration, the Wound that Festers - Hakog, False Wind and Last Whisper of Deceit - Lalacheewa, Who Sups on Ash and Sorrow

1388.title: For We Are Many
1388.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many
1388.body: The Things Below are legion, varying wildly in power and predilection, activity and ambition. Some are whispered of by peoples far and wide. Others are local terrors, gnawing at a single thread of the world that binds them. Following are half a dozen Things Below that may or may not exist in your game.

1389.title: Daagon, Who Waits in Deep Waters
1389.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › Daagon, Who Waits in Deep Waters
1389.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes gluttony/greed/jealousy, roiling flesh/mutation/transformation Instinct to take from the surface, to never let go - Send forth minions to do its bidding - Offer power, wealth, immortality - Demand a sacrifice - Twist its servants into wet and wriggling things Those foolish enough to brave deep water will tell you: something lurks below. Something patient, something vast, an abyssal mouth opening into a belly that can never be filled. Its countless squirming servants brave the dry and choking land to gather flesh, to gather bone. To steal all wealth and beauty. To take lives full of hope and promise, and feed them to the yawning dark wet jaws. See also: Blackwater Lake , the secrets of Gordin's Delve , and the Ring of Daagon .

1390.title: El'rash-Orra, Lord of Orbs, the Many-Eyes
1390.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › El'rash-Orra, Lord of Orbs, the Many-Eyes
1390.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes denial/shame/secrets/deceit, delusion/delirium/nightmare Instinct to reveal all manner of ugliness - Spy on someone's actions, desires, shames - Give knowledge (wanted or not) via dreams/visions - Twist someone's senses (of reality, of themselves) Should you find yourself alone, and yet convinced that you're being watched, it's best not to turn and look. For if you do, you might see a great and disembodied eye. Or worse: a faceless sovereign upon a silver throne, brandishing a silver orb. And should it stand, and let its robes fall open, it will be as the night sky with countless twinkling eyes that see all shames and doubts, all regrets, all guilts and secret fears that have ever been or those that might yet be. Lo! It will show them all to you! Gladly, it will show you! Whether you wish to see or not. See also: the Staff of the Lidless Orb .

1391.title: Hec'tumel, Pale Serpent! Slitherer In Darkness! Death Is Its Eyes!
1391.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › Hec'tumel, Pale Serpent! Slitherer In Darkness! Death Is Its Eyes!
1391.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes darkness/cold/despair, undeath/death/loss/grief Instinct to smother life, hope, and light - Sense powerful longings/emotions - Offer services, secrets, or power - Twist a bargain to its favor 'Tis a pale thing, a dead thing, a long reptilian creature with skulls where it should have eyes. It slithers through darksome, bone-strewn caves. Caves that drip with tears and lost hopes. It raised up the first sorcerers, gave them the power they craved. It asked only for little things, harmless things, things they'd never miss. And it delighted, yesss it delighted so, when the gifts it gave brought their recipients low. Brought them low, and everything around them, yesss. See also: the Hec'tumel Codex .

1392.title: Hlad, the Devourer, the Eternal Maw
1392.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › Hlad, the Devourer, the Eternal Maw
1392.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes hunger/need/addiction, destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance Instinct to consume reality - Erode strength, wholeness, life - Draw in the weak-willed - Grow in strength and power The Eternal Maw is a hole in reality, a howling vortex of destruction. To hear its song is to know that all things will end, to wish for the respite of oblivion. To look upon it is to see the void, to know the futility of struggle. To touch it is to feel all strength and courage fail, to face a terrible choice. Will you submit to its implacable hunger? Or will you become one of its infinite mouths? See also: the Hungering Maw of Hlad .

1393.title: L'bin'bozia, Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb
1393.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › L'bin'bozia, Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb
1393.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes roiling flesh/mutation/transformation, confinement/suffocation/pressure/abuse Instinct to horrify and overwhelm - Torment with nightmares - Turn someone's flesh against them - Offer them respite, at a terrible price In your dreams, it finds you. It grabs you with a host of hands, thick as tastebuds on a terrible tongue. The hands drag you down through water, through soil and stone, to a tomb of black basalt and splintered bone. They twist your head, unwilling, to look upon your host: a corpse, bubbling and blistering and wreathed in white flame, leering from a chair of squirming limbs, welcoming you to your eternal wretched home.

1394.title: Y'aaw'kara, the Howling Wind, the Flayer of Flesh
1394.breadcrumb: The Things Below › For We Are Many › Y'aaw'kara, the Howling Wind, the Flayer of Flesh
1394.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Themes hunger/need/addiction, cruelty/torture/violence/rage, wounds/injury/pain Instinct to spread pain and misery - Sense or amplify hunger/fear/anger/pain - Offer the strength of cruelty - Inflict the Howling Curse on someone To the people of the far north, Y'aaw'kara's voice is constant as the wind. For he is the angry word. He is the gnawing winter hunger. He is the dream of vengeance upon those who have wronged you, or those who simply have more. Greet him, and you shall see a starving wolf with the body of a starving man, bleeding from a dozen wounds. Listen to him, and you shall hear his wheezing breath, howling like a blizzard gale. Invite him in, and never be prey again. See also: Barrier Pass , the Whitefang Mountains .

1395.title: Corrupted sites
1395.body: These are places where the influence of the Things Below has taken hold, where their power seeps into the world. If the site itself hasn't been established, combine the terrain in which it is located with a feature.

1396.title: 1d12 feature
1396.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Corrupted sites › 1d12 feature
1396.body: - 1 Nothing to mark it but the terrain itself - 2-3 A distinctive (un)natural landmark (big tree, natural arch, spring, etc.) - 4 Signs of great destruction (ash fields, crevasse, lava flow, mudslide, etc.) - 5 A beast's lair/burrow/den - 6 A tomb/barrow/memorial, left by the locals or their ancestors - 7 An old dwelling/gathering place of the locals or their ancestors - 8 A ruin of the Makers , or a lingering sign of their presence - 9-12 Deep water, the depths obscure, conceals the site (roll 1d8) Once the site is established, pick or roll its themes The Things Below to identify the nature of the Thing corrupting the place (or, if you know the Thing is one already established, apply its themes to the site). Pick or roll the cause and severity of the corruption. Populate the place with artifacts and dangers associated with the Things Below, plus discoveries and dangers appropriate to the surrounding area.

1397.title: 1d12 cause
1397.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Corrupted sites › 1d12 cause
1397.body: - 1 Erosion/shifting earth/the implacable passage of time - 2 A failed attempt to wield primordial power - 3-5 An artifact/a sorcerer's bones/remains of a corrupted beast/etc., left to fester\ - 6-8 An offering of flesh/blood/trauma, intentional or not\ - 9-10 A summoning/an invocation/a misguided invitation\ - 11-12 A seal or binding that kept prior corruption in check, now weakened\ (roll d10 again for the original corruption) - \ Decide whether this was intentional, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (1-2: intentional; 3-4 misguided; 5-6 accidental/incidental). If it was intentional or misguided, who did it and why? If accidental, what happened?

1398.title: 1d12 severity
1398.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Corrupted sites › 1d12 severity
1398.body: - 1-3 A shunned place: full of disquiet and unease , not actively dangerous but where it's easy to draw upon their power - 4-6 A hungry place: full of whispers and visions , calling the unwholesome, trapping and consuming the unwary - 7-9 A poisonous place: full of unnatural phenomena , corrupting those who visit - 10-11 A spawning place: where emanations congeal and manifest - 12 A wound in the world: spreading rot, bleeding horror, getting worse The dangers from a lower level of severity can be found in higher levels, too. A hungry place is full of whispers and visions but perhaps disquiet and unease as well. If a corrupted site will be a source of ongoing trouble, write it up as a threat (a MacGuffin ), with an impending doom and grim portents that reflect it getting worse. Look to the list of causes (previous page) for ideas about what could make a corrupted site worse, and what sort of new dangers it might start to manifest.

1399.title: Cleansing corrupted sites
1399.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Corrupted sites › Cleansing corrupted sites
1399.body: The Things Below are loathe to relinquish what power they have in the world, but they can be bested with bravery, cunning, and knowledge. Some PCs (like the Blessed) will have powers or arcana that let them bind or contain the evil in a place. Absent such power, the PCs will need to Make a Plan. Consider such requirements as: - You must learn the name of the Thing Below that taints this place - You must find the seed of corruption within the site - You must create/obtain \ \ , with which to bind the evil (next column) - You must repair the bindings that previously held back the corruption - You'll need the help of a powerful spirit/Fae - It'll take days/weeks of prayer and ritual cleansing - You must wait until high noon/the solstice/the equinox/the full moon - The best you can do is stop it from getting worse - It will require burning sacks of bendis root (Value 2) - You'll risk being corrupted yourselves - You'll risk making things much worse To bind corruption, one might use… - … a tree, especially an elder tree; - … a large piece of stone/crystal/amber/ dark ice ; - … a ring of runes, deeply etched; - … a spike/vessel of bronze, orichalcum , or black iron - … a Fae domain ; - … a barrow/cairn/vault/urn/vessel, sealed and sanctified; - … a perpetual holy flame; - … the bones of a pure soul, properly carved and prepared; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely. The PCs are following the river Sruth to its source. I've decided that they'll pass a corrupted site along the river. I've no particulars in mind, so I roll a 6 for feature (tomb/barrow/memorial of the locals or their ancestors). That makes me think of the Barrow Builders Barrow Builders . For themes, I roll 6 (destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance with "fire, ash") and 8 (corruption/disease/decay with "vermin, poison, acid, filth"). I roll a 2 for the cause (attempt to wield primordial power), and a 3 for severity (a shunned place). I discard my first instinct to make it a sorcerer's tomb and decide instead that it's a cairn on a low hill, overlooking the river. Inside lie the remains of a sorcerer-king's child. The sorcerer tried to call them back from beyond the Last Door, but succeeded only in blighting the hillside with fell power. To this day, the stones are hot to the touch, steaming in rain and snow. Nothing grows there, and a swath of sickly, barren soil runs down the hill to the river, where it becomes bubbling, poisonous muck.

1400.title: Red crystal
1400.body: Pulsing, warm, and thirsting for blood, like cruelty solidified. Red crystal is associated with the most violent and hateful of the Things Below. It grows in places they have corrupted, sometimes jutting from the earth in prodigious quantities. Creatures corrupted by the Things Below might have claws or fangs or antlers made of the stuff. Red crystal has subtle effects, but can be put to many uses. For example: - Those with power over others find it fascinating; the victimized find it terrifying (plenty of people find it both) - It causes tempers to flare, stokes resentment, pushes folks to act on impulse - It absorbs any blood it touches, growing warmer and glowing brighter - Sorcerers can fashion it into potent talismans, or use it to fuel their magic - Demon-hunters sometimes use it to lure their prey into traps

1402.title: Various treasures
1402.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › Various treasures
1402.body: - A crude stone figurine of some beast, the claws and fangs exaggerated and seemingly stained with blood (Value 0) - A ◇ pot full of dust (3 uses, fragile , Value 1 to the right person) that causes a racking cough, weakness, maybe even paralysis - A ◇ lantern (5 hours, reach , area , magical , Value 1) made of twisted black metal; its flame casts pale light that leaches color and chills the air - A chunk of red crystal (previous column), shaped to look like a leering skull (Value 1 to the right person) - A ◇◇ large tome of supple vellum (best not to ask the source) full of ramblings in an archaic dialect, unsettling images, and arcane diagrams (Value 2) - A ◇◇ human(ish) skull but with six eye sockets and jutting lower tusks, carved with disturbing patterns and inlaid with silver (Value 3 to the right buyer) - A twisted elder tree , found with deer licking at its sap and ignoring the approach of threats; the sap can be refined into a highly addictive drug

1403.title: A black menhir
1403.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › A black menhir
1403.body: magical , immobile A number of these megaliths have appeared in the Great Wood of late. They are jagged chunks of basalt, tall as a tall lad, etched with intricate patterns that could be writing, almost. The earth around them is broken and disturbed. They are unmarred by moss or lichen, untouched by shoot or vine. When you study the intricate markings and let your mind wander , you receive a vision or intuition about the current situation. The GM will describe it. But first, roll +WIS: on a 10+ , pick only 1; on a 7-9 , pick 2; on a 6- , alas, all 3 are true. - Part of what you learn is false or misleading (but what?) - You start to suffer nightmares, of horrors shifting in the earth - The menhir asks a question about your shame, guilt, or fears—answer truly

1404.title: A deep, dark shaft
1404.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › A deep, dark shaft
1404.body: A hole in the ground, maybe 5 feet wide, at least 100 feet deep, ending in a watery chamber or underground lake that contains a Mouth of Daagon . When you linger near the shaft , answer one of the following: - Of whom are you most jealous, and why? - What do you wish to possess, more than anything? - When did you last feel trapped and helpless? When you toss something down the shaft , Daagon devours it utterly. When someone falls down the shaft , they are gone, body and soul. When you feed the Mouth of Daagon someone or something beloved (by anyone, not just you), Daagon gives you a gift.

1405.title: 1d6 gift
1405.body: - 1 A physical change (scales, rubbery flesh, gills, a tentacle, etc.) - 2 An item of luxury or status (Value 2) - 3 A precious item (Value 3) - 4 A "priceless" item (Value 4) - 5 A way to best your rivals or defeat your enemies - 6 A way to acquire great power or long life Whatever the gift, it arrives soon: while you sleep, in your dreams, or via vision or coincidence or happy accident.

1406.title: An iron spider
1406.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › An iron spider
1406.body: magical , indestructible About the size of one's palm, half an inch thick, rusted around the edges, with legs folded up on the back. A hidden button makes the legs extend and reveals their bladed tips. Sometimes, when you go to retrieve it, it's not quite where you left it. The spider is hungry when you find it. When you plunge the hungry spider into a person's flesh , they lose 1d4 HP every few seconds until it's torn free (1d8 damage, messy , ignores armor). Should the spider drain them to 0 HP, it becomes sated. When you plunge the sated spider into a person's flesh , they take 1 damage (ignores armor) and will not age for 1d10 years. If they are middle aged or older, they regain some vigor of their youth. Then, the spider becomes hungry. Each time someone uses the spider to extend their life, their features change subtly, becoming blander, waxier, more pallid, less specific. Should they die after exceeding their mortal lifespan, they become a revenant , hungering for blood.

1407.title: An obsidian pendant
1407.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › An obsidian pendant
1407.body: magical A lump of volcanic glass, impossibly black, set in a pendant and hanging from a leather cord. The glass sucks up light from nearby sources, causing them to dim. (The sun and moon are unaffected, as they are far, far away.) More subtly, the pendant also sucks up kindness, making individuals in its presence more callous and self-centered. When you wear or carry the pendant openly for a few minutes or more , your eyes adjust and can see clearly in all but pitch darkness. But also, your instinct for the session becomes "Callousness: to disregard the feelings, needs, or wellbeing of others."

1408.title: A serpentine armband
1408.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › A serpentine armband
1408.body: magical , beautiful , Value 3 Cast from silver that never seems to tarnish, with what appear to be two small rubies in its eye sockets. It seems like it might not fit—but what do you know, it does. When you wear the serpentine armband openly for all to see , NPCs find you magnetic, becoming jealous and possessive of your attention. Tempers flare quickly, and you might need to Defy Danger or Persuade others in order to take your leave. Ask each PC you encounter how the armband affects them. When you remove the armband after wearing it and basking in the regard of others , you find yourself pining for that feeling of being adored. Roll +CON: on a 10+ , you'll get over it; on a 7-9 , either put the armband back on (and keep it there) or mark a debility; on a 6- , you cannot sleep until you put the armband on and wear it openly around others.

1409.title: A silken noose
1409.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › A silken noose
1409.body: magical A length of fine cord, tied into a noose with a few feet hanging free. It's remarkably tough, but a bronze blade will slice right through it. If untied and left unattended, it re-ties itself into a noose. When you slip the noose around someone's neck , it cinches tight—snug and uncomfortable, but the victim can breathe. When your victim lies or refuses to answer a question , the noose tightens and strangles them until they die or gasp out the truth. When you (or anyone else) tries to remove or loosen the noose , it starts to strangle the victim. If you (or they) stop, it relaxes. If you (or they) persist, it quickly crushes the victim's windpipe. When the victim dies , the noose slackens and can be removed.

1410.title: Minor arcana
1410.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › A silken noose › Minor arcana

1411.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1411.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
1411.body: - 1 A strange pendant - 2 A ragged fur cloak - 3 A disturbing mask - 4 A silvery glass bottle - 5 A vellum scroll - 6 A whispering… word?

1412.title: Major arcana
1412.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Artifacts › Major arcana
1412.body: These are almost certainly associated with the Things Below. - The Staff of the Lidless Orb - The Demonhide Cloak - The Whispering Rocks - The Blood-quenched Sword - The Hec'tumel Codex - The Red Scepter - The Ring of Daagon - The Hungering Maw of Hlad

1414.title: Disquiet and unease
1414.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Dangers › Disquiet and unease
1414.body: Places touched by the Things Below are wrong . Most people and natural beasts shun such places, and spirits of the wild either flee or become tainted themselves. As you describe the environment or make any of the GM moves below, show off the themes The Things Below and instinct The Things Below of the Thing Below behind all this. - Introduce something ominous (a cold spot, a dramatic gust of wind, mist/fog, a foul stench, etc.) - Make something seem worse than it is - Imply that allies might betray them - Ask about their fears/doubts/shames/animosities - Have an NPC get edgy/act weird/freak out - Reveal a lack of natural spirits, or only unsavory ones, plus a hateful power that yearns to be called forth

1415.title: Whispers and visions
1415.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Dangers › Whispers and visions
1415.body: Where the influence of the Things Below is strong, people hear voices, see things that aren't there, experience powerful emotions. As you describe the environment or make any of the GM moves below, show off the themes The Things Below and instinct The Things Below of the Thing Below behind all this. - Twist their senses - Lure them deeper/closer - Keep them from sleeping - Fill them with growing dread - Overwhelm them with terror (next page) - Stir an unsavory emotion (anger, jealousy, despair, hate, resentment, etc.) - Whisper a suggestion or compulsion - Show an NPC struggling to keep it together, to keep control - Have an NPC succumb to the whispers/their fear/their emotions When you do as the whispers suggest , mark XP and they can now compel you. When the whispers compel you , mark XP if you do as they bid. If you resist, roll +WIS: on a 10+ , do as you wish; on a 7-9 , choose 1: - Act as compelled, but divert yourself at the last moment - Struggle for control until someone/something snaps you out of it - Hurt yourself (1d4 damage, ignores armor) and immediately regain control

1416.title: On a 6-, pick 1:
1416.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Dangers › On a 6-, pick 1:
1416.body: - Mark a debility, lose 1d4 HP, and do something drastic to regain control - Run with it, coming to later having done gods-know-what

1417.title: Terror
1417.body: When you are overwhelmed with terror , and do anything other than flee, hide, or cower, pick 1: - Work yourself up to the task (ask the GM what happens in the meantime) - Act rashly and recklessly (incurring additional cost or consequence) - Mark a debility of the GM's choice, and act immediately

1418.title: Unnatural phenomena
1418.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Dangers › Unnatural phenomena
1418.body: As the corruption of a site grows stronger, the world starts to twist and unravel. As you describe the environment or make any of the GM moves below, show off the themes The Things Below and instinct The Things Below of the Thing Below behind all this. - Present something impossible and disturbing (bleeding walls, water pooling on the ceiling, darkness that light won't penetrate, etc.) - Have light sources dim/sputter/snuff out - Reveal that food has spoiled/drink has fouled - Inflict a debility (from fear/cold/ennui/hunger/fatigue/nausea/despair/etc.) - Eat away at their gear, have it fail for no good reason - Trap them in a maze of time/space/perception - Threaten/hinder/grab/tear at them with the environment - Corrupt them The Things Below

1419.title: Corruption
1419.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to make monstrous The power of the Things Below can infuse a person, beast, or spirit, corrupting them. This could happen when they… - … call on the Things Below for power, intentionally or not - … spend time in a corrupted site , one that's poisonous or worse - … attempt to cleanse someone or something of corruption, and things go wrong - … are harmed/infected by an artifact or emanation of the Things Below, by one of those who bear their taint (next page), or by a corrupted being . Add these to the list of GM moves for afflictions: - Give them a gift The Things Below, or increase one's potency - Inflict a mark upon them The Things Below, or make one worse

1420.title: PCs and corruption
1420.breadcrumb: The Things Below › PCs and corruption
1420.body: If a PC possesses a major arcana associated with the Things Below , then the arcana's mysteries and consequences reflect the PC's growing corruption. Likewise, the Thrall playbook insert reflects a PC being corrupted by the Things Below. If a PC has one of those arcana or inserts, allow its mechanics to drive their corruption. Don't give them additional gifts or marks unless they are corrupted in some additional way. Should a PC become corrupted by the Things Below without gaining a major arcana or insert, then ask the player how they want to handle it. Do they see the corruption as a threat to their character, something to rid themselves of and struggle against? If so, write it up as a threat (threat type: affliction ) and focus on increasingly problematic marks. But if the player wants to explore the power that's now available to them, and the cost of that power, then create a playbook insert for them, using the Thrall insert as inspiration.

1421.title: Those who bear their taint
1421.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Those who bear their taint
1421.body: - Most deathless ones (page 46) - Servants of Daagon (page 56), emerging from fog or deep water - The Fomoraij (page 136) and their infected servants - Some of the Green Lords' chimerae: llamudwr (page 220), swyn (page 222), and zrajedak (page 223) - False dead, like barrow wights (page 44), the Anan Gllo (page 81) or nemur-vojak (page 476) - Fen-trolls (page 123), suarachan soothsayers (page 125), maybe even fen-walkers (page 117) - Bhoka (page 490) and kyaakara (page 493) from the Whitefangs and beyond - Thraulgwyn (page 247), the pale demons of the Labyrinth (page 242) - The Suileach (page 93) and other horrors of the Dread River (page 86) - Possibly the crinwin (page 58), the Crombil (page 62), hdour (page 234), or the nosgalau (page 272)

1422.title: Corrupted being
1422.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Those who bear their taint › Corrupted being
1422.body: To create a corrupted being, start with the original NPC, threat, or monster stats and consider how or why it was corrupted . If the being is a spirit and it normally would have a sacred site , create it as a corrupted site instead. Identify the themes The Things Below, aspects, and instinct The Things Below of the Thing Below responsible for the corruption. Then pick or roll gifts and marks, up to 3 of each.

1423.title: 1d12 gift
1423.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Those who bear their taint › 1d12 gift
1423.body: - 1 A terrifying presence/aura/gaze - 2 An ability to pass unnoticed (in certain conditions) - 3 Immunity to certain harms (poison, disease, fire, cold, drowning, etc.) - 4 Mind games/false sensations, inflicted on others - 5 Minion(s)/spirit(s)/creature(s), always lurking or called up as needed - 6 Regeneration/rejuvenation/immortality - 7 Serendipity/luck/hexes/misfortune, bestowed on others - 8 Some power to torment/bind/harm at range - 9 Transformation/mutation/growth - 10 Uncanny insight/inexplicable knowledge - 11 Unnatural resilience (Armor 4 except vs. bronze, +4 HP) - 12 Vicious/terrible/mighty physical attacks

1424.title: 1d12 mark
1424.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Those who bear their taint › 1d12 mark
1424.body: - 1 Contagion/pollution/transmission of their corruption - 2 Compulsions (per whispers and visions ) - 3 Intolerance (to something natural/pure/sacred) - 4 Nightmares/visions/hallucinations - 5 Physical mutations (scales, reptilian eyes, claws, warped limbs, etc.) - 6 Some sort of taboo (can't cross a line of salt, can't lie, etc.) - 7 Strange appetites/needs/fascinations - 8 Unhealthy/unnatural appearance - 9 Unwholesome presence (puts children/natural beasts on edge) - 10 Unnatural signs (no shadow, no reflection, aura of cold, etc.) - 11 Vulnerability to bronze (its touch burns, its presence distracts) - 12 Wounds that heal slowly (but they regain HP normally) Consider these gifts and marks in light of the Thing and original being. How has their corruption made them both more and less than what they once were? Revise their stats, tags, qualities, and moves to reflect all of the above. Add the corrupted tag. If the being is a spirit, alter its manifestation to reflect the themes and aspects of the Thing that corrupts it. Bronze weapons can always harm a manifest corrupted spirit, and might ignore its armor. How has corruption changed the being's behavior? Are they merely a conduit for their master's instinct? Do they struggle to maintain their own identity? Has their corruption combined with their original personality in an unusual way? Rewrite their instinct to suit. The Seasons Change roll indicates that a threat makes itself known or gets worse. I decide that Iwan, an aggrieved villager, has unwittingly started to dally with a corrupted spirit. I like the idea of an evil glasbren Spirits of the Wild sensing Iwan's resentments and "courting" him. I start by working up the glasbren's tree as a corrupted site. I get 4 for a feature (signs of great destruction), an 11 for the cause (seal/binding weakened and roll 1d10 again), and a 4 on the reroll (an artifact/remains festering). For severity, I roll a 9 (a poisonous place). I think some evil artifact was bound by an old tree in the glasbren's grove, but a fire swept through and only the glasbren survived. It's deeply scarred and corrupted by the artifact. Looking at the names of Things Below, my eye catches on "Ta'l Themyin (Burning Child, Cruel Infant, Prince of Rage)." I pick themes of "destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance (fire, ash, dust)" and "cruelty/torture/violence/rage (red crystal, spilled blood)." I pick aspects of "fire/light/haze/cloud/vortex/void" and "a youth/a child/an infant." For instinct, I pick "to inflict harm, cruelly and unnecessarily." I think the Red Scepter Red Scepter is the artifact buried in the glasbren's grove! I pick gifts of "immunity to certain harms" (fire), "serendipity/luck/hexes/misfortune, bestowed on others," and "vicious/terrible physical attacks" (a fiery grasp). I think it is marked by "strange appetites" (burnt and bloody offerings). Combining these elements with the glasbren's curiosity and penchant for bonding with mortals, I end up with this stat block:

1425.title: CORRUPTED GLASBREN
1425.body: Solitary, spirit, devious, magical, fiery, beautiful, corrupted HP 19; ARMOR 1 (lacks organs) DAMAGE searing grasp d10 (hand, grabby) SPECIAL QUALITIES tethered to its tree, fireproof INSTINCT to spread cruelty, pain, fire - Travel freely between fires, burnt trees - Sense resentment, anger, wrath, and cruelty - Manifest a beautiful form of cinders and flame (unharmed by stabbing, vulnerable to water and bronze) - Stoke the flames of conflict - Offer affection/confidence/power in exchange for "gifts" of fire and blood

1426.title: Cinderboar
1426.body: Group , gluttonous , hardy , vicious , corrupted HP 14; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage gore d8+2 ( hand , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities fireproof; hot as banked coals; bleeds oily, choking smoke Instinct to destroy, especially the most stable people/things - Snort and giggle - Start a fire, incidentally - Gnaw through and eat anything, eventually - Refuse to die, persist beyond reason There is a burnt and blasted place, where the Steplands abut Ferrier's Fen . It once boasted a Stone Lord fortress: grand, a tower eternal, lit by a fiery gem. But the servants rebelled. The seals were sundered. Ta'l Themyin, the Burning Child, shattered the gem and toppled the tower and laughed and laughed and laughed. His rage still boils to the surface, poisoning any beast bold enough to forage in these ruins. His cruelty sends them forth, to uproot and topple, to sunder and burn.

1427.title: Morgais
1427.body: Solitary , spirit , hoarder , devious , watery , beautiful , corrupted HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs) Damage watery tendril 1d8 ( close , reach , grabby ) Special qualities tethered to a skull or trinket, deep in its pond Instinct to collect drowned souls - Manifest as a youth or beast, shaped from water (unharmed by cutting or stabbing, unless made of bronze) - Entrance with beauty, song, or grace (see below) - Drag them under, revealing their loathsome nature - Laugh merrily as they drown The spirits of even shallow ponds can be callous and uncaring. How much worse, then, are the spirits of those deeper pools that have claimed the lives of mortals? For—willing or not—they have made offering to Daagon, and let it in to their watery hearts. When you listen to the morgais' song or let your gaze linger on its graceful form , your heart fills with longing. If you go to it willingly, mark XP. If you wish to turn away , roll +WIS: on a 10+ , you see the ugliness beneath and can think clearly; on a 7-9 , you shake yourself free but the longing remains—mark miserable or dazed (your choice); on a 6- , you approach before you realize what you're doing.

1428.title: Cultist
1428.body: Group , cautious , magical , corrupted HP 6; Armor 0 (or by worn armor) Damage d8 (tags by weapon) Special qualities marked by the Things Below The Things Below Instinct to belong, to feel important - Use one of their gifts The Things Below - Make a sacrifice for the cause - Have second thoughts, maybe? Folks are capable of all sorts of dark things, if someone else suggests they do them. Especially if they're desperate. Especially if the person leading them is someone they trust, or respect, or admire. And if that first little dark deed comes without any consequences, or any consequences to them? Well, then the next dark deed might not be so little, hmm?

1429.title: Sorcerer
1429.body: Solitary , cunning , magical , cautious , corrupted HP 14; Armor 0 (or by worn armor) Damage iron blade d8 ( hand ) Special qualities marked by the Things Below The Things Below Instinct to aggrandize themselves - Use one of their gifts The Things Below - Amass power through study/sacrifice/rites - Unleash their power - Reveal a plan or preparation Some seek out corruption, embrace it. They might not have chosen the path, but at some point they chose to keep walking, believing the power on offer was worth the cost. Some think they are in control. And perhaps they are—for now. Sorcerers are often in possession of one or more arcana , which they do not hesitate to use. They may or may not be followed by cultists (see above).

1430.title: Thrall
1430.body: Solitary , undead , hardy , magical , corrupted HP 16; Armor 0 (or by worn armor) Damage d10 (tags by weapon) Special qualities marked by their master The Things Below Instinct to explore their condition or to hide their condition or to serve their master's whims - Use one of their gifts The Things Below - Struggle against their master's impulses - Act on their master's impulses - Recover from terrible, even mortal injury When the Last Door stands open before you, what will you do? Some step through bravely, with a nod to the Lady of Crows . Others stagger through, weeping and wailing. Others—consumed by longing, vengeance, or duty—spit on fate, turn their back on the Last Door, and cling to this world for a time. But what if… you simply want to live? What if, when faced with oblivion, you called upon one of the Things Below and offered yourself up to them? What if they said yes?

1431.title: Wretch
1431.body: Horde , hardy , terrifying , corrupted HP 7; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage malformed appendages d6 ( hand , close , grabby ) Special qualities horribly misshapen, bound to their prison, burnt by bronze (+1d6 damage) Instinct to make the pain stop - Moan, whisper, wail - Reveal themselves, terribly - Heal wounds, regrow limbs, get back up The victims of L'bin'bozia (Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb) find themselves turned into… things. Furniture. Decoration. Masses of flesh where it's hard to see where one poor soul ends and the other begins. They do not die of old age. They cannot starve. They must endure, trapped in the cave or the mire or the tomb, or wherever they met their fate. They must persist, in pain, begging for some outsider to come and end their suffering.

1432.title: Emanation
1432.body: The Things Below are well and truly trapped—bound within the earth, to the earth. Even during the Time of Cataclysm, they did not walk free. They are tethered as long as the world remains. But there are cracks in the world. Places where their influence bubbles up. Deep water is the surest of these, which is why it is often filled with horrors. But any place can bear their taint. The power of the Things Below usually just corrupts what's already there. But sometimes, it coalesces. It creates something new, something terrible. A spirit or being with a will and purpose of its own. Scholars call these "emanations." They are not truly part of a Thing Below. They are... discharge. Leavings. Spawn. To create an emanation, identify the Thing Below it spawned from, including its themes The Things Below, aspects, and instinct The Things Below. Identify, too, the corrupted site it inhabits. Pick or roll for its origin.

1433.title: 1d12 origin
