1433.body: - 1-2 Arose in the aftermath of great devastation/atrocity - 3-4 Burst forth from a corrupted being that absorbed too much power - 5-6 Called forth, via a terrible ritual - 7-8 Given shape by worship/adoration/feeding - 9-10 Let into the world by accident/hubris, a failed attempt to bind/cleanse/wield the power of the Things Below - 11-12 Spawned spontaneously from a truly corrupted site Imagine the emanation that forms as a result. It will share at least some of the themes and aspects of the Thing from which it spawned, and might share its instinct. An emanation has its own memory, arguably its own personality. It might communicate with its progenitor, or do its bidding, but an emanation is its own thing.

1434.title: Hand of Daagon
1434.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Hand of Daagon
1434.body: Solitary , large , emanation , terrifying , cautious , curious HP 23; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage many hands d12+1 ( hand , close , grabby , ignores armor) Special qualities blind; tremorsense; reaches through matter like it was water Instinct to feed the Mouth - Sink into the stone, or emerge from it - Provoke abject revulsion - Reach through armor, clothing, flesh, bone to take what is most valued - Retreat with its prize, back to the water In a deep deep cave, where light has never shined, there is a lake. In that lake, there is a Mouth of Daagon: ever-hungry, ever-greedy, consuming all that it is offered. And in the caverns around that lake? There you will find the Hand: ever-groping, ever-grasping, dragging all it finds of value back to the Mouth.

1435.title: Voice of the Eternal Maw
1435.breadcrumb: The Things Below › Voice of the Eternal Maw
1435.body: Solitary , spirit , emanation , devious , terrifying , hungry HP 19; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage scream d10 ( near , area , reload , ignores armor) or unnaturally large and toothy maw d10+2 ( hand , messy , 3 piercing) Special qualities tethered to the vortex, unable to leave the mountain Instinct to create more mouths, more wraiths - Compel someone with whispers (see The Things Below), even from afar - Possess a traveler, especially one who is hungry, weary, or tired - Drive its host to feast - Leave its host corrupted, withering, doomed to become a wraith Hidden in the southern arm of the Huffel Peaks, there is a gorge that splits a mountain. The gorge is cloaked in constantly churning mist, cold and clammy. At the base of the gorge is a thin split in reality, a vortex, a manifestation of Hlad the Devourer, sucking in everything it touches. The mountain and the gorge are shunned now. The Hillfolk and the Ustrina who live nearby know better than to approach. But the Barrow Builders fed the vortex. They brought it gifts, and offerings, and living, screaming sacrifice. In time, these offerings gave the vortex a Voice. And the Voice roams the mountain still.

1436.title: Three Coven Lake
1436.body: Deep in the Steplands lies a large lake, eight miles long by three. The waters are murky, often choppy, shallow in some places, in others 100 feet deep. Rocky islands poke from the water. Bluffs line much of the shore. But look closer. Those islands are the feet of ancient towers, and those bluffs are riddled with rock-cut rooms. For long ago, in the days of the Stone Lords , this was a mighty city. Teirstered, they called it. The City of Three Stars, rivalled only by great Dulenn . Picture it! See it in your mind! The stone and crystal spires, rising from the lake. Their glowing tips of makerglass , shining in the night. The walkways, stretched between the towers, so that one might walk the valley's length without ever setting foot on shore. Those walkways were more than just convenience. They formed a mighty rune, a seal that helped to bind the power of the Things Below . The ceaseless march of countless feet powered the rune, like how water turns a wheel. When the Stone Lords' servants rebelled, they sank the city of Dulenn in one night. But here, in Teirstered, things took longer. Mobs rose up against their masters and besieged them in their towers. They broke the walkways, called up horrors from the deep. Towers swayed, then crumbled, then fell beneath the waves. Soon, the city lay in ruins. But it was not abandoned. The Barrow Builders fought over its bones. They sought the treasures of their fallen masters, and to seat themselves upon their thrones. Their armies spilled each others' blood. The waters drank it up. In time, there was little left to fight over. In time, the Things Below withdrew. The tyrants of the Barrow Builders were cast down, or imprisoned, or left alone to rot. Teirstered, the City of Three Stars, was no more. But the blood-drunk waters of Three Coven Lake remained. Three Coven Lake

1437.title: Impressions
1437.body: - So… much… water… (gulp) - Calm water: reflections of the bluffs - Choppy water: cold and dark, the angry churn and spray - Broken buildings, glimpsed beneath the water - Echoes off the bluffs: waves, voices, birdcalls, mournful screeches - At night: motes of light in the deep - When chilly: fog—thick, wet, gloomy - In winter: a white expanse, frozen solid, windy, slick, snow-covered

1438.title: The Three Covens
1438.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › The Three Covens
1438.body: Three lights can be seen every night, like ghostly flames: one among the northern bluffs, one on the south shore, the third upon (below?) the waters. Hillfolk call them "the Covens" or the "the Witch Lights." They hesitate to say more. Who or what are the Covens? That's for you to decide. But perhaps they… - … burn in memory of the three conspiracies, or covins, that rose up against the Stone Lords, and the witches who led each one; - … were once the "Three Stars" of Tierstered, makerglass gems burning with eternal flame; - … are banefires , each one imprisoning an undead sorcerer; - … are spirit-catchers, enthralling ghosts and keeping them bound; - … are ghostly hordes standing vigil over mass graves; or - … are some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

1439.title: Hooks
1439.body: - A foe of the PCs makes their lair near the lake - An ally went to explore the lake but never returned - Hillfolk are gathering near the lake, in large numbers - The PCs acquire the key to the Vault of Skol Paasco (see next page) - Someone aims to destroy the dam (draining the lake, flooding the Sruth, dooming Marshedge)

1440.title: Getting there
1440.body: To get there directly from Stonetop… - First you must travel to the Steplands, which will take at least 4 days. - From there, it will take at least 3 days and a corresponding amount of supplies. - You need a knowledgeable guide; but - Even then, you risk getting lost. To get there from Marshedge , follow the River Sruth upstream to the lake, and… - It will take at least 4 days, and a corresponding amount of supplies; and - You need a knowledgeable guide or you risk getting lost. Either way… - The terrain itself is treacherous, you risk injury on the way; - The way is grueling, you risk exhausting yourselves/your resources; or - The way is perilous, plagued with danger.

1441.title: Areas
1441.body: - The Gwazhi: A sizeable river that feeds the lake, flowing from the Huffel Peaks . - Surrounding hills and valleys: Once full of farms, mines, villages, manors. Site of many Barrow Builder battles. - Western shores: Where the Gwazhi enters the lake. Marshy, silty, gross. Frogs, birds, bugs. - Northern shore: Piles of boulders and a great looming bluff. Treacherous shorelines. - Southern shore: Gentler (but still rugged) shorelines, with smaller bluffs a ways back from the water. - Eastern shore: Deep water. Sheer bluffs joined by a Stone Lord dam. - The Lake itself: Shallow near shores but plenty of deep, open water. Hides many dangers and many treasures. Freezes over in winter.

1442.title: Sites
1442.body: - The dam: A mighty wall between two bluffs on the eastern shore. Granite blocks, mortared in place with makerglass. A huge pipe drains water into the river Sruth. Beware the whirlpool . - Glassworks: The westernmost bluff on the south shore, hollowed out. Partly collapsed, wholly looted, still full of cold, echoing furnaces and huge globs of makerglass. - The Academy: Part of the north bluff. Maker-sized dormitories, workshops, and lecture halls. Gutted and vandalized. Used by Barrow Builders as a fortress. - Amphitheater: Carved into a bluff, overlooking a large cove on the southern bank. Impossibly good acoustics. The site of countless bloody sacrifices. - The Vault of Skol Paasco: A large, rocky isle in the middle of the lake, a tower's foundation clearly visible. A heavily-warded makerglass door guards the untouched cellars.

1443.title: Terrain
1443.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

1444.title: 1d12 bluffs terrain
1444.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Terrain › 1d12 bluffs terrain
1444.body: - 1-2 Cliff/ledge/cleft/crevice - 3-4 Scree/rubble/boulders - 5-6 Overhang/plateau/outcrop - 7-8 Tree(s)/shrub(s) - 9 Waterfall/pool/travertine - 10 Barrow - 11 Walkway/stairs/balcony, carved into the stone - 12 stone lord site Three Coven Lake

1445.title: 1d12 shoreline terrain
1445.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Terrain › 1d12 shoreline terrain
1445.body: - 1-2 Marsh/mire/mudflat - 3-4 Beach/shingle/shallows - 5-6 Drop-off/strong current/undertow - 7-8 Jumble of boulders/rubble - 9 Barrow - 10 stone lord site Three Coven Lake - 11 Island/sandbar and roll d10 again - 12 Cove/bay/inlet and roll d10 again

1446.title: 1d12 stone lord site
1446.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Terrain › 1d12 stone lord site
1446.body: - 1 Sunken head of an enormous statue, coated in algae. Sings a haunting song each sunset. - 2 Vaulted chamber, littered with pieces of an obsidian mirror once 30' across. Shards reflect brief vignettes of days long past. - 3 Four-story "tower" carved out of a bluff. Crystals inside emanate light, awe, reverence. - 4 Bluff-sized building of seamless stone. Inside, a warren of unstable, bone-strewn apartments. - 5 Double doors, crudely barricaded. Beyond: a cracked crystal, a broken ward, lingering evil. - 6 Big island made mostly of huge, curving stone slabs. Alcoves hold signs of ancient refugee camps: cookware, salvage, keepsakes. - 7 Spiral stairs, rising 100' from a pile of rubble. Makerglass, giant-sized steps, draped in vines. - 8 Big blocky building. Intact. Doors buried under rubble/scree. Holds a cache of spare construct parts. - 9 Warren of rooms carved into a bluff. Once a Stone Lord's manor, then a warlord's fortress, finally the site of her demise. - 10 Long stretches of narrow stone walkway, mostly buried by silt and shallow water. Covered in algae, mud, faded runes. - 11 Rocky island far from shore, like a great hand holding a broken stone platter. Ancient (?) skulls are heaped on every surface. - 12 Makerglass tower, largely intact but fallen sideways into the lake. Full of wonders, algae, eels, stale air pockets, Stone Lord ghosts. The above are just examples. See Stone Lords if you need to create more or different sites. Most sites were reclaimed by the Barrow Builders at some point. Most sites around Three Coven Lake were long ago stripped of anything valuable. If a site contains some sort of treasure, there must be a reason. It could be… - … inaccessible (buried, locked, flooded, well-hidden, etc.) ; - … corrupted by the power of the Things Below; - … dangerously haunted ; - … protected by constructs , perilous stone , bound spirits, and/or other magic; - … currently inhabited; or - … some combination of the above.

1447.title: Discoveries
1447.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1448.title: 1d6 discovery
1448.body: - 1 A change of terrain Three Coven Lake and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 An opportunity (next page) - 4 An artifact of… (next page) - 5 An encounter (next page) - 6 Something related to the portal (next page) 1d6 Opportunity --- --- 1 A shrine to the Blood-drunk Queen , or some other sacred site 2 A painful psychic imprint, revealing something useful about these ruins 3 A route/path to an intriguing site 4 A boat/raft (roll d6 for condition) 5 A good place to rest/restock/lay an ambush 6 A long-buried ruin, recently exposed by a rock/mudslide 1d6 Artifact of… --- --- 1 … the Things Below 2 … death and the undying 3 … the Stone Lords 4 … some other group of Makers 5-6 … the Barrow Builders 1d6 Encounter --- --- 1 People of some sort 2 Witches or their ilk 3 The restless dead 4-5 Local wildlife 6 Other entities Pick or roll for their disposition. Let the result suggest the specifics of the encounter. 1d6 Disposition --- --- 1 Scheming/deceitful/in disguise 2 Afraid/injured/in trouble 3 Wary/furtive/sneaking about 4 Annoyed/struggling/working 5 Unaware/indifferent/at rest 6 Friendly/curious/playful 1d6 Related to the portal --- --- 1 A reference to it (a sign, an icon, a written description, a rune, etc.) 2 A hint to its location (a map, directions, a detailed mural, etc.) 3 A means to charge it (like a power-crystal ) 4 Documentation: a time-worn missive , the sigils of other portals, a treatise on the axiom of local contagion, etc. 5 A token that unlocks the door and bypasses the guardians 6 The portal itself (see below)

1449.title: The portal of Teirstered
1449.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › The portal of Teirstered
1449.body: Hidden away in some vault—guarded by constructs, traps, and spells of misdirection—there stands a slab of white stone, 40' by 40', inscribed with a circle of deep-cut runes. The circle is a portal , connected to those in the Ruined Tower , the Labyrinth , Vor Svetelik , and so forth. It requires a power source. If one is provided, it can be used via Opening the Way .

1451.title: Natural hazards
1451.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Natural hazards
1451.body: - A stretch of water and reason to brave it - A bluff to climb and a reason to climb it - Poor footing (mud, unstable rubble, scree, slick wet stone, etc.) - Rockslides or mudslides - A gap or barrier in a path/walkway - Jagged rocks to fall on - Swarms of midges or mosquitos—annoying, distracting, painful, diseased

1452.title: Unnatural hazards
1452.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Unnatural hazards
1452.body: - Unstable construction from the days of the Stone Lords - Perilous stone - Traps left by the Barrow Builders - Corrupted sites and all that they entail - Cursed objects - Darkness and the risk of getting lost, deep inside the bluffs

1453.title: The lake itself
1453.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › The lake itself
1453.body: - Instinctive dread, from a generational fear of water - Steep, unexpected drop-offs - Poor footing (slick rocks, mud, plants and other snares) - Strong, often unseen currents - Choppy water, waves up to 4' on extremely windy days - Shivering cold, the threat of debilities or even hypothermia - Getting dragged down by your own gear - The fact that they probably can't swim See also: The Blood-drunk Queen . Drowning When you plunge beneath the water , hold Breath equal to 1 + your CON. If you first took a deep breath, hold 2 extra Breath. When you make a move while under water , and that move involves a roll, choose 1 of the following after the move is resolved: - Spend 1 Breath - Mark a debility - Drop to 0 HP; you're drowning and soon to be at Death's Door

1454.title: Thin ice
1454.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Thin ice
1454.body: The lake starts to freeze in early winter and thaws in early spring. It's usually frozen at least 4" thick (safe enough to walk on) by midwinter. But it doesn't freeze uniformly; it can be 2' thick in one place and just a few inches thick only a few yards away. - Footsteps fall with a hollow noise - Water/slush well up around your feet - Something underfoot cracks, lurches - SPLOOSH Anyone falling through the ice risks panic and drowning. If they manage to escape, they're probably already suffering hypothermia .

1455.title: The whirlpool
1455.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › The whirlpool
1455.body: Near the dam at the east edge of the lake, a great drain draws in water and empties it into the river Sruth. When one or more PCs get too close, start the following: - ☐ They're caught in the current - ☐ They're dragged closer, and can no longer swim/paddle free - ☐ Down the funnel they go - ☐ Impending doom: They are sucked into the pipe When you get sucked into the pipe , take 1d10+2 damage with advantage (ignores armor) and roll +CON: on a 10+ , you get spat out into the river, alive but soaked and beaten (mark a debility); on a 7-9 , you get spat out but mark a debility and lose 1d4 large items from your inventory and 1d4 small items; on a 6- , as a 7-9 but also you are at Death's Door.

1456.title: People
1456.body: - Hillfolk : exiled, on patrol, on the hunt, on a rite of passage, etc. - Antiquarians , adventurers , or legionaries , exploring the ruins - Bandits , seeking shelter or after some big score

1457.title: Witches or their ilk
1457.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Witches or their ilk
1457.body: - A necromancer , treating with the many many dead - A hdour or sorcerer , seeking/hoarding power - Cultists and/or a thrall , living among the ruins - A gouzadn , quite possibly corrupted - A deathless one , who's been here all along

1458.title: The restless dead
1458.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › The restless dead
1458.body: - A multitude of ghosts , haunting battlefields, mass graves, and sites of more intimate death - Barrow wights guarding some "royal" tomb - Phantom riders , still serving their long-dead warlord - A waterlogged pyped , diving for bones of the drowned - A small army of Anan Gllo , in stasis, awaiting fresh blood - Shades bound by old Stone Lord magic

1459.title: Local wildlife
1459.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Local wildlife
1459.body: - Bat swarms , cave bears , grochslons , or kleztigrs , lairing in bluffs or ruins - Cougars , wolves , or spitting drakes on the hunt or lurking in wait - Aurochs , boars , horned drakes , or a brawzbedr , foraging or watering at the lake - Any of the above, corrupted by the Things Below

1460.title: Other entities
1460.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Dangers › Other entities
1460.body: - Stone Lord constructs in various states of repair - Spirits of the wild , quite possibly corrupt - Watery terrors: luglfsk , jellied horrors , llamudwr , or servants of Daagon

1461.title: The Chained Beast
1461.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › The Chained Beast
1461.body: Solitary , large , corrupted , aquatic , terrifying HP 20; Armor 2 (fur and scales) Damage toothy maw d10+3 w/advantage ( close , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) or raking claws d10+1 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities chained to a heavy block of makerglass, takes +1d6 damage from bronze, soothed by a well-sung song Instinct to gorge itself - Scent blood, anywhere in the lake - Shoot through the water - Snatch prey, pull it under - Regenerate any wound not dealt by bronze - Drag its mooring across the lakebed, slowly and painfully It was a gift from the Things Below, a present to some Barrow Builder tyrant. They bound it fast and kept in the waters nearby, feeding it a steady of diet of screaming, bleeding victims. It's still out there, sleeping for years or decades at a time, roused by copious amounts of fresh blood in the lake—or by a few drops somewhere nearby.

1462.title: Screech eel
1462.body: Horde , small , aquatic , stealthy , cautious HP 3; Armor 1 (speed), 2 vs. attacks from above the water Damage tearing bite d6 ( hand , messy , 1 piercing) Instinct to feed on easy prey - Slink up on potential prey - Screech to attract other eels (barely heard above the water) - Attack in a frenzied swarm, all bites and screeches and churning bloody water About 5' or 6' long. They mostly prey on fish or fowl, hunting silently and by themselves. Somehow, though, they learned that people are delicious. Too big for one eel to tackle, sure. But people in the water are clumsy and oblivious. So when a screech eel spots someone in the water, it calls out to its friends. And when enough of them have gathered, they feast. Three Coven Lake

1463.title: The Blood-drunk Queen
1463.breadcrumb: Three Coven Lake › Screech eel › The Blood-drunk Queen
1463.body: Threat ( magical entity ) Instinct to slake her thirst for violence and suffering - Appear in dreams, or to those who are drowning: a gaunt and long-limbed queen, her mouth stained crimson - Wash up a secret/treasure, to tempt fools or stir up violence - Turn the lake against those who have not appeased her The lake has always had a spirit. A fickle goddess, beautiful and bountiful, sometimes harsh and greedy. But then the walkway-seal was broken, and horror seeped into the waters. Then the multitudes were drowned, and the goddess drank their deaths. Then came generations of violence, and she lapped up all the blood that was spilled. When you make a bloody offering to the spirit of the lake , roll and add… - … +1 if you do it at a proper shrine; - … +1 if the victim dies in agony & fear; and - … +1 if you spill the lifeblood of a human-sized creature, or +2 if you spill substantially more than that. On a 7+ , you have appeased the spirit of the lake and She will not actively torment you or your companions (for this visit, at least). Also, on a 10+ , hold 2 Preparation (per the Bolster move), which you or a companion can spend on any roll related to exploring or navigating the lake, or facing its dangers.

1464.title: Titan Bones
1464.body: About two days' hike from Stonetop stands a lone hill, rising from the Flats (page 126\). A huge, fossilized skeleton is visible on one side of the hill. The Highway runs past the hill to the east; a wayside sits at its foot. The hill rises over 300' above the prairie. The northern, leeward face is steep, rocky, cliff-like. The other sides are rugged but manageable slopes, covered in sparse, gnarled oaks and smaller brush. The crown is flat, sparsely wooded, the highest point for nigh on 100 miles. When Stonetop or the Hillfolk need to signal each other, they light a great bonfire up there, on the top of Titan Bones.

1465.title: Impressions
1465.body: - The hill on the horizon, never seeming to get closer until it's right up on you - Those bones, bigger than they ought to be, bigger than any beast you've seen - A sense of smallness, insignificance - Southerly wind: ceaseless, buffeting - Riders approaching, the anticipation as you wait Tip: Up top, portray NPCs with short, loud sentences, like they're shouting over the wind. Ask, "What?" if players don't do the same. Down at the wayside, keep looking up and away, like NPCs are distracted by the hill.

1466.title: Questions
1466.body: - Which of you has been (t)here before? Why? - Do the bones peek out of the sheer rock face, or jut from the southern slopes, or lie flat on the crown, or what? - According to the tales, what are they bones of? How did they come to be here? - What superstitions surround the bones? What little rites do folks observe in their presence? - What other oddities have you found on or around the hill?

1467.title: Meeting place
1467.body: A footpath leads from the wayside, climbing the rugged slope in a series of switchbacks. It takes half an hour to reach the crown at a reasonable pace, but a fit person could charge straight up in just a few minutes (at considerable risk of injury or mishap). When the PCs wish to summon the Hillfolk to a meeting, they must: - Travel to Titan Bones and climb the hill - Wait for a clear day, with no rain or haze - Spend the best part of a day gathering fuel and stoking a bonfire - Tend and feed the bonfire all through the night and into the early morn - Wait for the Hillfolk to arrive Choose or roll for how long before the Hillfolk arrive, what happens while they wait, and the disposition of the Hillfolk who show up. It's usually a small group of riders who respond to the signal, maybe with a spirit-talker . 1d6 how long ----- --------------------------- 1 6 or 7 days, maybe longer 2-3 4 to 5 days 4-5 2 or 3 days 6 A day or less, somehow 1d6 while they wait ----- --- 1-2 A danger (next page) manifests! 3 Travelers approach on the road 4 Folks get antsy/bored/irritable 5 Someone makes a discovery: something odd, out of place, recently unearthed, etc. 6 Time passes pleasantly (how so?) 1d6 disposition ----- --- 1 Angry about something, perhaps openly hostile, perhaps trying to hide it 2 Blustering, proud, keen to show off and make a point 3 Out of sorts, possibly in trouble, maybe looking for help 4-5 Reserved, aloof, wondering what this is about, if it's a waste of time 6 Curious, eager to trade and share news Should the Hillfolk light a signal fire themselves, folks in Stonetop will likely see the pillar of smoke first, then the fire as the sun sets. If it burns through the night and into the morning, that means the Hillfolk wish to meet. Meetings take place on the wayside at the foot of the hill, under the protection of the pavestones' wards against violence . Hillfolk will assume that anything else is a trap. - How often do meetings like this occur? - When was the last meeting called? By whom? What was it about? - With which group of Hillfolk do y'all normally meet? - How do you and the Hillfolk traditionally greet each other? What other customs do you observe? - Where exactly do folks typically light a signal fire? What's special about it? - What else can be found up on the hill's crown?

1468.title: The titan's bones
1468.body: The fossilized bones on the hill are clearly ancient, and far bigger than anything has a right to be. But what are they the bones of? That's for you and your group to decide, but perhaps they are the bones of… - … one of the three great beasts vanquished by Tor ; - … one of the First Creators , slain by the Things Below as they were imprisoned; - … some fell beast brewed up by the Green Lords ; - … a mighty Stone Lord construct , not a creature at all; - … a colossal emanation of the Things Below, a world-ending horror, vitrified by Stormcatcher 's Stormcatcher’s tower magic; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

1469.title: Ruins of the past
1469.breadcrumb: Titan Bones › Ruins of the past
1469.body: Today, Titan Bones is little more than a landmark and a meeting place, but what was it in the past? That's for you to decide, but perhaps it was once… - … a site sacred to the Makers , a place of pilgrimage; - … a fortress or trading post of the Stone Lords ; - … one of the Tempest Lords' sky-islands, crashed or landed and (temporarily?) unable to fly; - … part of Stormcatcher 's Stormcatcher’s tower domain: a lookout, a laboratory, a power station, etc.; - … a fortress from the time of the Barrow Builders , likely haunted from the battles fought here; - … a corrupted site , a place of great evil, now mostly cleansed or at least contained; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely. Whatever you decide, consider how that interacts with your decisions about the titan's bones (see previous column). For example, if they are the bones of a great beast and the hill was once a sky-island, did the beast and sky-island bring each other low? Did the beast (or its bones) power the sky-island? Or what?

1470.title: Dangers
1470.body: - A nasty turn of the weather - The nosgalau , calling out to those on the hill - The ghosts of those who died here or at nearby battlefields - A prairie fire lit by an errant cinder - Bandits , encamped on the hill or drawn by the signal fire - Something big and dangerous, on the hunt or lairing on the hill, like a beznpol , a frythanc , or a kleztigr - A nest of hlomren , infesting the hillside - Spirits of the wild: a brennauwed , a flock of andalau , or a fadyn - Local blue magpies , some real jerks - A rockslide or mudslide - Something inspired or suggested by the true nature of the titan's bones, and/or the ruins of the past

1471.title: Tor, the Rain-maker
1471.body: Tor, the Rain-maker, also called the Thunderhead and Slayer-of-Beasts, is one of the gods in Stonetop's pavilion of the gods. He is the god of wind and rain, weather and storms, patron of warriors and hunters too. Many tales are told of his victories, and yes, of his foibles, too.

1473.title: 1d12 theme
1473.body: - 1-2 Rain/snow/good clean water - 3-4 Wind/weather/the sky/clouds - 5-6 Storms/thunder/lightning - 7 Courage/strength/prowess/endurance - 8 Generosity/gift-giving/celebration - 9 Exuberance/confidence/folly - 10 Surprises/moodiness/whims - 11 Wrath/rage/destruction - 12 Prophecy/doom foretold

1474.title: Questions
1474.body: According to the tales: - How did Tor come to be (a god)? What's his origin story? - Which god is his rival/lover/ally/kin? - What three great beasts is Tor said to have slain? - What natural feature did he shape in a fit of rage? - From what predicament did a mortal once rescue him? - What gift did he give to Stonetop/humanity? - How did he lose one of his fingers? - What is one of the names he goes by when he walks the world as a man? Use the answers to help decide what Tor's true nature is Gods and Religion.

1475.title: Hooks
1475.body: - The PCs encounter a nine-fingered stranger . - During a violent storm, multiple villagers dream of a terrible beast, unleashed. - One of Tor's rites is disrupted; drought afflicts the village. - A wandering priest of Tor arrives, offering to cast runes and tell fortunes. - Travelers praise some new/foreign god of strength/storms/war, and heap insult and derision upon Tor.

1476.title: In Stonetop
1476.body: Tor is the most popular god in Stonetop. The Stone is sometimes called his anvil, or the quiver for his javelins. The Cistern is sometimes called his drinking horn. The locals use many colorful phrases that invoke his name. What is Tor's shrine like? Perhaps it is… - … laden with whisky and gifts, trophies and charms; - … just a simple idol, for his true altar is the Stone; - … home to a smoldering fire, constantly tended; - … shrouded, held in awe, approached by only the worthy; - … some variation on the above; or - … something else entirely.

1477.title: Ask the PCs:
1477.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › In Stonetop › Ask the PCs:
1477.body: - Who tends Tor's shrine? Is it a formal position (like a priest), or what? - How is Tor depicted? - What symbols adorn his shrine? - What do locals do to ask his blessings? - What do they do to avert his anger? - What are Tor's high holidays? How is each one observed?

1478.title: ...and elsewhere
1478.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › In Stonetop › ...and elsewhere
1478.body: How widespread is the worship of Tor? That's for you and your group to decide, but perhaps Tor… - … is a local deity, Stonetop's patron but largely unknown elsewhere; - … is worshipped throughout the World's End, but dismissed as a barbaric god by those from Lygos and the South ; - … is widely known and worshipped, and served by a wandering order of priests; - … is known the world over, but often with very different aspects; - … some variation on one of the above; or - … something else entirely. In any other community that knows and worships Tor, consider/ask: - Which of his themes (previous page) do they emphasize most? - Is he gladly worshipped? Given due respect? Feared and appeased? Or what? - Do they maintain a public shrine/temple? If so, where is it and what's it like? - Who conducts rites in his honor? - What unfamiliar tales do they tell/rites do they perform/symbols do they use? If the worship of Tor is unknown to a people: what other god or gods of the sky, storms, weather, and/or violence do they worship? (See Gods and Religion for guidance.)

1480.title: Various treasures
1480.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Artifacts › Various treasures
1480.body: - A ◇ fulgurite, sand or soil vitrified by lightning, sacred to Tor ( fragile , Value 0) - The claw/fang/tusk/horn of a great beast, carved with prayers to Tor (Value 0) - A pouch of runes, the kind used for divination (Value 0) - An ingot of pure aetherium , left as an offering (Value 1) - ◇ Aetherium-tipped javelins (thrown, magical , forceful , loud , dangerous , +2 damage, all out, Value 2) - A ◇◇ large, clear bottle; the pale liquid sealed inside rises when rain, snow, or storms approach ( fragile , Value 2) - A pair of mammoth tusks, carved in Whitefang Mountain style The Whitefang Mountains, depicting a storm god bearing gifts and slaying beasts ( immobile , Value 3)

1481.title: Minor arcana
1481.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Artifacts › Minor arcana

1482.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
1482.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Artifacts › 1d6 minor arcanum
1482.body: - 1 A half-buried plaque - 2 A path in the woods - 3 A cloak, richly embroidered - 4 A cracked flute - 5 A corroded spearhead - 6 A fine drinking horn

1483.title: Major arcana
1483.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Artifacts › Major arcana
1483.body: The following might be associated with Tor, or at least revered by his followers: - The Azure Hand - Storm markings

1485.title: Associated entities
1485.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Dangers › Associated entities
1485.body: - Elemental spirits , especially those of rain, snow, storm, and wind - Andalau , who love to dance before a storm - Frythanc and thunder drakes , who rank among Tor's most honored beasts - An elemental vortex , especially of lightning or wind - The Pale Hunter , perhaps, who might be Tor's child/rival/lover/etc. or someone whom he cursed

1486.title: Harsh weather
1486.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Dangers › Harsh weather
1486.body: - Lashing wind and rain - Thunder and lightning (see below) - Poor visibility - Slippery/sodden ground - Flash flooding - Hail storms—annoying, pelting (d4 damage), smashing (d6 damage) - Mudslides - Cold wind and rain, sleet, snow, hypothermia & frostbite

1487.title: Lightning
1487.body: When you get struck by lightning , it's not necessarily a sign of Tor's displeasure. But it probably is. - Flash in the distance; rumble ominously - Paint the sky with bright-white bolts - BOOM! Deafen and disorient them - A bolt starts a wildfire - Hair stands on end, skin and spine tingle - A bolt strikes nearby (d10+2 damage, reach , area , forceful , loud , ignores armor) - A bolt strikes directly (Death's Door)

1488.title: Drought
1488.body: Threat ( affliction ) Instinct to deplete resources Rain and snow are Tor's life-giving gifts. But what if he withholds them? Or worse, is unable to give them freely? - ☐ The Stream dries up, the Cistern runs low - ☐ The crops are stunted (generate Surplus with disadvantage) - ☐ Everything/one goes unwashed; disease spreads Ferrier's Fen - ☐ Impending doom: Crops and gardens fail, livestock die/are slaughtered (steading marks lacking and generates no Surplus) Something interesting: Past droughts have been broken by making the proper offerings/completing some quest, but the details are unclear—perhaps you could commune with a spirit of the wild to learn more? Something useful: You know what offering needs to be made, or what quest needs to be completed—Make a Plan with the GM to determine the details.

1489.title: Nine-fingered stranger
1489.body: Solitary , spirit , magical , enigmatic , moody , curious HP 22; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. black iron Damage by weapon d10+2 (tags by weapon, + forceful ) Special qualities unable/unwilling to solve problems (only causes/presents them); unaware of its true nature Instinct to test people, set events in motion - Manifest as a nine-fingered man - Present a challenge (next column) - Cloud their judgement, make this all seem reasonable - Bestow a (dubious?) gift or curse - Vanish mysteriously Many tales tell of a stranger met in an unlikely place. The stranger is in some sort of pickle, or challenges our hero, or presents a tempting opportunity. Whatever else happens, the stranger ends up vanishing, but not before our hero notices his missing finger—and realizes that they just met Tor. When the tale's hero rises to the occasion, they win a gift from the stranger: a treasure or a magic item, a secret or a prophecy, or maybe just some useful bit of advice. But when the "hero" comes up short, they wind up cursed or "win" some treasure that brings them ruin and woe. When you introduce the nine-fingered stranger, pick or roll a manifestation and the challenge he presents. Consider, too, what gift or curse he might dispense: an arcanum, an answer, a bit of advice, a blessing (e.g. hold 3 Preparation, per the Bolster move), a curse (a threat , a magical affliction ), etc.

1490.title: 1d6 manifestation
1490.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Nine-fingered stranger › 1d6 manifestation
1490.body: - 1 A brash youth - 2 A formidable warrior - 3 A put-upon father - 4 A wealthy merchant - 5 A mysterious/cagey traveler - 6 A hapless old man

1491.title: 1d6 challenge
1491.breadcrumb: Tor, the Rain-maker › Nine-fingered stranger › 1d6 challenge
1491.body: - 1 A test of might (a duel, a wrestling match, a beast to slay, etc.) - 2 A test of prowess (a race, a climb, an archery contest, etc.) - 3 A test of endurance (a drinking contest, a long trudge, an all-night watch, etc.) - 4 A test of courage (a terror to face, a fate to accept, an injustice to prevent, etc.) - 5 A test of honor (a chance to steal, or cheat, or rig a bet, etc.) - 6 A test of kindness (someone wretched to help, some wrong to put right, etc.)

1492.title: Useful or Valuable Flora
1492.body: Pick one of the following, or create a new kind of flora for the PCs to discover. Either way, keep in mind when and where the PCs will encounter this. If you're presenting something useful, it should be useful now (perhaps with a bit of care or effort). Don't present them with an apple tree in winter and claim it's useful. If you're presenting them something valuable, it should either be something they can harvest now, or something that they can feasibly come back and get later.

1493.title: Wild food
1493.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Wild food
1493.body: Mushrooms, morels, asparagus, leafy greens, dandelions, all sorts of berries, cherries, plums, apples, beans, nuts, bulbs, tubers, etc. Can gather 1d6×◇ provisions (1d6 uses each). If you like, roll 1d6 to determine effort required and/or palatability.

1494.title: Poison
1494.body: Death caps, hemlock, belladonna, arum, foxglove, fire lily, oleander, yew, the wrong parts of many edible plants/mushrooms or medicinal herbs, etc. Can gather 1d6 doses ( dangerous ). If you like, also roll 1d6 each for potency, rapidity of onset, and ease of delivery. A skilled herbalist could refine it, make it more potent, rapid, or easier to use.

1495.title: Medicinal herbs
1495.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Medicinal herbs
1495.body: As a poultice, chew, incense, tea, extract, essential oil, etc. - Treats injuries/aches; use in lieu of supplies to Recover (1d6 uses) - A remedy for a debility, illness, infection, or poison (1d6 uses) - Valuable in ◇◇ bulk to a skilled herbalist (Value 1), the source of a medicine (or a poison)

1496.title: Useful materials
1496.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Useful materials
1496.body: - Vines that could work as rope - Saplings, good for setting snares - Saplings, straight and sturdy, good for arrows/bows/spear shafts - Large tree, toppled in the Great Wood, a good source of timber - Good kindling (dry leaves/needles/twigs, birch bark, etc.) - Dry wood/peat for burning

1497.title: Sample specimens
1497.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens

1498.title: Brightberry
1498.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Brightberry
1498.body: A thorny shrub with delicious berries that glow with soft, golden light ( hand ). In spring, it smells like baking honeycakes. Harvest berries as ◇ provisions (3 uses, fragile ), but they burn away within hours. Dig up a ◇◇ small shrub, pot it, and nurse it to health ( tricky ); merchants will pay handsomely for it (Value 2).

1499.title: Sleeping elksheart
1499.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Sleeping elksheart
1499.body: A giant mushroom, sometimes big as a house, found in dark forests. Fills the air with paralytic spores ( inhaled ); the mycelium digest the victims. Carefully collect spores to use as a poison ( dangerous , Value 1). Burn the cap to destroy the spores, cut it up, and feed a town with it (1d4 Surplus, immobile , Value 2).

1500.title: Violet lotus
1500.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Violet lotus
1500.body: A lily, grows alone along rivers/streams in protected eddies. Inhale its burnt petals to ask the GM a Seek Insight question about whatever situation you wish. A tincture of the flower makes a lovely perfume (Value 1).

1501.title: Twisting pine
1501.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Twisting pine
1501.body: An evergreen that rotates as it grows, resulting in twisting trunks. The sap flows freely when tapped, but hardens quickly unless bottled up and protected from air. Use a vial in lieu of supplies to Recover (seals wounds, flakes away days later). Harvest a ◇ jar (Value 1) to use as a sealant or a pottery glaze.

1502.title: Vanta's Blade
1502.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Vanta's Blade
1502.body: A low, herbaceous plant with broad, blade-shaped leaves that absorb light. Patches of the plant look like holes in reality. A ◇ pouch can be made into a rich dye, ink, or paint (Value 2), but Hillfolk and Manmarchers consider it cursed and will burn patches (and bearers) of it on sight.

1503.title: Hartwood
1503.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Hartwood
1503.body: A small tree with a distinctive two-pronged shape, found growing atop rocky outcrops. Climb up, chop off a branch, and burn it; apply the ashes to skin as a ward against possession.

1504.title: Snowembers
1504.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Sample specimens › Snowembers
1504.body: Pretty flowers found only in certain snow-covered meadows of the Huffel Peaks . The petals are edible and warm, even in the dead of winter, but they turn to mush soon after picking. Can harvest 1d4+4 provisions with a few hours' effort; those who partake grow flush and count as wearing warm clothes for the next day or so.

1505.title: Questions
1505.body: - What's distinctive about it? How did you spot or recognize it? - What is it (sometimes) called? - Who taught you to recognize/use it? - What superstition hangs over it? - According to the tales, how did this plant come to be? - Why is it thought impossible to cultivate? - What bird, critter, or bug eats it/lives in it/pollinates it/spreads it? - Who back home would be most excited by this find?

1506.title: Cultivation
1506.body: Should the PCs wish to cultivate a useful or valuable flora, then Make a Plan. Some example requirements include: - You must acquire a source of seeds/spores/cuttings/sprouts/etc. - You must learn the secrets of its cultivation - You must commune with the spirit of the flora and earn its respect - The steading must Pull Together to clear a plot for and plant the flora - It'll take a season/a year/multiple years for the flora to mature - You risk attracting a threat/it failing to thrive/it spreading unchecked Also establish the benefits of success, such as: - Access to a new special possession - Extra Surplus in a particular season - A trade good (Value 1-2) produced each spring/summer/autumn/winter - Extra HP regained when Recovering - Extra folks fed when you use a mess kit and supplies

1507.title: Creating new flora
1507.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora
1507.body: Think first about where it's found, how and where the PCs encounter it. With that in mind, decide how it's useful/valuable, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1508.title: 1d12 it's useful/valuable...
1508.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 it's useful/valuable...
1508.body: - 1-6 … in the field, here and now - 7-10 … to folks back home or in some other community - 11-12 … both in the field, here and now, and to folks back home, or in some other community

1509.title: 1d12 in the field
1509.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 in the field
1509.body: - 1-3 1d6×◇ provisions (1d6 uses) - 4-5 As a poultice/medicine/remedy; use in lieu of supplies to Recover or to treat a debility (1d6 uses) - 6 Replenishes 1d6 Stock to a Blessed's sacred pouch - 7-8 Fills a need (for shelter, fuel, kindling, water, material, etc.) - 9-10 Grants a benefit (like a four-leaf clover that adds 1 to a single key roll, or moonwort that clears the mind of beguilement) - 11-12 Provides a path forward or a solution to a problem (shrooms to commune with a spirit, a weed that masks your scent, poison fruit that might defeat a foe, bait for a trap, etc.)

1510.title: 1d12 to folks back home
1510.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 to folks back home
1510.body: - 1-2 A present, a curiosity, something pretty or nice (Value 0) - 3-4 Material for common crafts/trades (fiber, wood, medicine, oil, salt, tannin, etc., Value 1) - 5 A luxury (seasoning, perfume, dyes, cosmetics, etc., Value 1) - 6 Something sketchy (a drug, a poison, etc., Value 1) - 7 An offering, a charm, a ward, a counterspell, etc. (Value 1) - 8 Food for many people (counts as 1 Surplus, immobile , Value 2) - 9 Raw material for a steading improvement, like a wagonload of timber ( immobile , Value 2) - 10 High-quality materials (bright and permanent dye, potent medicinal herbs, etc., Value 2) - 11 A wonder (glowing flowers, floats in the air, fashionably dangerous, etc., Value 2) - 12 A legendary substance (extends youth, ensnares the will, brings prophecy, etc. Value 3) If it's not already obvious, pick or roll for the form-factor in which it's useful or valuable.

1511.title: 1d12 form-factor
1511.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 form-factor
1511.body: - 1 As a small item - 2 As a ◇ pouchful - 3-4 As a ◇◇ sackful or bundle - 5-6 In large quantities ( immobile ) - 7-9 Roll 1d6 again, but it requires extensive effort to harvest/process/make valuable - 10-12 Roll 1d8 again, but also it's fragile and/or cumbersome Next, pick or roll for 1d4 qualities and a distinctive trait.

1512.title: 1d12 quality
1512.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 quality
1512.body: - 1 Attracts/houses a pest or danger - 2 Dangerous to harvest/handle - 3 Harmful to the soil/biome - 4 Horrid taste/odor/texture - 5 Finicky/delicate/spoils quickly unless properly processed - 6 Steeped in superstition/said to be cursed/a bad omen - 7 Associated with a specific god/entity/hero/legend/tale - 8 Easy to work with - 9 Especially strong/durable/potent - 10 Delicious/aromatic/beautiful/supple/otherwise pleasing - 11 Rare/legendary/thought extinct - 12 Pick or roll an additional way in which it's useful/valuable

1513.title: 1d12 distinctive trait
1513.breadcrumb: Useful or Valuable Flora › Creating new flora › 1d12 distinctive trait
1513.body: - 1 Always found with some type of hazardous growth Green Lords - 2 Always found with (hidden by?) another, less-useful growth - 3 Grows/blooms only in particular/uncommon/unlikely conditions - 4 Grows in a particular pattern - 5 Attracts/houses some innocuous bug/bird/critter/spirit - 6 Intricate pattern/texture - 7 Distinct shape/configuration of leaves/stems/trunk/flowers/bulbs - 8 Leaves/flowers/seeds/fruits/pods that look like something else - 9 Strong, particular odor - 10 Bright/garish/unusual color - 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice Now, considering all of the above, and where and how it's found, decide: What is it? Pick 1: Lichen, moss, algae, fungus, mushroom, fern, grass, sedge, plant, weed, vine, climber, creeper, briar, shrub, succulent, deciduous tree (willow, ash, oak, alder, birch, elm, fruit, maple, chestnut, rowan, walnut, etc.), conifer (cedar, pine, fir, juniper, cypress, spruce, yew, etc.). What part is useful/valuable? Pick 1-4: Trunk, stem, shoot, limb, body, wood, flesh, bark, skin, root, tuber, thorn, leaf, fruit, gourd, nut, seed, spore, flower, pollen, pod, cone, sap, oil, resin, ash, etc. How is it prepared/used? Pick as many as make sense: Eaten, chewed, rubbed in, used as a poultice, burnt, smoked, ground, pulverized, stripped, soaked, cooked, infused, reduced, tinctured, mixed with \ \ , carved. What is it called? Mix and match words however makes sense (like "raven's ash" or "witchpetal"). Maybe translate it based on who named it (e.g. to Welsh for a plant local to Stonetop). autumn, bear, bell, berry, bitter, black, blossom, breath, bright, burr, cat, cave, claw, clover, crow, day, dock, dog, drake, dye, elk, eye, feather, finger, flax, foil, fox, gold, greater, green, hair, hanging, hart, heart, hemlock, hood, horn, ivy, lesser, lily, lotus, midwife, mint, mist, moth, moon, night, old, orchid, pad, paw, pepper, petal, poppy, potter, priest, puff, rain, raven, red, river, rose, rough, shade, silver, skunk, sky, smooth, snake, spotted, spring, sun, sweet, star, tanner, thistle, toe, tongue, violet, water, white, wild, wilting, wing, witch, wort, anything from the lists above, \ \ .

1514.title: Ustrina
1514.body: These mask-wearing people dwell to the west, deep in the Huffel Peaks . Every now and then, they emerge from the Labyrinth and visit Gordin's Delve , trading finely worked goods, wonders and the odd bit of orichalcum . - Masks of polished metal, only vaguely humanoid features - Voluminous black robes and strangely ornate headdresses - Faint chemical smells: a whiff of rust, or ozone, or sulfur - Tinny, echoing voices, like someone down a well, speaking an obscure dialect Tips: speak formally and proceed all proper nouns with "the" ("the Rhianna," "the Gordin's Delve"); talk into an empty soda can (or at least with your hand in front of your mouth).

1515.title: Names
1515.body: Portuguese-sounding, but often ending in "-ix": the Avilio, the Brenix, the Caesana, the Donatix, the Emitia, the Flagio, the Galvaix, the Jacintix, the Lucisia, the Marcix, the Numia, the Otavix, the Paolix, the Ruia, the Sibonio, the Tafia, the Valerix.

1516.title: Questions
1516.body: - Which of you has encountered the Ustrina before? In Gordin's Delve, or elsewhere in the Huffel Peaks? - What is it about the way they move that unnerves you so? - Who did you know in Gordin's Delve who tried to rob them, and what doom befell them? - What's the oddest thing that you've heard of the Ustrina asking for in trade? - Even though you've never seen it, what do you think they really look like?

1517.title: Lore
1517.body: Something interesting: The Ustrina dwell among the ruins of the Forge Lords , and are heirs to many of their arts. And while few outsiders can tell, there are sects among them—the Peridita, the Albedo, the Citrinita, and the Rubedix—with rivalries and politics aplenty. Something useful: Ustrina (or their ancestors) were servants of the Forge Lords, and each group holds the relics of their old masters in great esteem. They might sell artifacts of other Forge Lords to outsiders, but they will pay handsomely to have the artifacts of their own masters returned.

1518.title: Hooks
1518.body: - Ustrina haven't appeared in Gordin's Delve for six months; one of the Bosses offers a reward for information. - Mining crews go missing in the First Delve ; survivors are blaming Ustrina. - Members of one sect hire someone to rob a group of another sect (discretely, of course). - A trio of masked, black-robed figures have been seen poking around the Ruined Tower , but why? - A merchant from Gordin's Delve brings an Ustrina to Stonetop. It wishes to study the Stone or Maker-ruins about town, but a trio of Ustrina (from the same sect) soon follow, looking for it.

1519.title: True nature
1519.body: The Ustrina never remove their masks, robes, or gloves. Rumors abound, of course, but if anyone reliable knows what they truly look like, they aren't telling. The true nature of the Ustrina is up to you and your group to decide, but possibilities include: - Flesh and blood, much like us, but with elaborate traditions of privacy and decorum - Mutant scavengers of the Forge Lords' ruins, horrible to behold, disguised when they deal with humanity - Naught but elemental spirits , animating a simple skeleton of bronze - Constructs of impossibly intricate clockwork, animated by glowing soul-gems - Ancient servants of the Forge Lords who have slowly replaced flesh with clockwork - A bunch of smaller creatures animating an elaborate puppet

1520.title: Trade missions
1520.body: Ustrina come to Gordin's Delve a few times every season, emerging from the First Delve . Some have contacts in the town, and approach those contacts directly. Others approach individuals seemingly at random, offering to trade. For any given trade mission, pick or roll what they're offering and what they want in return, using the tables on the following page. Their wants and needs are inscrutable, making them difficult to haggle with and fairly easy to offend. They are often willing to make arrangements now and return in a few weeks or even months to make the transaction. Pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1521.title: 1d12 what they're offering
1521.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Trade missions › 1d12 what they're offering
1521.body: - 1 Shards of pottery, scraps of frayed fabric, odd pits of stone, or other junk (Value 0) that they genuinely regard as precious - 2-3 Finely wrought items (Value 2) like an ◇ exquisite bronze statuette, a silver-alloy dagger ( hand ), a ◇ gold-embroidered cloak ( warm ), a perfectly balanced ◇ short sword ( hand , close , +1 damage, 1 piercing), etc. - 4-5 A curiosity or wonder (next page) - 6-7 Precious metals or jewels (Value 3), like a ◇ bar of gold, a ◇◇ chest of ancient silver coins, a gem-encrusted gold ring, a few sapphires - 8 1d4 small ingots of orichalcum - 9-10 Some artifact of the Forge Lords - 11 An artifact from one of the other Makers - 12 Some "priceless" treasure (Value 4), like a sapphire the size of your eye, a ◇ chest of gold coins, a bejeweled gold crown, a cartload ( immobile ) of ◇ fine steel swords ( close , +1 damage, 1 piercing)

1522.title: 1d12 what they want
1522.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Trade missions › 1d12 what they want
1522.body: - 1 A dozen or so... (roll 1d6 or pick) - 1 … children/infants/newborns - 2 … healthy human adults - 3 … human adults, of whatever quality - 4 … fresh human corpses - 5 … human corpses in whatever state - 6 … urns full of human blood (they'll provide the urns) - 2 1d4 cartloads of a specific exotic trade good, like silk, a certain spice, amber, aurochs horns, etc. (Value 4) - 3-5 A particular artifact of the Forge Lords (they'll describe it) - 6 Some other, specific arcanum (they'll describe it) - 7 A strange and difficult task (like slaying some horror in the Labyrinth) - 8 A chest or similar amount of... (roll 1d6 or pick) - 1 … makerglass - 2 … dark ice - 3 … aetherium - 4 … redwood - 5 … black iron - 6 … red crystal . - 9 1d4 units of a specific exotic trade good, like ◇◇ bolts of silk, ◇ pouches of a certain spice, pouches of amber, ◇◇ pairs of aurochs horns, etc. (Value 3) - 10 1d4 cartloads of a particular raw material, like timber, tin, a certain type of cut stone (Value 2) - 11 1d4 dozen goats/pigs/hounds (Value 2) - 12 1d4 cartloads of grain, flour, and other common foodstuffs (Value 2)

1523.title: Curiosities and wonders
1523.body: Perhaps crafted by the Ustrina themselves, or pilfered from troves of the Forge Lords. If the Ustrina do know the secrets to creating these things, they aren't inclined to share.

1524.title: Artificial limb
1524.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Artificial limb
1524.body: crude , magical (?), Value 3 A clockwork hand, foot, arm, or leg, with straps and buckles to attach it. Impossibly intricate, and with practice it's nearly as functional as the real thing. It cannot feel, though, and is prone to jamming up and needing repairs.

1525.title: Barrel of anthracite
1525.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Barrel of anthracite
1525.body: immobile , dangerous , Value 3 Lumps of lustrous, gray-black stone, like coal but drier and much more useful. A small lump catches fire as readily as dry wood and burns all night with the heat of a good cook-fire. It casts only dim light ( close , area ) and there's almost no smoke, but the smell is something awful.

1526.title: Brass sphere
1526.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Brass sphere
1526.body: ◇, magical , Value 2 A heavy lump of smooth metal that fits in one's hand. When you touch the sphere with bare flesh, it glows with an orange light ( reach , area , ○○○ hours). It recharges an hour of light for every hour that it sits in a hot fire or coals.

1527.title: Bright-sticks
1527.body: fragile , dangerous , Value 1 A small box containing 3d4 stiff metal rods, about the size of incense sticks. Snap one, and each piece bursts into a blindingly bright, blisteringly hot flame (d8 damage, messy ) for a few seconds while it burns away to ash.

1528.title: Clockwork bird
1528.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Clockwork bird
1528.body: ◇, fragile , magical , Value 3 Made of tin and set with agate eyes. When you wind it up and let it run, it will dance and chirp loudly for a few minutes. When you wind it up and twist one of its feathers just so, it will sit and wait, only starting to dance and chirp when a creature enters its line of sight.

1529.title: Ever-burning ember
1529.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Ever-burning ember
1529.body: ◇, fragile , magical , Value 3 This ceramic pot has a number of thin slits along its shoulder and a ring of rune-etched brass around its neck. The pot is always hot, painful to touch, but it won't cause blisters outright. Inside is a glowing ember of redwood with a fire spirit bound inside. The ember will smolder forever as long it stays in the pot and isn't completely submerged.

1530.title: Pale metal gauntlets
1530.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Pale metal gauntlets
1530.body: ◇, fireproof , magical , Value 3 Made of a pale metal, lighter than it should be, and intricately carved with geometric patterns. The insides are an impossibly fine chainmail of the same metal. They seem to absorb heat, and are often damp. When you wear the pale metal gauntlets , you can pick up and manipulate even white-hot steel without harming your hands (the rest of you, though, has no special protection from heat).

1531.title: Self-burning lamp
1531.breadcrumb: Ustrina › Curiosities and wonders › Self-burning lamp
1531.body: ◇, reach , area , ○○○○○ hours, Value 2 A bronze oil-lamp with a small valve on the top. When you screw the nozzle open, a hot blue flame pops out of the nozzle. When you screw the nozzle shut, the flame snuffs out. The lamp itself gets blisteringly hot while running, and once empty of fuel, only the Ustrina know how to refill it.

1532.title: Vor Svetelik
1532.body: The Green Lords called this place "the Great White City." It was a place of beauty, learning, and study. But when the Fae rebelled, the scholars of Vor Svetelik were tasked with breeding beasts of war. When the Green Lords turned in desperation to the Things Below , the city's waters fouled and its white trees grew sickly. When other Makers shattered what is now Ferrier's Fen , this city became the Green Lords' last refuge and the site of the war's last battle. The Green Lords made the invaders pay. They laid traps. They sent their loyal servants to die in waves. They unleashed new horrors, each worse than the last, retreating all the while. And when the Fae and their Stone Lord allies reached the center of the city, they found it… empty. Naught remained of the Green Lords, except tunnels leading deep into the earth, collapsed behind them. The Stone Lords retreated to their hills. The Fae fell to bickering and their armies disbanded. Those few mortal servants of the Green Lords who survived were left to fend for themselves, eventually becoming the Forest Folk . And Vor Svetelik, the Great White City, was left a blighted, infested ruin.

1533.title: Impressions
1533.body: - A scraggly canopy in the valley below, hints of streets and buildings, a broken white tower - Pale and spindly trees, bearing withered leaves and oozing black sap - A steady, damp warmth, like foul breath, even in the dead of winter - That feeling you get when you move a rock and see the grubs and worms squirming beneath, but constantly, everywhere you look

1534.title: Hooks
1534.body: - An artifact that the PCs desperately want lies in the city's ruins - A southern antiquarian knows where to find the ruins, and is leading a substantial expedition there, using Stonetop as a basecamp - A Fae chieftain is besieging the ruins, attempting to finally exterminate the Fomoraij - An earthquake cracks open the ruins; horrors vomit forth into the surrounding woods

1535.title: Getting there
1535.body: The ruins of Vor Svetelik lie in the Great Wood , wherever you (the GM) need them to be. They should be at least a few days' travel from Stonetop. To find the ruins, PCs will need a guide or detailed directions. - A Fae, a spirit of the wild , or the Forest Folk could guide them, but wouldn't want to. All sane beings shun the ruins. - A Green Lord ruin might contain a map or directions, but the PCs would risk getting lost. Such instructions would be centuries out of date! - Magic like the Sylvan Web could help the PCs find the ruins themselves, though they'd still risk getting lost on the way. - The West Road once led from where Stonetop stands to Vor Svetelik. It's broken and long-buried, but could show the way to one who knew what to look for.

1536.title: Areas
1536.body: - Outer slopes of the hills surrounding the city , jagged, overgrown, littered with Forest Folk glyphs and invaders' graves. - Inner slopes of those same hills , steep and unstable, trees thinning out as one descends. Signs of old battle abound. Stone Lord constructs still stand watch. - The city's outskirts , hiding ruins of manor-houses and plantations. - The city's center , tightly packed with ruins, corpses, and danger. - The undercity , old sewers, cisterns, tunnels, basements, and vaults; linked via erosion, collapse, and beast-burrows.

1537.title: Sites
1537.body: The following can all be found in the city's center, though still some distance apart. - The Conservatory , once a latticed dome of living wood and glass, now burnt, broken, shattered. Bound fertility spirits mournfully tend to hazardous growths . - The Menagerie , mostly intact. Cubbies line the walls, each with tissue (fur, tooth, etc.) from a different beast. A plasmic horror dwells in a central pit. - The Great Library , broken white tower, poking through the canopy. Lower levels largely intact—grand, overgrown. Heavily warded, a few chambers still perfectly preserved. Littered with the remains of invaders. - The Necropolis , cluster of ziggurats, domes, and monuments near the city's center. Ransacked and vandalized, with few tombs intact. Deeply haunted. - The Hallow Tree , amphitheater carved from a single, massive, fossilized tree stump. Green Lords once met here to share and debate. All that remains is a basalt slab, piles and piles of bones, and hints of tunnels long-ago collapsed.

1538.title: Terrain
1538.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

1539.title: 1d12 surface terrain
1539.body: - 1 Stretch of true death, where nothing has grown or decayed for hundreds of years - 2 Pool/fountain/stream/standing water—dark and unwholesome - 3 Hill/cliff/outcrop, perhaps a place where the land buckled and split\ - 4-5 Stretch of sickly white trees - 6-7 Infrastructure/bridge/aqueduct/cistern/sewer\ - 8-9 green lord ruin\ (see next page) - 10-11 Rubble, peeking out from dirt and groundcover - 12 Crevasse/sinkhole, like a scar in the earth\

1540.title: 1d12 undercity terrain
1540.body: - 1 Flooded chamber - 2 Running water - 3-4 Old tunnels/shafts, some stretching for miles - 5-6 green lord ruin (see next page) or part of one, buried/sunken/toppled\ - 7-8 Burrow/warren/tunnels, dug out by… something\ - 9-10 Stairs/shaft/ladder/ramp, going up and/or further down\ - 11 Crevasse, maybe open to the sky, maybe not\ - 12 Sinkhole, leading down down down <sup \ </sup Might connect to the undercity/surface

1541.title: 1d12 green lord ruin
1541.breadcrumb: Vor Svetelik › Terrain › 1d12 green lord ruin
1541.body: - 1 Arched, petrified trees, forming a half-cylinder. Largely collapsed. The remains shelter a stone slab (3 by 6 paces) and empty canopic jars. - 2 Buried vault, cracked, mostly full of dirt. Inner walls lined with bas relief vines in runic shapes. - 3 Crumbling walls of a ziggurat. "Inside" of walls bear shifting mosaics of colorful moss, showing history of the Fae. - 4 Earth-covered mound. Entrance sealed by living roots (part with command word). Purple crystals line interior, gently glowing. - 5 Foundation of white/green/gray stones, hints of faded scrollwork, hinting at long-gone structures. - 6 Half-buried dome, 15 paces across. Exposed timbers of uncut tree trunks. Broken gate of woven antler.

1542.title: 1d12 green lord ruin
1542.breadcrumb: Vor Svetelik › Terrain › 1d12 green lord ruin
1542.body: - 7 Half-dozen structures of rust-red trees, grown in beehive shapes. Untouched by time. Inside: cauldrons, misshapen bones, carapaces. - 8 House-sized pile of dirt, hints of worked stone beneath. Inside: a modest dwelling lit by glowing ambers. - 9 Intact cellar, too small for Makers. Door is stuck, but works. Lit by glowing lichen. Holds ancient foodstuffs; crumbles at touch or breeze. - 10 Massive tree trunk, 10 paces wide, broken off at top. Remaining wood glints of silver. Entrance to hollow is embossed with ivory runes. Black sap drips from interior walls. - 11 Ramparts of iron-infused trees, sporting long thorns, blocking what once were streets. Rusted, crumbling, uprooted in places. - 12 Underground "cage" made of tree roots infused with iron. Still intact, no apparent entrances. The above are just examples. See page 214 if you need to create more or different ruins.

1543.title: Discoveries
1543.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1544.title: 1d6 discovery
1544.body: - 1 A change of terrain Vor Svetelik and roll again - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 sign of battle (next column), strangely well-preserved - 4 An artifact of… (next column) - 5 encounter (next page) - 6 A notable site or point of interest (next page)

1545.title: 1d6 sign of battle
1545.breadcrumb: Vor Svetelik › Discoveries › 1d6 sign of battle
1545.body: - 1 Bones/bodies of pre-Forest Folk, Stone Lords, or their human servants, bearing the marks of violence - 2 Bleached bones of a Green Lord, peeking out of dirt or rubble, entwined with root or vine - 3 A shattered construct of the Stone Lords - 4 Structures laid to waste: shattered, sliced clean through, dissolved, cracked apart, scattered as though dropped from a great height, etc. - 5 The psychic imprint of mortals and Fae dying in battle, perhaps even a haunted Fae domain - 6 The earth itself, still scarred by terrible magic: rent, blackened, melted, vitrified, petrified, reduced to ash, still-smoking after all these centuries, etc.

1546.title: 1d6 artifact of...
1546.body: - 1 … the Things Below - 2 … the Fomoraij - 3 … the Fae - 4 … the Stone Lords - 5-6 … the Green Lords

1547.title: 1d6 encounter
1547.body: - 1 A Fomoraij's infected servant , single-mindedly pursuing some task (digging, building, searching, carving, writing, etc.) - 2 A Fomoraij's infected servant , desperately seeking release - 3 A spirit of the wild , curious, indifferent, or just confused - 4 Someone or something trapped in stasis (petrified; in a time-pocket, in a magical cell, etc.) but free-able - 5 The bound shade of a Green Lord or their mortal servant, mournful and suspicious but not outright hostile - 6 Someone seeking relics or treasure: crinwin , Fae , southern adventurers or antiquarians , maybe even Forest Folk

1548.title: 1d6 point of interest
1548.breadcrumb: Vor Svetelik › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
1548.body: - 1 A tunnel/path/connection to a Fomoraij's lair , open, sealed, or ajar - 2 A number of waystones , cracked and ruined and dangerous to use - 3 A monument, honoring a Green Lord/Fomoraij with whom the PCs are familiar - 4 A sacred site , home to a long-bound spirit, somehow untainted by the Things Below - 5 An exposed stretch of Makers' Road , its warding magic still intact - 6 The cracked portal (next column)

1549.title: The cracked portal
1549.breadcrumb: Vor Svetelik › Discoveries › The cracked portal
1549.body: Somewhere deep in the ruins is a huge slab of white rock, etched with a magic circle. A large crack mars the stone and the circle, yet they still thrum with power. The circle is a portal , connected to those beneath the Ruined Tower , the Labyrinth , near Three Coven Lake , and so forth. The portal can be opened (see Opening the Way ), but it is dangerous and unstable. When you open the cracked portal (from either direction), roll +INT: on a 10+ , the portal opens but pick 1 from the list below; on a 7-9 , the portal opens but pick 2: - Any who traverse it risk serious harm —1d10 damage ( messy , ignores armor) - You draw the attention of some dangerous primordial entity, like an archon or a voidblight - The cracks grow bigger; subsequent attempts to use this portal will have a cumulative -1 penalty On a 6- , the cracked portal shatters violently (1d10 damage, reach , area , messy , forceful , ignores armor) and leaves a rift in its place. The other portal cracks (or shatters, if it was already cracked).

1551.title: Hazards
1551.body: - Ground collapsing underfoot, dropping (deeper) into the undercity - A dangerous climb—up or down—and a reason to attempt it - Spells of war, trapped in stasis, crackling in the air and waiting to spring - Weird distortions of time and space - Fae glamours , concealing a place or waiting to ensnare - Hazardous growths , run rampant or deployed as defenses

1552.title: Podburst vines
1552.body: As enemies prepared to invade, the Green Lords layered their city with living boobytraps. Many remain. The most common are fast-growing vines that bear large, ominous pods. - Many tendrils cover a structure or path, obvious or hidden beneath soil/brush - The tendrils tense and writhe when something disturbs them - A seedpod contracts, then explodes ( reach , area , see below) - A chain reaction starts, one pod exploding after another

1553.title: 1d6 explodes with...
1553.body: What do the exploding pods do? Pick 1 or roll. 1d6 Explodes with... ----- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1 … iron-infused seeds, like shrapnel (d10 damage, messy , 1 piercing) 2 … concussive force (d4+2 damage, forceful , loud ) 3 … seeds that rapidly burst into thin, thorny, ensnaring vines (d6 damage, grabby ) 4 … a cloud of spores that blind/sicken/choke/etc. (d4 damage, debilitating ) 5 … pollen that glows and/or attracts horrors (like blade wasps , next page) 6 … iron-infused spore-dust, clinging to any it touches

1554.title: Deathgrip vines
1554.body: In some parts of the city, you'll find hunks of stone cracked apart by vines. The vines bear clusters of burrs all up and down their stalks, which easily fall off and cling to clothing, gear, hair, even skin. The burrs are annoying, maybe a little painful, but not (immediately) harmful. Throughout the ruins, though, are old sigils of power. When these burrs are brought within a few paces of the sigils, they rapidly sprout and start to grow. And burrow. And grip. And tear. When you try to free yourself of sprouting deathgrip vines , roll +STR or +DEX (your call): on a 10+ , you can get free if you pick 1 from the list below; on a 7-9 , you can get free if you pick 2: - Tear vines from your flesh (1d8 damage, messy , ignores armor) - Lose/discard 1d4 × ◇ worth of items - Someone else (who isn't in a similar plight) needs to help you On a 6- , take 1d6 damage ( messy , grabby , ignores armor) and the vines continue to grow, burrow, and constrict. Each time they deal damage, increase the damage die by one step.

1555.title: Blade wasps
1555.body: Like wasps, but bigger and with iron-infused stingers. Hey, at least they're not venomous. When you are stung by a single blade wasp , it hurts, draws blood, and takes some effort not to cry out. When you get swarmed by blade wasps , take 1d6 damage ( messy , ignores armor) and you're wracked with pain. Doing anything other than curling up and writhing means that you're Defying Danger (likely with CON). Each subsequent time you take damage from the swarm, increase the die one step (max d12). When you flee a blade wasp swarm , roll +CON: on a 10+ , you escape the swarm but pick 1 from the list below; on a 7-9 , you escape the swarm but pick 2: - Suffer damage from the swarm - Mark a debility from blood loss - An important piece of gear is lost or ruined - You run straight into another danger

1556.title: The Undying
1556.body: - Ghosts of mortal soldiers, reenacting ancient battles - Specters of those subjected to the Green Lords' most terrible experiments - Hollow Fae , wounded by iron, abandoned by their fellows - Wraiths of Green Lords or their mummified servants, their canopic jars destroyed - Mummies of Green Lords Green Lords and their servants Green Lords, safe in their still-standing tombs

1557.title: Chimerae
1557.body: - Llamudwr , lurking in darksome waters or flitting through the night sky - Rhagedn , still keeping watch - Swyn , their entourages in tow, congregating in Vor Svetelik every dozen years - Zrajedak or some other sort of chimera bred for war, ensnared by Fae magic or still in stasis, waiting to be deployed

1558.title: Other creatures
1558.body: - Just about any sort of beast found in the Great Wood , but twisted and mutated - Crinwin , perhaps doing decidedly un-crinwin things - The infected servant of a Fomoraij Fomoraij - A Fomoraij itself Names, deep in the undercity.

1559.title: Nemurvojak
1559.body: Horde , construct , undead , corrupted , fearless HP 11; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze Damage iron-wood spears d6 w/advantage ( close , messy , 1 piercing) or bare-handed rending d4 with advantage ( hand , grabby , messy , 1 piercing) Special qualities bound by its last orders; shuns the sun or holy light Instinct to revel in their prey's fear and suffering - Rush a foe with an unearthly howl - Lash out with a mass of black, viny tendrils ( reach , grabby ) - Brutalize an already fallen foe When the Fae rebelled, some of the Green Lord's mortal servants—the ancestors of the Forest Folk —sided with the traitors. But not all. Most remained loyal, or at least too afraid of their masters to risk turning on them. Their loyalty, alas, did not save them. At first, the Green Lords would plant demon-seeds in only the bodies of servants who had already fallen. But when they saw just how effective those animated corpses were, well… the Green Lords were nothing if not practical. Many of these horrors remain in Vor Svetelik, bound by orders to protect the city or certain sites within. Thank the gods they were not allowed to roam free.

1560.title: Plasmic horror
1560.body: Solitary , large , construct , amorphous , terrifying HP 19; Armor 2 (membrane, lacks organs) Damage pseudopod d10+3 ( hand , close , reach , grabby , forceful ) or acidic engulf d10+5 ( hand , grabby , forceful , messy , ignores armor) Special qualities child-like intellect; susceptible to strong wills Instinct to recklessly explore the world - Ooze through the smallest cracks or openings - Assume the form (wholly or partially) of any being it touches (from small to large size) - Reveal its true form, shockingly - Grow (and gain 1d8 HP) by consuming a living thing - Spawn a smaller version of itself These… things… served as blank canvasses for the Green Lord's flesh-weaving. Once, they were kept carefully locked away in laboratories and breeding pits. Now, with their creators gone all these years… well, who knows where they might be found? Why haven't these plasmic horrors spread beyond Vor Svetelik? That's for you to decide, but perhaps… - … some Fae magic keeps them contained; - … they require some nutrient that is only available here; - … something in their cellular programming keeps them from wandering far; or - … a thrumming artifact emits some sort of song that they are loathe to leave. When you command a plasmic horror in the tongue of the Green Lords , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , it does as you say, and accepts you as its master; on a 7-9 , it does as you say for now. When a plasmic horror thoroughly consumes a living being , it gains 1d8 HP, even if that takes it above its maximum. When a plasmic horror spawns a smaller version of itself, it loses a number of HP and the new version starts with that same number. Plasmic horrors can be "locked" in their current forms by a particular incantation and a drop of Green Lord blood. Good luck getting a hold of either.

1561.title: Waystones
1561.body: The Great Wood and Ferrier's Fen are dotted with massive markers of pale gray stone, moss-covered and largely overgrown, deeply etched with the curling, branching runes of the Green Lords . Fae magic Fae binds the waystones together.

1562.title: Lore
1562.body: Something interesting: The waystones mark entrances to the Fae paths. If one can access the paths, they can quickly travel great distances. The paths, however, are fraught with peril. Tales tell of travelers being lost or entrapped for years or even centuries. Something useful: To step onto the Fae paths, you must leave behind all iron and steel, plus one of the following: - Follow the footsteps of a Fae guide - Be able to see through Fae magic and glamours, via Old Mother's Wine or a similar effect - Go at a certain time (under a \ \ moon, at midnight, at sunrise, etc.) and either... - ... have Fae blood in your veins; or - ... open your mind with drugs/alcohol/delirium/exhaustion/etc. Each waystone connects to at least one other, and most connect to a few. When you step on the path, you see the routes laid out before you, and can sense roughly where in the mortal realm each of the adjoining waystones lies. Travelling the Fae paths is dangerous, but safer when you go with trusted companions.

1563.title: Walking the paths
1563.body: When you walk the Fae path from one waystone to the next , roll +WIS: on a 6- , you lose your way and find yourself in peril—tell us what chases or lures you from the path; on a 7+ , you arrive safely, only a few minutes having passed, but first tell us what tried to chase or lure you from the path and why it failed; on a 10+ , you can also intervene and save someone who has lost their way, but only if you can tell us how. If you roll a 6- and someone saves you , don't mark XP. When you lose your way and none of your allies save you , decide if you wish to keep playing this character.
