If you do, you will escape from the Fae domains, a relatively short time having passed in the real world, but for you it might have taken days, months, or even years. Pick 2: - You're different now. Change your instinct, change your look, and lose one of your moves, replacing it with another move available to you. - You lost something important: a move, a precious item, the memory of a loved one, an earnest hope, etc. Tell us what you lost. - You owe some favor to a Fae—one who ensnared you or one who rescued you. Work with the GM to determine who. - Days, weeks, or months have passed in the mortal realm, instead of minutes or hours. - You emerge at a different waystone than the one you intended to reach. When a follower loses their way and no one saves them , their fate is in the GM's hands. If a player needs inspiration, have them roll the Die of Fate. 1d6 lure --- --- 1 The need to escape from danger, heartbreak, or shame 2 Someone lost to you, calling out 3 A child, beast, or loved one in distress 4 A revel, a meal, a drink, or some other pleasure of the flesh 5 Something of great beauty or wonder 6 Something of great value, or a chance to learn something of great import

1564.title: Stepping off the paths
1564.breadcrumb: Waystones › Walking the paths › Stepping off the paths
1564.body: When one steps off the Fae paths, they might find themselves in any number of Fae domains. It's nearly impossible for a mortal to navigate between them, as the ways are ruled by dreamlike logic and secrets and ancient Fae pacts. A guide is all but essential, but even the Fae might struggle to access or escape certain realms.

1565.title: Hooks
1565.body: - Something dangerous has learned to use the waystones, emerging near the one closest to Stonetop, causing trouble, and retreating. - Someone was lost to the Fae paths years or decades or even centuries ago. Now they reappear, stumbling back to Stonetop, thinking that only hours or days have passed. - Someone the PCs care about steps onto the Fae paths and doesn't return.

1566.title: The Whitefang Mountains
1566.body: The Whitefang Mountains form the northern border of the World's End, running from the Huffel Peaks in the northwest, all along the Great Wood and the North Manmarch , to the Dread River and beyond in the east. Beyond them lie nothing but the Frozen Wastes . The mountains are tall and jagged, steep and forbidding. Yet they hide almost indescribable beauty: waterfalls, deep pine forests, sweeping meadows. They also hide a surprising number of settlements. Barrier Pass is closest to Stonetop, but many more lie beyond.

1567.title: Hooks
1567.body: - Storms around Tor's Fist grow stronger; the colors shift to red and yellow - A band of kyakaara slips out of the mountains and is eyeing Stonetop - A southern merchant is intent on finding a mountain village, to trade for ivory and fine wool - Mountain apes hold an artifact/scrap of lore in their hoard - An apprentice shantyara needs help retrieving her dead master's sword from the bhoka who killed him

1569.title: Always
1569.body: - Snow-covered peaks, like mighty teeth - Wind: howling, whispering, gusting - Monkeys screeching and chittering in the valleys, leaping through the trees - Prayer-flags fluttering in the wind - The elation of reaching a summit; the despair of realizing it was just a ridge, and you're only half-way

1570.title: Spring
1570.body: - Slopes and vales bright with rhododendron: purple, red, and orange - Trickle and rush of snowmelt

1571.title: Summer
1571.body: - Lush green below; stern, colorless peaks above - Birdsong echoing and echoing

1572.title: Autumn
1572.body: - Meadows turned brown and red and gold - Mist filling the valleys, clouds gliding by above

1573.title: Winter
1573.body: - Snow cloaking the valleys, choking passes - Tears freezing, sweat soaking you under warm clothes

1574.title: Tor's Fist
1574.body: Almost straight north of Stonetop, this lonely mountain juts above its neighbors. It's not the tallest peak in the Whitefangs, but it's tall enough. When summer storms roll up from the south and crash against the mountains, the worst of them swirl around Tor's Fist. They'll lash the peak with lightning, and in the aftermath, green and purple lights dance around the crown.

1575.title: What's up there?
1575.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Tor's Fist › What's up there?
1575.body: Clearly Tor's Fist holds some mystery, but its nature is for you to decide. Perhaps the mountain's peak holds… - … the tomb of Stormcatcher , or her son, or her lover; - … a cleft, from which the Stone itself was carved; - … one of the last thirteen Rime Lords , still in stasis, protected by powerful tulpas ; - … a potent storm-spirit, corrupted by the Things Below The Things Below, but bound there by ancient Rime Lord magic; - … the domain of a powerful, temperamental Fae ; - … a shattered portal , torn open and ruined; - … a people of great stature, who worship Tor ; - … the last of the Tempest Lords , grumpy and aloof; - … some combination of the above; or - … something else entirely.

1576.title: Chart a Course
1576.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Tor's Fist › Chart a Course
1576.body: To reach the summit of Tor's Fist: - You'll need to wait until late spring or summer to have any chance - First you must travel to the Foothills or Barrier Pass - From there, you must travel to the base of Tor's Fist - It'll take at least 5 days (from the Foothills) or 6 days (from Barrier Pass), and a corresponding amount of supplies - You need a knowledgeable guide or accurate map - You need to bring warm clothes, climbing gear, and fuel for cookfires - The terrain is treacherous; you risk injury - The way will be grueling; you risk exhausting yourselves/your resources - The way is perilous, filled with dangers - From the base of Tor's Fist, you can attempt to reach the summit - It'll take at least 5 days, lest you get altitude sickness - You need to bring a skilled mountaineer - The climb is treacherous and grueling; you risk injury, exhaustion, or both - You risk drawing the attention of whatever it is that's up there - You must brave the final ascent: a sheer climb in heavy winds

1577.title: Terrain
1577.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1578.title: 1d12 terrain
1578.body: - 1-2 Glacier/snowfield/snowpack - 3 Lake/river/stream/waterfall - 4 Waterlogged soil, frozen or muddy - 5 Forest/woods/stand of trees/shrubs - 6 Sheer rock face/bluff/escarpment - 7 Summit/peak/false peak - 8-9 Steep slope/switchback trails/ridgeline - 10 Ravine/canyon/gorge - 11 Gentle slope/meadow/valley/pass - 12 Cave/cavern/grotto If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

1579.title: Discoveries
1579.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1580.title: 1d6 discovery
1580.body: - 1 A change of terrain (previous column) and roll again. - 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.) - 3 An encounter (see below) - 4 A point of interest (next page) - 5 A natural resource (next page) - 6 A settlement (next page)

1581.title: 1d6 encounter
1581.body: - 1 Monkeys, curious and mischievous, or perhaps signs of mountain apes The Whitefang Mountains - 2 A patrol from a fortress-monastery The Whitefang Mountains, suspicious of the PCs - 3 Someone in dire straits - 4 Villagers/layfolk, traveling or tending herds, wary - 5 Mammoth herders The Frozen Wastes, stiffly polite - 6 A tulpa Rime Lords, a spirit of the wild Spirits of the wild, or a Fae Fae, curious or indifferent

1582.title: 1d6 point of interest
1582.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Discoveries › 1d6 point of interest
1582.body: - 1 A settlement (see below), overrun/destroyed by kyakaara - 2 A place corrupted by the Things Below - 3 A place haunted by the dead or undying Death and the Undying - 4 A site sacred to the spirits of the wild or perhaps a Fae domain - 5-6 A ruin or artifact of the Rime Lords

1583.title: 1d6 natural resource
1583.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Discoveries › 1d6 natural resource
1583.body: - 1 Vein of ore/mineral deposits/valuable stone (slate, marble, pigments, etc.) - 2 Game, dangerous to hunt: cave bears , mammoth , etc. - 3-4 Game, hard to reach/prone to bolt: monkeys, hares, goats, etc. - 5 Useful or valuable flora - 6 Safe place to rest, take shelter

1584.title: 1d12 settlement
1584.body: - 1-2 Camp of mammoth herders , likely empty/abandoned in warmer months - 3-6 Farm valley ( hamlet ), The Whitefang Mountains - 7-8 Farm valley ( village ), The Whitefang Mountains - 9-10 Lone dwelling: hermit's cave/hunter's lodge/shepherd's camp/etc. - 11-12 Fortress-monastery

1585.title: Mountain folk
1585.body: The Whitefangs host a surprising number of settlements. Most are small farming valleys, even more isolated than Stonetop. But there are also a number of fortress-monasteries, like Barrier Pass . Something interesting: The mountain folk's ancestors were disciples of the Rime Lords , and Rime Lord teachings still influence the culture. But time, isolation, and competing philosophies have led to vastly different local traditions. An idea or practice that is lauded in one village might be ridiculed in the next. And in the village beyond that? It might get you killed. Something useful: Communities occasionally trade with their nearest neighbors, and folk in one settlement are familiar with the ways of the next—at least well enough to avoid offense or violence. But if you seek a guide or informant, find one who's actually been there, lest you have your ears filled with exaggerated nonsense.

1586.title: Names
1586.body: Nepalese- and Tibetan-inspired: Aandha, Araayin, Bisheej, Choden, Dawa, Dhaar, Dorji, Duga, Fayurma, Hopan, Jamya, Jyat, Keshhay, Ksali, Kundari, Kunza, Lhamo, Mitis, Nobu, Nyado, Passan, Pratar, Puyari, Radhaa, Ravar, Sajadr, Sonam, Supar, Tani, Tashi, Tenzi, Tseri, Wanchu, Yonta.

1587.title: Questions
1587.body: - Who among you have traveled here before? When and why? - Who do you know (in Gordin's Delve, perhaps) who hails from the Whitefangs? - Why do you think storms swirl around Tor's Fist? What do you think those strange lights are? - Did you once live in a farm valley, or a fortress-monastery? - What drove you to leave? - What behavior in Stonetop is strictly taboo where you once lived? - What practice is common where you once lived, but utterly baffling to folk in Stonetop? - How is (are) the language(s) of the mountain folk different from those of Stonetop? - Do they worship \ \ \ up there? If so, how is that worship different than in Stonetop?

1588.title: Farm valleys
1588.body: The valleys of the Whitefangs can be surprisingly pleasant. Streams flow almost year-round, freezing over but still running throughout winter. Below the tree line, tall skinny conifers grow in abundance. Many of these vales hold steadings.

1589.title: Steading names
1589.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Farm valleys › Steading names
1589.body: Amchatala, Bhatand, Bhungadi, Dhulgad, Dhurankat, Ghumjun, Heledhan, Khutan, Methala, Naungadi, Potyata, Sakokoch, Thulalu, Thundind. Size hamlet ( 25 to 75 souls) or village ( 150 to 350 souls) Population -1 to +1 Prosperity -1 to +1 - Trade with... - ... other farm valleys (food, spouses) - ... mammoth herders (ivory, meat, dark ice , see Dark ice) Resources - Farming: terraced fields, barley, lentils - Livestock: goats, sheep, milk, meat - Fine wool - Timber - Hunting/fishing - Trades: weaving, dyeing, bronze-smithing, etc. Defenses -1 for a hamlet , +0 for a village - Some spears, slings, bows - Some bronze tools/weapons - Protection from a nearby fortress-monastery For each steading, pick or roll what their neighbors say they are. Then pick or roll up to 2 features and up to 3 things that are true of everyone here and/or no one here. Imagine what a functional steading would look like with these traits. "Odd" behaviors make sense to the locals, and likely help the community survive/cohere/adapt. Ask yourself how! 1d12 neighbors say they are… --- --- 1-2 … monstrous/immoral/irresponsible 3-4 … treacherous/greedy/lazy 5-6 … arrogant/brave/reckless 7-8 … boorish/primitive/lazy 9-10 … generous/gullible/friendly 11-12 … wise/noble/holy 1d12 feature --- --- 1 Surroundings are tainted by the Howling Curse 2 A bhoka dwells nearby 3 Homes are made of dark ice ; inhabitants are inured to cold 4 Time passes slower here than outside 5 Gifted spirit-walkers defend the steading in dreams 6 A famous adept or false adept dwells here 7 Entrance is secret/well-defended 8 Fae live within the steading, hiding or in plain sight 9 Useful or valuable flora grows in abundance 10 Gingkara serve and protect the locals 11 Possess a major arcana 12 Weather is warm/pleasant year-round

1590.title: 1d12 everyone here...
1590.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Farm valleys › 1d12 everyone here...
1590.body: - 1 … abides by a caste system - 2 … eats some unappetizing food - 3 … writes wishes/prayers on rags, flapping in the wind - 4 … is accompanied by a clever, loyal pet - 5 … is beautiful/strong/skilled in \ \ /etc. - 6 … lives communally, with no personal possessions/space/family - 7 … reads/studies/debates the teachings of a/the Rime Lord(s) - 8 … refers to (certain?) things/people only indirectly, poetically - 9 … speaks truth, however blunt/unkind - 10 … stops to pray/sing/meditate/dance at specific times each day - 11 … trains as warriors/practices pacifism - 12 … wears/bears a particular type of clothing/accessory/decoration

1591.title: 1d12 no one here...
1591.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Farm valleys › 1d12 no one here...
1591.body: - 1 … bears weapons (or type of weapon) - 2 … consumes meat/alcohol/\ \ \ - 3 … cuts their hair/grooms/bathes - 4 … discusses a particular topic/practice - 5 … eats/sleeps/has sex/ages past a certain point - 6 … fears deep water (they swim, fish, etc.) - 7 … goes into the woods/up the mountain/beyond the vale/etc. - 8 … speaks aloud/inside/to strangers/etc. - 9 … touches another's skin/hair/shadow - 10 … shows their skin/face/hair/etc. - 11 … speaks ill of others/admits hardship - 12 … tolerates a particular color/material

1592.title: Fortress-monasteries
1592.body: At least a dozen fortress-monasteries remain, scattered throughout the Whitefang Mountains. Barrier Pass is one of the largest and most powerful. Each is dedicated to preserving the teachings of the Rime Lords —or at least, preserving their version of them! Size hamlet ( 25 to 75 souls) Population +0 Prosperity +0 - Trade with nearby farm valleys (food, livestock, wool) Resources - Tithes from nearby farm valleys - Terraced gardens (barley, lentils) - Livestock (goats, sheep, milk, meat) - Fine wool - Parchment, vellum, ink - Trades: bronze-smithing, weaving, dyeing, tanning, scribing, etc. - Rime Lord artifacts Defenses +2 - Guardians - At least one adept - One or more tulpas - Weapons/armor of bronze, maybe dark ice - Defensible position A typical fortress-monastery has 25-50 residents, split between monks and guardians , plus a handful of layfolk. Most receive food and material support from a nearby farm valley or two. In turn, guardians patrol the region and monks (ostensibly) guide, teach, and serve as an example for villagers. Place each fortress-monastery in a Rime Lord ruin , and create the adept who presides over the monks there. Referring to the previous page, roll or pick what their neighbors say they are, a feature for the fortress-monastery, plus up to 3 things that are true of everyone here and/or no one here. Finally, pick or roll a trait:

1593.title: 1d12 trait
1593.body: - 1-2 Oppresses/extorts/ignores nearby villages, rather than serving them - 3-4 Recently lost its presiding adept; succession unclear - 5-6 Home to multiple sects, each led by a different adept - 7-8 Hides/protects one of the last thirteen Rime Lords - 9-10 Very small (Population -1, only 10 to 15 souls); roll again with d8 - 11-12 Relatively large ( village , up to 300 residents); roll again with d8 Use the ruin's theme(s), the adept's instinct and moves, and the fortress-monastery's details to inform the philosophy of the local sect(s). Name the sect(s), mixing and matching words from the list below. A fortress-monastery is typically named after its sect or a prominent local feature. Ape, Avalanche, Black, Breath, Bronze, Clear, Closed, Cloud, Crystal, Dancing, Dark, Day, Deep, East, Empty, Eye, Fire, Fist, Five, Flower, Flowing, Four, Frost, Grey, Hand, Heart, Ice, Implacable, Inner, Lake, Lonely, Mind, Mountain, Night, North, Open, Outer, Pale, Palm, Relentless, River, Silent, Single, Sky, Snow, South, Spirit, Spring, Still, Stone, Storm, Stream, Three, Tranquil, Two, Unmoved, Waking, West, White, Will, Wind, Winter

1595.title: Commonly available
1595.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Trade & Barter › Commonly available
1595.body: The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in the settlements of the Whitefang Mountains—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll for Trade & Barter. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Iron, glass, and southern fineries are usually in demand. goods value ------------------------- --------- Fine wool garment 0 ◇◇ bolt 1 cartload ( immobile ) 2 Parchment, a few sheets 1 Ink, vial and quills 1 ◇ Empty book, parchment 1 +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs. livestock value ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Whitefang goat ( sure-footed , curious , wooly ); HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ) Instinct to explore; can butcher for ◇ provisions (6 uses); produces fine wool; sickly in warmer climes 2 Sheep ( timid , hardy , wooly ); HP 3; Damage d4 ( hand ); Instinct to follow the herd; can butcher for ◇ provisions (6 uses) 1 Sheep hound, follower ( keen-nosed , herder , fierce ); HP 6; Damage d6 ( hand , grabby ); Instinct to bully, threaten; Cost care & grooming 1

1596.title: Special items
1596.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Trade & Barter › Special items
1596.body: The following might be acquired in the settlements of the Whitefang Mountains, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. When PCs Trade & Barter in a settlement, they use the local Prosperity (with disadvantage if it's a hamlet). On a 6, they don't mark XP; no one here can/wants to trade—maybe try the next settlement? goods value --- --- Robe/shawl, fine wool, intricately patterned ( warm , beautiful ) 1 Ivory a bit of finery 1 a substantial piece 2 a mammoth tusk ( immobile ) 3 cartload of mammoth tusks 4 Dark ice ( magical , see Rime Lords) a small piece ( magical ) 1 ◇ a large chunk ( magical ) 2 with special characteristics +1 a functional tool or weapon +2 An object of black iron Primordial Powers +2 +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs.

1597.title: Services and favors
1597.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Trade & Barter › Services and favors
1597.body: PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by locals, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price. service/favor value ----------------------------------------------------------------- --------- A few nights of simple food and shelter 0 A day's labor 0 A crew of farmers, working for a week 1 Consultation with a monk 1 Audience with an adept 2 Weeks of guided study with a monk 2 A mountain guide, to lead a trip to... ... a nearby farm-valley 0 ... another known, nearby spot 1 ... a dangerous/unknown spot 2 ... a secret/deadly place 3

1599.title: Hazards
1599.body: - Getting lost (maze of mountains, canyons, passes, and valleys) - Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it - Steep slopes, unstable footing (narrow paths, wet stone, scree, thin waterlogged soil, mud, crumbling rock) - Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes - An unexpected obstacle/hazard: go forward and risk it, or backtrack and lose half a day? - Mudslides and rockslides (page 376)

1600.title: Harsh mountain weather
1600.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Dangers › Harsh mountain weather
1600.body: - Baking sun, dry air - Fog banks/low clouds, blocking vision and soaking everything - Wind: drowning out voices, threatening to toss you aside - Rain and wind rolling up from below - Rain and sleet: pelting you, soaking you, threatening to wash you away - Thunder or lightning: making you flinch/jerk/jolt, triggering rockslides - Snow: hiding a drop, burying a pass, making trails impassable - Brutal cold, leading to hypothermia & frostbite (next page)

1601.title: Avalanche
1601.body: Everyone who lives in these mountains learns to spot the signs. Instinct to gather mass, to bury all in its path - Have one cascade down a distant slope, majestic and terrifying - Point out the snowpack, looming on the slopes above - Note how a storm adds mass to the snowpack - Point out the dimples as sun/warmth undermine its structure When an avalanche starts above them, start the following: - ☐ A chunk of snow gives way - ☐ It gains mass, momentum; it'll be here in a matter of minutes - ☐ It rushes towards them, imminent - ☐ They're buried under snow and ice (d8+2 damage, forceful , ignores armor) - ☐ They're trapped, disoriented, suffering hypothermia & frostbite (see below)

1602.title: Hypothermia & frostbite
1602.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Dangers › Hypothermia & frostbite
1602.body: It gets cold in the mountains. Very, very cold. - Start them shivering, despite fire/warm clothing - Test their endurance on even simple tasks - Have them lose their footing/grip - Inflict a debility - Have frostbite set in (d6 damage, ignores armor) - Have frostbite get worse (+messy) - Compel them to do something foolish (go to sleep, shed clothing, etc.)

1603.title: Altitude sickness
1603.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Dangers › Altitude sickness
1603.body: Dangerously thin air starts a couple thousand feet below the snowline, and gets worse the higher you go. Locals are accustomed to the thin air, but even they must be careful. - Test their endurance to keep going, or just to breathe - Inflict a debility - Reveal that they've missed something - Have them collapse, unable to continue - Fill their lungs with fluid, swell their brains, have them roll for Death's Door Something interesting: Disciples of the Rime Lords practice techniques to thrive in the thin air, but they take months or years to master. Something useful: One can acclimate safely by ascending no more than 1,000 feet each day, and even then, extreme exertion can be dangerous. A tea steeped from purple drakewort can alleviate symptoms, but an overdose can cause death by suffocation.

1604.title: Monsters
1604.body: - Predators: cave bears , cougars , and wolves - Spirits of the wild - Wraiths of those who died of starvation and want, or corrupted by the Howling Curse - Tulpas , free-roaming or bound to something nearby

1605.title: People
1605.body: - Guardians, monks, or novices from Barrier Pass - An adept - A false adept , or their minions

1606.title: Bhoka
1606.body: Solitary , large , cautious , magical , corrupted HP 16; Armor 1 (hide) Damage talons 1d10+3 ( hand , close , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities blinded by full daylight or holy light, takes +1d4 damage from bronze Instinct see below - Slink through trees and shadows These hulking brutes dwell in dark places: caves, north-facing slopes, and deep mountain forests. They're hairy things, hunched and wild, only vaguely looking like people. They'd be little more than dangerous beasts, easily avoided, if they weren't driven by an obsessive vice—and if they didn't wield the wild elements themselves. Pick or roll for the bhoka's instinct and the associated moves.

1607.title: 1d6 instinct/moves
1607.body: - 1 Instinct to bask in the fear/adoration of others - Bellow like thunder, terrifying and awesome - Fling lightning, 1d10+3 damage ( far , forceful , loud , reload ) - 2 Instinct to steal finery, companionship, and precious things - Skulk about a steading, choosing its targets - Turn themselves (and anything they take) into flowing mist - 3 Instinct to sate its never-ending hunger - Chomp off a limb and gulp it down, 1d10+5 damage ( hand , messy , 3 piercing) - Create a maelstrom of wind, sucking everything nearby towards its gaping maw ( near , area , reload ) - 4 Instinct to lash out and avenge itself for even the slightest offence - Snatch someone and fling them against a wall/tree/ally - Transform into a swirling cloud of wind, hail, sleet, and lightning; 1d10+3 damage ( area , reach , forceful , reload ) - 5 Instinct to be lazy, to force others into servitude - Drag someone off into the night - Freeze things: flesh, moisture, the very air - 6 Roll again twice Also, pick or roll one:

1608.title: 1d6 special trait
1608.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › Bhoka › 1d6 special trait
1608.body: - 1 Huge (+4 HP, +2 damage) - 2 Four or even six arms (+ area to its damage) - 3 Lashing tongue/tail, 1d8+1 damage ( reach , grabby ) - 4 Scales of ice/rock (Armor 3) - 5 Wings (can fly) - 6 Regenerates wounds, unless from ritually prepared weapons Something interesting: The people of the Whitefangs fear bhoka and are well aware of the dangers they pose, but they never hunt them. They seem content to drive them off or appease them. Something useful: The monks claim that bhoka descended from Rime Lords who were corrupted by the Things Below . They cannot abide the sound of the Rime Lords' meditative chants, recoiling and fleeing from anyone who performs such a chant without faltering.

1609.title: Guardian
1609.body: Group , organized , hardy , skilled HP 10; Armor 2 (hides, shields) Damage bronze or dark ice-tipped weapons 1d8 (tags by weapon) Instinct to protect their charges - Use the terrain to their advantage - Take a blow meant for another - Perform a feat of endurance, agility, or will ( reload ) The fortress-monasteries of the Whitefangs each maintain a cadre of warriors, recruited from local farm valleys, bands of mammoth herders, or failed novices. They focus more on the arts of battle than the esoteric ways of the monks.

1610.title: Ice drake
1610.body: Horde , small , cautious , stealthy HP 3; Armor 0 Damage claws, beak, or wing-buffet d4 ( hand ) Special qualities cold-proof, camouflaged, winged Instinct to spook easily and overreact - Burrow/dig into snow or ice, to forage or hide - Shake off a blast of clinging, freezing mist, 1d4 damage ( close , area , reload ) - Scatter and flee to the skies Like cranes, long-legged and long-necked, with long whipping tails. They're hardly a threat in warmer months. But in winter when their scales and feathers grow laden with hoarfrost—shepherds watch for their sign and avoid them. Wander into their midst, and you'll get coated in a clinging, frozen mist that often leads to hypothermia & frostbite . Ice drake fat and blood never freezes. Properly rendered in a kitchen, it makes an oil used to insulate leather and wool garments. A flock of 8-12 drakes yields a ◇ jar of ice drake oil (Value 1 locally, Value 3 in the south).

1611.title: The Howling Curse Threat (affliction)
1611.breadcrumb: The Whitefang Mountains › The Howling Curse Threat (affliction)
1611.body: Instinct to make people into monsters In the Time of Cataclysm , the voice of Y'aaw'kara plunged the north into madness. Whole villages turned against each other. The survivors were reduced to marauding beasts. The Rime Lords sacrificed themselves to contain the Howling Curse, but it lingers in places where cruelty and violence have caused great suffering. Places tainted by the Howling Curse are corrupted sites , hungry places or worse, full of whispers and visions (see The Things Below) that push people towards bloodshed, cruelty, and savagery. People tainted by the Howling Curse carry such whispers and visions with them. When you give in to the bloodlust of the Howling Curse , pick up to 3 options that are not yet permanent: - ☐ Your fingers grow long, your grip grows terribly strong ( grabby , forceful ) - ☐ You grow stringy and gaunt, and revel in pain; gain +1 armor - ☐ You hunch and giggle; gain +4 HP (even above your max), and your instinct becomes "Cruelty: to hurt others." - ☐ Your eyes turn feral; you can see in the dark, but red is the only color you see - ☐ Your jaw distends, you grow fangs ( hand , messy ), and you gain a taste for blood and raw meat When the violence comes to an end , roll +1 for each option you have chosen. If you wrought great carnage, roll with advantage. On a 10+ , the options you chose are permanent, and you are now tainted by the Howling Curse (if you weren't already); on a 7-9 , the options you chose linger until you vent your rage on a person or thing of your choice; on a 6- , don't mark XP, and both the rage and the options you chose fade as you catch your breath—only nightmares will remain. When all five options above are permanent, you become a kyakaara (next page) and cease to be a PC. Tell the GM to write you up as a monster and as a threat.

1612.title: Kyakaara
1612.body: Horde , hardy , organized , fearless , sadistic , terrifying , corrupted HP 10; Armor 1 (toughness) Damage rending fingers/claws d4+2 w/ advantage ( hand , grabby , forceful , messy , 1 piercing), or crude weapons d6 w/ advantage (tags by weapon) Special qualities inured to cold, take +1d4 damage from bronze, see only in shades of red Instinct to delight in pain, savor fear, and indulge in every cruelty - Drive their prey to panic - Maim or traumatize someone - Surround, harry, dogpile, drag down - Drag off a captive, to eat or torture These are the Howlers, the Ones-Who-Laugh, those who have succumbed to the Howling Curse (previous page). They are gaunt and hairy, dressed in rags, with jaws that have grown into snarling, fang-filled snouts. They pierce their own flesh. They hunch and howl and cackle. During the Time of Cataclysm , kyakaara roamed freely; now they are thankfully rare. Treat them as a threat, beasts, with the following grim portents and impending doom. - ☐ They take up residence near a community and stalk likely victims - ☐ They kidnap locals, forcing them to commit atrocities upon each other - ☐ Surviving captives become kyakaara; their numbers swell - ☐ Impending doom : They overrun the community, slaughter many, corrupt the rest, then set out towards the next village

1613.title: Monk
1613.body: Group , cautious , disciplined , hardy , magical HP 10; Armor 0 Damage staff d8 with disadvantage ( close ) Special qualities spirit sight; inured to hunger, cold, deprivation Instinct to preserve their version of the Rime Lords' teaching - Demonstrate their willpower - Take an extreme, unyielding position/stance/course of action - Do something impossible, in line with their sect's teachings Consider creating custom instincts and moves for each group of monks, based on the teachings and practices of the fortress-monastery and sect from which they hail The Whitefang Mountains.

1614.title: Novice
1614.body: Group , cautious , disciplined , meek HP 6; Armor 0 Damage staff d8 with disadvantage ( close ) Special qualities inconsistent spirit sight Instinct to veer between their masters' teachings and their base needs - Do what their superiors ask - Hesitate, balk, flinch - Show great promise, or great weakness

1615.title: Mountain ape
1615.body: Group , cautious , stealthy , hoarder HP 6; Armor 1 (thick fur, hide) Damage grasping hands d8+2 ( reach , close , grabby , forceful ) Special qualities arboreal, cold proof, keen senses of smell and hearing Instinct to keep their secrets - Hide in the trees or snowdrifts - Try to lure/spook intruders away - Reach out, snatch an intruder by surprise - Crush/rend/break a grabbed foe, d8+2 damage w/advantage ( hand , forceful , messy , 1 piercing) Like orangutans but taller, more powerfully built, with thick, shaggy white hair. They dwell in small bands, in Rime Lord ruins or sacred sites that are far from any well-trod trails. They venture out to hunt and gather, but avoid people as much as they can. Each band of mountain apes collects things: a particular type of plant or flower, the bones of certain animals, bits of writing, dark ice , etc. They keep these hoards in a central place, hidden away, revered and treasured even more than their own children or their lives. Particularly large or well-established bands will have an alpha ( large , leader , +6 HP, +1 damage) who does nothing but protect the hoard.

1616.title: Shantyara
1616.body: Solitary , emotionless , clever , stealthy , hardy , skilled , terrifying HP 18; Armor 2 (thick hides/furs, skill) Damage orichalcum sword d10+2 w/advantage ( close , messy , 2 piercing) or bronze knife d10 w/advantage ( hand , 1 piercing) Special qualities spirit-sight; immune to mental manipulation; inured to cold, hunger, pain, deprivation; hard to surprise Instinct to cleanse the world of the Howling Curse - Stalk a suspect with perfect patience - Condemn the tainted for all to hear - Try to avoid bloodshed for the untainted - Mercilessly cut down the condemned, and any who protect them - Deftly counter an attack they see coming Only a handful of these "tranquil killers" remain, three or four at most. They wander the Whitefangs, hunting kyakaara and anyone else corrupted by the Howling Curse . They are welcome in any settlement, but that welcome is often stilted, formal, fraught. Few are comfortable under a shantyara's piercing gaze. Shantyara recruit apprentices from orphaned or unwanted children. Many flee from the harsh, interminable training. Most of the others die from exposure or deprivation or one of the many dangers such a life entails. A rare few last long enough to be useful companions ( HP 10; Armor 1; Damage d8; Instinct to earn their master's respect). And once or twice every generation, a master will deem an apprentice worthy, and take them to a sacred cave, and burden them with one of the shantyara's orichalcum blades.

1617.title: Ages of the World
1617.body: Most events are listed in the order they occurred, but events in italics and starting with ? are approximate, their specific placement unclear. Did construction begin on the Makers' Roads before or after the Tempest Lords' capital sank? Did Brennan become Marshedge's marshal before or after the Forest Folk disappeared? That's for you and your group to decide, if it matters.

1618.title: The Primordial Age
1618.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › The Primordial Age
1618.body: Eons ago, over a span beyond reckoning. - Light fills the void; the Things Below were already there - First Creators become aware of themselves - ? The first of the great spirits arise - Things Below stoke the Creators' egos, urge war - First Creators forge the world and trap the Things Below inside - Tcaventes, Shackle and Key , is set to guard the Utterwell - ? The gods (page 178\) do many legendary things - ? The earliest peoples evolve or are created

1619.title: Time of the Makers
1619.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › Time of the Makers
1619.body: Millennia ago, over a span of centuries (at least). - Rise of the Tempest Lords ' civilization Tempest Lords - Tempest Lords establish their empire - Rise of other Maker groups The Makers - Tempest Lords found "Northernmost" (the Ruins at Road's End ) - Tempest Lord homeland sinks, empire crumbles; other Makers grow in power - ? Construction of the Makers' Roads begins (page 270) - ? Green Lords breed the Fae - ? Manmarchers come from across the Dread River - Stormcatcher establishes her Tower in the Flats - Fae rebellion! the Green Lords call on the Things Below - Stone Lords destroy the heart of the Green Lord's territory, creating what is now Ferrier's Fen - Siege of Vor Svetelik ; Green Lords retreat, become Fomoraij - Stone Lords' servants start to plot rebellion and embrace the Things Below Were the civilizations of the South founded during the Time of the Makers, or even before? Or perhaps during the Time of the Barrow Builders?

1620.title: Time of Cataclysm
1620.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › Time of Cataclysm
1620.body: Centuries ago, over a span of decades. - Stone Lords' servants rebel, unleash the Things Below, sink Dulenn and create Blackwater Lake - ? "Northernmost" overrun by horrors from the Dread River - Stone Lords hunted down by rebels - Teirstered falls, becomes Three Coven Lake - Refugees start heading to Stormcatcher's tower - ? Howling Curse causes Rime Lord civilization to nearly collapse - ? Volcanic eruptions, Moratia Rubra destroyed, Forge Lord society collapses Forge Lords - More refugees arrive at Stormcatcher's tower - Surviving Rime Lords sacrifice themselves to contain the Howling Curse - Stormcatcher's tower falls

1621.title: Time of the Barrow Builders
1621.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › Time of the Barrow Builders
1621.body: Centuries ago, over a span of generations. - First barrows built to honor freedom fighters - Emanations of the Things Below roam freely, are worshipped/appeased - Warlords/sorcerer-kings vie for power, build more elaborate barrows - Battles fought over Three Coven Lake, the Ruins at Road's End, etc. - Refugees, enslavement, human sacrifice, all-around bad times - Subjects of the Barrow Builders overthrow/abandon/imprison their tyrannical rulers - Certain bands of Barrow Builders flee into the Labyrinth

1622.title: The Current Age
1622.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › The Current Age
1622.body: Starting a few centuries ago. - Power of the Things Below ebbs (reasons unclear) - Barrow Builder culture gives rise to Hillfolk culture Hillfolk - ? Marshedge founded - ? Stonetop founded - Region stabilizes, trade routes established - Gordin's Delve settled, Foundry established Gordin’s Delve

1623.title: Recent Memory
1623.breadcrumb: Ages of the World › Recent Memory
1623.body: The last decade or so. - ? Brennan and the Claws start raiding - Earthquakes become more common - Forest Folk disappear - ? Brennan hired as Marshedge's marshal - Crinwin , nailadd , etc. grow in numbers

1624.title: Artifact Creation
1624.body: Use this procedure when you need an artifact, but none of the existing options fit. Or, use it when you have an idea that you want to flesh out. As you work through each step, take notes on your decisions or results.

1625.title: 1. Origin and theme
1625.body: Pick or roll for the artifact's origin. Then, pick or roll for one or more themes from the indicated entry. Allow the theme(s) to inform everything else about this artifact.

1626.title: 1d12 origin
1626.body: - 1 The Barrow Builders - 2 Death and the undying - 3 The Fae - 4 The Forge Lords or the Ustrina \ - 5 Gods and religion, roll 1d12: - 1-2 Aratis - 3-4 Danu - 5-6 Helior - 7-8 Tor - 9-12 Other gods - 6 The Green Lords or the Fomoraij \ - 7 Primordial powers - 8 The Rime Lords - 9 Spirits of the wild or the Forest Folk \ - 10 The Stone Lords - 11 The Tempest Lords - 12 The Things Below <sup \ </sup Use the theme table from the first listed entry. For example, use the Forge Lords themes for the Ustrina.

1627.title: 2. Nature
1627.body: Pick one or roll, then proceed to the indicated sub-step and follow the directions there.

1628.title: 1d12 nature
1628.body: - 1-3 It's a mundane item, relic, or artifact (next column, step 2a) - 4-6 It's an object or amount of strange material , step 2b) - 7 It's priceless step 2c) - 8 It has an extraordinary property step 2d) - 9 It houses a spirit or sentience step 2e) - 10-11 It's a source of lore step 2f ) - 12 It produces a magical effect step 2g)

1629.title: 2a. Mundane
1629.body: It's no magical wonder, but it's still interesting, useful, and/or valuable. Pick or roll for why they might care.

1630.title: 1d12 why they might care
1630.breadcrumb: Artifact Creation › 1d12 why they might care
1630.body: - 1 It'd make a good trophy/gift/thing to show the kids (Value 0) - 2 It's useful—like a tool, a weapon, supplies, etc. (Value 0 or 1) - 3 It's useful but also unusual, specialized, uncommon (Value 1) - 4 It's fine, beautiful, excellent (Value 1) - 5 It's of a distinct origin/style (Value 1 or 2 to a collector) - 6 It's inherently valuable—silver, trade goods, etc. (Value 2) - 7 It's a true luxury and/or a symbol of status (Value 2) - 8 It's precious, but only to the right buyer (Value 2 or 3) - 9 It's exquisitely made, a thing of beauty (Value 3) - 10 It's inherently valuable—gold, gems, exotic goods, etc. (Value 3) - 11 It's a clue, or it hints at the bigger picture, and roll 1d10 again - 12 It presents an opportunity: a key, a resource they might mine, a map, etc. Now, go pick or roll for its form step 3).

1631.title: 2b. Strange material
1631.body: Pick one or roll, and follow the instructions for that entry.

1632.title: 1d12 material
1632.body: - 1-2 Makerglass , most associated with the Stone Lords; pick or roll for its form and characteristic Various treasures, then proceed to synthesis step 4) - 3-4 Aetherium , found near Tempest Lord sites like the Ruined Tower ; pick or roll for its form and characteristic Tempest Lords, then proceed to synthesis step 4) - 5 Orichalcum , most associated with the Forge Lords; pick or roll for its variety Forge Lords, then proceed to synthesis step 4) - 6-7 Dark ice , created by the Rime Lords and their disciples; pick or roll for its form and characteristic Dark ice, then proceed to synthesis , step 4) - 8 Black iron , most associated with primordial powers and Aratis ; pick or roll for its form Primordial Powers, then proceed to synthesis , step 4) - 9-10 Red crystal , a manifestation of the Things Below; pick or roll on the next table, and do what it says - 11-12 Redwood , a natural tether for spirits of the wild often used by the Forest Folk ; pick or roll on the next table, and do what it says

1633.title: 1d12 red crystal/redwood
1633.body: - 1-4 Full of untapped potential (Value 1 or 2 to the right buyer); proceed to form , step 3) - 5-7 Has an extraordinary property (next page, step 2d) - 8-10 Houses a spirit or sentience step 2e) - 11-12 Produces a magical effect step 2g)

1634.title: 2c. Priceless
1634.body: What makes it so much more valuable than its form would dictate? Pick one or roll.

1635.title: 1d12 priceless because...
1635.body: - 1 Causes those who see it to covet it (Value 4) - 2 Great historical/religious importance (Value 4 to the right buyer) - 3 Only a few (1d12?) were ever made, if you trust the tales (Value 4 to the right buyer) - 4 Haunting beauty (Value 4) - 5-6 Beyond meager mortal arts (Value 4) - 7-8 Made of or with precious metals/gems/materials (Value 4) - 9 Transcendent, inspires those who see it (Value 4) - 10 Symbol of authority over a people (Value 5 to the right buyer) - 11 It has some other property that makes it Value 4 or 5 - 12 It's a hoard of various treasures (Value 5, immobile ); pick or roll 1d6 for form , step 3) as many times as you like Still here? Go pick or roll for its form step 3).

1636.title: 2d. Extraordinary property
1636.body: Pick or roll one. Remember to keep the theme in mind!

1637.title: 1d12 property
1637.body: - 1 Moves and acts of its own accord - 2 Absorbs \ \ \ , stores its power (fire, lightning, hatred, stillness, etc.) - 3 Seamlessly blends materials in an impossible way - 4 Emits \ \ \ (flame, light, sound, silence, a feeling, etc.) - 5 Is much, much \ \ \ than it should be (heavier, harder, faster, quieter, etc.) - 6 Cannot be \ \ \ (broken, burned, melted, detected, etc.) - 7 Fills those who look upon it with \ \ \ (greed, rage, peace, joy, etc.) - 8 Cuts right through/can harm \ \ \ (stone, ghosts, etc.) - 9 Can only/cannot be perceived by \ \ \ (Fae, spirits, the dead, murderers, etc.) - 10 Glows/thrums/vibrates in the presence of \ \ \ (gold, magic, lies, poison, etc.) - 11 Protects against/repels \ \ \ (emotional magic, fire/heat, ghosts, etc.) - 12 Roll twice, but treat one of the results as a drawback or flaw Artifacts with extraordinary properties might also have an additional special characteristic. Pick or roll:

1638.title: 1d12 extra characteristic?
1638.body: - 1-6 Nope, go pick or roll for its form step 3) - 7-8 It houses a spirit or sentience step 2e) - 9-10 It's a source of lore step 2f ) - 11-12 It produces a magical effect . Appendix B - Artifact Creation step 2g)

1639.title: 2e. Spirit or sentience
1639.body: The artifact serves as a tether for a spirit, or otherwise acts as its home. Pick or roll for the nature of this binding.

1640.title: 1d12 type of binding
1640.body: - 1-2 Imprisoned and locked away (though imperfectly) - 3-5 Fettered, enslaved, forced to serve - 6-7 Serves willingly, bound by its own nature/desires - 8-9 Trapped by accident, desperation, twist of fate - 10-12 Dwells here naturally, spontaneously Then, pick a spirit or sentience appropriate to the binding, origin, and theme. If you're unsure, roll 1d12.

1641.title: 1d12 spirit/sentience
1641.body: - 1 A corrupted spirit The Things Below, and roll again - 2 A ghost - 3 A shade or similar imprint of someone's personality - 4 Something from beyond the Last Door, like a dool spirit or gwyllgi - 5 A Fae , oddly enough - 6 A little god - 7 A tulpa , or the intellect of a construct - 8 A spirit of the wild - 9 An emanation of a Thing Below The Things Below - 10 A fledgling spirit, and roll 1d8+1 again - 11 An archon or other primordial entity - 12 A multitude of... (roll again) Now, go pick or roll for it's form step 3).

1642.title: 2f. Source of lore
1642.body: Pick or roll for the knowledge imparted and how it's recorded.

1643.title: 1d12 knowledge imparted
1643.body: - 1 The truth/secrets behind a group or figure of legend - 2 The weakness/history/origins/lair of a major established threat - 3-4 The hazards/secrets/history/location of a mysterious or legendary place - 5-6 The workings/secrets/history/location of a magical item or arcanum - 7-9 An artifice or technique lost to antiquity - 10-12 The workings of a ritual/spell—pick or roll for its function and drawback Appendix B - Artifact Creation, then return here

1644.title: 1d12 how it's recorded
1644.body: - 1 Known to an entity bound to the artifact; go back to spirit or sentience (previous page, step 2e), and do what it says - 2-5 Inscribed on/in the artifact: ancient runes or writing in a long-dead language - 6-7 Adorning the artifact: carvings/paintings/decorations/diagrams - 8-9 Scrawled on/in the artifact: a cypher, mad ramblings, strange annotations, etc. - 10 As a vision/dream, imparted on whoever touches/enters/approaches - 11-12 Inscribed on/in the artifact: clear to those who can read the language Now, go pick or roll for it's form step 3).

1645.title: 2g. Magical effect
1645.body: ^magical-effect-2g Pick or roll for the magic's function (what it does) as well as its drawback. Combine them with its origin and theme to imagine what spell, power, blessing, or curse is involved. 1d12 function ------ --------------------------- 1 Create/conjure/craft 2 Defend/repel/secure 3 Destroy/slay/consume 4 Contain/bind/capture 5 Enhance/refine/purify 6 Reduce/diminish/suppress 7 Sustain/heal/repair 8 Sense/identify/reveal 9 Hide/disguise/confuse 10 Manipulate/control/compel 11 Transform/combine/reshape 12 Roll twice and combine

1646.title: 1d12 drawback
1646.body: - 1 Scarring: leaves a lasting thematic mark on you/the target - 2 Dangerous: can have destructive, unwanted side effects - 3 Demanding: takes great effort to cast/trigger/maintain - 4 Discordant: draws unwanted attention - 5 Fickle: fails at inopportune times - 6 Weak: easily countered by will/fortitude/preparation - 7 Indiscriminate: imprecise in its targets/duration/effect - 8 Withering: weakens you/the artifact/the target - 9 Restricted: works only in specific, thematic conditions - 10 Costly: requires sacrifice, consumes resources - 11 Slow: takes time to cast/manifest - 12 Roll twice For artifacts that produce a magical effect (as opposed to spells or rituals), how often or frequently can it be used? Pick or roll.

1647.title: 1d12 usage
1647.body: - 1-3 1d6 uses, then it's consumed/ruined/powerless - 4-6 1d6 uses, then it must be recharged/replenished - 7-8 A few charges, which might be lost with each use; recharges with time or effort - 9 At will, but each time is riskier/less powerful/more costly; resets with effort or slowly over time - 10 At will, but there's a cost/risk with each use - 11 At will, though limited by practicalities - 12 Continuous; it's always going Now, go to step 3 , below, to pick or roll for its form .

1648.title: 3. Form
1648.body: If it's not already established or clear to you, pick or roll for the artifact's size.

1649.title: 1d12 size
1649.body: - 1-2 A small item - 3-4 A ◇ item or object - 5-6 A ◇◇ item or object - 7-8 An immobile object or place - 9-10 Roll 1d8 again, but it's hard to extract, access, and/or take advantage of - 11-12 Roll 1d10 again, but it's also crude , fragile , cumbersome , and/or dangerous Now, what is it, exactly? Pick or roll. Interpret the results liberally, keeping in mind the origin, theme, and other details about the item. Let yourself be inspired! Note: an artifact might very well be made from or with an appropriate strange material , step 2b), even if you didn't get that result. Maybe your Forge Lord sword that absorbs heat and flame is made of aetherium, or has a red crystal for its pommel.

1650.title: 1d12 what is it?
1650.body: - 1-8 Something more common (next page) - 9-12 Something less common (next page)

1651.title: 1d12 more common
1651.body: - 1 Agriculture: seed, spade, flail, hoe, fork, scythe, plow, millstone, etc. - 2 Construction: nail, brick, tile, trowel, timber, masonry, wall, etc. - 3 Container: lock/key, pouch, box, urn, chest, cell, vault, etc. - 4 Domestic: utensil, spindle, vessel, broom, foodstuffs, crib, loom, etc. - 5 Fire: incense, oil, candle, lamp, lantern, sconce, brazier, hearth, etc. - 6 Liquid: oil, spirits, vial, wineskin, cup, jug, barrel, fount, bath, etc. - 7 Protection: talisman, helm, cloak, armor, shield, door, wall, etc. - 8 Remains: ashes, scale, fang, horn, organ, bone, hide, skeleton, etc. - 9 Textile: fibers, scarf, footwear, rope, clothes, furs, bedding, tapestry, etc. - 10 Tool: needle, file, chisel, saw, mallet, shovel, anvil, workspace, etc. - 11 Trade: coin, coins, satchel, scales, wheel, sled, cart, wagon, etc. - 12 Violence: knife, poison, sword, rod, staff, bow, spear, banner, etc. For example, if you started with "a ◇◇ item or object" and then got a 4 on the table above, that could be a quern stone or a small cauldron. But "an immobile object or place" plus a roll of 8 above might a mammoth tusk, or a whole ossuary!

1652.title: 1d12 less common
1652.body: - 1 Art: pigments, brushes, carving, figurine, chisel, painting, statuary, etc. - 2 Beauty: cosmetics, jewelry, comb, torc, circlet, mirror, robes, regalia, etc. - 3 Curiosity: wonder, device, enigma, doohickey, toy, etc. - 4 Husbandry: collar, brush, prod, bridle, saddle, harness, stable, etc. - 5 Raw material: powder, salt, chemics, grains, ingot, ore, slab, timber, etc. - 6 Music: notation, bell, chime, flute, rattle, horn, lyre, lute, drum, etc. - 7 Natural: flower, feather, shell, rock, wood, tree, spring, cave, etc. - 8 Prosthesis: eye, teeth, hand, foot, arm, leg, crutch, wheelchair, etc. - 9 Religion: symbol, offering, idol, altar, ceremonial \ \ \ (roll again) - 10 Specialized equipment: astrolabe, level, gear, decanter, still, kiln, etc. - 11 Writing: ink, runes, stylus, scroll, parchment, tablet, codex, archive, etc. - 12 Highly decorated and/or exquisitely made more common object (previous column) Note: an artifact might very well be made from or with an appropriate strange material , step 2b), even if you didn't get that result. Maybe your Forge Lord sword that absorbs heat and flame is made of aetherium, or has a red crystal for its pommel. For example, if you started with "a small item" and rolled a 10 on this table, you might see "decanter" and think of a little vial of perfume or maybe poison. And maybe it's the liquid that's special, not the container!

1653.title: 4. Synthesis
1653.body: Consider the details you've established. Imagine the artifact that they suggest. Fill in any missing details and start making final decisions. What is this artifact, exactly? What was it originally for? How did it come to be, or be here ? You don't need a full history of the item. But try to picture something that makes sense, something that at least hints at a story. As you make these decisions, it's okay to change or discard some of the details you established earlier, or to interpret them loosely or metaphorically. They're meant to spur your creativity, not to limit it.

1654.title: 5. Write-up
1654.body: Once you've got a clear picture in your mind, write up the artifact as one of the following: - A brief description and some tags, like 20 words or fewer - A detailed description , with a title, some tags, and a few sentences about what it is, what it looks like, what it does (if anything), what it involves - A brief or detailed description (as above), plus one or more custom player moves , each with a trigger, results, and maybe a roll - A steading improvement , something that, with time and effort, can provide lasting benefit to the village. - A minor arcanum , a little card you give the player with a description and requirements on the front, and the benefits of meeting them on the back. - A major arcanum : a half-page playbook insert, with unlockable moves/options and consequences for using them. Keep in mind who you're writing this up for. Anything you give to the players (tags, physical descriptions, custom moves, arcana) should be clear and concise. But an artifact's history, backstory, details on where it's found, the nuances of how it works… that's all for you, to inform how you introduce it and use it in play. Write out as much as you, personally, find helpful. If the artifact's Value hasn't been established, consider whether it should have one and what it might be. See Book I for rough guidelines. If you expect the artifact will be a source of trouble, also write it up as a threat (a MacGuffin , probably, or maybe a magical entity or even an affliction ). Refer to the artifacts throughout this book for examples. See Book I for more guidance and some worked examples.

1655.title: Minor Arcana
1655.body: [!MINOR ARCANUM] An old scroll case ◇, fragile A leather scroll case, cracked and age-worn. Inside is a tattered letter and a pair of small, bluish ingots. It's a letter, in an older Hillfolk dialect, from an apprentice to their master. It describes a mental exercise to draw latent power from aetherium. You can learn Laoj Daveth's Galvanic Infusion from the scroll, but… - …you must translate the letter. - …it'll take a few weeks of dedicated study. - …you'll use up one of the aetherium ingots (or another source of aetherium). - …you risk electrocuting yourself and/or using up the other ingot. ^an-old-scroll-case [!MINOR ARCANUM] Laoj Daveth's Galvanic Infusion When you touch a piece of aetherium and draw its power into yourself , roll +CON: on a 7+ , you absorb the power and gain 1d8 HP (even if this puts you above your max HP); but on a 7-9 , the aetherium is drained and crumbles into a chalky mass of verdigris. The spell lasts until you dismiss it or until you roll a 6- on any move. While it lasts, you need not sleep and remain highly alert and focused—gain advantage on any rolls with WIS. If you remain awake for more than a few days, your body grows ever more exhausted and you risk hallucination, paranoia, and addiction. When the spell ends, you lose 1d8 HP. ^laoj-daveths-galvanic-infusion [!MINOR ARCANUM] A folktale There's this old tale, about an old herb tender from Marshedge that tricks a fen-troll into eating an enchanted seedpod. She promises to go get the troll more food if he promises to then let her go. Of course, the troll breaks his word and tries to eat her, but the seedpod bursts into a mass of gwir-nettles when he does. You can learn the secret of the enchanted seedpods, but need one of the following: - A Marshedge herbalist, steeped in lore, to teach you the ritual - The spirit of a gwir-nettle, friendly or bound, to reveal the secrets of its birth ^a-folktale [!MINOR ARCANUM] Truth Seeds When you soak a pair of gwir-nettle seedpods in your own blood, whisper secrets over them, and say the words of power , they become truth seeds ( magical ). When you and another each eat a truth seed and make promises to each other , the seeds stay in your guts and ensure that the promises are kept. When you break your promise , the truth seed in your gut sprouts violently into a full-grown gwir-nettle, dealing 1d10+3 damage with advantage ( messy , ignores armor) and now you've got a gwir-nettle growing out of you. If the other party breaks their promise , they suffer the same fate. ^truth-seeds [!MINOR ARCANUM] A small, strange book fragile A small manual written on what appears to be sheets of supple leaves. It is written in the common script but in a strangely poetic sequence of nonsense syllables. The manual reveals the recipe for an elderberry wine that brims with Fae magic, but to learn the recipe, you either... …need help from a Fae poet, or perhaps one of the Forest Folk; or... …risk getting the recipe wrong, poisoning or cursing whoever drinks the wine you brew. ^a-small-strange-book [!MINOR ARCANUM] Old Mother's Wine Lost memories: ○○○○○○ When you sacrifice a memory to a berry-laden elder tree , tell us what you gave up. Mark 1 lost memory and roll 1d6; if you roll less than your lost memories, change your instinct and clear your marks. When you harvest the berries and spend a season making wine from them , gain a skin of Old Mother's Wine (5 uses, magical ). When you get tipsy on Old Mother's Wine , expend a use. For a night and a day, your eyes are open to Fae magic, meaning: - You see the truth behind Fae illusions and glamours - You are immune to Fae enchantments - You can freely enter Fae domains and walk the Fae paths - Time passes normally for you while in Fae domains ^old-mothers-wine [!MINOR ARCANUM] A giant's dormitory magical In a ruin of the Stone Lords is an old dwelling that bears the psychic imprint of its inhabitant, a stoneshaper of some considerable skill. When you enter the dormitory , you are overcome with a vision of this student inventing his first spell. You can learn this spell, but… - …you must have some talent for magic. - …it will take weeks of repeated exposure and study. - …you risk lasting harm to your physique and health. ^a-giants-dormitory [!MINOR ARCANUM] Aalz Galt's Sudden Sinkhole When you sing a rumbling note and focus your will upon a point of earth or stone , mark a debility and roll +INT: On a 10+ , the earth or stone that you focus on remains intact, but the earth or stone beneath it crumbles, dissolves, and flows away, creating an empty pocket about as wide as your arms can encircle and about as deep as you are tall. The surface seems stable, but is prone to crack or break under stress. On a 7-9 , the song works as above, but it siphons even more energy from your body. Mark another debility and take 2d4 damage (ignores armor). ^aalz-galts-sudden-sinkhole [!MINOR ARCANUM] Carvings in a cave At the bottom of a sinkhole is a limestone cave. A moldering skeleton slumps against one wall, showing signs of having been hanged. Yet carved into the cave walls are runes, caked over with mineral deposits. You can study the runes and learn the secrets they contain, but... - …you must carefully restore the runes over a few weeks of work. - …you risk ruining them as you work. - …you must decipher the old writings, which are similar to Stone Lord runes. - …it will then take a few days of study. ^carvings-in-a-cave [!MINOR ARCANUM] Shell Game of Souls Souls (max CON): ○○○ When you touch a dying, intelligent being, speaking their name and words of binding , roll +INT: on a 10+ , you bind their soul to your flesh; on a 7-9 , you bind their soul but weaken yours—reduce your max HP by 1d4+1. Your flesh can bind a number of souls equal to your CON. They manifest in dreams and struggle for control when your mind or body are weak. Write their names here: When you roll a 6- on Death's Door , you can release a bound soul to go through the Last Door in your place. If you do, you survive as if you rolled a 7-9. Your body, though, still bears the marks of that which would have killed you. ^shell-game-of-souls [!MINOR ARCANUM] A half-buried plaque ◇◇, cumbersome , magical A bronze plate poking out of the soil, somewhere in the Flats or near the Dread River. It's dented, a bit warped, with Maker-runes and esoteric diagrams etched into the surface. How the heck did it end up here? The plate contains the workings of a spell, which you can learn but… - …you must first dig up & clean the plate. - …you must decipher the Maker-runes. - …it'll take a few weeks of study. - …you risk harm to your voice, your hearing, and/or your neighbors as you practice. ^a-half-buried-plaque [!MINOR ARCANUM] Thunderous Bellow When you channel the storm primeval and utter a thunderous bellow , take 1d6 damage (ignores armor) and roll +CON: on a 10+ , everyone nearby (except you) must choose 2; on a 7-9 , everyone nearby (except you) must choose 1. - Drop what they're carrying and cover their ears - Be deafened and disoriented for a few moments - Stagger a few steps away from you ^thunderous-bellow [!MINOR ARCANUM] Runes around a ruined hall magical In a ruined citadel of the Forge Lords, the grand hall lies empty. The throne is melted slag. The ceiling is shattered. The pillars are scorched and cracked. But intact around the outside of the room are a series of flowing runes. Close study reveals them to involve both fire and privacy. The runes repeat themselves and can be copied, but... - …it takes a few hours of careful work. - …you risk a transcription error, but to what effect? ^runes-around-a-ruined-hall [!MINOR ARCANUM] The Fiery Veil When you precisely inscribe the flowing runes of the fiery veil along the outside of an area , the area is warded for as long as the runes remain intact. When anyone tries to scry on the warded area from afar, or view it from the spirit realm , they see only a curtain of fire. If they insist on peering through the curtain, they are burned for 1d10 damage (advantage, ignores armor) and appear to those inside as a ghostly image wreathed in flames. ^the-fiery-veil [!MINOR ARCANUM] A grim peat mound Not far from Marshedge, a ways into Ferrier's Fen, lies a peat mound where no birds nest and flowers will not bloom. Since the town's founding, the people of Marshedge have buried certain criminals here, alive. Over the years, the land has birthed a spirit that hungers for the dying breaths of the condemned ( Book II ). It is young and eager, and can be set to work upon criminals, but only if you... - …find a way to communicate with it. - …convince it that you will use its mark only to condemn the guilty. ^a-grim-peat-mound [!MINOR ARCANUM] The Tomb-bog's Mark When you use mud from the tomb-bog to mark someone with the sign of Aratis and pronounce them guilty of a crime , you can place a geas upon them. Phrase it as one of the following: - You must never again \ \ \ \ . - Henceforth, you must always seek to \ \ \ \ when you have the chance. Should they fail to follow the geas , their life is forfeit and the tomb-bog spirit will track them down and smother them. Should they prove themselves innocent to the tomb-bog spirit , your life is forfeit instead. ^the-tomb-bogs-mark [!MINOR ARCANUM] A richly woven rug ◇◇, magical About 4 feet by 7 feet unfurled, woven with stylized patterns of fire and flame. Flickering Maker-runes can be seen in the weaving, always out of focus but hinting at power. You can unlock the secrets of this rug, but you must first… - …be marked by fire, losing yourself to the panic or pain of burning. - …then, spend weeks in meditation upon the rug and studying the now-clear runes. - …allow yourself to be burned by a terrible flame, refusing to cry out and mastering your fear. ^a-richly-woven-rug [!MINOR ARCANUM] Fear of the Flame Heat: ○○○○○○ When you spend an hour or so on the rug, meditating on the fear of fire that all natural things possess , you hold Terror and mark 1 Heat. Terror : When you unleash the Terror you hold , all who see you fear you as they would fear the flame primeval, until you dismiss the effect. When you dismiss the effect , roll 1d6. If the roll is less than your Heat, then clear all your Heat and you painfully manifest a permanent sign of your connection to the flames: a blistered scar, reddish skin, glowing eyes, a sulfurous smell, etc. When a season passes without you unleashing Terror , reduce your Heat by 1. ^fear-of-the-flame [!MINOR ARCANUM] A tattered mantle ◇, warm, magical Found on a skeleton that has been stripped clean of flesh and that bears what can only be described as bite marks all along its bones. Close inspection of the mantle reveals sigils woven in silvery thread and human teeth sewn into the fringe like decorative beads. You can unlock the power of this mantle by… - …wearing it for three days and three long, long nights, suffering through the nightmares. - …bringing to heel the slavering wraiths that are tethered to it. ^a-tattered-mantle [!MINOR ARCANUM] Mantle Wraiths When you let slip the wraiths tethered to the mantle , treat them as followers. They are loathe to return to the mantle; you must spend their Loyalty or Persuade them. ^mantle-wraiths [!MINOR ARCANUM] Mantle wraiths Group (3), spirit, undead, terrifying, vicious HP 13; Armor 1 (amorphous) Damage life drain d8 ( band , ignores armor) Instinct to run rampant Special qualities powerless in daylight - Whisper their longings to the wearer - Manifest a form of shadow and cold (harmed only by silver and salt) - Hurl themselves at the living - Suck the vitality from their prey Cost souls feasted upon (Loyalty ○○○) ^mantle-wraiths-stats [!MINOR ARCANUM] Rune-etched pillars magical Deep under a giant, ruined fort is a vaulted chamber, once a storeroom but looted long, long ago. A giant skeleton lies just inside, face-down, its skull and ribs staved in. Four pillars support the roof, carved with a series of huge runes that thrum with power. You can unlock their secrets, but… - …you must spend a few days studying the runes and memorizing their sequence. - …you must meditate for a few weeks on the magic that infuses them. - …you risk triggering the spell contained in the runes, and collapsing the entire structure. ^rune-etched-pillars [!MINOR ARCANUM] Shattering Words of Aalz Childric When you carve the shattering words of Aalz Childric into stone or rock , designate a key word or phrase. When you or another speaks the key word or phrase and wills the spell to trigger , you roll +CON: on a 10+ , the rune-carved stone shatters and is immediately reduced to rubble; on a 7-9 , the stone cracks and buckles, slowly breaking apart into a number of pieces. ^shattering-words-of-aalz-childric [!MINOR ARCANUM] A huge wooden sphere immobile Half-buried and largely overgrown, about 4 feet across and weighing hundreds of pounds. It is strangely well preserved and adorned with intricate pictograms. A handful of other spheres rot nearby. The pictograms depict some sort of recipe, which you can learn but you must... - …first dig up and clean the sphere. - …spend weeks studying the glyphs. - …acquire some fresh ffyrnig root. - …acquire the bile of a wild boar. And then… …you risk getting the recipe wrong, and lethally so. ^a-huge-wooden-sphere [!MINOR ARCANUM] Ffyrnig Tonic When you pickle fresh ffyrnig root in a suspension of boar bile for two full moons , it becomes a skin of ffyrnig tonic (3 uses, magical ). When you take a draught of ffyrnig tonic, pick 1: - Regain HP equal to ½ half your max - Clear a debility Also, you have advantage on your next roll to take bold, physical action. But until you take such an action, you have disadvantage on any rolls that require patience, care, or thoughtfulness. When you drink a second draught of ffyrnig tonic before getting a good night's sleep , it has no effect other than to make you ill (mark a debility). ^ffyrnig-tonic [!MINOR ARCANUM] A scroll & a bone flute ◇, magical A scroll, written in a cramped hand and seemingly nonsensical script, with a series of graph-like diagrams. It is accompanied by a flute of bone (from a human arm?). The notes reveal themselves to be a cipher; the graph is perhaps the notation for a song. You can unlock the secrets of the scroll and flute, but… - …you must first decipher the notes, which will take a few weeks of effort. - …the first time you use the flute, you risk getting the music wrong and aggravating the dool spirit. ^a-scroll-a-bone-flute [!MINOR ARCANUM] Song of the Dool Trees When you stand among dool trees and play the proper tune on a flute of a murderer's bone , you summon a dool spirit and bind it to your service. Treat it as a follower. Only one dool spirit will serve you at a time. ^song-of-the-dool-trees [!MINOR ARCANUM] Dool spirit Spirit, terrifying, stealthy, devious HP 13; Armor 1 (amorphous) Damage feast on fear d6 ( hand , close , ignores armor, disadvantage) Instinct to take things too far Special qualities powerless in bright light - Sense a victim's doubt and worries - Shape sound and shadow to unnerve and frighten - Manifest as its victim's fears (harmed only by one who masters their fear) Cost new, exquisite fears (Loyalty ○○○) ^dool-spirit [!MINOR ARCANUM] A clear, natural pool High in the hills, a stream of snowmelt fills a deep pool in the rocks before flowing on. At the bottom of the pool is a large deposit of lodestone. Rusted old blades (many sized for giants) can be seen clinging to the lodestone. There is magic here; to unlock its secrets, either... …spend a week or so experimenting at the pool, then roll +INT: on a 10+ , you figure it out; on a 7-9 , no luck; on a 6- , you are interrupted by danger; or... …get a firsthand account of the pool's powers in use. ^a-clear-natural-pool [!MINOR ARCANUM] Ward of Still Waters Strain: ○○○○○○○ When you cut yourself with iron or steel and toss the blooded metal into the pool , you form a bond with the waters. So long as you carry a vial of the pool's water on your person, you are protected by the ward of still waters. When you are so protected and subject to magic that attempts to sense or affect your thoughts, actions, or emotions , the magic targets the still waters of the pool instead—possibly befuddling the magic's source. Each time the ward of still waters protects you, mark 1 Strain and then roll 1d6. If your roll is less than your current Strain, the water you carry loses its potency. When you return to the pool and renew your bond , clear all Strain. ^ward-of-still-waters [!MINOR ARCANUM] An oversized codex immobile , fragile A book, about 2 feet by 3 feet, just over 6 inches tall when closed. The cover is made of silvery cedarwood planks. The sheets are made of fine vellum, stretched from the hides of some enormous beast. The pages are full of calligraphy, large and precise, with many diagrams showing meditative poses and strange geometric patterns related to heat, water, and ice. To unlock the codex's mysteries, you must… - …decipher the script, written in a form of the Rime Lords' alphabet. - …spend months studying the text and practicing the techniques therein. - …risk permanently damaging your health as you master them. ^an-oversized-codex [!MINOR ARCANUM] Ice Weaving When you will dark ice to form , lose 1 HP, +1 HP for each of the following you desire: - It's enough to make a small wall or encase a person (else, it's ◇◇ at most) - It's hard as stone - It's solid to spirits, able to block/harm/hinder them Then, roll +CON: on a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1, or pick 2 and lose 2 more HP. - It forms quickly, even violently (else, it takes minutes, even hours) - It forms at up to near range (else, it forms at only hand range) - Its shape is as intricate as you can imagine (else, it's just a crude block) The ice persists until it's broken, melted by intense heat, or you let it melt. You cannot regain the spent HP while it persists. ^ice-weaving [!MINOR ARCANUM] A chain of rusty locks ◇, magical A chain, six links long, each link hung with a corroded bronze padlock. 1d4+1 locks bear a stylized face, frozen in a rusty grimace. The keys to these locks are lost to the ages. When you hold one of the face-adorned locks and look into its eyes , you can hear the voice of the departed soul bound within: hateful, desperate, pleading to be set free. You can master these locks by… - …convincing one of the shades to reveal how it was captured. - …convincing a shade to reveal what happens when a lock is opened. - …finding a way to open the locks. ^a-chain-of-rusty-locks [!MINOR ARCANUM] The Six Locks of Sajua Disturbance: ○○○○○○○ When you smear the blood of the dying onto an open Lock of Sajua and then close the lock , roll +CON: on a 10+ , their soul is bound in the lock; on a 7-9 , their soul is bound, but all mundane metal in your presence rusts or tarnishes, corrodes and weakens. When you extract a promise from a bound soul and then open the lock that binds it , the soul is compelled to fulfill the letter of its promise before it goes its own way. Tell the GM to treat it as a ghost (though not necessarily as a follower); the open lock serves as its tether, at least for now. Each time you release a soul, mark 1 Disturbance and roll 1d6. If you roll less than your Disturbance, you hear the call of the Pale Hunter in the distance. Expect a visit soon. ^the-six-locks-of-sajua [!MINOR ARCANUM] A gold ring magical This simple, golden band catches the light and shines, even in darkness. Its very presence fills goodly people with peace and hope, and servants of darkness with jealous hate. You can unlock the secrets of this ring, but must first… - …wear the ring continuously for 40 days, witnessing the sunrise each day. - …perform an act of charity while wearing the ring, one that changes the recipient's life. - …participate in the devout and proper worship of Helior. ^a-gold-ring [!MINOR ARCANUM] Helior's Shining Ring Daylight: ○○○ When you spend the better part of a day wearing the ring and worshipping Helior in full sunlight , hold 1 Daylight. When you wear the ring in shadows or darkness and spend 1 Daylight , the ring shines forth with holy light ( near , area ) that is painful for creatures of darkness to look at. It glows for a few hours, until you extinguish it, or until another effect (like the Lightbearer's Invocations) uses up its fuel or snuffs it out. Alas, the ring also shines like a psychic beacon while it shines, alerting servants of darkness for miles around to your presence and location. ^heliors-shining-ring [!MINOR ARCANUM] A path in the woods Deep in the Great Wood sits a stone, carved with the crude pictograms of the Forest Folk, showing a trail fraught with trials and leading to great power. If you set forth upon the trail at midsummer, you can unlock its mysteries, but you must... - …follow the twisting paths in silence, until you spy the White Hart. - …chase the Hart for miles and miles, until it leads you to the Tree of Nerth. - …wrestle the strange serpent that lives in the tree ( Book II ), crushing the life from it and eating its still-warm heart. Should multiple people follow the path on a given midsummer, only one of them can reach the Tree of Nerth. ^a-path-in-the-woods [!MINOR ARCANUM] Nerth Serpent's Vigor Vitality: ○○○ When you crush the life from the Nerth Serpent and devour its still-warm heart , hold 3 Vitality. While you hold Vitality, you age very slowly.
